ai_summary: Agnes Scott College offers a mixed experience for students, with many appreciating the close-knit community and the supportive professors who are genuinely invested in their students' success. Small class sizes facilitate personal connections; however, some reviews cite issues with the administration and the campus atmosphere, which some find unwelcoming or cliquish. Academically, the rigor is noted, but there are conflicting opinions on whether the support and resources match the high tuition costs, particularly for non-STEM majors. Student life appears limited, especially on weekends, and many students express dissatisfaction with the dining options and overall facilities. Despite these challenges, some students highlight the unique opportunities for leadership and growth available at Agnes Scott, alongside a focus on diversity and inclusivity within the student body. Overall, potential students are encouraged to evaluate both the strengths and weaknesses of the college before making a decision.

reviews.text:
My overall student experience at Agnes Scott has been wonderful. I appreciate the small classroom sizes so each student has a personal relationship with each person. The professors are accommodating and care about students' livelihoods and education. Also, the campus is lovely and likes to engage in its local community, which is great!
Some things I would like to see change is an improvement in the student housing situations. The Residence Life departments need to be improved, as well as the living situations. On campus housing is by no means inhabitable, but there are a lot of maintenance issues with pipes, elevators, etc.

ASC is great depending on the person. It is very expensive and even though you get a scholarship for the first year it is very hard to keep it. The dorms are deteriorating and there is no AC or elevator. Commute if possible, decatur is a lovely place and worth your visit. Food is horrendous but there are plenty of walkable places off campus. Social life is incredibly clique-y, intimidating, and awkward if you don’t know people prior to attending. I am majoring in Biology with an interest in marine science and my professor is incredibly knowledgeable and passionate, but I can’t say the same for all profs. Regarding the current political climate, ASC has promised to protect and provide for all of their students regardless of identity. However, being a women’s college, there are a significant amount of transphobic students. I have never had a problem with professors or admin being transphobic and they are very accommodating and care about your mental health.
I love Agnes Scott! I have had the best experience so far! From Journeys to SCALE to where I am now such an amazing college and I am so grateful to have community like this!

So far, I'm really enjoying my time at Agnes Scott. Everyone here is so welcoming, and the location couldn't be more convenient. Whether I need to grab groceries or head out for a bite to eat, the downtown area is just steps away from campus. I truly love everything about this school. I'd definitely recommend it to anyone who prefers a smaller, close-knit community and appreciates the charming, almost Harry Potter like atmosphere.
I like how helpful everyone is and the amount of resources available for students. All my professors are kind and understanding.

Wonderful school! Very warm and welcoming. The professors and students are smart and dedicated. Almost all staff are accommodating and kind. There are always fun events happening on campus. Party life is next to none. Food is decent for college food. Surrounding food options are extremely good and affordable. Try Noodle and Poké Bar. Don't go to Rebel Teahouse. My biggest gripes with Agnes are (1), limited dining options, and (2), poor building maintenance. It can take a very long time for Agnes to respond to dorm repair requests if they ever do. Classrooms vary between very hot and very cold, and the AC breaks sometimes. The elevator in my freshman dorm building was broken for the entire year (still is), and I had to move out of my dorm hauling boxes down the stairs. Elevators all across campus break extremely often. And (3), protocol for dealing with bad roommates isn't great. That experience was hellish for me, partly because Agnes did not handle it as well as they should have.

My life is a horror movie. it makes me so sad to see girls come here expecting a cool indie liberal arts college experience and watch their spirits get crushed in real time. Go to a women's college but do not go to agnes scott college. Resident evil food, moldy showers, rat infested kitchens (this is why mollies grill closes down all the time! we have rats and they keep it a secret), awful awful awful administration who only cares about student wellbeing as long as we are happy enough to keep paying tuition. if you're coming here on scholarship, be cautious because tuition rises 3k a year and yes you will have to pay for it. almost everyone loses scholarship after their first year, especially pell grant. i lost my scholarship unexpectedly with a 3.8 GPA (not for any reason other than the school couldn't afford it) and had to take this fall semester off to find another school because tuition is insane. off campus housing is a myth, so are single rooms. Elevators and AC don't work ever

I wish I could tell my past self to not choose Agnes. There’s not many opportunities for students who want to pursue a career within STEM, and if you want to do engineering or computer science, you have to enroll in dual degree programs at either Emory or GA Tech, which are very difficult and time-consuming as a working student that’s lower income. I had a bad roommate my first year, and nothing was done by Residence Life to accommodate me despite meetings; housing and dining is costly for what you get overall, and the process to live off-campus is made difficult for students. Plus, there’s no payment plans for the health insurance if you can’t afford it, you will be dropped automatically from classes if you can’t pay by the due date. The student body is quite cliquish, and I found it hard to connect with other people, despite attending events and joining clubs. I regret the money and private loans taken out to attend a lackluster campus with nothing to gain in return.

Please please please do not fall for the marketing. I did. The campus is gorgeous and that's the only good thing I can say about this place, seriously. Never in my life have I felt so disregarded by an institution, thinking about how much money I wasted here makes me cry. The meal plan is upwards of 7k a year for an awful dining hall that's never open. The staff is severely underpaid and the student organized living wage campaign was banned as an organization. P. Zak is not a good person. Prepare to pay insane amounts of money for moldy shoebox dorms, which BTW you will share a bedroom with someone all four years unless you get medical accommodations for a single. Journey's is cool but I personally had an awful experience as the prof leading mine had multiple allegations against her and treated students of color terribly, which the school has yet to do anything about (yes she has been reported several times). Go somewhere that cares about you as a student and not a well of money.

Truly a school that encourages your curiosity for so many different subjects, cultures, and experiences that I don’t believe High School prepared us for. Here, with every event, SCALE experience or class, you become less afraid of the world and grown more into who you are and the leader you could become.

As a freshman at Agnes Scott College, my experience so far has been welcoming and exciting. The community is really supportive everyone from professors to upperclassmen wants to see you succeed. I’ve felt comfortable asking questions and getting help when I need it, which made the transition from high school to college a lot easier.
I really enjoy the small class sizes and how the professors take time to get to know you. The campus is beautiful, and there are always events going on, so it’s easy to get involved and meet people. The SUMMIT program has already helped me think more globally and gain confidence in my leadership skills.
One thing I’d like to see improve is having more weekend activities and maybe more variety in the dining hall. But overall, I’ve had a really good start and I’m excited for what’s ahead!

In my experience at Agnes Scott College, I witnessed my best friend become hospitalized for food poisoning, I was stalked by other students on campus and told police who did nothing, was heavily bullied, my roommate would scream at me and I was ignored for months despite sleeping in my car, and I even had an art professor have to teach a hybrid class due to the fact that she had 7 title nines against her, keeping her from being able to teach in a class with students without supervision. My safety was constantly at risk while I was a student on campus, and it was very concerning how the campus refused to close at night, even just by locking the gates. There have even wild stories of students being followed back to campus at night and random people entering the dorm halls, which goes without any proof since there aren't cameras in the dorm rooms or halls. Do not let the marketing team fool you: this school is a trap and a nightmare, I was diagnosed with PTSD after leaving!

If you care about getting in, getting your degree for some serious tuition $$$, and getting out, this is the school for you. I really wouldn't recommend Agnes to anyone who wants to have a good time while getting their undergrad. I will say, the school is extremely small which makes it a lot easier to succeed within the community, but that doesn't mean much to the rest of the world. I hate to write a review like this, but I also would hate to see a dozen more girls come here under false pretenses and be as disappointed as I (and many others) have been. This is not a school for normal people. I will lay it out flat: furries are everywhere. Everyone is extremely woke, and I'm a leftist myself, but it really does come to a point. The arguments here made me want to rip my hair out. These girls can be really immature and the environment is extremely negative at times. Going out was never really a priority for me, but with every passing day I wish more and more that I went somewhere else.

I went here for 4 years of my life and it ruined me. The school shrinks with each class and it’s like reliving the worst days of middle and high school. There is no community as the board ruins everything and takes your money to do nothing with it. I hated this school.

I would give anything to go back in time and warn myself not to come here. ASC operates about as efficiently as a girl scout camp; super strict and prioritizes not letting students leave rather than improving student life. Transferring was made to be a very difficult process but I'm so glad I did it. I wish I could wipe my memory this school actually left a stain on my life and on my resume.

ai_summary: Reviews of Allegheny College consistently highlight the strong academic environment , characterized by rigorous courses and dedicated professors who are genuinely invested in student success. Many students appreciate the small class sizes , which facilitate personal connections and offer ample support during office hours. The college’s focus on interdisciplinary learning —requiring a major and a minor from different divisions—encourages critical thinking and broadens academic exploration. However, there are concerns about the administration's responsiveness to student needs, particularly around issues like financial aid and campus safety. The campus, set in the small town of Meadville, is described as beautiful and conducive to building close-knit relationships, although the local area offers limited off-campus activities. Socially, students find a balance between academics and involvement in campus life, with a variety of clubs and events, although some express a desire for more engaging activities. Overall, while Allegheny College provides a supportive and academically challenging environment, it faces challenges in administrative responsiveness and local engagement.

reviews.text:
Attended 2005-2009 and loved my experience there. Just like everything else in life, you get out what you put in.
Small liberal arts school in a college town. A lot of personalized attention from faculty and staff. Curriculum can be demanding and the senior comprehensive project is the final hurdle, but we are there to learn, right? When not studying, there are plenty of on-campus extracurricular activities that will never leave you bored. You will make lifelong friends while there, and even after graduation, form instant friendships with any alumni you meet since it is rare to find another "Gator" in a sea of graduates from larger universities. The alumni network may be small but it is strong, and alumni will go out of their way to help each other out.
Definitely a hidden gem to seriously consider.

Allegheny College provided the groundwork for a highly rewarding career in medicine and healthcare administration. Having graduated in 1986 with a degree in chemistry (I was pre-med) and a Spanish minor, I was extremely well-prepared for both medical school and my later career as a healthcare executive.
My experience at Allegheny provided the groundwork for lifelong learning and the development of strong analytical and writing skills. I felt deeply engaged and supported during my time at Allegheny due to the small class sizes and highly engaged professors. Beyond academics, Allegheny was a place where I forged lasting life-long friendships. It is a truly remarkable place, and the shared experience among its graduates creates a committed network of alums and bonds that last a lifetime.

It was four of the best years of my life. Allegheny prepared me for the future. With no idea what my plans were the US Army called so I signed up. After that it was into sales in the textile industry in 1969. Still in the industry. So often some of the best compliments paid to me are my ease in communicating with suppliers and customers, which makes me feel proud that the Allegheny days were great for me and helped me to have a great career.

Choosing Allegheny College was one of the best decisions I’ve ever made. From day one, I felt supported, challenged, and genuinely cared for as both a student and a person. As a resident advisor, I built meaningful relationships and developed leadership skills I still use today. Serving as a teacher’s assistant allowed me to work closely with professors who truly invested in my growth and pushed me to reach my fullest potential.
Being a member of an athletics team taught me discipline, teamwork, and time management, while also giving me friendships that will last a lifetime. Allegheny’s community is special—faculty know you by name, students support one another, and you never feel like just a number. Beyond the classroom, I gained real-world experience through two incredible local internships, and the college’s caring alumni network consistently showed up to support and mentor students.
Allegheny didn’t just prepare me for a job; it helped shape me into the person I am today!

I graduated in 1964. I was introduced to Allegheny by my father, also attended, around age 10 when he took me for the first time with him for a reunion. You could say we are an Allegheny Family. My Dad '29, his three brothers, cousins, my Brother "68 and his wife '69, and my son 1999, all attended Allegheny. The college gave us a stepping stone into a variety of fields. My Dad worked for Bell Telephone of PA followed by my brother who eventually worked for Verizon. I went on Jefferson Medical College and then a career in Orthopedic surgery. My son became a PhD in Neuroscience because of his Allegheny experience. He worked at Hershey Medical Center and now is at Novartis Pharmaceuticals in Boston. We all got a firm grounding in being able to perform well in our chosen professions from our Allegheny experience.
I took the science courses needed to attend medical school and then added enough History, English and "Liberal Arts" that to this day I use as guidelines for life.

Outstanding educational and social learning experience!! Attending Allegheny College in Meadville, PA was incredibly reward with professors who truly care about student success. Small class sizes foster meaningful discussions and close connections, while the liberal arts curriculum encourages curiosity and growth across disciplines. The beautiful historic campus, surrounded by nature, creates an inspiring environment for learning and reflection. Student organizations, athletics, and diverse cultural events provide opportunities to build lifelong friendships. Allegheny’s emphasis on both academic excellence and personal development makes it a place where students thrive and leave prepared for the global workplace.

Allegheny was one of the best decisions I made. The academics were great. Class sizes were small and the Professors knew and cared about their students. I continue to maintain a friendly relationship with several Professors years after I graduated, and I know from friends who attended other colleges this is unique to Allegheny. The senior thesis (i.e. comp) still serves as a talking point whenever you meet an alum years after graduation, and the rigors of the reading, critical thinking/analysis, writing, and the discussions rather than lectures in class prepared me extremely well for law school so that I was running circles around my law school classmates.
Socially, there were frat parties and on campus houses parties, dozens of clubs to join, and the college hosted events. Meadville itself wasn't too big, but Erie was only 30 minutes away. Peak N Peak skiing as a gym class was fun. Greek life exists but isn't overwhelming, and people are friends regardless of whether they were Greek.

Allegheny College is an absolute gem. I had a great experience as a student. Initially I was hesitant to go there; looking back it was a life changing decision. The campus is beautiful during all four seasons. I always felt safe walking around campus.
The academic experience helped me to explore areas I didn’t know I had interest in. As a political science major, I took advantage of a partnership with American University for a semester. I was able to get that “big city” experience and return to Meadville.
Each summer I was able to secure an internship for credits. This was vital to helping figure out how I would start my career. It also helped me realize areas I didn’t enjoy. I am happy I figured that out prior to graduation.
The majority of the professors and staff are invested in providing opportunities for students. I worked in different departments and volunteered to help with alumni weekend, graduation & conferences. I am grateful to be Gator!

Allegheny was an ideal blend of experiences for me - academically challenging and inspiring, with small classes, engaged professors and high expectations; a small student body where it was easy to form deep friendships; a beautiful campus that felt like a magical little bubble; and a fun extracurricular and social scene. I lived on campus all four years, which not everyone did when I was a student, and while I loved the creaky old buildings I am glad that there are many modern options now and that Brooks Hall is getting an update. I'm also glad that the college has worked hard to expand diversity on many levels since I was a student - racial, ethnic, income and geographic. Allegheny offered me excellent paths to internships and study abroad, all of which made my college life richer and helped me have a job lined up immediately. I made lifelong friends at Allegheny and love being involved as an alumni. It's a special place - visit and you'll want to be a part of it.

My experience at Allegheny College fostered significant personal and academic growth. Transitioning from an inner-city school to a smaller town was challenging, but the supportive staff made the adjustment easier and helped me feel welcome in the community. Their commitment to student success was evident, and I recognize that I could have benefited even more by utilizing the resources offered.
As an alum, I believe Allegheny College effectively prepared me for professional life. The liberal arts education provided adaptability and equipped me to collaborate successfully in team environments. I am grateful for the four years I spent as an Allegheny Gator.
Overall, my time at Allegheny College was transformative, and I am confident that the skills and values I gained will continue to serve me well in the future.

I loved my Allegheny experience! Allegheny prepared me well for medical school especially in terms of writing, leadership, communication, and critical thinking skills. I really appreciated the community at Allegheny and would recommend Allegheny to anyone who is looking for amazing faculty mentorship, early work experience/internships for future careers, and opportunities to grow as a person, student, and citizen.

I came to Allegheny College from philly, and it ended up being one of the best decisions I made. The professors genuinely cared, and the classes pushed me to think in ways that have carried over into my career. I loved getting involved in the Meadville community too. The town sometimes gets a bad rap, but I found it full of welcoming people, local spots with character, and chances to really make a difference. The social scene was okay, though not the main focus, and campus safety and administration were quick to shut things down. The freshman dorms honestly suck, but the upperclassman housing is much better once you get there. Overall, Allegheny gave me experiences, friendships, and a foundation that still shape who I am today.

The support from the professors was incredible. I felt they really took an active interest in my classroom success and life/job success. I left Allegheny feeling extremely prepared to handle myself in any business setting. You will get a well rounded education and the tools needed to be successful. Just have to put up with the Meadville winters (which are beautiful!).

Allegheny provided me with much of the foundation for pretty much the rest of my life. Its strong academics challenged me to broaden my world and learn how to problem-solve. It taught me how to communicate my ideas effectively and how to stand up for them, or, if necessary, modify them when I learned more.
The relationships I made at the school have greatly enriched my life and have lasted these many years since graduation, which is a testament to how the school helps foster them.
It is a place where the professors push you to explore your limits (as I did).
I think it provides a huge value for the money spent -- the tuition dollars you spend here are an investment in yourself and in your future. Looking back at my time there, I can safely say that my investment has paid dividends multiple times over.

Allegheny College is a perfect place to immerse yourself in learning and set the stage for your future. I had the most incredible experiences with professors in all disciplines across campus who were not only interesting and engaging, but deeply invested in our success as their students. Class sizes were such that we knew the professors well and felt comfortable reaching out during class or office hours to discuss our coursework or continue an interesting conversation sparked during class.
Outside of class, I built the most incredible relationships with fellow students that are still among the best of friends today. We were all very involved in various organizations on campus and greek life, which played a big role in the social scene (during my day at least!).
I still cannot believe that we did not take full advantage of the gorgeous outdoor surroundings! Meadville has stunning natural surroundings - maybe I was just too busy studying! I cannot recommend Allegheny enough.

ai_summary: Amherst College stands out for its rigorous academics and strong community connections, with many students highlighting the personalized attention from professors and the collaborative environment fostered by small class sizes. The Open Curriculum is frequently praised for allowing students the freedom to explore their interests without the constraints of core requirements. While the campus offers a beautiful setting and significant resources, some reviews pointed out challenges in the social scene, noting that it can be dominated by athletic groups and that there may be difficulties in inclusion for some identities. Dining options received mixed feedback, with many students feeling they could be improved. Despite these critiques, many still found the experience rewarding, particularly in terms of networking opportunities and the supportive alumni base.

reviews.text: They hype the small-school mentoring but it is pretty weak here. An upperclassman recently told me I might not get any research opportunities in my four years here, and I now see that could definitely be the case. From professors to advisors to the Career Center, no one has given me real help with opportunities and my high GPA doesn't seem to matter. If I can't find a way to get professional experience, I'll graduate at a real disadvantage. If you're considering Amherst, make sure you ask a lot of questions and get real info not propaganda. My friends at Ivies and my state flagship honors college have sailed into amazing internship opportunities, while I have nothing so far. Maybe my luck will change, maybe it won't.

At Amherst college I am a student athlete. I have really enjoyed my time at Amherst mainly because of the excellent professors, and the positive social enivornment. Many of my peers are not only kind, but they are also academically motivated which has pushed me to work hard in return. Amherst also has a great career center I have taken full advantage of in finding internships, and a great alumni network. The alumni from Amherst are very willing and open to speak and help students through their careers. Additionally, there is a very close-knit network and community at Amherst because it is a small school. Not only are the students close, but students are close with facility members, athletic advisors, and the alumni. Furthermore, the food at Amherst has significantly improved this past year with the new head of food, he makes sure that the food is both delicious and healthy which is important for athletes such as myself. Overall, Amherst has been an amazing, rewarding experience.

Out of all the schools in the country, I feel that Amherst College is one of the schools that cares for its students the most. The support students receive financially, administratively, and emotionally, makes higher education achievable for all students. Plus the food is pretty great

Amherst has an incredible balance between being a very prestigious school that I feel proud to attend, and also maintaining a campus culture where it's easy to get involved in anything you might hope to do. You're truly encouraged to explore your interests here.
The student body is tiny, which means it's hard to feel lonely because you see people everywhere. This is wonderful 90% of the time, except if you want to avoid someone.
People go out, but parties are very small and contained, with the exception maybe of some sports team parties. I personally love this aspect, because I always feel safe, especially with the school's amnesty policy. But if you're into crazier nights out, this may be a deterring factor for you.
Don't let the dining comments sway you–– Val is honestly pretty good and they do the best they can, though I hear dining is expected to improve in the next few years.
Ethnic diversity could be better, especially for Native students, but financial aid is amazingly good.

At Amherst College, it is the people here that enhance your experience. You meet people that has a lasting impact on your career or life. You fall in love with the people that makes you feel attached to this college. The college does put money into its students, the amount of money the school will put into student activities and resources exceeds that of 4 other schools in the area. The academic are challenging but the professors are accommodating. I highly recommend the ethic studies departments because the professor in these departments are stellar.

I really enjoyed my time at Amherst College. The open curriculum and a wide variety of courses allow you to explore all your interests. The environment is very supportive and the school is always hosting various events to engage the students to foster community. The school is working to bridge the athlete/ non-athlete divide but I think nonetheless it exists. The dining hall food could also use some diversity.

Really grateful for this school. Invaluable academics and lifelong connections. The classes are hard (in different ways for humanities and STEM), but if you work hard and show up the professors want to see you do well. You can build strong personal connections with professors; I am still in contact with several even a year after graduating.
I was a freshman during covid so dining and social scene are wayy better than what we were used to, but if it’s not enough for you you can hop on over the Umass.
My favorite thing about Amherst is that there is a very strong sense of community, it’s not one of those gigantic universities where people stare straight through each other. And there is a real feeling that the school is looking out for students and wants to take care of us. Of course it’s still an institution, but there is way more by way of student care, events, free food, and also spaces that facilitate difficult and important conversations than I have seen at any other school.

Took classes as a five-college student in 2022 and 2023. The first one, a high-level English class, was terrible. Without even meeting me, Prof. A. B. emailed, "This class had included international students and non-first-language-speakers in the past, but those who succeded have had near-native fluency in English". He questioned my fluency for not taking a 400-level course, though it wasn’t a prerequisite. I had to interview to prove myself. I reported it in 2023, then had a virtual meeting with their staff, but heard nothing after. It's their so-called DEI. I had a pleasant experience with the college's French professor later. Still, I am teaching English not French to "native speakers" today.
Loved how close knit you became with professors! I think the social scene could be improved to cater to more diverse options instead of "frat-like" parties.

The community is very small, meaning you see the same people around campus all the time and are basically guaranteed to see someone you know in the (one) dining hall on campus. I think this fosters a good campus community, but sometimes it can feel limiting. Luckily, the surrounding area is really nice, with a good amount of small-town things to do if you look. It's definitely not like being in a city, but friends and I had fun going apple picking, strawberry picking, and visiting the Yankee Candle Factory. The fall foliage is truly beautiful, and if you are a nature person, this school is for you! Boston is also ~2 hours away if you really need to visit a major urban environment. The financial aid is excellent if you qualify, and funding for on-campus jobs and summer internships is fantastic!
Dining hall food really needs to be better, but academic life was amazing. Great professors who really care about your education and research opportunities.

Amherst College is often celebrated for its rigorous academics, close-knit community, and the beauty of its New England campus. Students frequently highlight the small class sizes, which foster a collaborative environment where professors are accessible and deeply engaged with their students. The Open Curriculum, a distinctive feature of Amherst, allows students to shape their education without being bound by required core classes, offering them a high degree of intellectual freedom. Many students appreciate this flexibility, as it encourages exploration and allows for a more personalized academic journey.
The college's commitment to diversity and inclusion is also noteworthy. Amherst has worked to create an environment where students from a wide variety of backgrounds feel welcome, supported, and valued. While the college is small, it offers a strong sense of community and intellectual curiosity that resonates with many students.

Coming from the Midwest, it was a big decision to come to Amherst, but one that I am incredibly happy with. I decided to go to Amherst after I visited campus (which Amherst paid for) and everyone on campus-- professors, students, and faculty-- were all very helpful and excited about their experience at Amherst. Professors care more about their classes and their students than any other teachers I've ever had. The workload is definitely not easy in any sense of the word (at least as a Biology major), but it is for the most part achievable and professors are kind and understanding. You'll have to work hard, but it will pay off. You learn a lot. The party scene is good if you're an athlete, and good half the time if you aren't an athlete. My main bad thing about Amherst is that there is a pretty significant wage barrier, which means that half of the students are out of touch. But there is also a lot of first gen/low income students, so you'll still find your place. I'd choose Amherst again

Being at Amherst College has been one of the most fulfilling times of my life. The people I interact with every day add a unique perspective to my life, the professors I am taught by are not only incredible educators but also understanding, compassionate, and lively people who genuinely want to help and engage with their students. I could not ask for a better school, and I am so happy that I chose Amherst. Go Mammoths!

I loved my time at Amherst. I was there for the conversations with interesting people -- and I got them. So many fascinating people, who wanted to talk to me about all these fascinating things! My friends and I would camp out in the campus cafeteria when it opened for dinner and folks would cycle through our table -- eat, discuss, talk, and keep going. Professors were the same way -- always open to challenges with office hours. Those late night studying and doing homework and realizing something new and incredible -- that was why I wanted an education, and that's what I got. Sure, the party scene was a bit lackluster, and campus culture sports could've used more energy, but it was so nice otherwise. I loved my time at Amherst, and hope that future students will have the same wonderful time.
Also access to high levels of power through the alumni network? Fantastic. there's a whole world out there, making decisions on the rest of the populace. And it was Amherst that showed me how.

ai_summary: Augustana College - Illinois offers students a vibrant campus environment characterized by a close-knit community and supportive faculty. Many reviews highlight the strong academic programs , particularly emphasizing the accessibility and dedication of professors, which fosters personal connections and encourages student success. While the liberal arts curriculum is appreciated for its breadth, some students express concerns about the quality of facilities, outdated housing , and issues with campus services, particularly in residential life and registration processes. Student life is lively, with numerous extracurricular activities available, although the party culture and location in a quieter area are seen by some as drawbacks. Overall, students value the supportive atmosphere and the numerous opportunities for involvement and personal growth, while also expressing concerns about tuition and living conditions.

reviews.text: I am currently attending Augustana and I have loved my experience so far. All my teachers are amazing and they are so willing to help their students. I also love the campus as it is filled with nature and you often see black squirrels, deer, and raccoons. I do not have much negative to say about Augie, other than to be sure to bring a fan if you are put in Andreen or Seminary Hall as those buildings do not have AC and it will get very hot. Also, I for one like the food, but some of my classmates are not big fans. I will admit that sometimes there are not much options for food, so I do end up leaving the dinning hall hungry, but luckily we have a market on campus that is basically a "gas station" style store, with plentiful smaller meals and snacks that you can purchase with your meal swipe.

Stay as far away as you possibly can from Augustana. They are a complete scam. This college used to be a very good college back in the 70s and 80s but nowadays they are a money-grabbing institution that gives no care to their students. I am in the Engineering program with disability accommodations and they failed to help me in choosing classes telling me to choose these classes instead of the main Engineering courses. They also told us they were and ABET accredited institution but it turns out they were declined and had to reapply. They also mistreat their students with disabilities by trapping you in rooms and treating you like garbage. They only care about their celebrity students and not the regulars. I wasted a semester of my college life and planning on transferring to College of DuPage for a quality education.
DO NOT APPLY FOR THIS CORRUPT INSTITUTION!!!

Augustana College has been a good for me because it has been letting me enjoy time outside and walk to all my classes. To me it’s like taking walks back at home but these are to different buildings and they aren’t from each other either. The dorm are so nice and not too small they are pretty spacious and you can’t hear your neighbors.
The liberal arts foundation really helped me focus on my interests, and I was able to be a triple major and graduate in four years while also being involved in several extracurriculars!

As an education major, I had the best faculty I could have asked for. The professors genuinely cared about the students and were invested in working together. Without them, I would not have been nearly as prepared as I was when I graduated. In fact, I had a job lined up in the area before I even graduated!
I also adored the Spanish department. While I did not have every professor, I had experience with many and was able to pass the Spanish-language requirements for a teacher with ease. My academic experience was great.
I cannot really comment on student life because I was much more interested in learning, but I did work in the special collections section of the library and I worked for one of the Spanish professors, aiding her in analyzing her data for her research study. I was also the treasurer for Latinos Unidos. I made lifelong friends there simply through the orientation group I had at the start of freshman year. I loved my time at Augustana!

Being on campus is like being a part of a close-knit community of 2000+. Not only are the professors extremely committed to ensuring that you get your tuition's worth, but the administrative body is also extremely eager to listen to any issues you may have. I didn't encounter a single issue at Augustana College that left me feeling alone or unheard. The only thing I was a bit iffy on is the campus food, but the dining staff is 100% open to suggestions.

I was the first in my family to go to college and I felt a little lost when I first got to Augustana. I found amazing support from my professors and other people I met here. I was able to graduate with a double major and a minor, and I look back with only happy memories of my experience.

What I like about this college is the size. There is about less than 3,000 students, so in the classroom you can truly have a connection with the professor and learn deeper than you would at any bigger school. Also, because of the size you truly meet so many people and find a true community that supports you. As an athlete, it is a great experience to be able to obtain a great education while maintaining time for your sport.

Augustana College is a great place to further your education. The Quad-City area provides ample opportunities to be involved in the community. The professors show care for their students both through academic support and by forming connections with their students. Their are plenty student groups and organizations that students can become involved in. The party culture is present enough if you would like to involve yourself in that, but separated enough that you don't feel like you have to if you don't want to. The downsides are minimal. The wifi is spotty at times and some of the first-year dorms are not cared for as well as others. The food in the dining hall is good (for dining hall food at least). Overall, it's a great school and a great place to be.

Augustana College – Illinois offers a supportive and close-knit academic environment with small class sizes and dedicated professors who genuinely care about student success. The campus is beautiful, and there are plenty of clubs, events, and opportunities for involvement. I appreciated the strong sense of community and the focus on personal and academic growth. However, I think the college could improve by offering more course options in technology-related fields, expanding support for international students, and enhancing mental health services. Overall, it’s a great place for students who value connection and well-rounded education.

When I first chose Augustana College, I was drawn in by its straightforward application process and welcoming environment for international students.
I pursued a major in Multimedia Journalism and Mass Communication (MJMC) with a minor in Communication Studies, motivated by my deep interest in storytelling, media, and the power of representation.
Some of my most meaningful experiences came through extracurriculars and campus involvement. Working at the Augustana Observer taught me the responsibility and urgency of student journalism. Serving as a Cultural House Coordinator allowed me to help create inclusive spaces for students of color. As an intern in the college’s Communication and Marketing department, I developed real-world content, storytelling strategies, and learned how to collaborate across institutional goals. Volunteering at Campus Closet gave me a chance to give back to the community in small but impactful ways.

A small college with diversity. Professors will help you a lot. The campus life is great but there is not much to do around the area. It is very slow-paced so it's mostly peaceful. However, the school does focus on student life and there are many activities going around all the time on the campus. Greek life is prevalent here, so there are parties going around. There are lots of clubs for you to join and feel included. The dorm can be bad, especially for the first year, because they are old and don't have AC, westerlin however is in better condition. But all of the dorms have their own pros and cons. The food is okay, there are good days and bad days but you definitely have a lot of options. The campus is mostly safe with the public safety walking around and not that much crime around the area maybe some car thefts. Dorm life might not be the best because of the conditions of some of the rooms but it makes so much easier to be a part of a community and socialize and have friends.

I enjoy everything about the school, from the dorms and the food to the classes and professors. The school is located on a beautiful campus and there are tons of on-campus activities going on all the time. Only negative is that they keep raising prices, which will make it unaffordable in the future.

Augustana College has been a very good experience for me. I believe that the environment of a small campus has allowed me to grow closer to a lot of people and professors. The student life outside of campus is very strong and inclusive, and the facilities are great.
I really like the classes and academics. I don't know much about other environments because I don't party or go out like that.

ai_summary: Austin College offers a unique experience characterized by a strong emphasis on rigorous academics and a close-knit community. Many reviews highlight the small class sizes , which facilitate personal connections with professors, creating an environment where students feel supported and encouraged. The college is noted for its excellent liberal arts education , particularly in pre-med and humanities programs, but there are calls for more attention to fields like business and arts. Student life is described as active, with numerous clubs and organizations, though some find it cliquey and note that Sherman, the surrounding town, lacks entertainment options. While the collegiate experience is deemed enriching, some students express dissatisfaction with outdated dorms and cafeteria food , suggesting room for improvement in these areas. Overall, Austin College is viewed as a suitable choice for those seeking a personalized education in a smaller, community-oriented setting.

reviews.text: I like that it has community feel. I play baseball here and love the camaraderie among the team. You are assigned a mentor your freshman year that guides you through your entire 4 years, helps with classes or any additional help needed.
I am enjoying my time here. Most of my professors are amazing, but there are definitely some that are not my favorite. The dorms are not the best, but they could be worse.

Overall a very good experience with professors and courses offered, received a lot of support as a STEM major and student-athlete. Good balance of school, athletic, and social life offered, with a variety of clubs and areas for all different types of people.

Austin College is my home away from home. Close enough so that I don’t feel homesick, it’s an excellent place to get a higher education. I majored in biology, and all my professors were excellent and taught me great things about the world. I never felt disrespected or discouraged from pursuing my dreams and goals. Even when COVID disrupted my freshman and sophomore years, I still felt so much love from the professors and staff. The students were excellent, and I met many lifelong friends through college. The surrounding city of Sherman is a decent place, with many great local restaurants, though the night life isn’t readily obvious unless you go looking for it. Overall, Austin College is a great place to get your undergrad education, and I’m proud to be a Roo!
It is a great liberal arts experience, the professors are the best. When it comes to learning and getting a quality education this is what you are looking for in a professional college.

I love the small campus community that allows everyone to get to know everyone no matter your major. It’s a close-knit community that gives students the power to always have a helping hand. I am athlete, yet made friends with people from all walks of life.

Austin College, is a school that promotes academic growth and integrity, where it lacks they always make up for it with the strong community and easy access to staff. They also look out for the students with disabilities and help out when students cannot afford to go on one of their study abroad trips.
Austin College is a very diverse but small college. Everyone is welcoming and the environment feels like home.

Austin College is an amazing college if you prioritize rigorous academics over pretty much everything else pertaining to the "college experience". The professors and courses are phenomenal, and the small class sizes make it really easy to foster relationships with other students and professors. However, Sherman as a town is not ideal. The town doesn't care for the college nor are there any job opportunities in the town for college graduates, but you're about 40 minutes from Collin County and an hour from Dallas, so it could be worse. The caf is okay but lacks variety. Many people after freshman year just opt to skip the meal plan and make their own food. Student life is fine, but I found it was a bit "clique-y". There's a pretty good selection of student orgs to join, plus an okay Greek Life scene for a school this small. Overall, if you want an amazing academic experience and are willing to give up the "college experience" to do so, AC is for you.

Austin College is a small school, giving access to a small student to teacher ratio. This aspect was great for me because as an introvert, I had a hard time meeting new people and making friends. However, at a small school like Austin College, everyone knows everyone, making it a family-like student body. The education here was wonderful. The professors are great at teaching in a way that helps you actually learn and make the topics interesting. My grades were fairly good in highschool, but after entering Austin College, I have been maintaining a 4.0 with much less effort than how much I put in highschool.
It was great, nice place very comfortable if your looking for something nice and something that feels welcoming and understanding this is the place.

The best liberal arts college in the south hands down. The campus is beautiful year round, the professors love teaching and working with students, and the campus community is friendly and inviting. While were not very good at most athletics, the student populace still will show out to all the sporting events. We also won an online tournament to determine the best Division 3 party life scene from 2023-2024. Greek life is strong on campus and there is always something new to do. Academics are challenging but worth it. One thing I would like to see change is for the Business department to receive more love and attention from the administration as a large portion of the student body takes business related classes, but sometimes it feels as thought the pre-med students have a wider range of opportunities presented to them by the school.

I have been a student at Austin College for a semester now, and I can confidently say that I am enjoying my experience! The faculty truly makes the school. All the professors I have had have been vastly diverse in their methods of teaching, and each has taught me how to become a better college student. The class sizes, which usually don't surpass 25 students, have allowed me to connect with my peers easily and have facilitated the transition from high school. However, if there is one thing I would change, it would be the quality and maintenance of the dorms, as well as the food in the cafeteria. Some of the dorms should be renovated, while the food could use some more variety. Overall, I am happy to attend Austin College, and I am looking forward to spending my next years in this beautiful campus!

The surrounding community has a lot of drugs, and there are certain groups at school that strongly struggle with drugs. Known drug dealers were attending this school, some living on campus. I give a very low rating and want to spread caution for anyone wanting to go here because the safety of students is not a priority before the reputation of the school when it comes to upper management that deals with cases. In my time here, many girls who came forward about serious incidents were dismissed and things were swept under the rug. This was such an issue that an instagram page of girls sharing their stories of injustice when asking for help from the now former dean. The higher ups do not take title iv seriously.
There are some professors who really do care and are very understanding when life things happen. There is free counseling available on campus. They are understanding with medical leaves due to mental health.
I've only visited the college for sports but i've visited like 3 times. I like the college community they always makes sure that everyone is included.

ai_summary: Bard College offers a complex and often polarizing experience for students. Academically, many reviews highlight the strength of the faculty and the small class sizes , which facilitate deep discussions and personalized learning . The college promotes critical thinking and interdisciplinary studies, making it appealing for intellectually-driven students. However, the social scene tends to be cliquey and can feel isolating, which contributes to a sense of loneliness, especially for those who struggle to make connections early on. The campus is described as beautiful and surrounded by nature, enhancing the overall college experience, but some students express frustrations about poor housing conditions , inadequate dining options, and a lack of nearby amenities. While Bard is noted for its robust student support services, critiques regarding the student body’s social dynamics, particularly involving wealth and elitism, are frequent. Overall, Bard is recognized as a unique institution that is best suited for those seeking a rigorous academic environment and who thrive in a more subdued social setting.

reviews.text: Bard is a really interesting place. If you want to be in the arts, its a great place to get very connected to artists in the north east, especially NYC. You're either going to love or hate the student culture. People are liberal in a very unique way. You'll find some of the most intelligent and kind people you will ever meet at Bard, you will also find some of the most insufferable and "performative" ones on the planet. The professors are total gems. Bard has the ability to attract incredibly interesting, accomplished, and well connected professors - they are probably the best thing about the school. Before you go to Bard, talk to 3 current students. One from NYC, one from LA, and one that isn't white. That should give you a good feel for what you're getting yourself into.

Bard is a truly unique and amazing place, there's no where else like it. I am in the Conservatory program as a voice major, and I've learned more from Rufus in the past year than I have in all of my previous years of music education combined. As for the poetry program, my other major, the faculty are all brilliant. The academics here are the real show. As for student life, it really isn't that bad. They just built their fancy new dorms, which I live in, and which slay.
Bard is a rural campus with a smaller student body, so don't expect extravagance, but for a small school in upstate New York, I never find myself bored or in lack of things to do. There's a real active student body here which host events from protests to parties, and the local town of Red Hook is really cute and has a whole ton of events too. There is also an amazing amount of opportunities through the Bard offices, I was able to go abroad twice on Bard's dime.
Overall, Bard is good school with phenomenal academics.

Amazingly talented professors and staff. The students are very engaged with their work and everyone comes with a different niche and passion. I learned just as much from my peers and their life experiences. Politics department has a wide range of thought and ideas. Food got better as time went on and the dining halls were renovated. Nearby towns were very accessible and have cute restaurants and stores. Kingston is a fun city as well! Great great music scene. I would highly recommend this school if you don’t mind being around some interesting people!

I loved the academics at Bard, but I found that the school was not very student living friendly. Bard, despite being so expensive, does not put a lot of effort into the dorms, has overadmitted students which has left students living in the "trailer parks", and the dining hall plans are also horrible. There are only 2 meal swap opportunities on weekends, meaning on top of an already expensive school, I found myself having to buy groceries to backup what my meal plan lacked. Bard is very isolated so social opportunities are very limited and not much happens on campus. I think this school is great for people who can find their group of people and aren't worried about having stuff to do off campus and can entertain themselves, but otherwise I feel like a lot of students I have talked to feel like we have settled for Bard, and not that we are head over heels for this college.

The school over admitted freshman before my sophomore year, meaning I had to live in a temporary dorm built within a parking lot squeezed into a single-sized room with a roommate. The dorming situation is sometimes dire, most of the time inconvenient. Typically people are neat, but some people lack the ability to plunge toilets or recognize the fact that they in fact are not owed the world and other people use the same facilities (this trend can be seen not just in the use of bathrooms, but in the general conduct of certain students who are entitlement embodied). If you are familiar with social exclusion this school will only compound your sense of isolation. Cliques are ever present and perpetuating of a high-school after high-school vibe. You will likely find yourself hard pressed to come across those who are willing to meaningfully converse with you about opposing viewpoints. People are sometimes callous in their opinions. If you are this type of person, apply and if not, don’t.

The college is large, beautiful and rural. There are many amazing opportunities on the campus including the Fischer center theater and the art museum. Each dorm is different and most of them are small and not the typical box like building you would get at a traditional campus. The first year dorms sometimes are small. My room is a non typical shape and makes it a challenge to share with a roommate. I love my academic classes, but the first year registration process was challenging. I also find the food a challenge as there are not many options and Bard is isolated so there are few off campus options. There is a shuttle but it is also limited so most of the social activities are on campus.

Bard College has a calm, beautiful setting that makes it a great place to focus on your studies. My academic experience has been really rewarding—challenging but also personal, with professors who are genuinely invested in their students. It’s definitely a serious place to study, but it’s also run with a lot of empathy and kindness. That said, being an undergraduate here can be tough at times. For non-traditional students especially, the community can feel a little distant or even lonely at first. I’ve found that speaking up and staying grounded emotionally makes a big difference in feeling connected and thriving at Bard.
My biggest frustration has been the food on campus. The options at Kline Cafeteria and Manor Dining leave a lot to be desired in terms of quality and variety. If you can’t easily get off campus and have to depend on the bus system, it can feel a bit like a food desert.

My experience with Bard College has welcoming. From the moment I explored the campus I was struck by the sense of creative freedom and intellectual curiosity that defines the community. The faculty and staff I’ve interacted with have been supportive and engaged, and every conversation felt thoughtful and personal. I appreciate Bard’s commitment to independent thinking, the arts, and social responsibility, all of which align with my values and learning style.
I really enjoy how connected Bard is. There are many opportunities to go study abroad or to learn about a topic through a super interdisciplinary approach.
I love it, it's its own little bubble that feels like a safe space for a diverse, niche, unique group of people.

This place is horrible. These kids didn’t just fail to grow out of high school awkwardness, they weaponized it. Once they found each other, they decided they were normal, and anyone with balance or perspective was the enemy. The result? An echo chamber where hurt people hurt people and call it justice.
Dissent is dangerous. One off-script thought and the mob descends. They don’t engage. They excommunicate. This place doesn’t tolerate cancel culture; it nurtures it. Miss a Thought Crime update? You’re out.
Mental health? Just branding. Behind the curtain is despair. Suicide is a real thing here. Admin feigns concern, then feeds the machine.
It’s a hollow, hateful place wrapped in glitter and pride flags, where empathy is cosplay and cruelty is currency.
If your kid is sensitive, funny, or believes in real dialogue - run. This school chews up young minds and spits out broken shells or obedient clones. And if you’re an athlete? Don’t. Just don’t.

I started as a student at Bard College at Simon's Rock, but I am continuing with Bard College because the previous campus is closed. I love studying, and I am so grateful to Bard for providing me the opportunity to study and pursue my dream. I really love Bard and all the faculty at this institution.

My experience at Bard has been incredible. The students and staff welcome you with open arms and make it a priority to protect your peace and make you feel seen. This school is very focused on the well being of its students and the world in general, keeping up with current events and taking time out of planned curriculum to talk through public issues. The academic practice is innovative and thought provoking. The only "complaints" I have about the school is the cleanliness and safety of housing. Most of this does fall on the students who leave messes or don't follow dorm specific rules, but some issues in the dorms are product of Bard not being proactive about student health. Overall, the education I'm receiving is nothing like I've ever experienced. I feel smarter by the day.

Bard exceeded my expectations! I chose this college for its discussion-based classes and accessible professors. Courses are not your typical Bio 101 where you sit in large lecture halls trying to stay awake. We were out in the field collecting samples and doing graduate-level genomic sequencing back at the daylight-filled lab. The science building is one of the many newer spaces on campus, with Fisher Theater being another favorite. Next year, the new performing arts lab will be another place for us to rehearse and perform. The vibe on campus is chill. Students here are smart and many are driven either academically, athletically, musically, or many other pursuits -- there are so many options to be yourself here without being pretentious or competitive
Bard College provides an incredibly rich academic experience, with plenty of options to broaden your horizons and get engaged with the community, both locally, nationally, and even globally.

ai_summary: Reviews of Barnard College reveal a unique mix of strengths and challenges that potential students might consider. Academically, the college is praised for its rigorous programs and engaged professors , who are often described as supportive and accessible. Students appreciate the opportunities that come from being part of Columbia University, including access to libraries and extracurricular activities. There's a strong emphasis on individualism within a community of motivated women, which fosters a sense of empowerment . However, the competitive atmosphere can be stressful, and some reviews mention a lack of community and challenges with social interactions, particularly for those who may not identify with the dominant culture. While the location in New York City is often highlighted as a major draw , reviews express concerns about the cost of tuition and financial aid support. The dining options and living conditions have also received mixed feedback, with some calling for improvements. Overall, Barnard is characterized as a place for students who thrive in an intellectually stimulating environment and are prepared for the ups and downs of a city college experience.

reviews.text:
To prospective transfer students, I’d like to warn that it can be hard socially.
When you get here that it’s very important to be self confident, extroverted, and/or willing to work hard to put yourself out there. New York and the campus can be overwhelming if you’re coming from a smaller place/campus and you have to be ready to adjust quickly to make sure you can branch out. Yes, Barnard is a compact campus, but it’s in Columbia and there are definitely some lingering impacts as people don’t really talk before class/get to know each other. I’d say clubs/student groups are the best bet and you should make sure you really dedicate yourself. Students are pretty clique-y and independent, so once they lock down social groups, that’s typically their go-to and they will be less likely to branch out.
Tour guides will never mention this for obvious reasons so this isn’t meant to alarm but to inform!
I loved Barnard. The professors were so caring, and you really do get the best of both worlds with access to Columbia's resources and being in NYC.

The course selection and professors are top notch. I am getting a terrific education here. There are a lot of clubs so you can also find people passionate about your interests and do something together. The kids are generally great. And you cannot beat the NYC setting. It's just nice to be surrounded by world-class institutions and be able to get to all of them on the subway. There is also a good amount of support for students, especially with Beyond Barnard. You can get meaningful internships either on campus or in the city more broadly. IMO on-campus housing is not great -- pricy and mediocre quality -- but there is a lot off-campus if you have time to look and know what you want. If using a realtor, find one who specializes in Morningside Heights and Manhattanville.

My experience at Barnard has been defined by intellectual curiosity, close mentorship, and a sense of belonging in interdisciplinary research. I came in drawn to questions about human behavior, and Barnard gave me the space — and the encouragement — to explore those questions across psychology, data science, and the social sciences. Small classes and accessible faculty made it easy to form meaningful academic relationships, and those relationships ultimately shaped the direction of my work.

i like that barnard has some nice profesores and interesting courses offered, while having a unique relationship with columbia, making there a lot of interactions. howver, with recent implacement of rules that supposedly enhances campus safety and life experience, many feel that their acedemic and free speech rights has been limited.
Profesora are great and there are plenty of research opportunities available. Small class sizes. Nice location within NYC.

Barnard offers a lot, especially with it's connection to Columbia. Althroguh it is small, you don't feel restricted because you are in the middle of New York City. Academics are great and social scene is really fun. I would describe the culture as "work hard, play a little bit less hard."
Barnard is a place where smart, driven women support each other and think big. You get a close community and full access to everything at Columbia.

I enjoy the welcoming and inclusive environment of the college, as well as being under Columbia University to be able to pursue my biomedical major there. However, I would like to see change in their efficiency when students are reaching out. I would also criticize their generosity with financial aid packages.

wonderful experience with a particular femine vibe. Small campus which means students are closely nitted. No major green space which represents a shortcoming. Most of the 4-acre land is occupied by tall buildings and sometimes people will fell squeezed in a small space. One of the main issues of the college is its endowment, which is rather small compared to similar colleges its size and prestige.

Relatively small campus and city environment. Students are addressed elegantly and teachers are readily available to help the students. Dining facilities are average and relatively small based the number of students in the campus. Outdoor exercises space are limited due to city environment and only two stripe of riverside parks are available for outdoor activities, otherwise only very small green field on campus can be utilized. Student source and quality are very good due to the attraction of the New York City. A lot of them (except those who got waitlisted and later off the list) had GPAs of close to 4.0/4.0 and all kinds of outstanding activities including national awards, research achievements, club leaders, volunteering champions. Most of the students came from their high school class ranking within the top 10% and overall raw acceptance rate between 6-7% which falls within an elite group of top universities. Overall campus (including Columbia and Barnard) have more girls.
I loved barnard's location and the student body. I think the diversity could be improved and these days, their failure to preserve academic freedom is troubling.
Professors are genuinely there to support and inspire students to be bold and be a leader. Students are ambitious and passionate about niche areas, which makes for an amazing college environment :)

Had a wonderful time at Barnard, felt really supported by other students and the faculty really care. The women's college network and Columbia alum network have been really strong and helpful in navigating my career post graduation!
I loved going to barnard. They allow really safe ways to get around New York city through the subway and CARES. I also love getting access to so many more library's and rescources through Colombia

ai_summary: Bates College is known for its tight-knit community , where students, professors, and faculty members genuinely support each other. The academic environment is rigorous yet collaborative , with small class sizes that foster meaningful relationships between students and instructors. Many reviews highlight the outstanding faculty who are approachable and invested in student success. However, concerns have been raised about the college's lack of diversity , with a predominance of privileged backgrounds among students. The social scene is often described as friendly and inclusive , though some find it less vibrant compared to larger universities, with limited weekend activities and a quieter party atmosphere. While many appreciate the beautiful campus and excellent dining options, critiques include dorm conditions and the surrounding area's safety. Overall, Bates offers a unique liberal arts experience with strong academic support, though potential students should consider the college's social dynamics and geographic context.

reviews.text: The community of Bates is unmatched, not just your peers but also the professors and all of the staff. It's a community that looks out for each other and has you looking out for everyone else.

I didn’t fully expect it before enrolling, but Bates is really a perfect storm academically. There is a wealth of incredible expertise in the Bates faculty, the majority of whom have credentials that are second to none, and the level of access that students have to their professors definitely seems to give us a leg up over kids at other institutions. But speaking specifically to what sets Bates apart, it’s hard not to notice how mission driven the college is. Often when I toured colleges in high school, they tended to feel like businesses and think-tanks first and schools second. This is not true of Bates. Even in comparison to other NESCACs, Bates feels much more genuine. The faculty cares about students and are happy to work with us to ensure that we can be successful, and given Bates’ pretty remarkable graduate school placements, year in and year out, this combination of elements seems to be working. Especially for kids hoping to get an MA or JD, I strongly suggest looking at Bates.
I have enjoyed the campus and location of Bates College. It is located in such an amazing area and there are endless opportunities to go adventure nearby.

One year out from graduating bates and coming here was one of the best decisions I’ve ever made. The education I received as a bates student made me a more thoughtful, intelligent and compassionate person, which is exactly what I set out to do. I am leaving this experience with life long friends and countless positive memories. DO NOT LISTEN to anyone trying to hate on Lewiston. It is a beautiful city filled with life and fascinating history. Lewiston played a MAJOR role in my education as a whole. Not taking advantage of the Harward (community engagement) center during your time here is doing yourself an enormous disservice.
I definitely felt supported by my professors in and out of the classroom. I had trouble related to physical accessibility on campus, but there has been a lot of discussion about making it better.

Bates College is a great place. Most of the students and professors are super nice and friendly to everyone. Professors are always available and actually care about students. All the professors of the classes that I've taken can remember everyone's name on class.

This school will give you a classic small New England Liberal Arts education. If that is what you are looking for, Bates is a great choice, especially for people who love the outdoors. I have learned a lot in all of my classes and there are a lot of smart and passionate students here. However, it is not very diverse and there is not a big party scene. If this is something that would be hard for you, I recommend taking a least a semester to study abroad.

This is my first year at Bates and I definitely like it, but there are a few things that dont add up. Bates is considered difficult to get into, but it seems that the majority of the students here are fairly lazy, like where is the competition we were told about during application? Academics here are challenging most the time, but a lot of people dont REALLY want to learn, they want to use chatGPT and get assignments out of the way. Aside from that, if you dont play a sport, it is pretty hard to find a social circle or get invited to events, it is quite cliquey. All that aside, Bates is a good place, but I know its important for perspective students to see all sides of a school and these are some of the things I was not aware that I wish I was when I committed here.

I really like the party vibe at Bates. There are always parties on Thursday, Friday and Saturday night. Also, this is a place that you can truly explore your interest in yourself because there's really nothing around here. But I would like to see that Bates could be more inclusive to international students and the athletic vibe could be more inclusive as well.

Bates has a beautiful student community. You will meet people that will be in your life forever. Academics are rigorous but in the best way possible. Winters are long but great for outdoor activities.

Bates College in Lewiston, Maine, is an outstanding institution known for its rigorous academics and vibrant campus life. Small class sizes ensure personalized attention from dedicated professors who are both experts and mentors. The campus community is inclusive and active, with numerous clubs, organizations, and events that engage students in various interests.
The beautiful campus, coupled with Maine’s natural charm, offers a serene environment for study and recreation. Bates is also committed to sustainability, with eco-friendly initiatives evident throughout the campus. The college’s dedication to inclusivity, social justice, and community service fosters a welcoming environment for all students.
Career services at Bates are excellent, providing resources and opportunities that prepare students for successful careers post-graduation. Overall, a Bates education is an enriching journey that prepares students to be thoughtful, engaged, and impactful members of society.
small lib arts college, very white. It’s the artsier and more granola version of Bowdoin or Colby. Doesn’t have frats, but the varsity teams replace them.

If you are looking for a small liberal arts school where you know everyone, Bates is the place for you. This may be idyllic to some. With knowing nearly all 1,800 students, I can say Bates needs to work on diversity, equity, and inclusion. In academics Bates speaks of it a lot, but it has not translated into social life. Bates is considered a PWI (predominantly white institution) and also has low economic diversity; less than 40% of students receive any form of financial aid. The feelings of an upper-class PWI have been often described as feeling like a boarding high school. The thing that saves and sets Bates apart from other institution, in my opinion, is the academics and professors. Especially in smaller departments, it is common to be close with your professors and make friends in them for years to come.

Bates was a huge disappointment. The dorms are atrocious. Picture the very worst dorm room, and then you have an idea what Frye house is like. There is very little economic and racial diversity. Teachers were good, but the school is just not a welcoming place at all.

Bates is a cozy campus that has as little as 1,500 students on campus or less. The main focus of the college is education and advancement. The professors, coordinators, and advisors all genuinely care about your well-being and your future and will go to any lengths and through any hoops to make sure you are comfortable and prepared for the rest of your life during and after Bates. The academics are challenging, yet you will not be left without help from professors or students should you need it. There is a rich party life, yet it is very easy to avoid and without pressure should you wish to stay away. Extracurriculars and sports are also very prominent and easy to get into. Very good college!

ai_summary: Beloit College generally offers a unique blend of academic rigor and a closely-knit community atmosphere, which many students appreciate. The professors are frequently described as passionate, approachable, and genuinely invested in student success, fostering strong relationships in smaller class settings. The campus itself is noted for its beauty and walkability, with various green spaces enhancing the environment. However, there are significant concerns regarding food quality and campus facilities, including issues with dorm conditions. The student life experience can be polarizing; some find it welcoming and inclusive, while others note a lack of diversity and a somewhat cliquish social scene, particularly among athletes. The college's remote location also contributes to a sense of isolation for some, and while financial aid is highlighted as a plus, tuition is perceived as relatively high given the overall experience.

reviews.text: I am a second semester freshman at Beloit College, but so far I have enjoyed my time here. I have felt like the academics are very high quality and push me to be my best. The teacher to student ratio allows students to always feel seen and always find help in their academic journey. I am also an athlete here at Beloit, and I have had a great experience with that so far as well. I have had great coaches and teammates, with a great athletic culture here. I feel that the social life at Beloit is not great, but I have found good friends making it better. I also feel like the dining hall food is not great, and the bathrooms do not always have hot shower water.
