Those are minor points, but otherwise I have had a positive experience here at Beloit College.

I mean so far it is a good experience, I would highly rate it if you are studying any humanities subject. But if you want to study Science related subject you can look for other LAC's or Universities but their faculties are really helpful.

Beloit is a small, vibrant campus with a rich student culture. While the quality of classes can vary based on the professors, with some teaching styles not being for everyone, overall I've found the quality of education to be quite high.

I enjoyed my time at Beloit College in the last four years. There were definitely moments where I wanted to give up and drop out, but I received a lot of help and hope from my professors and advisors, all of whom truly believed that I could accomplish great things. The faculty in the psychology department stimulated my interest in this field, so much so that one of the reasons I enjoyed my college experience was applying what I learned in my psych classes to my personal life, which has made me grow into the adult I am today.
I would love to see the food services change for the better. The main food option here isn't that appetizing. There are other options on campus that are very good alternatives.

Overall, I love attending Beloit College. I feel that I do better academically in a smaller classroom setting and having personal relationships with my professors sets me up for academic success. I also play a sport at Beloit College which has allowed me to form lifelong friendships along with learning the concept of balance through sports and academics.

I have spent one semester at Beloit College and overall I really enjoy going there. Classes have been educational, and they provide a very excellent transition to college. Even as an athlete who missed a lot of orientation events, I felt very comfortable, settled, and was able to know people due to their programs.

Beloit college is the perfect place if you are an introvert but sometimes an extrovert. not only it allows you to grow while experiencing new stuff, it also provides an opportunity for you to reflect on your journey and learn from your mistakes.

The teachers are very understanding when it comes to playing collegiate level sports that require students to miss some classes and they allow students to make up work for the most part as long as you communicate. I met my two closest friends at school.

The college is great academically, I loved all my classes and they always challenged me acedmically. The school is one of the most diverse places I've ever been. Almost none of the dorms have Ac even when they claim they do and the food is pretty horrible but I don't plan on leaving because if you get through August/September it gets pretty great.
There's also a pretty great party scene. Alot of people call this a “wet” campus from how appropriated drinking and smoking is.

From the moment I stepped foot on Beloit Colleges campus, I knew this was the school I had to attend. This is where I want to grow as a person and learn the skills needed to be successful in life. The plus side to the amazing education they have, I was offered the opportunity to play for the women's soccer team. I have played soccer my whole life and when the chance rose for me to go to school and continue playing soccer, I was beyond grateful. I can't wait to see the adventures that await me as I step on and off that field. During my orientation I met some many different kind, warm hearted people....teachers, staff, academic advisors, students, coaches were all there to listen, understand. During the tour, hearing the history of Beloit and the purpose truly amazed me. Walking the halls of the future school I would attend was mind boggling. I can only imagine what the best 4 years will be like but I am excited to begin my journey with such a " we are for the students school".
Overall, I appreciated that there were many classes that focused on the social sciences and how they applied to the “hard” sciences.

Beloit is in a small city which can be good and bad. It’s easy to get to know everyone because the students, schools and companies are very well acquainted. The smallness allows for students to create good relationships with professors and join many programs. On the other hand, the size reduces the number of events and fun activities in close range. For many students, it is easy to feel isolated. In addition this is not really an ideal environment for minorities; there has been various race related encounters on and off campus. There are little to no minority faculty either. Along with that, the athletic department has a close net community yet most f the teams have losing streaks which is improving but the structure does not appeal to the mental strains that come from being a student athlete. On the bright side Beloit is place where freedom of expression is advocated for as long as your white and male but the space very whimsical at times.

I liked the small, close-knit community that Beloit College offered. I felt like I was supported inside and outside of academics, and made really meaningful connections with other students and professors. The dorm life and campus meals could be improved, but Beloit was still a wonderful place to complete my undergraduate degree. I transferred into Beloit my sophomore year, and it was the best decision I ever made.

It is really good, small college over 1,200 students. The atmosphere is amazing here. I love the student to teacher ratio. I have easier access to resources such as libraries, laboratories, and other facilities. This can make it more convenient for students to utilize these resources for research and study. Smaller class sizes facilitate more interactive and engaging discussions here at Beloit. I feel more comfortable expressing my opinions, and professors can better gauge the understanding of the material. In a smaller community, we as students find more opportunities for leadership roles in student organizations, clubs, or other extracurricular activities. This can contribute to personal and professional
I love the professors here if you want to view the world and your own practice critically and grow personally and professionally come to Bennington.

Bennington has been a great experience thus far. No college is perfect, but the opportunities that Bennington offers, such as Field Work Term, set it apart from other schools. I can take any class that sparks my interest, and the professors here care about each of their students.

Bennington College has been a life changing experience for me. I’ve discovered my passions and gotten to take the classes I want to, becoming self-motivated to succeed in my chosen fields (Sculpture/Drama/Community Building). Overall, the professors genuinely care about each of their students and with the small class size, they have the opportunity to interact 1:1 with anyone who needs advice or extra support. Bennington has allowed me to build a resume that has a breadth of experience for the job world I am soon entering through both on/off campus experience and Field Work Terms.

This college is a great fit for anyone who is both motivated and open to exploring things outside their comfort zone. It is crucial to take initiative and be proactive about your own education, and if you do so the faculty will be responsive to it. The community is worth being involved in and usually very engaged. Though the school is small and as such sometimes has only scraps to work with, this any prospective student should be aware of, but the good side of this is that there is a sense of solidarity between everyone working and learning.

I love my classes at Bennington College, I can study niche classes that are not offered at many other colleges, and my teachers are very invested in my personal growth. In the second term of freshman year each student gets a "plan committee" which is a group of professors who help the student develop a plan, our version of a major. The plan is more tailored to the individual students interests, which gives students more freedom with their course selection. This freedom allows one to have a very well rounded understanding of what they want to pursue. The on-campus housing is also amazing, students live in real homes on campus, which makes the community feel much closer. Campus has a very close-knit feeling, I have made so many new friends, and joined so many clubs that led me to finding my own community.
The faculty-to-student ratio spoiled me for all other academic settings in the best possible way. Living on campus helped me grow not just as a student but as a young adult and community member.
Fantastic visual arts faculty, beautiful rural location, kind and respectful professors. Freedom to create an educational experience that is unique and fulfilling.

Bennington is great for self-directed learners with a multi-disciplinary approach to study. It is a college that gives you a lot of freedom, which can either make or break someone. I personally appreciated the freedom, and formed meaningful mentorships from my teachers and friendships with the local workers of the college. It is also set in a quiet serene area, which is great for self-reflection, saving money and a gentler pace to life, but can get isolating, especially during winters. The school could benefit from a larger African student population, better medical facilities and better support for international students.

I have worked at Bennington College for nearly three years, and I love what an inspiring and creative place it is. Students and faculty are constantly working on interesting projects, and that inspires me to be more creative in my own life. Plus it is just a generally kind and welcoming place. And it's totally gorgeous. I feel lucky to come here to work every day.
Bennington make me feel like I can be myself. The school is incredibly creative, and pushes for students to find their passion in the arts. I feel incredibly supported, and love my school.

What an absolutely beautiful educational experience. I felt immensely seen and valued by professors I worked with. I created connections with peers and faculty that I had been dreaming of and was welcomed with open arms into any discipline I dabbled in. While the administration made quality of life difficult since the day I stepped foot on campus, I’d like to think I did my best to change what was within my control. I established good connections with parts of the staff that really were the heart and soul of the college and spoke my mind to those who weren’t as much. The social aspect was a bit tough at first, and I quickly had to learn to enjoy time alone, but I think that those who value the things I did during my time here will be those who continue on the true legacy of Bennington students; individuals who value their independence, honor their true selves, are motivated and avid learners, and have a deep-rooted urge to create and enjoy community around them.
Overall, amazing. I love it all especially the sceneries and how close it is to real nature. Apart from that, academics which could be significantly MORE rigorous
No college is perfect but overall I really loved studying at Bennington! The unique school structure is not for everyone, but I feel that I was able to thrive because of it!

The teachers are absolutely phenomenal!! The time and care taken by all faculty and staff at Bennington to advise, encourage and support students in each student’s individual academic journey is astounding. The education you get at Bennington is first class and among the best out there. It’s goes beyond just academics, and is an all encompassing life experience, where one learns life lessons and lessons about one’s self along side rigorous academic studies.

I completed my undergraduate degree in Biology and Environtmental Sciences at Bennington college. Studying STEM in a interdisciplinary environment allowed me to delve into music, literature, and costume design.

ai_summary: Berea College stands out for its no-tuition promise , making it a viable option for low-income students seeking higher education without the burden of student debt. Many reviews highlight the challenging academic environment and dedicated professors who genuinely care about students' success, fostering strong connections in small class settings. The unique labor program offers practical work experience, though reviews indicate some dissatisfaction with the management and communication within the program. Students appreciate the diverse and close-knit community but note the lack of nightlife and recreational activities on campus, making social life somewhat limited. Complaints about outdated facilities and poor dining options are common, along with frustration regarding logistical issues such as transportation and accessibility for those with dietary restrictions. Overall, while Berea College offers valuable opportunities for personal and academic growth, prospective students should weigh the rigorous demands and social limitations against its financial benefits.

reviews.text:
In exchange for a so-called free "education," you give up everything. First, negative experiences are illegal. They'll rabidly follow you for life. I've had alumni contacting my employer to have me fired. The student body looks like it's on the lookout for somebody to lynch. I once sent a survey asking if residents want shower timers (my job). The next day, I received 120 hate emails, had my bike vandalized & tires flattened. Someone spread rumors that I was trying to limit showers.
They justify everything, "since it's free anyway." No lecture notes? Hey, it's free. No requirements listed? Hey, it's free. Random grading by "TAs"? Hey, it's free. Ideological conditioning unrelated to class? Hey, it's free. Classes turned upside-down to give unfair advantages to their allies & sabotage others? Hey, it's free. $5/hour work? Hey, it's free. Over time, the justifications grow longer than a mile, while the benefits they think they're providing are largely imaginary. Overall, go elsewhere.

I'm convinced that there's no greater evil on earth than the "people" who work and teach here. They lie everything, gaslight everyone, and use every means at their disposal to shape the narrative to their benefits. Whatever you read or learn about this hellscape can be fake, manipulated, or applied only to select few groups who will be given enormous privileges over other students. Any amount of loans is worth it. I promise.

My experience has been amazing at Berea College. They create so many opportunities for each student and allow us to grow as individuals. By coming to Berea College you gain soft skills through working on campus and job skills.

This will be the biggest mistake of your life. Berea is one fraud of a school. They lie every step of the admission process (and I suspect most of the reviews here are lies as well). Most of the majors are more or less a joke and only exist as a means to attract more "poor" students who can bring in scholarships. It has the highest ACT scores but the lowest postgraduate earnings in the region.
Be sure to research the average starting salary from this school compared to the nation average. Download the database at College Scorecard and take special notes at early career salaries. You get what you pay for. Additionally, students that meet the financial eligibility requirements for this college will typically be eligible for similar offers from state universities, especially in the student's home state...Do not be fooled by the PR department's doublespeak; you will regret attending this school.

I really do believe that Berea college has been helpful to many students who are looking to receive an education without financial stress. It has a very diverse population, and you get the chance to have wonderful experiences. I studied Abroad twice with the help of Berea College. It is an academically rigorous institution, but the outcomes are so worth it! Students are receiving that liberal arts experience while also being able to focus on their interest and career goals. I would not have picked another college to have attended for undergrad; I am eternally grateful for Berea.

Berea College is a special school that helps students who need financial support. It offers a great education without charging tuition. The teachers care about students, and the school encourages hard work and helping others. Berea gives students the tools to succeed in life.

My overall experience at Berea College has been positive. I’ve appreciated the unique opportunity to earn a quality education without the financial burden that comes with most colleges. The work-study program stands out because it teaches responsibility, time management, and real-world skills alongside academics.
What I like most is the strong sense of purpose on campus. The faculty care, and the small class sizes make it easier to build meaningful connections. There is a clear focus on service, learning, and personal growth.
That said, there is always room to improve. I would like to see more support for mental health services and better communication between departments. Sometimes, navigating resources or getting clear answers can be frustrating.
Overall, Berea is a place that pushes you to grow, and it would benefit from investing even more in student support systems.

I am an international student from Brazil, and I don't pay anything to go to Berea College; as a low-income, the only thing I have to pay is taxes to the U.S. government because of my scholarship. Actually, I'm being paid to study here; because of the labor program, I was able to buy unnecessary things like electronics and still pay my taxes.
There is always something going on on campus, an event, a party; we have shuttles every week, Outdoor Adventure trips every week, and there is always something to do inside Berea College. The city sucks; we have no bus line to the students, although everything is within walking distance. There is nothing to do in the town never.
The academics are great! We have amazing opportunities with the help of Student Success and Transition and Career Development, to help you navigate all 4 years of school. The food is bad. Berea busy is real, you will be busy always. The community is great. You will know everyone in the school. I love Berea.

The people who work here are incompetent idiots that can’t do their job. The academics are good, you get a good education, but the awful faculty, the bad student life, the horrible food at the dining hall, the most boring environment, and the most disrespectful athlete students, make this place a living hell.
The general education program here makes getting a single major a hard thing to do, let alone people who double major. The amount of useless classes you have to take to fulfill the general education requirements is abhorrent and wastes time for your major. It’s not awful, but the way the credit system is arranged, makes it hard to get everything you need.
The dorm rooms are actual cardboard boxes with trash in them. The have mold, cracked walls, broken windows, and broken ceilings. The rooms can barely fit 2 beds and 2 wardrobes, they are so small and unlivable
The professors, to an extent, care about you and want you to succeed. If you study for class, you will do fine in them.

The commitment to diversity and overall community made me feel at home away from home. As well, the work program gave me a resume and skills that provided a head start for my career. The small classroom sizes and low student to faculty ratio gave me an intimate experience that allowed me to really connect with my professors. As well, it prepared me for Grad School in such a way that made it a breeze. Lastly, it allowed me to graduate with only two thousand dollars in loans. Great affordable experience!

Overall, the college experience here is nice if you find the right people to be around. It is hard to enjoy your time here without friends because you are pushed passed limits that you would not be expected to at another school. However, they provide a quality education for little to no cost to someone. Even though it does seem to give more financial aid to students of color than non-colored students of the same or lower-class status.

Berea College is a great option for students from low-income backgrounds who would like to graduate without any debt. They have many programs in place to give students opportunities that would have otherwise cost thousands of dollars. I had a great undergraduate experience and I'm very grateful to have good to college without paying for any tuition.

Berea's CS major's 1st-year postgraduation income is 34,000$. Math major's 2nd year income is 17,000$ (other years are privacy suppressed). The incomes are for the year 2022, inflation-adjusted. Pell Grant graduates from almost anywhere earn 2-3 times as much, with far lower average ACT scores (25 vs 20). The college manipulates reviews. If you attend it based on positive reviews here, remember that you will be eligible for a class action sometime in the future.
Starting out as a first-year student at Berea College has been good. So far, I have no student debt, and the classes have been great. I've also made a lot of friends as well. Friendly place.

Say you are in your final year taking a required class. An extension has been denied. A random TA they put in has a grudge against you and gives you failing grades (TAs are classmates in other classes). The professor neither corrects them nor even replies to emails. You are stressed the hell out. That's on top of the assignments being a total waste of time. And then, they have the nerve to expect you to be "grateful." Any amount of loan is worth it if you could avoid what I went through, even if the chance is 5% (the chance is ~50%, as I gather from my friends). Would you board a plane if the chance of an aviation disaster is 5%? There are many colleges out there.

ai_summary: Bowdoin College is often praised for its friendly , welcoming community and top-tier academic offerings, with many students highlighting the exceptional quality and dedication of the professors. The college's need-blind , no-loan financial aid policy also receives positive remarks, although concerns about a lack of diversity and inclusiveness persist. Student life revolves around a tight-knit campus culture , with opportunities for extracurricular involvement such as the Outing Club and various events hosted by college houses. However, some reviews indicate a perception of elitism among the student body and a social scene that can sometimes feel limited, especially for non-athletic students. While the campus is beautiful and the food highly rated, the isolating location in Maine could be a downside for some. Ultimately, Bowdoin is recognized for providing a supportive environment that caters to both academic and personal growth, though the college experience can vary significantly based on individual preferences and backgrounds.

reviews.text:
Bowdoin has been the most accepting and incredible community I’ve experienced. People are kind, humble, and genuinely care, students show up for each other, professors know your name and make time, and the administration feels responsive and supportive. Academics are rigorous but collaborative, with discussion-based classes that value curiosity over competition. Campus life is inclusive, and it’s easy to get involved across clubs, arts, and athletics without ego. The Maine setting adds to the sense of community and balance.
If I could change a few things, I’d open more seats in high-demand courses, keep broadening faculty and course diversity, expand mental-health and wellness support (especially in winter), increase funding for internships/research, and add more weekend programming that get students off campus and doing fun activities in the other more urban towns. Overall, I love Bowdoin.
Bowdoin is the place to be if you want to grow as a person and as a global citizen. Wouldn’t change it for the world.
Amazing small liberal arts college, with exceptional academics, excellent athletics, beautiful surroundings, amazing food, great housing options, and strong community.

Attending Bowdoin was one of the best choices I made. As a west coast native, I was anxious initially attending a school in a very niche state like Maine. However, I've come to realize that the undergraduate education I received has overprepared me for the rigor of medical school. Professors present case based application style learning, and offer abundant research opportunities to expand your knowledge. There are rarely "multiple choice exams" and you are held accountable for your knowledge and mastery of the material. The small school environment has allowed me to grow from an introvert into an extrovert, which has helped me in my pursuit of patient care. Looking back, while Bowdoin was academically difficult in the sciences, I can't help but and think about how fortunate I was to have such an intentional and applied education. Yes, it's possible (and likely) you won't get straight A's here, as the standards are high, but you will become more educated and competent.

Bowdoin works very hard to make sure students needs are scene and advocated for. In times when I have felt support lacking I have been able to reach out to staff and often times they will connect with resources or create new resources.
I have tried a lot of new things at Bowdoin that I never thought I'd do. I didn't think I'd be an officer on an athletics team or even be involved in athletics. I didn't think I would get to work as a learning assistant in my first two years, or meet with the student affairs people to create meaningful change in my community.

Though I was somewhat tentative moving far away (and to Maine, no less) for college, Bowdoin has become a creative and intellectual home for me. It's an institution that attracts passionate, engaged, and empathetic individuals with such a rich variety of interests, and a sense of open, kind interconnectedness really defines the community. The professors have been spectacularly formative, design thoughtful and compelling courses, and are interesting researchers and humans in their own right. Brunswick, Maine is a beautiful place to live as well, being close to the coast, and especially in campus housing. Additionally, the amount of student funding is unparalleled; I've been able to pursue research opportunities and art workshops/conferences because of it. My only major qualm is the lack of diversity: I was surprised by the concentration of wealth, and how majorly white Maine is overall, but to an extent, that's to be expected from an elite institution in the Northeast.

If you’re reading this review, know that this was written by a current student. AVOID this place if you can. I know the money is tempting, it’s the reason a majority of us are forcing ourselves to stay here. But at the result, a good chunk of us are medicated (they love to deny it). They have cults to make themselves feel important. They drink themselves silly to deal with the school. The social scene is DEAD (parties, friends, incompetence professors etc.). The school is isolated in the town of Brunswick which is mainly occupied with retired old people. Unless you are filthy rich, you’ll struggle unnecessarily. The school will offer to pay for certain things. It doesn’t take away from the fact that the students don’t want to be there. It’s all a fallacy, them chanting “you belong at Bowdoin”. That’s what’s they tell themselves to obtain the degree that we all know carries a lot of power in this world. It’s not worth it. And if you are already here, I’m sorry for what you’ll endure.

I've loved my time at Bowdoin. Everyone is so friendly and loves to have a good time, with academics of course coming first. Professors are amazing and always willing to help, school spirit is great, and the location (especially the Amtrak station) makes it so easy to get to the airport, beach, mountains, lakes, etc. It's really easy to get involved in student activities like clubs and club/intramural sports. The college is so financially supportive of every student with need, giving every student on aid $350 towards a winter coat and boots. I'm just about to finish my first year, and I can't wait for the next 3.

Bowdoin was a wonderful albeit very stressful place to attend college. I loved how seriously everyone took their academics (sometimes too seriously!) and the professors were all awesome. I made the best friends of my life here because so many students were so kind, passionate, and well-rounded. The Brunswick area is so cute and very safe. Diversity and party scene could be improved.

I absolutely love Bowdoin!! I'm a current first year, and I could not be happier with my decision to apply early and come here. When I talk to my friends from high school, almost no one is having as good of a college experience as the people I know at Bowdoin. Bowdoin CARES about your quality of life, and that's not true everywhere, while maintaining excellent academics. The housing is fantastic and the food is amazing (we have lobster bake at the start of every year and they have special meals for every holiday). I'm honestly excited for every meal! The college house system (where many sophomores live) host fun events every weekend, and so although we don't have greek life, there are always parties to attend if that's your thing (no worries if not! There's other things to do to!). My classes are interesting and there are many incredible professors. The people here are so kind and genuine. I promise you'll find your community at Bowdoin. Everyone at Bowdoin loves it. You should apply!

My experience at Bowdoin was really positive overall. The food was great, and being on a sports team helped me meet lifelong friends who felt like family. I also had the chance to connect with and learn from a diverse range of people in the classroom, which enriched my experience even more. The professors were great—knowledgeable, approachable, and genuinely invested in our success.
However, the advisor system could use a little improvement. At times, it felt like there was a lack of personalized guidance, especially when balancing academics and extracurriculars. More support and follow-up would definitely help students navigate their paths more smoothly.

I love Bowdoin! Professors are enthusiastic about their field of study and are willing to meet during "office hours" which are offered at least twice a week. Since it is a small school, it is easy to build personal connections with professors, which I really like. People at Bowdoin are kind and are passionate about their learning and are willing to help you. Brunswick is a lovely town to walk around, which offers variety of shops and restaurants if you want to go during the weekends. However, since dining is the best at Bowdoin, I don't usually go out to eat. You should apply to Bowdoin!!!
I should have gone to Colby or Bates instead. Even though they are less prestigious, I think they are more “nerdy” and open to neurodivergent students
I really liked Bowdoin; it has a unique charm and appeal that may attract students looking for a smaller school with great academics!

Though Bowdoin is a relatively small school located in a small town in Maine, it certainly has more benefits. The small size of the school allows each student to get one-on-one experiences and personalized approach from the professors. The amount of learning happening in the walls of the college, certainly exceeds its larger-sized competititors. The liberal arts nature of the college furthermore allows the students to explore different fields of interests, since they do not have to declare their major untill the spring of their sophomore year.

ai_summary: Bryn Mawr College offers a unique academic environment characterized by small class sizes and a highly engaged faculty dedicated to student success. Many students appreciate the collaborative atmosphere, support from professors, and the strong sense of community, often describing it as a tight-knit family . The college's emphasis on traditions and activism contributes to a vibrant student life , though some find the social scene to be quieter compared to larger universities. While the beautiful campus enhances the overall experience, the lack of diversity and challenges with student services are common critiques. Students value the opportunity to take classes at nearby Haverford, Swarthmore, and UPenn, which broadens their educational exposure. Overall, Bryn Mawr is seen as a nurturing but rigorous environment that prepares students well for future endeavors.

reviews.text: Academically rigorous environment, great professors who actually cared about student success, and many great programs that allow students to explore their interests. The alumni community is so strong, and people really do look out and support each other. The campus can be cliquey at times though

This school doesn't actually care about its students, despite what they claim. Firstly, I went through severe bullying, and there is no place to report this treatment or resolve social conflicts. I asked for tangible support from hall advisors, the dean, everyone... and they all said "just avoid them" with no consequences for the bullies. Secondly, I have been trying to find a job for an entire year, with no luck. Bryn Mawr work-study (it is literally in my tuition package) means nothing. It's just a way that makes your financial aid seem better than it is. Jobs are insanely competitive, even ones like a help-desk or mailroom. Even the dining hall, which is supposed to accept everyone, rejected me due to overstaffing issues. I reached out to financial aid and human resources, and even places that hire, who were unable to help. Sometimes job hiring is based entirely on connections, and they hire people who don't need to work and are not on financial aid for even menial tasks.

I love Bryn Mawr!! If you’re looking for a big school or somewhere with a huge party scene, it’s probably not a great fit. But if you want a small, liberal arts school with a tight-knit community, it could be the place for you! Academics and professors are huge highlights, along with the campus community and proximity to Philly.

I'm gonna start with the pros:
- Housing is pretty good, dorms are typically relatively spacious, not hard to get a single (unless you're a freshman w/o accommodations).
- The lemon prosciutto arugula pizza is insane. The pesto trapanese pizza also slaps.
- Tri-co situation
- Good campus events
- Lots of interesting, unique classes
- Not hard to get into most classes
- Good academic & career resources
- Beautiful campus
- Super gay
Cons:
- 80% of the food is mediocre. Another 15% is actively bad. 5% is good (i.e, the pizza mentioned above).
- No one knows how to do their laundry. It's disgusting
- Lack of club resources/funding
- No parties, Hav parties aren't great
- Grade deflation
- Study abroad options are pretty limited
- The language requirement. It's important that people learn some of another language, but every intro class is intensive. Unfair to 95% of the student body who isn't junking their jeans to become fluent in another language.
- The P.E requirement. Same reasoning.

Part two to my other review listing pros and cons.
More cons:
- Financial aid sucks. I received a $50,000 grant for my freshman year, but it got taken away this year because one of my parents' had a slight salary increase. I have friends with similar issues.
- Probably common at most colleges, but there's a theft problem. It's a significant issue in certain dorms.
- Sometimes BMC is so woke, it's a bit absurd. This is coming from someone who is liberal and woke.
- Dining hall hours. They. Freaking. Suck. After 8:00pm on weekdays and 7:00pm on weekends, you'll have to fend for yourself and scrounge or spend money. Also, library hours suck.
- Lack of certain majors/departments. For example, there's an Environmental Studies major (humanities based) but not an Environmental Science major, no Business major, and no Criminology major.
- STEM departments have some strict rules.
- Hard to create a schedule, most classes are at the same time, even worse if you're in STEM/have labs.

I chose Bryn Mawr because it claimed to be progressive and supportive. I found a very old-fashioned academic culture instead.
When I arrived sick, no one checked on me. That small moment summed up the place: if you struggle, you disappear.
Classes often rewarded sounding impressive rather than doing anything practical. The college was built to emulate Oxford in the Victorian era, and it still feels that way. If you want a PhD and to stay in academia, you may fit. If you want to build something new or make real-world impact, it can feel stifling.
I was assaulted by a coach, and the school did nothing until my mother, an attorney, pushed hard. No apology or support. Years later, leadership responded to my letter with a generic line and no acknowledgment.
Unpaid internships and networking favors students with money. Sensitive, idealistic students should ask how the school actually treats people when things go wrong. Look for a college that lives its values.

After nearly 30 years, my Bryn Mawr experience still serves me well. The faculty in the Chemistry department were fabulous when I attended and, as far as I can tell from a recent visit, maintain that strong tradition, encouraging students to acquire hands on research experience. My days doing research at BMC gave me skills beyond competence in the laboratory: I learned how to critically evaluate my own and others work, ask questions, design robust experiments, and analyze data in detail and with precision. Those skills continue to serve me well in science and in life.

I think Bryn Mawr’s great! It has a really rich history and there’s a lot to do and learn here. I think it’s a super supportive and welcoming environment where you just feel at home at. I wouldn’t change anything!

My overall experience at Bryn Mawr College has been rewarding and formative. I appreciate the strong sense of community and the college’s commitment to creating an inclusive, supportive environment. I’ve valued the rigorous academics paired with opportunities for leadership, research, and engagement beyond the classroom. The campus culture promotes both independence and collaboration.
There is a strong emphasis on empowering students to use their education to make a positive impact in the world. The college’s resources, such as the academic support services, and student organizations, helped me develop skills beyond academics.
There are areas where continued growth is necessary. Increasing accessibility and expanding financial support would help ensure that Bryn Mawr remains affordable and welcoming to all students. Additionally, enhancing communication between departments and administrative offices could streamline processes for students navigating academic or campus services.

Bryn Mawr College has a beautiful campus and a great network of highly intelligent and compassionate women throughout both STEM and the humanities. Its close connection with Haverford provides incredible opportunities and the overall experience in the BiCo is great.
Some highlights about Bryn Mawr include quality of life (ex. great dorm situation, great food, nice area, pretty campus, walkable, etc.), academics, professors, and student/alumni networks.
Rude admissions staffing. Not worth your money to be constantly disrespected due to challenges on their own end.
I wish there was more diversity, but I like how important academics are, and I also wish that they prioritize all sports equally
I loved going to Bryn Mawr! The chemistry department is amazing. You get to work one on one with professors and they make it easy to join a lab and start doing real research early on.

I have only visited a few times and have been accepted there for ED and I am going to play lacrosse there. But overall the coaching staff is very nice and welcoming and all the students are very friendly and there is much diversity there and I love all of the little traditions there.

1. Bryn Mawr College is a women college, but at the same time there is a Bi-Co which Bryn Mawr and Haverford share the same course selection site and it is great to take all kinds of classes
2. There are all kinds of traditions at Bryn Mawr College, such as Lantern, Hell Week etc. Each class will also have a color which people feel very connected.
3. The area is a bit far away from the city Philly, but the environment is super great and professors and students are supportive and approachable.

ai_summary: Bucknell University offers a blend of rigorous academics and a tight-knit community, often highlighted by its passionate professors and small class sizes that foster personal connections. Many students appreciate the supportive environment and extensive resources available, including a strong alumni network that helps with career placement. However, concerns arise around the campus's social scene, where Greek life dominates, leading some to feel excluded if they don't participate. The dining options receive frequent criticism for lack of diversity and quality. Additionally, Bucknell's predominantly white and wealthy demographic raises issues regarding diversity and inclusivity, resulting in some students feeling isolated or marginalized . While the campus and educational offerings are praised, the overall experience varies widely, heavily influenced by personal engagement in the campus culture.

reviews.text: This school is resting on a prior reputation and not interested in the interests of students or families in an increasingly competitive landscape. The school is in the middle of nowhere and has trouble retaining great teachers and the local staff in the facilities like the dining hall seems to despise the students. Dorms are old and outdated and was placed in an army barracks style room with four beds and no common area, bathroom, or privacy. Incredible for $90,000 a year. Orientation was like bad summer camp and administration is intractable around taking classes in other parts of the school. Based on people I knew, attrition approaches 10% freshman year and many people that stay are miserable, but scared to switch. With nothing to do in Lewisburg, there is an unhealthy hard alcohol scene in the dartys, and zero school spirit. No one attends football games and the school doesn’t organize other events. A true disappointment and transferring was the best thing I could have done.
Nothing else to say. Party school with rich boring kids. Hasn’t changed. Don’t recommend unless you like drinking everyday and working at your dads law firm.

Bucknell is a wonderful college which kind of gives small ivy vibes. However, there is not really a lot of diversity and there could be invested more into the athletics. The party scene is definitely really great relative to the size and location of the school.

Bucknell University is an amazing school. I am in mechanical engineering, and so far, the learning experiences have been phenomenal. I've learned so many things and joined new clubs and activities. I don't usually go to parties, but I've heard they are really nice and lively. Overall, Bucknell is a good school, especially for people who want to try new things.

In my experience, my professors have been friendly and willing to help students in need. I love the small school atmosphere. Class sizes seem to start around 20-30 for introductory courses and they will get even smaller as you narrow down your interests. Lewisburg, the surrounding town, is quite small. There are some very quaint streets with nice restaurants and shops, but overall there is not a lot to do if you're more of a city person. The party scene is very active if that's what you're into, and this can help make up for the lack of town activities. The campus is beautiful (both the grounds and the buildings) and there is a farm that will let students pick fruit sometimes. It can be pretty cold but it snows, which is a pro. You can't rush as a freshman, but there is a huge Greek life presence sophmore year and beyond. There is a Giant and a CVS within walking distance but there is also a shuttle that can take you there, as well as around campus and other locations like Walmart.

I did not like my time at Bucknell. The institution is pretentious and the students follow suit with this pattern. There is virtually no diversity at Bucknell, it is a small school that has the feel of a small high school. Everyone knows each other and everything about each other. Bucknell is not a place I would send my kids or advise anyone to go. Bucknell may look like a beautiful campus from the outside, but that is just the appearance they try to put out. The dorm bathrooms constantly dirty exemplifies this

I attended Bucknell University and overall had a very positive experience. The small class sizes and accessible professors made learning engaging and personal — faculty genuinely cared about students’ success. The campus is beautiful and well-maintained, with plenty of green spaces and a strong sense of community. Student organizations are active, so it’s easy to find groups that match your interests. Career services were helpful in arranging internships and networking opportunities. My main downsides were the relatively high cost and occasional limits in course variety for very specialized subjects. I’d like to see more financial aid transparency and expanded options for interdisciplinary courses. Still, Bucknell is a warm, academically strong place that I’d recommend to students who want close faculty relationships and an involved campus life.

I DId not have a job interview, my professors recommended me to Head Hunter for biotechnology. Best career ever! Ended up in later years (after 25 years traveling to give seminars and product demos) meeting 17 Noble Lauretes!! Last job at The Salk Institute, Neuroscience Dept Chief called me a Genius!! Wow Dr. HARCLERODE and Dr. Pearson taught me well. Yes I was a nerd- no frat parties for me: It was a good choice for me. Ray Bucknell!
More bioengineering, more concerts-- although we saw "the Boss" and personally met Stevie Van Zandt.

I loved my first year at Bucknell. I had great professors and I believe I learned a lot from my courses. The housing is great there, but the food is only okay. There is a big social scene and party life. Greek life is huge, but you will still be content even if you aren’t involved in it. Also is located in a cute town.

I have really enjoyed my summer at Bucknell thus far, and cannot wait to get into my freshman year. I feel very supported and safe on campus. I enjoy walking downtown and spending time with peers on campus. I wish that the dining hall was better.

In sum, my Bucknell experience shaped who I am and am becoming. I am very thankful that I met the right people, worked with some phenomenal professors, and had the opportunities I did during my undergraduate years. They are the cornerstones of a new, fruitful life. But it wasn't all pleasantries and sunny afternoons on the quad. I found that the social scene can be jarring at Bucknell. You began to wonder after a while where the "community" was hiding. People often acted entitled, had odd superiority complexes, and were shallow. Mind you, not everyone at Bucknell is like this. You simply need to be patient and search for people you actually enjoy being around.

This is an amazing school! As a freshman, I felt welcomed, liked and that I "fit in" from day one. Students and the Admin are always exceptionally kind and helpful. The students are very friendly and respectful to each other. The professors are top-notch, committed and very interested and available in assisting the students. Food choices are very diverse and with many options.
The campus is picturesque, well-maintained and with very nice, clean and comfortable dorm rooms. It was so easy to quickly adjust to college life my first year due to such a positive fostered environment.
I absolutely have no doubt personally and from what I have noted with upper classmates, the quality of education and preparedness received at Bucknell for my future- employment and other endeavors- will avail many successful opportunities. I am so glad to be a student here; it was a great choice for me- Absolutely no regrets.
I love Bucknell- it’s academics challenge me, and I’ve found a community here. However, the housing and food situation could be improved

I have really enjoyed my time at Bucknell. It is full of driven and intelligent students who push each other to be their best. It can seem like it is dominated by wealthy, white northeastern kids, but it is still a somewhat diverse place. Great academics and party scene, prime example of a work hard, play hard school.

Bucknell is an excellent school, with unacceptable dining services. DO NOT come here if you are a vegetarian/vegan, care about healthy eating, or have an allergy. Last week, my friend with a nut allergy broke out in hives after eating a salad (and that's not an isolated incident). There are no vegan/vegetarian/healthy options since Bucknell changed dining providers, and while they made changes over winter break, they made no adjustments to accomodate students who fall into any of these categories. Instead, they added more fried chicken, burgers, and crepes to the menus. You can't even get a hot vegetable in the Bison or Commons dining areas (where upperclassman eat). I've communicated with them through their feedback texts, in a committee meeting, with the nutritionist, and over e-mail and provided multiple ideas on how they could improve things. Nothing has changed. All they've managed to do is stop serving raw chicken and the ramen noodles that turned people's urine neon green.

ai_summary: Carleton College offers a strong academic environment with dedicated professors , many of whom are deeply invested in mentoring students. The rigorous curriculum is complemented by a supportive and collaborative community , fostering relationships among students and faculty alike. The college's unique trimester system allows for focused learning but can also lead to a busy, high-pressure atmosphere, with some students expressing concerns about workload and stress levels. While student life includes various clubs and a lively atmosphere on weekends, some students feel that the social scene can be limiting and that the college lacks diversity, especially in terms of socioeconomic backgrounds. The small-town location of Northfield is seen as both a benefit for safety and tranquility, but also as a drawback for those seeking more vibrant urban experiences. Overall, students appreciate the strong sense of community and support but desire improvements in areas like student services and attention to mental health.

reviews.text: Carleton has quality education and professors, though the academics are often so intense that students have to give up extracurricular activities and sleep just to avoid falling behind. The social environment is heavily clique-based and offers little opportunity for students to develop friendships later on. Social bullying and cancel culture are rampant and create an abusive environment for anyone targeted by it. The administration performs a lot of lip service claiming to support mental health and belonging, but does not actually do enough to address either of these aspects.
Great school. Great education, great support, abundant resources. The best school if you want to pursue higher education

Carleton is a very academically rigorous college. It is not a party school and it does not have Greek life. If you are here for academics, you will be well-supported by staff and professors, and you will find strong resources if you plan to attend graduate school after. However, if you are expecting a large or active social scene, you are unlikely to find that at Carleton. Northfield is a small town, and most students do not spend much time in it. Most parties are small house parties rather than big events.
I loved my time at Carleton!!! Would do it again. Cute, safe, everything you need but not more type of town with good academics!

I chose Carleton over other more "highly ranked" colleges (whatever that means; you should choose the school that fits you, not because of a number on some arbitrary list). I visited in early March and fell in love with the campus and the student body. Every student I met just seemed happy and supportive, which I didn't necessarily see at other top SLACs. The sense of community shows in every aspect of Carleton. When I arrived for orientation week with my parents, a squad of upperclass student athletes showed up to carry up all of my stuff to my 3rd floor dorm room. There were information tables set up all over the campus to answer any questions and offer assistance. The alumni had a greeting area to help welcome us and upon entering the school, a group of students cheered every time a new student appeared (one dressed up as a cow). Stations offering water and popcorn had been set up in front of the main student building. That sense of camaraderie has remained. I love it here!

I spent 2 years at Carleton before transferring to a top national university. I regret heavily my decision as I loved Carleton a lot more, way better classes/lectures, tight-knit community and overall amazing people.

it feels so isolating, living in a small college town is not for the weak. food is terrible and hard to get parking, and the public transport is somewhat reliable but takes 2 hours to get to target which is where the pharmacy is, and if you use walgreens you have to walk there yourself which is 30 minutes if youre sick (not reasonable).
Beautiful campus—all four seasons offered something different Erving unique. Excellent academics and professors.

Carleton's professors have been ranked highest in undergraduate teaching for good reason, but some of their majors lack in teaching students transferable skills. If you plan to go here, connect early on with your advisor and the career center, and make sure that the classes you are taking will set you up to be an attractive candidate for jobs after graduation. Otherwise, the draw of the liberal arts education may make the process harder for you.
It is difficult to get into classes one wants specifically STEM related. Professors are not always the best but are usually the only option one can take.

I like the community that I've found at Carleton with my varsity sport team and I think it has been the most rewarding part of going to school here, but I don't think it supports athletics very well even considering it is a D3 school. I think student athletes are not involved in decisions affecting them and have little autonomy. Outside of sports, the academics is very rewarding. While difficult academically, I think the quality of professors is very high and there seems to be a lot of opportunities to make connections with alumni. I think there is a somewhat diverse student body racially, but not in terms of socioeconomic status, which I think is somewhere Carleton falls short.

Carleton doesn’t offer a lot for the price tag. Campus is beautiful but lacks resources, and there is nothing to do in town (Northfield was the first time I couldn’t get an Uber). Academically, you will always be drowning in readings; when it comes to anything career-related, you’re on your own, the only help you’ll get is being told to “reach out to alumni.” The job search has not been fun.
Carls are introverted, cliquey, and prioritize work. If you don’t “find your people” immediately, you’re out of luck. Clubs frequently shut down due to lack of interest, and mental health issues are widespread. A lot of people just don’t socialize—we call them “Shadow Carls.” The professors are more open than the students.
Carleton was isolating, menial, and stressful, and my post-grad experience so far hasn't shown me it was worth it.
Also, fair warning: if you’re a man who likes men, the dating pool is practically nonexistent and Northfield is not known for being the most LGBT-friendly place.

When I tell people I went to Carleton, their reaction is usuall “where is that?” or “is that a community college?” Carleton and other liberal arts colleges try to tempt high school kids by saying their experience is unmatched, bragging about its #1 teaching rank. But honestly? Those teachers hold your hand like crazy, to the point where you’re not challenged. Carleton is not a place for self starters. In my experience, it’s a great place for people who always want to stay in their comfort zone, never becoming a self-starter. And the “Carleton Quirk” was not charming—picture all the awkward kids in high school in one room. You might not have graduate students teaching you like universities do, but instead, undergraduates do that. I prefer someone who already is in graduate school teach me than a classmate I saw partying a week ago. And some professors are racist and favor white students. This school has very limited resources, and finding a job and networking is awful after graduating.

All the professors I've had have been wonderful, I think that academically Carleton is a very strong school. However, I think that the administration should listen to student concerns, especially about social justice issues.

Carleton is academically rigorous and provides challenging but beneficial opportunities for learning. The professors are passionate and committed to teaching and most students are collaborative and passionate about learning. There are many different student orgs and I've been able to find a community I enjoy both in and outside student orgs. The general student body feels fairly homogenous --even though admissions highlights students with various interests, they all tend to be the same "type" of person. Carleton runs on a quick pace due to the trimester system but I love it. I'm able to only take 3 classes at a time and be done with classes/finals before we go on winter/spring break. Carleton is a predominantly white institution and that has been difficult for me as a student of color from a very diverse home city. I found my people eventually, but that is one things I would change about Carleton. Northfield is a nice, safe town and there are lots of cool ways to engage with it.

ai_summary: Centre College offers a unique experience characterized by its strong academics and a tight-knit community. Students frequently praise the supportive professors , who are approachable and committed to student success, providing personalized attention in small class sizes. The college excels in study abroad opportunities, allowing students to immerse themselves in diverse cultures while also emphasizing personal growth and leadership development. Despite its many strengths, some reviews mention challenges such as a lack of extracurricular activities and concerns about food options and diversity on campus. Overall, students appreciate the enriching academic environment , the chance to build meaningful relationships, and the stunning campus , while noting that the college may not fit those seeking a bustling social scene or extensive off-campus activities.

reviews.text: Good school with great faculty, just super expensive for no reason. If you like a small college and have the money then Centre is a great choice
Beautiful campus and wonderful place to continue education after high school! Danville is a really nice little town, and takes care of Centre students. I love it here!

Centre College provides a strong academic experience. The class sizes are small, and the professors genuinely care about your learning and success. I’ve grown a lot academically here, and I appreciate the opportunities for things like studying abroad and building close relationships with faculty.
That said, outside of academics, a lot of the experience feels average. Social life can be limited, especially if you’re not involved in Greek life or athletics. Campus activities and events could be more diverse and engaging, and the small-town setting doesn’t offer much to do off-campus.
Another major downside is the cost. Tuition and overall expenses are high, and while there is financial aid, it’s still something many students struggle with.
Overall, Centre is a good place for a strong education, but there’s definitely room for improvement in campus life and affordability.
Centre has been a great school. It’s a small liberal arts school with wonderful professors and so many extracurricular activities.

Centre College is amazing. The size of the school means that you get to form real connections with your professors, and there are so many opportunities that are super accessible like study abroad, internships, and research. I've met some of my closest friends here and have made so many life long connections. Centre has also been really great about meeting financial need, and there are many great scholarship programs you can apply for.

Overall, my experience at Centre has been very fulfilling. I have been able to not only cultivate my passions and explore new things, but also meet some amazing people who support me in all aspects of my college experience. Some things that I feel are lacking at the college are mainly communication between administration offices and financial issues. The administration promises a lot of things to incoming and current students, but there are times that those promises are not fulfilled due to financial issues. There have also been problems with grade deflation recently that administration has yet to acknowledge.

Centre College offers a close-knit, supportive community with small class sizes and professors who truly care. The academics are rigorous, and the study abroad opportunities are incredible—something that really sets Centre apart. I’ve grown a lot both intellectually and personally.
That said, the college could do more to support diversity and expand cultural programming. Social life can also feel limited due to the rural setting. Still, it’s a great place for those looking for a personalized, high-quality education.

Centre College shaped me in complex ways. As a first-generation Black immigrant student in a predominantly white, rural environment, I often felt isolated and unseen. That emotional toll impacted my academic performance during personal and family health crises. Still, Centre became a place of growth. I found purpose through leadership roles as a Posse Scholar, Resident Assistant, and research student. I conducted studies on reproductive health, developed diagnostic tools for underserved regions, and studied abroad in Mexico, gaining hands-on clinical experience. While I’d like to see more culturally responsive support and representation for students of color, I’m grateful for the relationships and opportunities I found. Centre pushed me to find my voice, advocate for others, and grow into a scholar grounded in purpose. It didn’t just educate me—it helped shape the future physician I’m becoming.

I love Centre so much! Classes are academically challenging in the best way and professors genuinely care about their students. All of my professors have always been willing to chat with me and they say hi when they see me around campus. Studying abroad is pretty much guaranteed; I was able to have a wonderful experience during a Centreterm trip and several of my friends have studied abroad for a semester and had a blast. I am on a sports team and on that team I feel as though I am part of a family. Centre puts a lot of attention into their athletics and we are able to thrive. Campus food has improved drastically since the hiring of Chef Desmond and Einstein's Bagels is always consistent. The campus is small enough to walk across in fifteen minutes from one end to another and the population is small enough that you can recognize a lot of people, but still be able to meet new people in your classes every semester. I would recommend everyone to take a look at Centre.

I feel that the college needs a lot improvement in it's student life experience. I feel that with such a small area in which it is located it could use the help of the community and staff to create more of an experience for those who may not be from the area or the state.
I love Centre College! I would recommend it to anyone who is interested. All of my friends love it too.
I love it here at centre college, i wouldn’t want to be anywhere else for my undergrad. I would like to see more parking lots around campus but other than that it’s great.

Centre college has been a wonderful experience so far. I have loved the relationships that I have built with the other students, but vastly more valuable are the relationships I have built with my professors. The Centre college professors teach classes of no more than thirty students which ensures that we each get personalized attention and care for all of our courses. Furthermore they are a constantly available resource for students through required office hours and ease of access. Without the professors Centre isn't Centre.

I've come from very far, and made a home here at Centre. This is a community that is supportive, understanding and inspirational, which will aid you in your journey of learning. The most impactful thing about Centre is its people! I will cherish the people I found here for the rest of my life.

Great experience for me and wouldn’t be where I am without Centre. I got to study in Europe and have a double major. All of my professors were amazing teachers and cared about me as a person. I got to play sports and learned as much on the field as off. My team mates were my best friends. I joined a sorority and that was fun too. Rush wasn’t stressful and I felt like I could join where I felt most comfortable. Housing gets better as you get older. I think they do a good job with it. Not sure why a couple others had a bad review. Centre alums are fiercely loyal and want to help eachother. They call us the Centre mafia haha

Claremont McKenna College is an incredible mix of interesting people, a beautiful campus with great weather, and an overall fun atmosphere, while having the priviledge to join some of the most interesting and thoughtful classes.

I had an excellent experience at Claremont McKenna College. I played football for the Stags, and the combination of elite academics and competitive athletics created an environment that pushed me to grow in every dimension.
CMC is best suited for students who want to challenge the status quo and learn how to think, reason, and debate ideas rather than simply memorize information. The professors are deeply engaged, classes are discussion-driven, and the culture encourages intellectual independence. You’re constantly surrounded by ambitious peers who care about ideas, leadership, and making an impact.
If you’re looking for a rigorous academic environment where students are pushed to develop strong judgment, analytical thinking, and the confidence to challenge conventional wisdom, CMC is an exceptional place to spend four years.
Overall, I enjoy my experience and the challenging academics that come with a balanced liberal arts environment, and faculty who care.
CMC has been wonderful for me, my academics are great, my workload is manageable, and the school helps with our internships

CMC is truly the most unique school. Certainly not for everyone, but perfect for me. The social life is what you make of it. Never going to be like a frat party, but amazing in it's own way. Every party feels like your best friends birthday. Such a tight-knit community where everyone truly cares about each other. People gossip, but in a caring way, not a cutting-elbows way. Academics are obviously top-notch, and career services are INSANE. They legitimately hold your hand and won't let you leave unemployed.

My son is a CMC student and I can't imagine a more amazing place. The professors are wonderful, the administration has been incredible, the campus is beautiful, the town is the nicest college town I've been to. Because of the consortium, you get all of the benefits of a small school combined with everything that a medium sized school has to offer. The high rankings are well deserved.

My brother went here not me so this is based on what I heard, so there you go.
just to keep it under 100 words.

Claremont McKenna College has truly exceeded my expectations in my first semester. The small class sizes foster close relationships with professors who genuinely care about their students' success. The academic rigor is balanced by an incredibly supportive community among peers and within the larger Claremont Consortium, which provides access to endless resources and opportunities. The campus culture emphasizes collaboration and leadership, creating a motivating academic and personal growth environment. Plus, the sunny Southern California weather and proximity to LA make it an exciting place to explore. Overall, it’s been an amazing start to college life!

My experience has been built through the people I hang out with. Claremont McKenna allows you to experience multiple encounters with people across the globe, as well as the alumni network is ultimately extent that gives you an opportunity to the workplace in a extended manner.

Having graduated this past May, I miss CMC immensely. The school's resources are unparalleled, enabling students to fund trips, internships, and other experiences. I was filled with gratitude every time I walked from my dorm towards Kravis for class or to work at one of the institutes.
One of the best things about CMC is its culture of smart, driven, emotionally intelligent people. CMC's culture is polarizing but not particularly exclusive - having not studied econ, lived in north quad, or attended many parties, I still felt like I was well-integreated into the community. That being said, some people feel alienated by the status quo on campus: Many students are the type that lean obsessive about advancing their careers and about 50% of families pay full price (almost $90k), and there are many people on campus who complain about these things.
If I had to do it all over again, I would 100% choose CMC. The classes, the campus, and the faculty and staff are unbelievably special.

The academic experience here is beyond amazing with so many opportunities and great professors. Small class sizes, great clubs, study abroad programs, guest speakers, etc. all help contribute to the environment here.
I am a senior at CMC. It has been awful. No fun events on campus. There is an active push by the administration to make the campus significantly less social. My biggest regret is going to CMC.

My college experience has been largely positive, especially when it comes to career development. The career network and support for internships are outstanding, providing numerous opportunities to connect with professionals and gain valuable experience. The funding available for all kinds of programs is another highlight—whether you're interested in attending conferences, pursuing independent projects, or something else entirely, there's substantial financial backing to help make it happen.
On academics, the professors are incredibly supportive and genuinely invested in student success. One downside is the limited offering is disappointing for those interested in creative disciplines.
It's worth noting that the campus can sometimes feel like a finance-oriented bubble. However, if you dig a little deeper, you'll find that it's possible to create your own community that aligns with your personal interests and values.
Good school. Small town vibe. Very easy to get from class to class and things of that nature. I liked it.
As I expressed in the last question, I had an opportunity to learn far from home, and my education was a life changing.

ai_summary: Colby College is known for its strong academic programs , particularly in the humanities and STEM fields, with professors who are generally supportive and engaged. However, the college faces criticism regarding its dorm conditions and food quality, which many reviewers describe as inadequate. The campus community is described as tight-knit, with opportunities for involvement in various clubs and outdoor activities, though the social scene, especially for partying, is often considered lacking. Some students express concerns about the lack of diversity and inclusivity , particularly for students of color. The college’s isolated location can be a downside for some, and while it offers a beautiful campus and potential for academic growth, students should be prepared for challenges related to housing and social life.

reviews.text: I’ve enjoyed my two years at Colby, the administration could certainly be more in touch with the students wants but overall good communication and on campus life
Colby creates a very welcoming environment for all students. The professors here want you to succeed.

There's great academic and academic focused opportunities that is superior to other colleges of similar size. There is a competitive athletics scene. However, the school is located in a rural area, limiting the city potential. As well, parties are hosted by athletic teams, and the athletic teams are very clique based.
The people at Colby are honestly so special. I had met some of my best friends here. I would categorize everyone as a “doer”, always looking for the next adventure
Love the new dining services. Professors are amazing. Parties could be better. I've had no problems with housing.

Heavy party/drinking/hookup culture, but easily avoided if desired -- you don't feel left out or socially ostracized if you don't party. Food, housing, etc. are all adequate, general daily social life and culture are phenomenal, students and professors are great, academics are strong. Lots of opportunities for professional and personal growth, trying new things, having fun, meeting people, etc.

Colby has always had a great sense of community. Like anywhere else, Colby has it's problems, but I was always impressed by how the community came together with humanity to overcome the inevitable difficulties that arrive. In addition, the academics were world class and focused on pushing students towards excellence. I absolutely owe my professors at Colby and the institution overall a ton of credit for all I've accomplished in my career.

The average cost of attendance is MUCH higher than what niche tells you. it cost me nearly 50k per year at Colby to attend. I was promised a full financial aid ride at Colby based on my parent's financial status and that was the only reason that I accepted going to Colby. Now, I am almost $200,000 in student load debt and my parents have paid all they can out of pocket to try and help me. The living conditions are terrible. The dorms are overcrowded and the college continues to admit more students than it has room for in housing so that they can get more money. The dining halls have had the department of health called on them several times due to the amount of raw chicken and dangerous foods they have served, and there is no longer a student health center on campus. What used to be the student health center was turned into a counseling center and students are now forced to go to the hospital if they need medical care regardless of the severity of their conditions. I could go on and on.

Academics and professors are fantastic people that do the best they can with the cards administration has given them. Besides that I was absolutely miserable here and dreaded going back after every summer. The food sucks, the student body consists of a ton of spoiled white kids from Massachusetts with no social skills, the dorms haven’t been updated in what seems like centuries to the point some dorm’s dry wall is falling apart and the ceiling in one of the dorms is falling in, the tuition is INSANELY overpriced for the quality of life and education.
I really appreciated being part of a passionate, driven community of students who wanted to get involved in all aspects of campus life.
I loved my time at Colby. The relationships I developed with my professors and the education I received is top tier. I made the friends of a lifetime and couldn’t be happier with my choice of school.

Colby is a special place to go to school. Professors go out of their way to know you as both a person and a student, which has helped me grow. I’ve even been to their housing and met their families. The academics are excellent, and professors care that you truly understand the material rather than just memorize it.
Outside of class, everyone is friendly, and there’s a surprising amount to do for a school of our size and location. Career opportunities are also great. DavisConnects, our career center, hosts plenty of events and is great at helping students find opportunities.
The only real downsides are housing and food. The housing office can be tough to deal with, and some dorms aren’t in great shape. That said, they’re building a new dorm and just switched dining providers (as of the 2025–2026 school year), so both should improve.
Overall, Colby is what you make of it. If you dive into the opportunities here, the college will help you go far.

Amazing school with some of the nicest people I've encountered. Staff are lovely and enjoy getting to know everyone. Professors are generally kind and very flexible, especially through accommodations and mental health struggles. School abides by a non-competitive environment which was the highlight of my experience. Although food can be subpar and housing can be a bit tight, I wouldn't have chosen another school to spend my four years. I found my best friends through my athletic team and housing. For a small school, I felt deeply connected to everyone, but also like I was a part of a greater organization.
My experience at Colby has been everything I expected and more. It’s easy to get involved, campus feels safe, and resources are available

I loved the community at Colby! I made some of my best friends. The school did an incredible job asking students what they want and need.
There are also tons of opportunities to get involved, which creates really good leadership opportunities and good experience.

ai_summary: Colgate University offers a unique college experience with academically rigorous programs and a strong sense of community. Students appreciate the small class sizes and engaging professors, which foster personal relationships and rich discussions. The university promotes a "work hard, play hard" culture, noted for its vibrant social scene , particularly through Greek life, though this can create a sense of exclusivity for those outside these circles. While there are numerous extracurricular activities and a solid alumni network that aids career opportunities, the campus is located in a very rural setting, which some find limiting. Additionally, concerns about the level of inclusion and diversity have been raised, particularly impacting students of color and those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds. Despite mixed feelings about campus life and challenges with dining options and housing conditions, many students feel grateful for their supportive environment and the opportunities provided at Colgate.
