reviews.text: As a student-athlete, I really enjoyed Colgate's commitment to athletics. Additionally, every professor I had was incredibly understanding, intelligent, and had a strong desire to see their students succeed.

I'm a parent. I had the benefit of sending two kids to college at the same time; one went to a state school, one went to Colgate. Colgate is around 3x the cost and fell short in every respect.
Communication with parents: minimal. Freshman orientation: lackluster. The food is abysmal. The dining hall is small, constantly filthy, and the food choices are incredibly limited. Sometimes my kid won't eat because it's too busy to get food in a reasonable amount of time or because the choices are so bad. There are really no other options, the town is so small.
If you don't have a car, you're reliant on the very slow shuttle. One day my kid was waiting in town for the shuttle in 20° temps and it never came.
Campus is not accessible and very low on diversity. They prioritize their wealthy students - which is most of them. There is no effort to integrate the middle class and low income students, and that isn't observation: it's backed up by their lousy social mobility scores.

Transferring out was one of the best decisions I've made in my life. Good academics, pretty campus. Student body is insufferable beyond belief. There is no joy here for a normal human being. Like a fever dream of a boarding school from the twilight zone. 0/10

Colgate is an amazing school if you fit a certain archetype. If you're white, generationally wealthy, and from the northeast, this is the perfect school. If you're not, I would not recommend coming here. It can be very isolating. The winters are brutal, the location is very remote, and the social scene is exclusive and elitist. Greek life dominates the party scene, and is generally a reinforcer of the school's elitism. Many students here have never had to interact with people from different economic backgrounds and never will. It sometimes feels as if you don't exist to a large portion of the student body. The academics are pretty decent, with most professors being great. As a senior, I've had a small close friend group whom all hold most of these sentiments. Honestly, if I could do college all over again, there is no way I would even apply here. My freshman year was enjoyable but things quickly went downhill.
The campus is very isolating, and the experience here is extremely hit or miss. Freshman year is underwhelming, and it doesn't always get better for everyone.

I graduated in the Middle Ages, I mean the mid-1900s. It was all- men then, and now is 55% female. The typical Colgate student, then and now, is a smart, social party animal who is probably a jock or jock wannabe and will go on to success as a lawyer, doctor, business executive or educator. I was. Colgate taught me to write and think, My father went to Colgate also, and was the same type person. Although a small school, it has big time athletics, and in fact had the only football team ever to go the whole season without allowing a point. Football, soccer, hockey, and basketball all have had nationally ranked teams, and the athletes are not cloistered in their own special dorms and classes- they are with all the other students. I had some All-Americans in my fraternity. Partying at Colgate has always been a legendary part of Colgate life. But it is “Study hard, party hard”, and most students are all business during the week.

The school is in a beautiful area with a lot of great people and opportunities. You do really have to apply yourself to get what you want from your experience there, but you can pretty much do anything you put your mind to.

Colgate seems to emphasize developing its students and their character. Students learn to be gritty up against tough academics and a unideal location. The social scene may be lacking for some, but for a small school it is very strong.

I am an alum from the 90s. I happened onto this site on a lark. Perhaps someone will find my long-term perspectives valuable.
Academics: extremely rigorous. I once figured that I read many hundreds of books during the college years and wrote almost as many papers. I had friends from local, well-known state schools that claimed to write less than 10 papers during their entire college careers, which amazed me. I have some additional experience with the Ivy League and well-known schools and feel the academic rigor at Colgate is at least as intense.
Social life: very limited in scope. It has maybe always been a go Greek or die sort of place. The Greeks are uniquely elitist - I have more easily intermingled with bastions of American WASPness than certain Greek elements while at Colgate. I feel this comes from a mix of two factors: tier-1 social elites always saw a place like Colgate as a backup, and pretenders always try harder.

I am currently a sophomore and what I have liked about this school so far is: the campus. Quite literally the only good thing about this school. Beautiful buildings with fantastic sunsets (except for the 14ish months of winter each year). Greek life is too important now that the jug is shut down. The 2 other bars are exclusive to juniors and seniors. There is nothing to do here besides drink at a frat house or stay home and watch a movie. Nothing happens in this town and when it does, it shuts down before 8pm just like every other business. I thought I used to love it, but at this point I can confidently say I made a mistake coming here.

Colgate University has a beautiful campus housed in the quaint village of Hamilton, NY. I enjoyed living there for four years, and my housing experience the entire time was quite satisfactory. I am satisfied with the education I received at Colgate. I was given the opportunity to explore my interests, with the full support of my advisor and professors. At the time of my graduation in 2017, Colgate still had a lot of work to do in terms of improving diversity and inclusion on campus, and in providing adequate support to minority groups.

Colgate gave me my real start in life in a variety of ways. I continue to support my Alma Mater in many ways. I still have many friends who I connect with to this day. My experience at Colgate has been a guiding light all my life.

As a freshman, I found the best thing about Colgate was the academics. My professors were all accommodating and provided ample opportunities to learn. There were plenty of office hours if I needed clarity and small class sizes made it easier to answer questions or pose them. Social life is harder as the school has a tendency to attract more upper-class students which leads to many people with an elitist view. Additionally, as a freshman, you have no access to any greek life parties.

I have found Colgate to thus far be a welcoming community, committed to fostering a diverse, safe space to promote student growth. Fellow students look to make genuine connections, while each driven by the rigour of academics. Professors are easily accessible and truly care for their students well being.

Colgate University was a fabulous place! Beautiful campus and peaceful surrounding Chenango Valley. From the first time you step foot on campus to graduation and reunions, there is a place for everyone and the Colgate alumni network is strong. Go 'Gate!

ai_summary: At the College of Saint Benedict, students often emphasize the strong sense of community and support available both academically and socially. Many reviews highlight the approachable professors and small class sizes , which foster personalized learning and meaningful relationships. The campus is described as beautiful, surrounded by nature, and is praised for providing a welcoming and friendly atmosphere where students feel at home. While the dining options and diversity could use improvement, most students value their involvement in clubs, activities, and the opportunities for personal growth through programs like study abroad. Sports and campus events also receive positive mentions, contributing to a vibrant student life , although some express concerns about the limitations of the party scene and the rural location. Overall, CSB is seen as a place that not only excels academically but also enriches students' lives through connections and experiences.

reviews.text: Love the nursing department - professors are my second moms - def check them out!! Beautiful scenery

The College of Saint Benedict in St. Joseph, MN offers a warm, values‑driven liberal arts experience that feels both intimate and ambitious. Professors are approachable, classes are small, and there’s a genuine focus on developing women leaders through intellectual, spiritual, and service‑oriented opportunities. The shared resources and co‑curricular life with Saint John’s University expand your horizons — joint events, shared academic programs, and cross‑campus collaboration make the community richer. Beautiful woodland and campus scenery, strong arts programs, and a deeply supportive student community really make everyday life here special.

The CSB community is an amazing place to live, work and learn. CSB and SJU are two institutions that come together to form a really unique academic experience that is truly one of a kind. Faculty are incredibly dedicated to student learning in a strong academic community. There are strong athletic programs, club sports and intramural opportunities on both campuses and a great opportunity to be involved in wide range of clubs and campus organizations.
St. Bens is the very best! A true community with small class sizes and great faculty. I have loved my experience in all aspects!
This place has been and will always feel like home. The community is inviting, and it is abundantly clear how much leaders, alums, employees, and students care about you and this place.

St. Ben's is right in St. Joe's cute little downtown with St. John's gorgeous wooded trails and lake access, providing the best of both worlds. Professors take time to really get to know their students and take an interest in them. Alums are generally thought very highly of in the professional fields.

I love the campus life and that night life activities are within a walking distance. I also love the beauty of the campus, especially St. John’s University. I love that my professors push me out of my comfort zone. For example, I am taking sociology 111 this semester and my professor Jake Jantzer is having us interview real people around the CSBSJU campuses for our documentary project and we get to make a documentary that relates to sociology. My group is doing our documentary on Monastics and monks and I have loved getting to talk to the monks in person and learn more about what made them want to be a part of the monastic community here at CSBSJU!
id describe csb+sju like a rich highschool. pretty clicky and dressing / acting different isnt really supported here.. people are mean

CSB and SJU truly becomes your community during your time on campus. Developed not only deep relationships with my peers, but faculty as well. The faculty truly care about who you are as an individual and get to know you well. The alum network is highly connected and takes care of CSB/SJU grads. The friendships I made with Bennies and Johnnies are still some of the deepest connections I have in my adult life.

Choosing CSB+SJU was one of the best decisions I’ve made. From day one, I felt welcomed into a community that is both supportive and inspiring. Both campuses are beautiful, but what truly stands out are the people—students, professors, and staff who genuinely care about your growth, not just academically but personally as well.
Classes are small enough that professors know you by name, and they go out of their way to help you succeed. Beyond the classroom, there are countless opportunities to get involved— sports, clubs, leadership programs, service projects, and connections with Saint John’s University students that expand your circle even further.
While some of the housing could use a bit of updating, there are new modern accommodations for upper classmen on both campuses. If you’re looking for a college where you’ll be challenged academically, supported personally, and surrounded by people who want you to thrive, CSB+SJU is a place worth considering.

My time at St. Ben's was truly wonderful and transformative. It's where I gained an incredible education, valuable career skills, and also met my best friends. The sense of community was so strong, and it made every day feel special. Sounds cheesy, but it's true!
I genuinely believe St. Ben's prepared me for life and my career in ways few other places could. The combination of strong academics and a valuable student employment experience gave me the practical skills and confidence I needed to succeed. Beyond all the learning, I had so much fun. It was a time of great personal growth and joy.
When I think about what could be changed, it's a tough question because my experience was so positive. If I had to suggest something, I'd say St. Ben's could continue to expand its career services, offering even more hands-on opportunities for all majors through internships and local partnerships. This would only enhance the strong foundation it already provides.
A campus that leans into its mission to elevate the student experience. A wonderful, welcoming campus focused on a four year residential experience with lots of clubs, activities, and athletics.
Campus is always buzzing with welcoming people and there’s quite a bit to do. One thing I would change is that I wish we had a beach at St Ben’s too instead of just St John’s!

as a full-time student at the college of saint benedict, my academic experience has been strong: professors genuinely care about students, classes encourage critical thinking, and small class sizes make it easy to get one-on-one help.

The coordinate relationship with Saint John's University makes it a truly unique experience. The opportunity to live with all women in great on-campus housing, but to have a co-ed experience overall was amazing. The faculty truly want students to succeed and do all they can to help students along the way.

ai_summary: At the College of Wooster, students frequently highlight the strong academic rigor and the exceptional support from professors , noting that small class sizes foster personal relationships and an engaging learning environment. Many appreciate the opportunities for independent study and research, which allow for personalized academic exploration . While the vibrant community and diverse extracurricular activities are often praised, some students find the small-town setting limiting, particularly in terms of social life and cultural activities. Concerns about campus resources, including dining quality and housing conditions, are common, with several remarks on the need for modernization and sensitivity from administration regarding student needs. Overall, while there are challenges, many students feel that the supportive atmosphere , academic opportunities, and tight-knit community make Wooster a rewarding place to study.

reviews.text: Something I really enjoyed was how many opportunities I was able to take advantage of while I was at Wooster. I got to take bagpipe lessons (and eventually join the Pipe Band), had the opportunity to take scuba diving lessons (and get certified), and took a lot of other fun courses that were outside of my majors and minors (such as archery, highland dance, etc.). I also got to participate in fun study abroad summer TREK programs, represent Wooster at Model UN Conferences in Chicago and New York City each year (and visit the UN headquarters in New York), and get paid for my summer unpaid internships through the APEX Microfellowship and APEX Fellowship. I also really appreciated how accessible the professors are in helping me one-on-one to answer any questions I have, help guide me throughout my learning (and Independent Study), and be such strong supporters for my peers and me.

The College of Wooster is a good school with rigorous classes and highly educated professors. However, the administration at the college is always making changes to lower the quality of student life. Whether it be raising tuition at an unnecessary rate, limiting student health resources, or adding in hidden fees to tuition, only mentioned once in an essay-long email, the school makes changes that make it hard to want to stay. However, all of the staff anf students I have interacted with through my time here inspire me to stay, and the opportunities to conduct hands-on undergraduate research starting as a first year are something

I went to Wooster for my first two years (3 semesters). I am now transferring.
I enjoyed Wooster for about a month, but then the college quickly got old; I wasn’t on any sports teams, and was only in clubs. To be honest, you really have to be on a sports team to make your experience even somewhat enjoyable, as there definitely is a bias towards athletes.
There were some aspects I liked: the area surrounding Wooster is very safe, mainly because it's in the “middle of nowhere,” but also because campus safety does a good job.
Food on campus is ok, but very lacking. If I could get rid of the meal plan, I would have.
One thing that I didn’t think about (I’m in business) is on-campus recruiting. At my future school, it is a large part of the atmosphere- at Wooster, it is non-existent.
I am happy to say that I will be transferring to a US News & World Report T20 school, so I can thank Wooster for that. However, I do wish I had chosen to spend my last 1.5 years elsewhere.

Wooster is a small liberal arts school in Wooster, Ohio.
Academics: The school was fairly underwhelming academically. The students are brilliant; however, the professors are not very good.
Career: The college does not prepare you for a career at all. A large part of my graduating class did not have a job or grad school lined up. The college has some of the worst career fairs I have ever seen. We had to go to Ohio State for some career and grad school fairs. Despite the lack of college help, alumni are willing to go the extra mile for you if you reach out to them to network. This was one of the main reasons Wooster helped me get my current job. I had to do everything on my own.
Campus life: Trying.
Food/Housing: You have to live on campus all four years, which means Juniors and Seniors live in dorms. Food is not bad at all; students like to complain about the lack of options, but for a school with monetary issues, you can’t be upset.
Is the college worth the price? NO.

The Professor and Academics at the College of Wooster are truly amazing but the housing and campus life could see some real improvement. The party scene is better than you would expect, but it is an adjustment if you are from a big city.
Professors at the College of Wooster seek to help you succeed and take you into account all the time.

The College of Wooster is one of the best kept secrets when it comes to small liberal arts schools in the Midwest. The sense of community among students, faculty, and staff is unmatched. The curriculum builds on itself from the First Year Seminar through the required Independent Study program to prepare every student to conduct research, analyze data, think critically, and communicate effectively. The experiential learning program paired with the research component ensures that students have a variety of academic and career experiences inside and outside of the classroom, better preparing College of Wooster graduates for an ever-evolving world. The student population is diverse, the faculty and staff genuinely care about and mentor the students, and the campus grounds are beautifully maintained. The College also boasts a new science building, renovated student union, renovated residence halls, and the state-of-the-art athletic facility. There's no place like The College of Wooster!

I'm a freshman chemistry major at The College of Wooster in Ohio and a member of the football team. I've really enjoyed my time here so far—especially experiencing the changing seasons. The instructors are supportive, and the campus community is dedicated to helping students succeed.

I love Wooster, it is a close knit school and the professors really do care about you and want you to succeed. However after a change in food vendors the food isn't as great as it used to be, some of the dorms are really old and small.
The relationship with the town was challenging at times--there were multiple instances of violence against students from the townies. The college did their best and the deans were good to work with.

As a freshman at the College of Wooster, I've had a mixed experience. The professors and academics are exceptional; they’re knowledgeable and supportive, making classes engaging and thought-provoking. However, the weather and location have been significant challenges. Coming from a warmer climate, I’ve struggled with the cold winters and gray skies. The small-town environment feels limiting, and I’ve found it difficult to connect with the surrounding area. While the academics are strong, the overall college experience here isn’t the right fit for me, and I’m beginning to realize I need something different.
I had a great 4 years at the college of wooster. I was able to learn a lot from great professors as well as make great friends.

Great academics, great facilities, good housing, good safety. Small school so overall many students are very connected. It is a great college if you are a small town kind of person who wants that tight community. However, if you aren't this type of person, this isn't the place for you.
College of Wooster is a supportive community, has diverse opportunities, and a strong focus on personal and academic growth.

The College of Wooster is considered very academically rigorous. Despite being in rural Ohio, this place is relatively safe for LGBTQ+ community. The professors really care about you and your success as well as the staff. Campus Safety is very active when there are threats near campus and will alert everyone as it happens and when it concludes. The only problem is the food gets bland after awhile and the administration makes questionable choices.

ai_summary: At the College of the Holy Cross, students often highlight the strong academic environment and dedicated professors as two of the standout features. Many reviews mention the professors' accessibility and commitment to student success, creating a supportive atmosphere that fosters personal and intellectual growth. The small class sizes enable meaningful connections between students and faculty, enhancing the overall learning experience. While the academics are generally praised for their rigor, some reviews indicate that the workload can be quite heavy. The sense of community also receives significant attention, with numerous students citing the welcoming and friendly nature of their peers and the close-knit campus environment. However, concerns about the food quality, housing, and limited diversity emerge in multiple reviews, alongside mixed feelings about the student life, particularly the heavy party culture that influences social interactions. Overall, Holy Cross is characterized as a place where students can thrive academically and build lasting friendships, although challenges related to inclusivity and administration's engagement with student needs are noted.

reviews.text: I thought it was an amazing opportunity. I think that anyone fortunate enough to be able to attend should take the chance.

6 months after graduation and still cannot find work ( over 100 resumes out), nearly half of the friends ( all good students) I graduated with are in the same boat. Career services had no connections or help. Was not worth the investment, especially seeing my friends with state college degrees all working.

I can’t really complain. The college allows for student exploration, in fact it is encouraged. Proffesors all have your best interest in mind and the school does a very good job pushing for community among students.

As a chemistry major on the premed track, I can honestly say Holy Cross has prepared me for the road ahead. Hoping to pursue a career in medicine, I am extremely happy to say that Holy Cross has given me the knowledge and motivation necessary to achieve my career goals. As an athlete as well, my coaches and teammates inspire me to become the best version of myself and utilize the lessons taught on the filed to become a great physician some day.

Holy Cross is all about building strong, free thinking communities. With a 17% acceptance rate for the class of 2029 - it has become increasingly hard to get in, but it’s well worth the effort. The administration and professors put a lot of time and energy into creating an enriching academic experience and a robust student life. It’s by no means easy, and your professors will push you very hard - but the friends you make here are forever, and the alumni network is amazing. I’m glad I chose Holy Cross and would do it again in a heartbeat.

The biggest issue that I have with the school is that after paying $500,000 in tuition over 4 yrs, my twin sons who both graduated Magna Cum Laude were given zero help finding work from career services and are currently unemployed with over 60 resumes out. Save your money and go to a state school and explore an internship, unless its Harvard or MIT, it makes no difference anymore, its just a cash grab.
The campus is beautiful. Lots of resources, good people. Lots of opportunities to get involved. Some professors are there for research funding over teaching.

The three words I'd use to describe a Holy Cross student are welcoming, intelligent, and fun. Work Hard Play Hard culture is strong. I learned so much and met the best people. I am smarter for going there, I am happier for going there, and I am a better person for going there. I highly recommend.

My experience of the school was that the small size of the school allowed for a more personal and interactive experience with professors and peers, dependent on if you find your interests in student groups and organizations. It can feel limited in the resources and student experiences it offers. However, the academics are strong and the professors really care that you learn and are challenged.

My overall experience at the College of the Holy Cross has been a mix of positives and negatives. The party culture is strong, especially from Thursday to Sunday, and smoking is quite common. While some students are highly focused on academics, the majority seem more relaxed, and only about 50 freshmen maintain a GPA above 3.5.
On the bright side, the professors here are very nice and supportive, which makes a big difference in the learning experience. However, the food and housing leave much to be desired. The quality of meals on campus isn’t great, and the living conditions could definitely be improved.
Very good school. Very safe, over hated food and dorms, students and professors are all very nice, party culture exists but is not overwhelming, important to put yourself out there early in the year

I have thoroughly enjoyed my time at Holy Cross. I have met tons of people from all over the US who have become good friends. Additionally, I have enjoyed all of my classes and my professors have been super helpful and are committed to my learning experience.

Holy Cross is truly a unique gem among the nation's liberal arts colleges that only seems to improve with each passing year. The College offers one of the best study abroad programs of any liberal arts institution and is often a noted highlight of a student's experience. Classes are often rigorous and a majority of the school's faculty are distinguished members of their field. Faculty members are also always more than willing to engage with their students outside of the classroom setting to offer any type of help/guidance needed. Attending Holy Cross is truly a wonderful experience both academically and socially!
I love my time at holy cross. The professors are great, food is one of the best around. I just wish that the dorms would be better and the bathrooms as well.

Attending Holy Cross has been transformative, blending rigorous academics with vibrant campus life. Engaging with passionate professors and diverse peers has fostered intellectual growth and lifelong friendships. Extracurricular activities, from clubs to sports, have enhanced my leadership skills and personal interests. The dynamic environment encourages critical thinking, creativity, and resilience. Overall, Holy Cross has nurtured mine and my peers’ development, preparing us for future challenges and opportunities.

ai_summary: Colorado College has a unique academic structure known as the Block Plan, allowing students to focus on one subject intensely for about three and a half weeks. Many students appreciate this immersive learning approach , helping them engage deeply with material, particularly in subjects like science and fieldwork-heavy courses. However, the block schedule can be stressful, and some feel it's difficult to retain information over such a short period. The professors are frequently cited as a strong asset, being approachable , supportive , and dedicated to students' success. Student life is described as friendly and collaborative , with a strong emphasis on outdoor activities, but there's a notable lack of diversity in the student body, which some find unwelcoming. While many enjoy the campus atmosphere and the community feeling, concerns about the administration, affordability, and mental health resources are common. Overall, CC seems to attract driven students who thrive in a rigorous but supportive environment, though it may not be the best fit for everyone, particularly those who prefer a more traditional academic structure or seek a more diverse setting.

reviews.text: I really enjoyed my experince at colorado college. The academics are challenging and push students to grow their knoledge and abilities.

I am a current first year at CC and I have been really digging into the life here. Not to say the school doesn’t have issues, it definitely has some but in my opinion the pros outweighs the cons. So here is a pro/con list.
Pros:
- Real, passionate, academic rigor for the better or worse.
-Campus gym has good quality lifting/cardio equipment and access to:
+A pool available to all during open swim hours
+A free climbing gym with free rental shoes
- Outdoors scene, cannot stress this enough.
- An outdoor gear rental shed that uses dining dollars
- Of course, the block plan. I know it sounds scary but it actually works really well and allows for fun trips every month or so!
Cons:
- Not great diversity on campus
- Dining dollar system is odd and can leave students hanging in the last week
- Classes with labs can take up next to 6 hours in class
- Dorms aren’t the best quality
- some online systems like housing can be a mess
hope this helps!

Although my experience at CC was not traditional due to the pandemic starting my sophomore year, I loved attending Colorado College. The block plan isn't for everyone, but it worked really well for me! Just make sure that it is a type of learning that you feel will be a good fit for you. Their financial aid was also pretty incredible; at least when I was there they had a policy where they meet all need so you are only expected to pay what is considered reasonable for your family based on the FAFSA. I will say that I didn't realize at first just how few students were on significant financial aid like I was, and how the majority of students were quite wealthy. There definitely is room for improvement in terms of diversity.

I loved the block plan, where you take one class at a time for a month. The immersive learning style worked great for me but seemed more difficult in STEM-heavy classes. There is lots of great nature nearby.

My time at Colorado College has truly changed how I learn and experience the world through the Block Plan. I absolutely love how the Block Plan facilitates hands on learning and intense focus, and feel like it is preparing me well for post grad opportunities. CC draws thoughtful and intentional students, and I've found that everyone here is eager to grow as a student. I personally love the small school party environment and love running into random people on any given night out. Students are so welcoming and I have just loved my time here, especially our fun traditions like Crooz, Fun Run, Block Breaks, and mixers.
Colorado College has been an amazing college experience for me so far!! I absolutely love all the people here and I've found it really nice to be in community with such bright minds.

Colorado College truly has it all- amazing professors, amazing community, amazing opportunities! Colorado College is an incredible institution that truly cares for its students for their time here and after graduation!
I would love to see the dining halls improve, and I wish they had more apartment options for sophomores/upperclassmen.

Overall, I am happy with my school. Living situation wise it could be improved but the academics are strong, Housing and food are terrible. But the professors are supportive, knowledge, and passionate.

It was a unique academic opportunity operating on the block plan. You learn how to pick up a topic fast and manage your time well. But it’s hard to retain and build on information.
Also, very catered towards a rich white outdoors college experience.
Colorado College has it's pros and cons. The deciding factor, however, is most often if the the Block Plan works for you. I would say it's 50/50 for most students.
Colorado College is a great place for people who are outdoorsy and like to ski, climb, or do any other athletic outdoor activities.

Our visiting professors for the Business and Economics department are impeccable. It is also quite fun to learn with the Block Plan system at our school. I'd like to continue seeing top-notch visiting professors, and to see more scholarship opportunities for business ventures.

I've really enjoyed everything about CC. The class schedule is manageable and the academic support is overwhelming. The food choices need to be better to support academic performance and quality of life.

Although this college promotes diversity, if you don't fit the “CC stereotype” it is very easy to get alienated. It is very similar to that of a small town, and some professors do not take their jobs seriously.

ai_summary: Connecticut College offers a picturesque campus with a strong emphasis on academics, where many students appreciate the supportive faculty and small class sizes that foster close relationships with professors. However, critiques frequently mention significant challenges , particularly around housing and food quality, with some students expressing dissatisfaction with the social scene, which tends to feel small or repetitive. While there is a sense of community , with students often noting kindness and inclusivity, there is a noticeable divide between athletes and non-athletes. The college's focus on a liberal arts education allows for exploration of diverse interests, but it has faced criticism for a lack of diversity among its student body. Opportunities for involvement and support systems, such as career services, are well-regarded, making it a unique environment for students who thrive in a tighter-knit community.

reviews.text: Cutting straight to the chase, Conn is a great school and you’re mostly gonna get what you expect. Academics and professors are the highlight, as I found CONNections made my curriculum and schedule 100x better. Beautiful campus, if you’re an outdoorsy person, Conn is for you. Campus life is generally pretty solid, and it’s a small school party scene so if you’re fine with houseparties, small local bars, and the on campus bar, it’s not bad. Plenty of people who also don’t party as well if it’s not your thing, it’s normal. Sports are alright, soccer gets fun because both teams are good every year (men’s team especially watching the ncaa tourney with the every year is fun). Food is not good, but they have tried to invest in it more recently. Housing is solid in my experience, and I didn’t get to live in new freshman dorms (The Plex). Nothing off campus but the on campus upperclassman housing is honestly solid. You’ll get out what you put in here, and I’m more than happy I went here.

Excellent small school experience enabled personal contact with professors and staff. Great internship, junior year abroad and cross-major opportunities were all great preparation for post graduation life.
Connecticut College is an excellent institution with stellar academics. Their liberal arts approach gave me the opportunity to explore wide-ranging fields, cumulating into a well-rounded education.
Honestly loved Conn. Super amazing place with incredibly strong academics, career advising, and campus vibe. So grateful for my 4 years here.
I enjoy the welcoming community here at Conn. Everyone is so excited to meet each other and join new things

A beautiful campus, amazing teachers, committed coaches, interesting and friendly students have made my freshman year and Connecticut College a great experience. There is plenty of academic freedom to explore your interests and plenty of academic support to help you along the way. There is a lot of structured support around academic and career advising and many opportunities to study abroad. The very engaged and engaging student body makes you feel welcomed right away.
Small college with a ho.e town feel. All of the professors really get to know you. You are not just another number.

Awesome campus and super supportive faculty! The place literally feels like heaven—it's an arboretum by a river. You've got everything you need: green spaces, a nice library, a good dining hall, and friendly faculty and staff. All you have to do is study and enjoy life.
Beautiful campus, great school community, and many on campus activities. Wonderful location with easy access to Boston and NYC by train. Get involved and you will have a wonderful experience.
I loved my time at Conn. The classes and professors were amazing, the facilities were great, and the food was always amazing.
Great academics and wonderful professors. They really care about students and how they do in class, but not a very lively campus.

I've been out a few decades but stay engaged through reunions and both of my kids have considered Conn (one may go!). I have absolutely wonderful memories of my time at Conn and I continue to be very impressed by other alumni - both from my class and alumni I meet randomly. In general, Conn is a place that values community rather than cut-throat competition among students. As a result, it attracts well-rounded, intellectually curious, globally responsible students who know they will have great opportunities to explore their interests at Conn. The faculty have always been top-notch, the campus is beautiful and right-sized and the athletics are excellent as Conn competes in the NEPSAC - the premier academic and D3 athletic conference. As far as student outcomes, you will find many critically-acclaimed authors, artists, journalists and political pundits, top lawyers, doctors, and business people. Also, many alumni who forged their own paths as entrepreneurs.

Thus far , my experiences at Connecticut College have been very positive. In particular, the relationships that I have made have been great. This includes professors as well. The small class room environment at Connecticut College has allowed me to develop long lasting relationships with my professors and has helped my grow as a student. Lastly, I am looking forward to the new Cro being completed as well as the new plans for Harris, which will be renovated during the summer of 2025.

Connecticut College is a great school if you are looking for community. We are a laied back group of camels. starting my second semester as a sophomore a history professor i have said we are a quiet group. I love the elaborate variety of classes the beautiful campus, the small size and the people. If you put yourself out there you can find your community. Though the party scene sucks (Campo) we work around it. Conn is a great place to countinue your education, there faculty work hard to get there students to a well place job. I could say more but i dont know what people want to hear.

Conn is a great school! Although it may be lacking in things like frats or D1 athletics, it makes up for it with its incredible professors, beautiful campus, and amazing student services. If you’re looking for a school that will accommodate your needs, Conn is the school for you. They have self-scheduled final exams, academic supports, Deans who genuinely care, and so much more.

ai_summary: Cornell College offers a unique academic experience through its One Course At A Time block plan, which allows students to focus intensively on one subject for 18 days before moving on to the next. This approach is praised for facilitating deeper learning and better time management, though it can be challenging and is not suited for everyone. The tight-knit community and small class sizes foster close relationships with professors and peers, contributing to a supportive learning environment. While the campus and town of Mount Vernon are often described as quaint and safe , some students express dissatisfaction with campus diversity, dining options, and dorm conditions. Overall, students appreciate the strong academic focus and opportunities for engagement in campus life, despite occasional frustrations with facilities and social options.

reviews.text: I'd love to see more diversity. Although, the professors are great, the food is amazing, and the block plan is good.

Cornell College in Mount Vernon, Iowa, is known for its unique One Course At A Time (OCAAT) curriculum, which allows students to focus on a single class for three and a half weeks, This approach lets students focus on a single class for three and a half weeks.

At Cornell College, the class size is no more than 25 people, allowing me and anyone in the class, to easily connect to the profesers. Along with that, with the block plan, I am able to focus on one class rather than multiple, allowing me to get into the head space and learn one subject at a time.

I really appreciate the community at Cornell College. The people there, the students, professors and all of the staff make it a comfortable environment. In all of the classes I've had, the professors have been able to make time to help me with whatever I needed. I honestly think it's because the school is so small and professors can actually take time to help students with certain information. It's especially important because we're on the block plan which means we have a lot of material to cover in only 3 and a half weeks and then we move on to the next class. I really love this college and all of the things I've been able to experience here and I'm so excited to go back in the fall.

The block plan really is amazing. It’s awesome to be able to hone in on certain topics while not worrying about wires getting crossed with other parts of your workload. Additionally, since Cornell is so small, it’s nice to have a community of people you recognize everywhere you look. That said, there are definitely issues with rumor and gossip, because everyone knows everyone. Think small-town Southern porch ladies, but on a college campus. There are also a host of issues with residence halls not having proper heating/cooling, finding mold in buildings, and/or accessibility concerns, but due to Cornell being four-year residential, you just have to suck it up because you are required to live here. Residence life and maintenance do their best, but it doesn’t always work out. I will say that most scenarios are generally receptive to student feedback, and allow students to campaign for their own wants and ideals. Overall, I’ve loved it here, and only have a few complaints.

I have had a great experience at Cornell College so far. Staff and professors are kind, welcoming, and put you first. The areas that I think need the most improvement are the dorms and the food at the cafeteria. Although there are some good options, there is often not enough to accommodate dairy- and gluten-free people or for people to be happy eating there every day. The hours are not very suitable for athletes schedules. I think if they put a little more work into it, taking students reviews into consideration, there would be more enjoyment. The dorms are small and there is only AC in a select few. However, they are all close to classes, athletic facilities, and the cafeteria. The community and dedication to students are why so many people enjoy their experience at Cornell College.

Cornell is an amazing little gem in an equally amazing little town, Mount Vernon. If you're looking for the rigor, small class sizes, and breadth of education of a liberal-arts college, I don't know of where you could do better than Cornell. Since its founding, Cornell has been a leader in social justice and women's rights. And it still adheres strongly to that tradition.

Although it's been decades since I attended Cornell College, I maintain close ties to the tight-knit community the College promotes through small class-size and campus living. I frequently return to events there and have life-long friendships developed through my time on campus. One-course-at-time is a unique and intense way to take classes, you are supported by professors and peers.

Cornell College was a great college to attend in regards to having a good education. There were professors who were extremely supportive and professors who did not support you enough. My main issue was that professors who did not support you enough (most of the less supportive ones have left thankfully). I included this part because I think it's important to have professors who are supportive of their students all throughout college and will not prevent them from moving forward. That said, I also want to mention that my experience as a black person was extremely negative. I have never felt so isolated and miserable due to hearing racist microagressive comments before I came to Cornell College (these were from ADULT students). The lack of diversity is definitely something that I felt held me back. If you decide to go here, keep in mind that it is in the middle of nowhere in Iowa and you may face microagressions or racism as a POC due to the lack of diversity.

I have loved my time at Cornell College and am excited to be in my sophomore year. I have found my soccer family, enjoy my classes and have a good balance with being a student athlete. The community is small, friendly and bigger cities are within just a few miles.
Ive only been at Cornell college for a week but its so amazing and welcoming and I really love the 18 day course dynamic.

My Cornell college experience was great. With playing sports, being involved with clubs around campus and helping out my community as a volunteer firefighter, I had so many things to keep myself busy and to meet many great people. I have already made life long friends just from being there only a year. Campus life is great with things going on all the time and everyone always hanging hangouts on the weekend. Also, with it being a small town there is always things going on around town.

I have had a pretty mixed experience with Cornell College. I think the biggest strength of the college is the professors/academics. I have been able to form a close relationship with my professors that has led to opportunities that I might not have had a larger university. The professor:student ratio is very good. The block plan works very well for me, although it isn't for everyone. In general, though, if you show up to class you will pass. The party scene isn't great unless you're involved in Greek life or sports. Some sports are good (women's volleyball <3), but there isn't really a game day culture. Underclassmen housing SUCKS. The only good dorm is Russell (now that Smith is gone), which is only open to some seniors. Mt. Vernon also is in the middle of nowhere. Get ready for 30+ min drives to get anywhere with anything to do/buy. At the end of the day though, I really enjoy the size of the school. The tight-knit community has been great for me.
I like Cornell, it's a good place to be. I have met a lot of long time friends and made a path of my own at Cornell

It was as incredible I couldn't have picked a better place to spend the next four years of my life. I would change the hoisting most likely because of the extreme heatwaves it doesn't feel smart to only have AC in some dorms, that leads to heat stroke.

ai_summary: Davidson College reviews highlight a unique blend of high academic rigor and a supportive community , making it stand out among liberal arts colleges. Students frequently praise the quality and accessibility of professors, noting their willingness to help and engage with students outside of class, fostering tight-knit relationships due to small class sizes. While the academic pressure can be intense and is sometimes described as cutthroat, most feel that the collaborative spirit among peers creates a positive environment. Student life, though solid, is somewhat limited compared to larger universities, with a friendly atmosphere that emphasizes both serious study and a "work hard/play hard" mentality, especially in athletics. There are concerns regarding diversity and inclusion on campus, along with critiques about the high tuition costs and occasionally inadequate mental health resources. Overall, Davidson is characterized as a place where students can thrive academically while forming lasting friendships in a charming setting.

reviews.text: I really enjoy being at Davidson. The food is great and there are a lot of options. The professors are super receptive and it is overall a great experience.
Davidson College offers a challenging yet supportive academic environment where all students are expected to put in effort and strive for success.

As a freshman coming into Davidson, the transition was a little rough having to balance my own schedule along with sporty and social life. However students figure their priorities and plan accordingly. Davidson has many outlets and resources for all students to utilize to make college easier.
I had a great experience overall. Everywhere has its challenges. Davidson help shape who I am and gave me some amazing experiences.
The food is the best. I love the Mac N Cheese and the fried chicken. The salad bar and desserts always have awesome selections!
Ive worked in higher ed for 10 years and no one supports the staff, students, and surrounding community like Davidson!

Davidson has such a welcoming and engaged community. The professors are super approachable and genuinely want to see you succeed. There are also tons of opportunities for research, grants, and projects even as an undergrad, which makes it easy to dive into things you’re passionate about.

The food is great in Vail Commons dining hall. Their menus change every week and there are many substitutions for dietary restrictions. Their dining hall is AllerCheck approved which means they take good care of my health and well being while providing me with delicious meals that are specific to my diet.
Their catering is phenomenal too! I love their Tex Mex Fiesta!
Great campus, great athletics, great students, great faculty, great staff, great service, great food. Davidson College is a Great Community!
Everyone is very friendly and always willing to help. They go out of their way to make sure you understand if you have questions. The food is great!
Most of all I enjoy the culture and the way Davidson College leadership supports diversity. It's apparent in the food served in the dining hall and the different events across campus.
Great food quality and options at all of the campus dining locations. The staff is always friendly and accommodating.
Great college experience. Wonderful community. Great food and lots of variety. We love Commons and Cafe.

A wonderful place for students who are scholars, athletes and ladies/gentlemen.
NOT ANIMAL HOUSE, but a great place to prepare for a top grad school and rewarding profession.
Love it! Great professors and small community provided connection I was looking for. Certainly recommend.

ai_summary: DePauw University offers a rewarding and engaging academic experience within a small, close-knit community. Many students appreciate the individualized attention from professors and the challenging curriculum , which is designed to prepare them for graduate studies and professional opportunities. While campus life is vibrant with numerous student organizations and events, some feel a predominant focus on sports and Greek life can overshadow other social options. The campus itself is characterized by its beautiful environment and safety, but students have noted that local amenities are limited, requiring careful planning for those without cars. Additionally, experiences with administration and aspects of student services receive mixed feedback , with some calling for improved communication and support. Overall, DePauw is seen as a place where serious academic pursuit coexists with a strong community atmosphere, though challenges related to diversity and transparency have surfaced in discussions.

reviews.text: I loved my experience at DePauw. I graduated in 2001, and I am sure a lot has changed . . . but to start with the academics were solid, the class size is small, and you really get to know your professors. The facilities are all beautiful and well-maintained. The campus is so safe and it's easy to get around. The greek system was FOUNDED at DPU, which is just incredible historically. The greek system created a built-in social system for over 80% of the student body. There was always something to do on the weekends, and friends were easy to make in your sorority or fraternity. The students take academics seriously and no one really parties much during the week, so you are also able to stay focused and work diligently. There is peer pressure to spend your evenings in the library--in a good way. I met the greatest people at DePauw, learned a lot, and had a ton of fun along the way. Also, the January winter term is unique and a great opportunity to snag an amazing internship!

While most buildings and spaces are recently renovated and up to date with services, there are some spaces that need more renovations. Some buildings have elevators that commonly are being serviced, and air-conditioners in dorms commonly break. Maintenance is quick to start working, but the inconvenience is still there.

Love everything about this place. It is not perfect, but it is perfect for me! Finding my fit was important to me, and I feel like I have found my community. I have great friends, interesting and engaged faculty, and I live in a beautiful sorority house. This is everything I was looking for and more. DePauw offered me competitive scholarships, and I pay the same as my brother does to attend Purdue. I feel like I am getting set up for the rest of my life!

In my opinion, DePauw offers a strong liberal arts education with small class sizes that make it easy to build close relationships with professors. Many faculty members are genuinely invested in students’ growth and are approachable both inside and outside the classroom. The campus is beautiful and tight-knit, which helps create a sense of community, especially for students who actively engage in clubs or academic programs.
That said, I think some areas could be improved. Career support is solid for certain fields, but I think students pursuing more specialized or technical paths often need to be proactive in seeking external opportunities. The local area doesn't have much going on. Professors here sometimes are "hit or miss".

Depauw is truly an amazing experience I do not regret my decision on going to Depauw. The environment, the students, the academics everything was exactly the way I imagined college to be. I learned so much at my time at Depauw and truly felt like I deserved a college degree.

My experience at DePauw has been highly positive and intellectually rewarding. The academic environment challenged me to think critically, manage my time effectively, and engage deeply with my coursework. Faculty members were approachable, supportive, and genuinely invested in student success, which made learning both rigorous and engaging. Beyond academics, the campus culture encouraged collaboration, leadership, and personal growth through student organizations and extracurricular activities. I was able to build meaningful relationships with peers from diverse backgrounds, enhancing my perspective and communication skills. Overall, DePauw provided a strong foundation for my academic development and future professional aspirations.

What I like the most is the close-knit community since we're a relatively small school. The professors are responsive (if you reach out to them too). However, the location is so boring and doesn't have much thing to do if you don't have a car, which could be depressing sometimes. I hope we could have more job/internship resource for international students too
DePauw University fosters the growth of diverse perspectives, while also protecting marginalized voices on campus.

Overall it is a good, quiet school. It is very small and often time you know most people. Which is both a good thing and a bad thing. I found my boyfriend at this school and I really love him and this school. The teachers are mostly kind and often know you by name.

I transferred to DePauw from a big public and it was kind of a mess there. They kept dropping programs \, including the one I originally went for, and I always felt lost in the crowd. It seemed like everyone just did their own thing, and it was hard to find my place or connect with professors who actually remembered me. When I got to DePauw, everything changed. The classes are way smaller, so I got to know my professors and classmates almost right away. It felt so good to be in classes that matched my interests without all the hassle. Academically, I found I could ask questions, which was huge for me. Socially, I made friends really fast—like, by the end of orientation I already had a group to hang out with. Everyone here seems to want you to get involved, and it’s completely different from how I felt at IU, where everything felt too big. DePauw just fits better for me. I have space to study what I care about and people to do it with. I wish I had started here from the beginning.

DePauw is the kind of place where you’re actually known by your professors, not just a face in a huge lecture hall. Most of my classes have fewer than 25 people. Classes push you to think for yourself, and you get a ton of support when you need it. There’s always something happening around campus, from concerts and guest speakers to club events.
In my first semester, I have job shadowing opportunities, and the alumni network is insanely helpful for finding connections in my field (communication). The campus is beautiful, especially in the fall, and there’s a real sense of community that you don’t get at bigger schools. Tailgating at the football stadium and then cheering on the Tigers feels like you are part of the team. People look out for each other and want you to succeed. It is pretty ideal!

Good school, Not as prestigious as it makes itself seem, but will randomly have some really cool visitors or events. From talking to alumni its definitely not what it used to be and riding off of old reputation. With how little students the school has, athletes make up a large percentage. They can be very cliquey and don't participate on campus as much as other students, plus a waste of money as the only sport that ppl watch is football, maybe people would watch the basketball team if they were good. Professors are pretty good for the most part. With the way the class requirements are, Its hard to really go in depth into a subject, even a major with how many intro classes are required. But campus is beautiful, there are a lot of clubs and organizations to be apart of. Dining hall is good. Dorms are alright. Financial offers are very generous so don't be scared by what the cost is listed as online.

Looking back on my time at DePauw, I realize now just how deeply this university shaped who I am—far beyond what I could see in the moment. DePauw didn’t just prepare me academically; it fundamentally nurtured my growth as an artist and a creative thinker. The campus vibrates with possibility, offering the mentorship, encouragement, and thoughtful challenge that are essential for anyone striving to find their authentic voice.
What sets DePauw apart is how it prepares you to engage with a complex world—not only as a skilled professional but as someone who understands their own voice and the value it brings. I left empowered, inspired, and fully equipped to enter the world with confidence, knowing I could make a difference. If you’re seeking a place to truly grow as a creative and a leader, I can’t recommend DePauw highly enough.

The things that I like about Depauw University is that it is a small school and have a wide range of classes that you could take. The things that I would like to see change is make the meal plan optional instead of requiring you to have a meal plan.

I am entering into my third year at DePauw University this coming semester. I have had a pretty good experience with DePauw so far. The academic buildings are beautiful and there are many wonderful places on campus to study. The food can be off and on especially if you have food restrictions. The housing is relatively good but can be small. The professors are very great most of the time. There are many opportunities for one on one work with professors if you are struggling.

ai_summary: Denison University offers a close-knit and welcoming community that values both academics and personal connections, which is a major draw for many students. The professors are mostly accessible and supportive, enhancing the intimate classroom experience that small class sizes provide. However, students have mixed feelings about the social scene; while there are many opportunities for engagement on campus through clubs and activities, some feel there is a lack of diverse social options beyond party culture. The town of Granville is described as charming, but its small size can limit off-campus exploration, especially for those without a car. Financial concerns are prevalent among students, with many feeling that the tuition does not fully align with the available amenities and support structures. Overall, while Denison has strengths in academics and community, there are notable areas for improvement, particularly in student life and administrative responsiveness.

reviews.text: At Denison you can really make it your own adventure. While I was an athlete I wasn't limited to just athletics, I was able to participate in research in the summer, fun art classes, explore other extra curriculars. Your freshmen year the dorms and food can be a little less desirable but after that you find your grove learn to enjoy the little things. The town of Granville is amazing and is worth visiting. Sports are a big part of campus but is not the whole world of the school. You can interact as much or as little with things as you please. Really made some of the best friends of my life here and enjoyed almost every second of it (even the crappy days are important).

Saw the overall niche grade — Denison has changed a lot since those ratings. The school is always evolving and improving across the board. I agree with most of the grades, but I’d adjust a few based on my experience:
• Campus: A
• Professors: A
• Location: A-
• Safety: A+
• Campus Food: A-
• Dorms: B+
• Student Life: A
Overall, Denison today feels stronger than what those older ratings reflect.

My overall experience at Denison University has been very positive. The professors are passionate and genuinely invested in their students’ growth — especially in the Financial Economics program, where I’ve been able to connect theory to real-world markets. The small class sizes make it easy to build close relationships with both professors and classmates, and the campus community feels supportive and collaborative.
Denison also provides great opportunities for involvement outside the classroom — from academic clubs like the Investment Club. I’ve appreciated how the school encourages a balance between academics and leadership.
If there’s one area I’d like to see improve, it would be expanding career networking opportunities and mentorship programs in finance. More connections with alumni working in the financial industry would make Denison even stronger.
Overall, Denison has challenged me academically, supported me personally, and helped me grow as a well-rounded student.
Way too small, nothing to do. Housing and food stink. Demanding academics but nothing to show for it as overhyped career center fails to land internships or jobs for students.

We're fortunate to experience all climates at DenisonU and it's great, we have a sprawling 1000+ acres of Campos with a 360 acre open air Biodiversity lab.
100% residential campus, and many more.

There are no words to describe my time at Denison. I think the opportunity to go to the school was really great, I met a ton of amazing people, and also had the opportunity to follow my dreams in grad school. The campus is beautiful, with also Granville being such a nice town. The professors are also very great, I did not have a professor who didn’t help me in some type of way. Everyone wants to be there, and give opportunities to grow. In my 4 years, I was also in athletics, which Denison really appreciates. The president is great, and shows that he genuinely cares about each student. Denison always has construction going on, as they are always renovating their campus. If I could do it again, I would.

Denison is an amazing school to focus on academics, but believe I personally would have benefitted from a bigger college. It is very small, so you know majority of student on campus, but you do get very close relationships with professors, and denison has an amazing career-ready center for all students

For some reason, I expected classes to be easy, like how high school was for me, but that is definitely not the case. It is very challenging, but it is good. However, depending on what classes and what major you are it can be pretty hard to balance a social life.

Do not be misled by what they say about meeting students’ financial needs fully—this is false advertising and simply not true. They interpret financial need in their own way, which has nothing to do with what is calculated by FAFSA and CSS. While nine other schools recognized my need at a certain amount, this school made its own determination and charged more than double what the others did. What a shame on the institution that attracts young innocent students by such low trick and wasting their time and energy. When I requested a meeting to appeal, the officer didn’t even show up for the scheduled Zoom call—I waited in front of my laptop for an hour. This clearly reflects the institution’s lack of professionalism and moral responsibility.

Denison was a wonderful experience! The professors and the academics were exceptional. The liberal arts education allowed for students to explore new interests in non-major fields. It was a big fish small pond experience and I highly recommend Denison!

As someone who grew up in a major city and attended a public high school in which white students were not the majority of the student population, this school is not diverse. A vast majority of the students who attend Denison come from rural suburbs and attended high schools with almost exclusively white children. I believe the diversity "A" rating to be misleading, and while the prescence of more than one student of color may seem diverse to the typical Denison crowd, this school is not diverse. The pictures on the website are wholly dicieving, and I would estimate at least 80% of the student body to be white. This environment may seem appealing to some, however I felt uneasy and uncomfortable by the imbalance for much of my first semester. I would also note that this campus is much more conservative than many people realize. I am not saying this is a problem however I feel that these are notable factors that prospective students should be aware of.

Academic advising had its limitations, particularly with my major. Some graduate prep and career resources were less accessible than expected, and alumni connections lacked depth.
Beyond academics, engagement with professors outside the classroom was limited, affecting my learning experience. Navigating administrative processes, from transfer credits to advising changes, required persistence and problem-solving.
To fill these gaps, I sought additional learning experiences elsewhere, gaining practical skills and exposure in different environments. These opportunities broadened my perspective and reinforced concerns about Denison’s support systems.
Denison has the potential to improve student experiences by strengthening academic advising, career preparation, and alumni engagement. More structured mentorship and graduate prep would be valuable. While my experience was challenging, I hope future students can make the most of what Denison offers.

Overall, I am enjoying my time at Denison. Granville is a cute little town but there isn't a ton to do within Granville. Luckily they have some really good restaurants. I also absolutely love my professors and my classes which is the reason I am here.

Current sophomore here, and I regret choosing Denison. I came for small classes, a close-knit community, and rigorous academics. While my professors are passionate, Denison fails everywhere else.
First, housing. You’d think that since students must live on campus, Denison would ensure decent accommodations. Wrong. Many dorms lack AC, making summer months unbearable. Now, the best dorms are reserved for freshmen, forcing upperclassmen back into first-year housing. This desperate move is meant to stop the rising transfer rate.
Then there's the food which has somehow worsened. Undercooked chicken, mangled fish—food poisoning is a daily gamble. Meal swipes at on-campus restaurants are limited, so we’re stuck with it. Yet, when tours come through, they roll out a fake gourmet spread.
Denison boasts a billion-dollar endowment. You’d think they’d use it to improve student life. Wrong again. Instead, they raised tuition by $5,000 while ignoring basic needs.
Denison is a scam.

Beautiful campus, excellent professors, and small class sizes are all stellar features offered by Denison. But the list ends there. Despite the billion-dollar endowment that’s constantly shoved down our throats, housing is dreadful and getting worse. The dining hall serves failed science experiments disguised as meals. Textbooks cost a fortune each semester—including a $400 language book—yet somehow the criminally high tuition doesn’t cover them. It also doesn’t cover basic supplies for art classes, not even the paint and paintbrushes.
The heavily hyped career center also fails to deliver. The so-called experts give poor advice, and many have no knowledge of the fields they claim to support.
I know several students transferring to OSU to actually get what they’re paying for, and honestly, I’m considering it too. Sure, I’d lose small class sizes, but passionate professors exist everywhere—and at least I’d have decent living conditions and food. Prestige isn't everything. Believe me.

ai_summary: Dickinson College offers a small liberal arts education characterized by dedicated professors and close student-faculty relationships, which many students appreciate. The coursework is challenging and engaging, with a strong emphasis on study abroad opportunities, which about two-thirds of students take advantage of. While the campus and surrounding Carlisle offer a quaint , charming atmosphere , some students find the social scene limited and cliquey, reminiscent of high school dynamics. The dining experience often receives criticism for its quality and variety, and the administration's handling of student needs, including tuition costs and living conditions, raises concerns. Overall, while some thrive in its supportive community , others question whether Dickinson fully meets their expectations, especially regarding diversity and campus life.

reviews.text:
I would not recommend Dickinson in the slightest. You're forced to live on campus all 4 years, and the chance you get a single, even as a senior, is basically 0. All of my friends in singles have"accommodations"-otherwise there's no chance. The cafeteria is awful, and meal plans are mandatory; even if you're in a dorm/apt with a kitchen, you must pay for a meal plan. Nobody cooks or buys groceries. I've had issues in all of my dorms (bugs, heating/AC, tiny, dirty bathrooms) and reslife is horrific. They don't care about students. If you go to them with an issue, they blame you.
Carlisle is so boring. There's no public transportation, so you're stuck on campus or walk to the Walmart (not Target). The surrounding area is super small and surrounded by highways, so you can't even walk/bike that far off campus. It's claustrophobic. Students rarely go downtown bc they're forced to pay an exorbitant meal plan and they don't want to spend money when they have food swipes.
Do NOT go.

Dickinson is a tight knit community with lots of different groups of people where you can find your niche and a group of people that are similar to you but can also push you to learn and understand more about different perspectives.

As a black girl at Dickinson, the lack of diversity can be isolating. However, many people, including faculty, are welcoming and friendly, and though it takes time to find a group, it can happen. Campus holds nice events, the food is well-prepared, housing is not bad, and professors are knowledgable. I received amazing financial aid.

Have to say, transferring out of here was the best decision i’ve ever made. Unless you are here on full scholarship, it’s not worth it. Only pros I can think of are the small class sizes and professors. Outside of the academics, this place sucks. Nothing to do in Carlisle or the surrounding area. Crazy price tag, considering nobody outside of PA even knows what the school is. I have to think the retention rate is skewed, as a ton of people I know have transferred out. You can easily tell who is here because they are smart and driven, and who bought their way here. You will see a ton of the Goyard bag, stuck up north east people, etc. The only reason the outcomes out of the school are good is because everyone’s dad gets them internships and jobs. Dickinson does not have any type of name brand. So if you want a very depressing and dead campus with a $90k+ price tag, this is the perfect place. Next to nobody is happy here.
Overall I would say I had a good experience. Professors were great and very understanding and care about each individual student. Not much to do outside of school events
Dickinson's community is fantastic. Students on campus care about each other and are proud in what they are involved in. It has been great being a student here.

Overall, Dickinson College offered me a multifaceted experience that has served as my foundation academically, professionally, and socially since my graduation in 2023. During my time at Dickinson, I was able to explore a plethora of academic routes and curricula, especially in the humanities department. The Africana Studies department offered courses that shaped my perspective of my own identity as a Black woman who comes from a lineage of Black American people. The English department also gave me a better understanding of my analytical strengths and weak points, allowing me to explore an array of interests that I would carry with me into my career in PR/media/marketing.
Dickinson also taught me that although I am a fellow Dickinsonian who shares similar experiences with other alumni, I am the creator of my future who must carve my own path. I met many people with different interests, and they taught me that I am a self-starter with grit, creativity and persistence.

My experience at Dickinson College has been very great. I have been able to gather a lot of experience that has helped me develop many of the skills I have today. I think there is a great community here that is welcoming and inviting. I do think there needs to be changed mainly regarding meal plans and housing options.

I think unless you are on a complete scholarship to Dickinson it is not worth it in the slightest. The academics are mid at best and the price just continues to increase without any increase in scholarship or aid either. Dickinson is only accessible to incredibly high economic backgrounds or low. To anyone in middle class, you will just collect endless debt with little to gain. Professors at the school take a lot of focus in their own research and projects to the point it feels like teaching is a secondary activity for a lot of them. The retention rate is bottom of the barrel with many professors flowing in and out so students have a hard time building relationships with them. The food is exceptionally meh and very few options for anyone with dietary restrictions. The area around the school is beautiful but Dickinson itself has old dingy dorms without any exceptions to live off campus. Overall I think the price is well over the actual worth of the school.

I had an incredible experience at Dickinson. It introduced me to some truly wonderful people who have become my closest friends. While there are certainly areas for improvement such as the food, housing, and some aspects of administration, the students themselves are what truly make Dickinson special.

Overall have had a very holistic experience at Dickinson. I went from wanting to transfer my freshman year to loving my school my senior year. This wasn’t just from finding my path with friends and extracurriculars, but the institutions efforts. Food is SO much better, and the burgess institute genuinely has changed campus. Nothing but good things to say!

I enjoy the small school/small town atmosphere. Overall my professors are good, although I feel a few have been out of touch with today's educational models. I play a varsity sport and love the team, my teammates and the challenge of collegiate athletics.

I couldn't be happier at Dickinson! It's the perfect fir for me, as someone with many interests. Going into college I was worried about balancing my academics with my family and social life as well as campus life. The professors are very accommodating and understanding! I was able to take classes that I wasn't necessarily good at, but interested in and not only learned a lot ( which more important), but surprised myself by passing. Professors offer many opportunities for extra help and extra credit outside the classroom, which is cool, because a lot of professors give extra credit if you attend an event on campus. The community here at Dickinson and in the town of Carlisle is the best! The only downside of Dickinson is the requirement to live on campus all four years and the meal plan options, but I most of my friends have similar problems at other schools.
