Dickinson is a unique community that greats a postive environment to grow as a student and a person.

The liberal arts education I received at Dickinson ABSOLUTELY set me up for success. I made great friends, had opportunities to serve in my community, and had the chance to grow my curiosity about a number of subjects which continue to inform my life today. Housing was adequate- it’s college - and the campus is beautiful.

I love my experience at Dickinson College, I just wish there were more scholarships to help me afford it. I would like to see improvement in the non-freshman living spaces, rooms, and bathrooms but overall I have had a great experience. I love my school and don't want to transfer but if prices rise again I may have to.

ai_summary: Drew University is characterized by its small , close-knit community and beautiful campus , often referred to as "the University in the Forest." Many students appreciate the faculty, noting that professors are generally caring and accessible, fostering strong relationships that enhance the academic experience. However, there are mixed feelings about student life; while some enjoy the intimate atmosphere, others find it cliquish and lacking in engagement , with limited social activities and nightlife. The quality of food and housing options also appears to be a recurring issue, with many wishing for improvements. Overall, students value the academic opportunities and strong connections made at Drew, but express concerns regarding the social environment and high tuition costs .

reviews.text: It has been a wonderful experience so far, lot's of amazing classes with great professors, lot's of college life and actives, very social and a great location, very local to the community, stores and restaurants. It's very clean, offers lots of diverse foods and actives. It has offered some amazing programs and is given lots of important opportunities.
I liked how Drew university had such a close knit community. It makes being there comfortable and feel homey. I would like to see more student activities on the weekends.

No other university’s biology department compares to Drew’s. They offer opportunities such as research, being published and much more. I the labs are immersive and go at a consistently good pace. The professors truly care about the students.

My experience at my school has been short of nothing but greatness. The community at Drew University is always so welcoming from the beginning with orientation all the way to meeting your professors everyone is always so warm and welcoming. Professors not only there to teach you but also get to know you as a student and push your growth. I have grown so much not just as a person but also within leadership spaces. I have been mentored by the best alumni network any student can ask for. Professors have presented me with amazing opportunities. The only thing I would change is having more and more student body participating in school events. More of an integration between student athletes and other student body. Student athletes tend to isolate themselves.

Drew University is overall a very welcoming campus. It is a very peaceful area surrounded by nature and a very cute down town area. There is barely any night life and the food is absolutely terrible. The professors are all very passionate and willing to help you. Small class sizes and the guidelines for the courses themselves are fair and clear. I transferred so I got the gen ed requirements waived, I would recommend doing that as those requirements are somewhat ridiculous.

So far, Drew University is a wonderful school. I just transferred after completing my associates at my local community college, and everyone was so helpful during the entire process, and I was able to get things sorted quickly. The atmosphere of the school is very calm and peaceful, and its location in the woods is something that I really enjoy. All of the professors so far are wonderful, and the classes are invigorating. It's a bit of an adjustment from what I'm used to, but so far I'm enjoying it greatly, and can't wait to spend the rest of my time pursuing my degree here.

As a first-year student at Drew University, my experience has been really supportive. My class sizes are small enough that I can interact with each of my professors and genuinely feel how they care about my success. I also appreciate the close community environment that exists on the campus. It makes my adaptation manageable and easier to understand and process now that I'm in a new environment and far away from my loved ones. One of the best examples of the support Drew offers to their students is their tutoring center, which I often use for my biology class. The tutors are students who have previously taken the class but are highly clarified for these positions. These students are the most knowledgeable, patient, and genuinely invested in helping other students to succeed in any material they find challenging. As stated before I use this center a lot and knowing that such resources are available whenever I need them has made a big difference in my academic performance and adjustment.

I love the small school environment and its beautiful campus. The classroom has an average of 15-25 students per professor. Most of my professors are genuinely kind people, they are very understanding and knowledgeable in their area of teaching. They often connect in-class material with real world experiences. As a student I value professors who are capable of not just lecturing but bring relevant information and be there for their students in a way that bigger universities can't.

While attending Drew University I've grown fond of meeting new people, personalities, & professors with distinct teaching styles. Additionally, being a sophomore yet a first year student, I've enjoyed the warm welcome from some of the faculty as well as the aiding upperclassmen when moving in & finding my classes. However I would like to see more help regarding financial aid appeals or overall just more organization in terms of eligibility & programs.

I liked Drew a lot in the sense it was trying to advocate for eco friendliness and lgbtq/diversity environments. However, the dining services have been devolving from the unfortunate hours change, to the food options for allergen free stations and halal stations being minimal. I think Drew tries it's best to have nice events for students, but it still feels isolating. Had I not made such great friends and strong academic relations with the professors of my department, I would've been concerned about my time here.

Drew University is a disgrace. Campus life is a joke. If you're not a freshmen, then they don't care. They let you believe they're gonna give you reasonable financial aid but then you're left in the dark your second year because again, you're no longer a priority. They gotta keep from going broke somehow! The food is disgusting. I had to DoorDash multiple times a week last year because they don't listen. They act like they do by making you do surveys about how to improve dining just to not do a single thing about it. They serve just about the same food at the only 2 dining halls. And if you don't like the trash food at commons, womp womp cause the EC isn't open on weekends and closes early. Emails go unanswered for days so if you have an urgent problem, don't even bother. Tuition is raised without warning. Remodeling the dining hall was done with our money because apparently thats more important than giving us good food instead? Oh but don't worry, you can make smoothies now!

Drew University is a great spot, everyone is super genuine and the food is AMAZING. It is a quieter party scene than a lot of schools but like most places, if you want to party you can find a party. The professors are also super genuine and the campus is beautiful.
Great experience party life so dead . Good did get better and the education is so good professors are nice

I am a Theatre and Spanish major at Drew. I am also a commuter student. Even though I stay long hours on campus, that allows me to really take in what the school offers due to my major and having to stay late most days. Through my theatre major, I have also been fortunate to have internships lined up for my senior year, so it shows how close-knit the Drew community is with each other, from professors to students, and to institutions nearby.

It's pretty great, as a resident. There are always late-night activities that you can go to and hang with your friends. However, as a commuter, they kinda suck, because there so late at night, so commuters tend not to go as much to those events. I've been both a resident and commuter, so I've experienced both sides. It's nice living for the perks, but cost and size wise not worth it, which is why I became a commuter; however it did take away from some of my enjoyment.

Attending Earlham as an undergraduate was life changing. The community of professors, staff, and students, didn't just encourage learning, it allowed for the exploration of ideas and challenged each student to dialogue and expand their world view. I was able to connect creatively and take on leadership skills by participating in the student run dance program, do a work study program in the local community that has influenced my career choices twenty years later, spend a semester in Philadelphia exploring a different region of the country, build a functional storage building on a farm, host a fundraiser for a cause I felt passionate about, discuss policy and the realistic application of said policies, and so so much more. I made lifelong friendships that continue to shape and bring joy to my life every day.
Attending Earlham positioned me to be an advocate for community building, life long learning, and gave me the skills for continued professional and personal growth to carry foward.

I read a bunch of these reviews by alums and while they remind me why I chose to come to Earlham, it’s clear the college is nothing like what it used to be. So many of the good faculty are leaving, tons of majors were just cut, and lots of the other things people talk about here just don’t exist. I was really excited to use my Epic money to do an internship this summer, but they changed the way we have to apply for the money and now a lot of the sophomores can’t get funded. There is very little to do and there are tons of houses that look like students should be able to live in, but we are not allowed. I feel like everything I read about earlham or was told by admissions two years ago is not true, maybe it used to be, but isn’t now. I regret my choice to come here, but worry it’s too late to go anywhere else. If you are thinking about going to Earlham make sure you talk to actual students- the coaches and admissions people make it sound way better than it is.
Terrible location, terrible food, nothing to do. I planned to major in music or accounting and both those majors just got cut.

I loved my first year at Earlham, but this year the president ruined everything. Over Christmas break they cut my 2 favorite professors and a really good math professor so now I can’t graduate. Everyone is really sad and angry and now there’s no music major, no SoAn, no environmental science, no accounting, and a whole bunch of other majors are gone too. All my friends are transferring and those staying can’t even figure out what majors are still being offered. It used to be worth the crappy dorms and bad food, but now it’s a joke.
Admissions will totally lie to you. Do not come here. Earlham sucks

I went to Earlham in the mid-1990s and it fundamentally shaped my direction in life. I had first-class professors who cared about each student and their learning. I participated in an amazing off-campus study abroad program that gave me a firm foundation in the language and prepared me to return to live abroad a few years later. I was able to participate in a wide range of campus activities that developed additional academic, leadership, and social skills. I came away from Earlham with a good grounding in the liberal arts as well as the sciences, as a biology major. Earlham provides a supportive community for high school graduates as they move into their next life chapter and prepares them for moving into the working world or additional schooling. After having recently participated in a class reunion, I also observed a common thread though those in my class that we had all benefited from our college experience at Earlham and that it had uniquely prepared each us for our next steps.

I was excited about possibly enrolling at Earlham based on location and what I had reviewed online. Went ahead and applied, then began attending virtual webinars, ZeeMee chats, and interacting with staff at the school. Things just kept getting weird compared to my other applications, then they just seemed to get creepy. I thought it was a solid, private Christian school. No, not by the standards I am use to. They should change the name to DEI University. The redundant requests for admissions materials, the lack of having their stuff organized became clear. Then are started getting videos of campus events and the profile picture of my admissions counselor. It became immediately clear this was not a place I would be comfortable attending over the next 4 years. I absolutely suggest doing your research deeply and look at current campus events and you'll know what Im talking about. It'll be OK to certain people, but it will absolutely NOT work for many more.

I am incredibly grateful for the experience I received at Earlham. I cannot imagine my college experience going any other way. I was able to compete in the sports I loved while getting an excellent, challenging education. The faculty and professors are the greatest. I have felt so lucky to have had the people who can now say they are close to me and have helped me grow in so many ways over these 4 years.
One thing is that some of the athletic teams aren't as competitive. Yes, the student-athletes work hard, and everyone is grateful to continue competing in their sport. Still, the athletics department falls short in that area, prioritizing successful, winning teams for Earlham. In the future, Earlham will turn this around when opportunities arise and can focus more on that side of things to truly expand the college.
Other than that, I have loved everything about Earlham. The community has made it what it is, and it is a special place.

The professors are the stand-out aspect. My classroom experience feels tailored to me and also communal with my peers. The professors go out of their way to make sure every student has a voice and has what they need to succeed in the classroom. They are all great mentors.
I love Earlham for the community and the academics. Professors here want you to succeed and they are willing to work with you. Everyone here is friendly and it truly feels like a home.
I love earlham I just would like to see our professors stay with us they all have made this place such a wonderful home for me
I had a great experience with this college. Most of the classes are discussion focused and there’s a lot of opportunities to know other people’s perspectives.

My experience at Earlham College has been that of a slow decline. The longer I'm here, the less intelligent I feel, the less I enjoy the people around me, the less I feel that people want to be my friend, and the less confident I feel in this institution. I have begun referring to Earlham as the loony school. Everything that something bad or unfortunate happens, I just say to myself, "I go to the loony school." I half expect to be handed a balloon when I cross the stage at my graduation in 2027.

Experience at Earlham so far has been mostly positive. All the professors I have encountered have been extremely supportive and willing to connect. You can truly see their passion and desire to help you shape and achieve all the goals you can have. The student body is highly diverse, comprising individuals from all over the world. It creates a space for everyone to share their experiences and foster an environment that values diversity. Some of my classes were dominated by American students, and only in these cases did I feel a bit out of my comfort zone because some discussions felt too US-centered. However, there were many other instances where professors attempted to incorporate international examples into their teaching. Housing conditions and food on campus need some improvement, considering that going back home for the weekend or ordering takeout is not always possible.

My experience at Earlham has been amazing. I have gotten to make so many friends and will have memories for a life time. It has been especially elevated by the outdoor education program and the access I have gotten to backcountry leading skills. I started my time at Earlham on the pre-orientation program August Wilderness and that has forever changed my life.

I loved my time at Earlham College. The teachers and students at Earlham were incredibly friendly and easy to interact with. My faculty, especially, encouraged a level of engagement I had never experienced in certain subjects, and they were all so clearly passionate about their subject of focus. My favorite part of Earlham was the college themed-houses, which allowed me to live with other students who shared similar interests to me. Overall, Earlham College is a wonderful place to attend for an undergraduate degree!

ai_summary: Emmanuel College in Massachusetts offers a unique experience as a small liberal arts institution located in the heart of Boston, making it easy to access various cultural and professional opportunities. Students appreciate the close-knit community and strong professor-student relationships , which stem from the small class sizes. However, many reviews indicate concerns about the administration and campus services, particularly regarding housing and student support. While the faculty is frequently lauded for their dedication and approachability, there are notable criticisms about the high tuition costs , lack of diversity , subpar campus life, and the school’s failure to effectively address student concerns such as mental health and safety issues. Many students also comment on the minimal social scene and cliquish environment, which can make it challenging to find a sense of belonging. Overall, Emmanuel's strengths lie in its academic environment and location, but there are significant areas needing improvement.

reviews.text: I enjoyed my time at Emmanuel's beautiful campus. I felt the the professors made their classes engaging and you very much so had to earn your grades.

My experience overall was amazing. The community on campus was super welcoming and I felt at home immediately. Being in the city is fun as well, and exploring Boston never gets old. The only thing I have to complain about is that there is no night life on campus; parties never happen because we have strict rules. But other than that Emmanuel College is great!

I'm a freshman at Emmanuel and I have to say that Emmanuel so far has been amazing. The people I've met here are people I can see myself staying in contact with beyond the four years I attend school here. The professors, really do want to provide you with the best learning experience, it really feels like they care and even more, so it's been really inspiring to losten to them and their stories of how they got here. It's a catholic school, which is perfect is you are or aren't religious as there's a lot of opportunity to explore that and home in on your religion if you want, and if you're not religious-they don't press it on you at all which is really nice. I think what I would like to see change is just more student life-for me it doesn't matter too much because of the amazing friends and city we're in but in general it would be nice to have more student gatherings and activities on campus.

Im in my first year of Emmanuel college, and I am extremely pleased with the school as a whole. The biology department specifically is amazing, the biology club offers a wide rang of events, and very helpful review sessions before tests! The biology department staff so far is very nice, and willing to take time to help their students. I really like Emmanuel, I never expected to feel like a college was my home. One thing i'd like to see change is the food, as it does not have a lot of dairy free options.
The small classes and opportunity to build professor relationships was great for my education, and you can't beat the location!
Emmanuel was a great place to attend college with supportive professors and small classes (on a college scale). I enjoyed my time at Emmanuel with the people I met, culture, and location.

Emmanuel College is a small but authentic institution, I have personally found my experience here quite lovely with only a few moments of unawareness. The people and staff here really give you patience and confidence towards being an adult, giving multiple chances for extra credit and tutors!

THREE-STAR SCHOOL. FIVE-STAR LOCATION (THEY KNOW IT). Beware of unfair attendance policy, undertrained dining hall staff (Cooking spray put directly on top of surrounding food, undercooked items, and uninviting staff), and unrealistic rules (respecting people who are blatant with their disregard for you as someone who deserves and demands respect). Dorm life was terrible. Hair everywhere, snot, bodily fluids, my hall mates seemed to have had no personal hygiene. Doors were slammed constantly, fire alarm drills were failed (leaving the building is hard), and everyone is cliquey. The winter sucks, have fun drinking yourself into more debt (financial, emotional, physical) than this overpriced school will put you in! Their grab-and-go stations are bomb, but are not usually substantial meals, therefore leaving you to wait in lines to eat things that are of questionable quality (def not rock bottom but nothing to brag about). Be a smart shopper, kids.

I love emmanuel college. it is the perfect school for me because of the academics, athletics and location. its the perfect distance from home for me and right in the city with so much to do within the area. I could not image myself anywhere else.
Emmanuel college quickly became my new home from the moment I moved in. The school provided a welcoming and open environment!

Every beginning has struggles and challenges, when I came to Emmanuel college I struggled just like any first generation student would, without any guidance on what to expect in college. But Emmanuel had resources and people that made sure I didn’t feel isolated. I became involved on campus which gave me another community I can rely on and most important, the school size of Emmanuel made sure I connect with my peers and professors who’d h made me feel more comfortable being at college because as a first generation college student i genuinely didn’t feel like i deserved to be here.
I have enjoyed my academic experience here at Emmanuel. I have been able to create great connections with my professors and always feel like I can ask them any questions I have.

My experience at Emmanuel is amazing. There are many ways to get involved here, and many opportunities to make connections both socially and professionally! I would like to see the housing situation change as well as some of the dining options!
Being in the Nursing Program has allowed me to become super close with my peer mates! Often partaking in study sessions, group projects, or just exploring Boston’s restaurants.

Emmanuel is a great school for community building! There are events every day for students held by clubs and RAs. There are also sports games that are free of charge to students and lots of larger schools in the area that students can explore. The Fenway neighborhood is a safe place and has a lot of dining options for students to eat off campus.

ai_summary: Franklin & Marshall College offers a distinctive blend of rigorous academics and a tight-knit community atmosphere that many students appreciate. Reviews highlight the college's supportive faculty , who are committed to student success and available for personal engagement, particularly in small class settings. However, the academic workload is described as challenging, often leading to significant stress among students. Social life at F&M is a mixed bag; while some students enjoy the variety of extracurricular activities and opportunities to engage in clubs, others express dissatisfaction with the limited social scene, especially after joining Greek life or athletics. The campus, located in Lancaster, PA, is often praised for its beauty and accessibility to nearby urban areas, although the dining options receive criticism for their quality. Some students feel a divide within the community regarding cultural interactions, while others note a growing diversity among their peers. Overall, F&M is seen as a place where dedication to academics and strong community ties can lead to personal growth, though the high tuition costs and challenges in navigating the social landscape are recurring concerns.

reviews.text: F&M is a hidden gem: Amazing and approachable faculty, brilliant students, outstanding sports teams, and students with talents galore! All in a great city - Lancaster!

As a junior physics major at Franklin & Marshall College, I can confidently say this place has exceeded every expectation. Nestled in historic Lancaster, Pennsylvania, F&M blends a tight-knit liberal arts vibe with rigorous STEM programs. Our physics department shines - small classes mean professors like Dr Krebs know you by name and mentor one-on-one for research. Last semester, I co-authored a paper using the college's cutting-edge lab equipment.
Beyond academics, Greek life is vibrant but optional, fostering lifelong friendships without overwhelming workloads. The Diplomats athletics scene pumps up campus energy, and urban perks like nearby markets add flavor. Sure, dorms could use updates, but the sense of community - think late-night study sessions turning into deep debates - makes up for it. F&M equips you for grad school or industry with personalized support and real-world projects. If you're passionate about physics in a collaborative environment, this is your spot. Go Dip!

So happy with my decision to come to F & M. Love the smaller class sizes and personalized experience. It offers so much if you take the time to take advantage of it all. I never feel bored and have found a home in so many different places on campus.

It is horrible and I am telling you do not come here no matter what . I genuinely think at any other small liberal arts school (if that’s what you are looking for) you will have a better experience. The academics are good but nothing crazy. School can get really hard if your schedule is bad but it’s not terrible. Going out scene there is nothing to do. I try and leave every weekend to visit other schools and my friends because it is so bad here. The food has to be the worst campus food in the country. 2 dining options and dining hall has the dumbest hours ever. IT LITERALLY CLOSES AT 8PM. It’s only open for breakfast till 10 am. Then lunch 11-130. Then dinner 6-8 it’s so dumb. Then the other place to get food is just horrendous.THIS PLACE SUCKS DO NOT COME HERE LITEEALLY 90 PERCENT HERE OF THE PEIOLE HATE IT

It's a good school and that's it. The professors are decent and care about you just like everywhere else. The academic experience is ok, not too stressful but can be hard to really slight over. From my experience as an international student, the social theme is very toxic and everyone feels kinda disingenuous and doesn't really want to connect with you. Campus life is boring asf if you're not into Greek Life. School spirit doesn't really exist and most (including me) don't really take pride of our school. Campus food is a huge disaster here like it's worse than only not delicious but sometimes you see undercooked or overcooked meat or bugs in the fresh bar. I am forced to buy the meal plan and have to get my own food most of the times, which is very disappointing. Nobody really cares about you most of the times. If you're local and your family is around you, and you're a middle-class white person into Greek Life and not really into urban lifestyle, this can be the right place for you.

I've had a great time at F&M! The sense of community here is wonderful, and the academics are challenging but not all-consuming. Many communities overlap, and so whenever you meet someone new, you always seem to share at least one friend.

The professors and levels of academics were generally fabulous. I am very allergic to mold, and because F&M is an old college, many of the buildings needed updating and had mold in them (also multiple of the dorms). The student accessibility services were fantastic. While this is not a party school you could generally find multiple things to do on campus most weekends.

Franklin & Marshall College has a great alumni network, options for resume building, professors, and student support. It is, however, incredibly overpriced, the party scene is troubling at best, and people are so isolated the school feels even smaller than it actually is. If you’re not into leaving your room, this is the school for you.

My overall experience with Franklin & Marshall College has been incredibly enriching. The community is welcoming and intellectually curious, which makes learning both challenging and inspiring. Professors are genuinely invested in students’ growth, encouraging critical thinking and personal exploration beyond the classroom. I’ve especially appreciated the small class sizes and the close faculty-student relationships that make discussions engaging and meaningful.
Beyond academics, the campus culture promotes collaboration, diversity, and global awareness, which has helped me grow as both a thinker and a person. Franklin & Marshall has provided not only strong academic foundations but also a supportive environment that prepares students to make a real difference in the world.
Nice school with a lot of opportunities, but unfortunately professors are very busy, Career Office doesn't have strong partnerships

First of all, if you're reading this review, please make sure you focus on the reviews from the past year or two when assessing F&M, you'll see that they're mostly outstanding. And my review is, too. My son is now a sophomore and he's really enjoying it. As everyone notes, the faculty and academics are superb and very challenging. He plays club hockey which is a lot of fun, and joined a fraternity -- the frats are fun and low key, not the toxic male environment as it can be at some schools. I am astonished that Niche gives the campus a C, the campus is beautiful. Equally as nice as most small liberal arts colleges. The dorms are no worse than at most schools. I think most important of all, it's really a positive and supportive environment, academically intense but not competitive in an unhealthy way. If you're considering schools like Bucknell or Lehigh or Lafayette, DEFINITELY consider F&M. (And they gave us some generous financial merit aid compared to Bucknell!)
I have worked in the admission office here. Great school if you're looking for a smaller personalized education with a very global experience.
Solid experience, could use a bit more diversity but understood because of the price tag of the school might not attract that.

As a staff member at F&M, I have an inside view and am so impressed with how deeply the administration, staff and faculty care about the students here. The entire summer is spent planning in detail how to welcome the first-year, transfer and returning students to campus so that everyone will feel at home and have both an immediate and enduring sense of belonging in the F&M community. It is common to see administrators and faculty walking across campus with students or sitting down with them for coffee. There are caring and skilled professional staff members providing individualized support all over campus in a multitude of capacities, such as student wellness and accessibility services, residential life services, leadership training, academic support, career and professional development services, and more. I would feel confident sending my children to F&M, knowing they will not only be challenged academically, but also nurtured holistically on a beautiful, safe and welcoming campus.

As an F&M alum, I credit the college with so much of my personal and professional success. The professors are wonderful, taking their time to get to know you, develop relationships, and support you throughout your learning experience. In each course I was challenged to think critically, explore new ideas and ways of approaching problems, and engage in high level academics while in a safe and supportive environment. I still am regularly connected to faculty from my time as a student, highlighting the lasting relationships that are easy to build on campus. The people at F&M — staff, faculty, fellow students, and the Lancaster community — care deeply about you and your growth during and after your time on campus. The city and county of Lancaster has so much to offer and campus is such a wonderfully beautiful place to learn and grow. I highly recommend F&M for anyone looking for a dynamic and engaging place to study, live, and grow!

ai_summary: Furman University offers a beautiful campus and a supportive community that many students appreciate. Academics are rigorous, with professors dedicated to student success, emphasizing personalized support and hands-on experiences like internships and study abroad programs. However, the social scene often draws criticism, particularly due to the absence of fraternity houses and a party culture that some find limiting unless they are part of Greek life. While many students enjoy the close-knit atmosphere and the variety of extracurricular activities, issues with diversity and the high cost of tuition are recurring concerns. Overall, Furman is seen as a great place for driven individuals seeking an engaging educational experience, although the campus culture may not resonate with everyone.

reviews.text: Literally a repeat of high school. The only thing to do there is join Greek life, and even that is not ideal. No Greek houses, and everyone starts to hate each other because the school is so small. Not to mention the place is genuinely cursed.

made some awesome friends. it was stressful but my professors changed my life in a significant positive way (and my friends did too). furman is beautiful and greenville is the best town ever. it's not the most selective school in the country, obviously, but it's pretty damn good and it's politically diverse and i'm so glad i went to furman.

If you are a religious fanatic or a loner then this is the university for you. The town is stupid, just for people trying to sell there nft or trying to open another millennial restaurant where the burgers are $30. 90% of the clubs are religious and someone called me a whore for wearing a tang top. They have "good" academics but how can you focus on school when your alone and your only option for friends are people who think church is a party.

Furman seriously gave me a horrible first year college experience. I feel like it was high school part 2. My housing situation was awful, I was lonelier than I have ever been, despite it being a very small campus, and the academics are just ok. I love Greenville, but Furman is not in Greenville, unless you have a car.
I loved my time at Furman! I really enjoyed all my classes. Furman is very challenging but prepares you for the future.

While I enjoyed my fellow students, professors, and classes, I would say that the Furman administration has a lot to learn when it comes to helping students who may need assistance during their time there. Anyone who may need financial or academic aid will soon find that the administration at Furman could not care less about helping you unless your family has donated an absurd amount of money to the school. There were also instances of hateful graffiti and sexual assault on campus that were handled very poorly by the administration. I would say that as is, Furman is a good-sized school in a nice location with wonderful professors and interesting classes. As long as you're a more 'cookie cutter' student, you will have no problems there.

My experience with the Furman University Child Development Center has been very positive. The staff are caring, professional, and attentive to children’s needs. They provide a safe and nurturing environment that supports social, emotional, cognitive, and early academic growth. The teachers have been the best part of my experience; they are warm, highly engaged, and introduce academic concepts like letters, numbers, and problem-solving through play and structured activities. Communication with parents is strong, and the facilities offer excellent indoor and outdoor opportunities. The most challenging part is the long waiting list, and extended hours or more flexible scheduling would help working parents. More variety in meals, snacks, and enrichment activities would also add value. Overall, I would love to see the Center expand so more families can benefit. Despite a few challenges, the combination of academics, caring staff, and a supportive environment has been excellent.

I went to furman university and felt nothing but sadness and dread, like my life was being sucked out of me by the poor choices of clubs, lame food, overly religious themes for a non religiously affiliated school. Ehh if you like it it's ok, not for me though.

I had a great experience and wish I could do it again. I lived in a cabin overlooking the lake. I have some of my fondest memories from Furman. I still keep in touch with the friends I made. Greek life was also fun.

I transferred in to Furman University and so far I couldn't be happier with the uplifting and supportive community that Furman has offered me. My advisors are supporting me and putting me in the right direction and I feel safe and comfortable to be myself on campus. I am excited to see what my next year will bring me.

My first year at Furman University was a challenge, but I am glad I made the decision to go here. The class offerings and professors are great, the campus is beautiful, and I got to live in a brand-new dorm. Additional food options on campus would be good, but the surrounding area has a lot to offer. My classes are not easy, and I appreciate being treated like an adult by the administration and faculty. Everyone seems to have a vested interest in my success and that takes some pressure off because I know I can ask for help and it will be given. I look forward to my next 3 years here and developing more connections.
It is very enjoyable and hospitable environment all around. Everyone is super supportive and welcoming that it truly makes you feel part of the community.

I had a negative experience at Furman. The faculty is so small and the gossip is so prevlanet that if you have a negative expereince with one professor, you are better off switching majors. I personally had an unjust and negative expereince with Professor Bressler in the Poli Sci department which carried over for years until I attended grad school. One professor can make a huge difference in a small college. There was not much diversity on campus and the Furman Bubble does not prepare you for the real world. I was much happier attending unversities that were open to new ideas and creative thinking.

Furman dining services is a very good place to work.... The managers there in the dining services are awesome to work for and very understanding.... I would tee totally recommend working in the dining services especially at the trone center... Very nice coworkers that love to cut up and laugh while at the same time working and being very productive to make sure the work day goes good and safe... Plus good food

The campus is beautiful, academics are rigorous but manageable. Most professors are very willing to help. Lots of opportunities and resources are available to you. It's the kind of school where everyone knows everyone, which can be good or bad. Many people find their own groups and don't branch out much, so it can take awhile to feel like you belong, but once you do it's an enjoyable place to be

ai_summary: At Gettysburg College, students often appreciate the small class sizes and dedicated professors who foster meaningful connections and a supportive learning environment . Academics are generally seen as challenging but rewarding, with opportunities for research and personalized guidance. The campus itself is beautiful and safe, located in a historic town with various social activities, predominantly driven by Greek life. However, some students cite a lack of diversity and concerns about the administration's responsiveness to student needs, alongside issues related to campus culture and opportunities for those not involved in sports or Greek activities. The dining options, while often praised for quality, can become repetitive over time. Overall, Gettysburg offers a close-knit community but can feel isolating for those not engaged in the party culture or extracurriculars.

reviews.text: Alumni network has been helpful in finding job in the local Philly network. I think that the liberal arts offering is critical as AI enters the workforce. I feel more well-rounded than my peers at internships, who struggle with communication and team work and thinking critically

I love my college, the professors are so supportive and encouraging and I have made long-lasting friendships here. I'm sad to be graduating, but happy I got to experience the college. The only con is the cost of tuition and the unnecessary spending and cuts, it seems whoever handles the budget is not doing the best job. Other than that, the college is great.

Professors truly cared about my experience. There were a few classes which I will always cherish. They truly changed my life. I was forced to critically think and engage with the world around me. I also loved the community work I got to engage with. I got to meet so many different people within the Gettysburg and Adams County community.

At Gettysburg College, I truly appreciate the small, close-knit academic environment where professors are supportive and approachable. With small class sizes, I feel seen and heard in every course, and I’ve found that faculty genuinely care about my success. I also value the many opportunities outside the classroom. From the wide range of clubs and leadership programs to study abroad options and public service initiatives, I’ve been able to explore my interests and grow. The dining hall (Servo) has been a highlight, offering good food and friendly service.
At the same time, I’ve noticed areas where Gettysburg could be even better. Housing varies in quality, and while dining is generally good, I would like to see more variety and healthier options. Despite these areas for improvement, I feel grateful to be part of such a supportive and inspiring college community.

My academic experience at Gettysburg was invaluable. My professors were some of the most amazing mentors and I took classes that defined my undergraduate experience. I was also able to participate in the Eisenhower Institute which is a great resource for Political Science majors as it provided me many opportunities to travel to Washington D.C. and meet with professionals in the field.

Gettysburg college is a liberal arts college that values its students developing a well rounded education. For that reason you have to take liberal arts credits/classes to graduate. This has made getting my degree a little more tedious and while some classes have been appreciated, I’d much rather focus on my major.

Attending this College has been one of the most frustrating experience of my academic life. They don't care about your success and only want your money. Course offerings are limited, especially in upper-level classes making it difficult to graduate on time. The administration is unorganized and unresponsive - good luck getting a straight answer from them.
Campus facilities are outdated, and while the website boasts about renovations and resources, the reality doesn't match the marketing. Housing is overpriced and poorly maintained, and dining options are limited and frequently unhealthy.
I wouldn't recommend this school unless it's your absolute last resort. I expected better, and I've been left feeling disappointed and underprepared.

My favorite things about Gettysburg College was its close-knit community and profound academic studies. Every where you went on campus you could recognize a smiling face that always made the campus feel like home. Even after I graduated, I am still friends with some of the most intelligent and amazing people I met through attending Gettysburg College. Academically, I always found a new challenge with some of the most acclaimed professors supporting me. The courses I took and professors I met at Gettysburg helped shape me into the driven and passionate adult I am today.

I like that there are many international students studying here. It gave me a new prospective to life in other countries. Since the college is small the professors tend to focus on their students, and it is easy to receive help here.
Love Gettysburg College. Great food. Cute surrounding town. Great Education. Professors have more time than at big schools. We go hard for a small school.
Students are very motivated to learn, and academic rigor is intense. However, students also love to have a good time.

As a queer, non-white student, there are places on campus and groups of people where you feel like you can fit in. However, it is a primarily white institution in both student body and faculty. I don't have any criticisms about academics for the most part, I've enjoyed pretty much all but one of my classes and I am currently a junior. There are a lot of clubs, but honestly unless you're looking for minority-based clubs, there's only a small percentage of POC in any club. I did choose to go here, but I think I was unprepared for the lack of awareness of upper-middle-class white students (who are the majority). I really appreciate the opportunities that the college has allowed me to find, and I can definitely say I've developed relationships and networks with the professors here. The faculty are generally great, but the administration (like most institutions) is focused more on making money and admitting students who will/can pay more for the college experience.

The school was great in general but since it’s a predominantly white school, student of color do not have great experience as the other students.
I liked the academics part of it, the professors were great and seemed to care about student success.

My experience at Gettysburg has been really nice so far, mainly because I was able to find people with my interests quickly. I would encourage everyone to attend to clubs and events that interest you!

Gettysburg College has changed a lot over the past 15 to 20 years. The school is more like a high school in how they treat their students. It is an excellent academic school; if that is what you are looking for, this is a good spot. It also has a good dinning hall, but that is it the bullet hole is gold but is not open early or late same with the dinning hall. There isn't really any food options besides those two. Social life is ok not great if you want to meet people and go out occasionally, this is not your place. The school is costly, and they have been trying to remove many different things from the students. The party scene/ Greek life is a shell of what it was, mostly in part from the staff that run it. They wonder why they lost money, but it's right in front of their eyes. They limit so much of what students do, and campus safety is a joke. They harass so many students. I wish they would change, but this is the way it has been.

ai_summary: Grinnell College stands out for its rigorous academic environment and dedicated professors who provide personalized attention , fostering strong student-faculty relationships. Many reviews highlight the collaborative and friendly nature of the student body, making it easy to build connections and find support. However, the social scene seems to be hit or miss—some students find it enjoyable, while others describe it as cliquey or lacking in vibrancy , partly due to the college's rural location. The administration receives mixed reviews, with concerns about neglecting student interests and managing resources effectively; students frequently express frustrations regarding food quality and the limited options for classes. Despite these challenges, numerous students appreciate the unique opportunities for research , the open curriculum, and the chance to engage with a diverse community passionate about social issues . Overall, Grinnell College offers a distinctive educational experience that prioritizes academic rigor and individuality within a small but diverse setting.

reviews.text: Grinnell can be a great school for the right students. For me personally, it wasn’t a great fit, but I know a lot of people who had a good experience here. The academics are rigorous and I was impressed by a lot of the professors here. The social scene is decent, but it can be hard to feel at home if you aren’t the Grinnell type. If you’d describe yourself as nonconventional, quirky, alternative, and nerdy, you’ll probably have. great time here. If you’ve ever considered joining Greek life or haven’t died your hair blue, this might not be the best fit for you. It is in a small town which I found charming, but if you’re a city person, you might not like it. That being said, it’s within a hour of Des Moines and Iowa City, so it doesn’t feel too remote. The food really isn’t great, and last I heard, they’re now forcing all students to be on a meal plan. Overall, strong academics and generous financial aid, but might be isolating if you’re not a very specific kind of person.
Grinnell is the place where I found my people. As a student athlete, I felt like I found a community where individuals were both focused on their academics/careers, while also loving their sport.

Grinnell College offers an academic and social community sourced in critical thought and social justice, allowing for a collegiate experience that emphasizes intentionality and social awareness throughout all areas of campus. Its small campus size is one leading piece that enables this intentionality to sustain. Our individualized academic advising, small class sizes, and the meaningful relationships that are made among peers makes the Grinnell experience live up to what is promised.

I attend Grinnell a while ago but I found it interesting that newer students had a lot of the same issues I did. I always wondered if I had made the best choice in going to Grinnell. I alway felt safe on Campus and in the town of Grinnell and I did not feel isolated. I did not feel my degree helped me and while I was able to get a good internship the career placement office was useless unless you wanted to work in a bank in Des Moines. The only students who got good jobs after graduation were kids from wealthy families who had connections. I did teach college courses for awhile until my school closed and the Grinnell degree did help me get hired.

It was too isolated, unsafe and did not have any decent career path if you weren't pursuing a fixed track anyway. The majority of students who went there came from very privileged backgrounds so any or all of their success all being tied to family and community connections independent from Grinnell College made who dominated grants or scholarships for internships those who already had connections, knew how to win departmental and/or section competitions for 4 credits + housing, food, professional and networking awards very unfair. The career center was absolutely terrible. Intellectual curiosity was not rewarded. I regret going.

The academic side of Grinnell was excellent in my experience; my professors were knowledgeable and considerate. However, the school was much less diverse than I was led to believe when applying. On the administrative side, some of Grinnell's recent practices have been frustrating, including recently making dining plans mandatory for all students, regardless of residence. Since COVID, Grinnell's social/party scene has declined, but finding friends is relatively easy. One unique aspect of Grinnell is the Union of Grinnell Student Dining Workers (UGSDW), which now represents all student workers at the College. For prospective students interested in labor organizing, UGSDW provides a great opportunity for practical experience.

Easy to build a relationship with your professors, not as much to do in a small town. Several incidents of townspeople being racists towards students of color, with insufficient action from the college.
Incredible academics, incredibly friendly community, dhall fold has become considerably better in the last year

Overall, Grinnell has been a good experience! The academics are certainly top-notch, and most professors are absolutely wonderful. Grinnell has a special student culture and dynamic that feels different from even other liberal arts colleges. Rural Iowa, albeit often a bit boring, is a surprisingly beautiful and certainly unique place to live for four years. I will say that the workload at Grinnell is really, really intense, and most people I know struggle mentally from the amount of pressure their courses put on them. It can be really hard to find a good work/life balance and this makes it a lot harder to make friends as well. The dining hall (D-hall, as we affectionately call it) is not as bad as students say, but it is certainly not Michelin-level cuisine either. Grinnell's administration is also really poorly run and can be frustrating at times. Overall, I'm happy with my choice, even if I regret it when I'm drowning in homework during my finals week.
Before arriving at Grinnell College, I was really hesitant about the going there, but after getting there and meeting the other freshmen and upperclassmen, I felt at home.
The academics are absolutely rigorous in nature but greatly rewarding. The college designs a wonderful campus community and resulting campus life, though on-campus food is lacking.
It is very homey and I feel like I really fit in here. All of the professors are really chill and you get to know them well.

Grinnell was by far the best four years of my life. The professors were outstanding. I was able to participate in research, an internship, and other hands-on and creative opportunities in and outside of the classroom. But what made it the best were the people, friends and professors alike.

As a student at Grinnell College, I have enjoyed the academics and athletics programs. I think that is a pretty good place to learn and connect with different communities and diversities. The party scene is not as good as big colleges, but the community and the faculty are very welcoming!

Very welcoming environment with challenging but worthwhile academics. Housing quality questionable, especially due to renovations causing people to be compressed into fewer dorms than what was intended.

ai_summary: Gustavus Adolphus College offers a close-knit community that many students appreciate, emphasizing strong relationships with professors due to small class sizes. Academically, students note the personalized attention and engagement in their courses, which enhances their learning experience. While there's a vibrant student life with numerous activities, organizations, and athletics, some students express concerns over housing and the campus's somewhat limited diversity. The campus atmosphere is described as welcoming and supportive , although a few reviews mention administrative challenges and the high cost of attendance . Overall, the college is characterized as a great fit for those seeking a smaller college experience with a strong sense of community, though opinions on specific academic programs vary.

reviews.text:
Parent of a current student. I think the academics are strong. Professors care. And the course content is relevant. Food is fine. Dorms are decent.
The problem with this place is the kids. If they’re not student athletes, they’re in their dorm rooms. It’s mostly kids with low expectations for social experiences. Yes, the campus activities board has done events. But there’s just not a lot to do.
I think it is a nice college. The class sizes are small which is what I prefer. There nothing right next to campus so unless you want to go on a bit of a walk, a car would be nice.

Gustavus is such a great place to go! You get such a well rounded education, and have access to so many opportunities that some colleges wouldn't offer. Gustavus faculty are incredible to work with, and they truly support you and what you want to do with your future. The classes are rigorous, but manageable, and you have so many resources you can use to help you succeed in college and in your classes. There are also so many ways to get involved at Gustavus, and the community there is genuinely so special.
The campus is very beautiful; lots of student activities taken place during and after school hours. The dorms are very cozy and staff are amazing.

I have been at Gustavus for one year so far, and I absolutely loved it! I was a part of the swimming and diving team that won the MIAC Championship this year which was really fun. We had a lot of teams perform really well in sports this year. I also had really good experiences with all of my professors and was able to excel in all of my classes. They had a lot of good resources to help navigate through the first year and prepare us for what is to come. I also made a lot of really great friends and I am super excited to go back next year!

Gustavus is a tight community! Everyone supports everyone, and you can't go anywhere without seeing a familiar face or someone you know. The professors are super supportive, and the small class sizes allow them to get to know you on a personal level. Athletics is another giant support system within the Gustavus community. All of the athletes support each other within the same sport, and support for other sports. The location of Gustavus is just 15 minutes from Mankato and about an hour from the Twin Cities. Aspects like location and student body make Gustavus a very safe, welcoming, and supportive campus.

I have loved Gustavus, it is a small college, so we are a community. You can make a lot of friends and support community super easily. The professors are super kind, understanding, and care for not only our education successes but also personal successes. It seems like there is something to do every day on campus through all of the clubs and campus activities. Our college supports; arts, science, music, athletics, and theater/dance equally. The students here are very easy to get along with but you also don't have to get along with everyone here because it is a big enough campus you won't see them every day, but you will see them sometimes.
Gustavus is such a great school but pricey on the other hand. I love gustavus it’s such a calm campus as well as the classroom sizes. You are able to bond with professors which is great!
It’s been ok. I feel that the social scene isn’t great and it can feel that you are trapped here sometimes. Most of the people are great but you have to make your time here great.
I loved my time at Gustavus! I made so many great connections with professors and staff who I continue to reach out to today!

I love the recutting process and the football team of Gustavo Adolphus university and I also love their academic system.
Thanks

I like the small class sizes because it allows me to make stronger connections with my peers. I also like the number of campus activities there are. It's easy to get involved through clubs, activities, and events. I would like to see a change in housing on campus. It's very difficult to get an apartment or off-campus housing while attending school here. It would benefit many students well-being and financial circumstances to make changes to the current housing system.
Definitely happy I went to Gustavus. I went for softball, and the athletics here are great. I love the facilities and coaches. The academics for Gustavus are also good.

Starting my freshman year at Gustavus Adolphus College has been an exciting and rewarding experience. As a football player and biology major, I’ve had to balance academics, athletics, and social life, but I’ve found great support here. With both my parents and my aunt being alumni, Gustavus already felt like home, and my time here so far has exceeded my expectations.
Football has given me an instant sense of community, with dedicated coaches and supportive teammates pushing me to grow. Academically, my biology classes are challenging but engaging, and the small class sizes allow for meaningful interactions with my professors. The hands-on lab work keeps me excited about my major.
Beyond academics and sports, Gustavus has a welcoming environment, rich traditions, and a beautiful campus. There’s always something happening, making college life both fulfilling and fun. So far, my experience has been everything I hoped for, and I look forward to the journey ahead.

Gustavus to me has been my home away from home. I had trouble finding who I was my first two years, but Gustavus has really great opportunities to find your people. The academics are truly one of a kind with professors being like friends and classes being interesting to take. The only downside is the outdated residential halls and a very limited cafeteria.

ai_summary: Hamilton College has received mixed feedback from students, with common themes emerging around academics, community, and campus life. Many reviews highlight the strong academic environment , small class sizes , and dedicated professors who genuinely care about student success. The open curriculum is praised for allowing students to explore diverse interests without being confined to general education requirements. However, some students express frustration with the perceived lack of diversity and the sometimes cliquish social atmosphere. The campus is described as beautiful, though the town's isolation can limit social activities, particularly for non-athletes or those not engaged in specific social groups. While the college offers a tight-knit community that many find supportive, some students feel that the social life can be underwhelming, resembling high school dynamics rather than a vibrant college experience. Overall, Hamilton College stands out for its academic rigor and close community, but students should consider their social preferences and openness to its cultural environment.

reviews.text: I had a wonderful experience at Hamilton College. There are lots of events on campus. The community is very welcoming. I had a lots of great professors.
Hamilton College was a great undergraduate experience for me. The community is small which makes it tight-knit and there's truly a place for everyone.

Academics were excellent, and based on what I know, this is the strongest aspect of the school. Unlike my experience as a grad student, I never felt like there were "gaps" in my understanding because professors very thoroughly planned their lessons, from pacing to when certain concepts were introduced. This is especially great at a small school where its easy to get help. The professors I did have were great in office hours (never skipping ahead, articulate, patient, etc) Especially if you're considering Biology, Economics or Math, I'd bet on a really great education at Hamilton.
That said, there's a bunch of minor inconveniences (to me at least) that accumulate to a 4 star rating. First off, the campus is extremely isolated. Second, the facilities/dorms weren't the best - this is mostly due to students making a mess, but sometimes the trash wouldn't get picked up, etc. Third, the school is SUPER cliquey which sucked for socially anxious me. Avoid this school if you are introverted.

While I graduated many years ago, I have been very impressed with the progress Hamilton has made over the years in becoming one of the best colleges in America. My academic training and access to Hamilton's robust alumni network, were major factors in my successful professional career. Hamilton is also one of the few colleges in the country that has both an open curriculum and need blind admissions policy. Although Clinton, NY is a very small town, the Hamilton campus is stunning and the sense of community is wonderful. The level of cultural diversity is also much greater than many other small liberal arts colleges.

My daughter attends and is a Biology major. Her academic experience in the department has been disappointing. If you are pre-med, the academic experience is probably best suited for you. For other students interested in other areas of biology including conservation biology and ecology, I don’t believe Hamilton is a great choice. The faculty are less student centered in my experience than comparable liberal arts schools and not very field- oriented. The overall pedagogy is conservative and lecture driven. I work in higher education and wished we had more carefully researched the department.

I loved my department and professors but never felt like Hamilton was the right place for me. I definitely had a hard time adjusting to social life because I was from the south. I was on a sports team and athletic facilities were top notch.

Hamilton is the place where I made my closest friends for life! They bring everything to you- amazing speakers, performances, and activities. The professors genuinely love teaching and are excited to to research with undergrads.

I honestly cannot thank more for what hamilton had to offer of which changed my life trajectory. I am now a PhD student and I never could have imagined when I was a freshman. Yes. It is isolating and sometimes can be lonely, but it def pushes you to get out of the comfort zone to explore and find out who you are and what you love. The professors were phenomenal and they really want to teach you. It is definitely predominantly white and waspie, but most people are inclusive and welcoming. Hamilton is not for everyone, but it was for me.

I look back on my experience extremely fondly. I grew as a leader, scholar, and athlete while attending, and I would not alter my four years on the Hill. The sense of community is special, and it is one of the aspects which I miss most.

Hamilton College offered a truly enriching experience—academically rigorous, supportive faculty, and a strong sense of community. I appreciated the open curriculum, which gave me the freedom to explore my interests deeply. The campus is beautiful and fosters meaningful connections, both inside and outside the classroom. However, I’d like to see improvements in diversity and inclusion efforts, as well as more mental health resources. Overall, it’s a place that challenges you to grow intellectually and personally.

If you are looking for a safe, welcoming home away from home, Hamilton is definitely the right place for you! The professors genuinely want you to succeed and know you by name - which is rare in college. I have always felt very supported here by friends and faculty. There is always someone to help you and I have loved it here thus far. :)

Don't dread how cold Utica can be, outside of the temperature Hamilton and the little town that surrounds it is full of opportunities everyone can enjoy. The cafe life off and off campus is everything you would want , affordable , aesthetically pleasing / homey, and so good. There are many events that happen every week at cam pus and are all very inclusive and welcoming, one day you may be in a group learning how to crochet and the next day you'll be watching student bands performing. Despite how little the town is there is always somethings to do and enjoy. For anyone wondering about diversity keep in mind its a small town in Upstate New York, you may not find people that look like you and you'll be given two choices: 1) To Educate or 2) To listen. Tell people your experiences to help broaden the minds of those in this small town; or listen to the experience of others. You will learn not to judge a book by its cover and that you are more similar to other than what you think.

Hamilton College has excellent academics. I learned more than I ever thought I could while attending. I would like to see the administration more responsive to the concerns of students. I think this is an achievable ask due to the new college president.

I have loved my experience at Hamilton college. Everyone that I have met is so smart, kind, and eager to learn. The community is so welcoming and tight-knit and it really feels at home when I am there. The professors are so passionate about what they are teaching that it is inspiring. There is an excellent student life and food at the college as well. The campus is beautiful as well as the area around it. It is extremely safe, one of the safest places I have ever been. I always am sad to go home over breaks, it has become my favorite place to be.

Hamilton College offers exactly what it promises; a challenging and engaging learning environment with a fairly diverse array of students. The professors are all genuinely enthusiastic about teaching, and students are qualified and determined. The atmosphere is one that will give you every opportunity to succeed so long as you work hard.

A Hampden-Sydney education is a transformational experience. As colleges have become universities with a voracious appetite for gorging on more students, Hampden-Sydney had maintained its connection to a time tested educational experience. Hampden-Sydney is not a place to go to get lost; it's a place to find yourself. Professors and administrators know you, and care. You learn to think, to speak, to write. You learn skills that are applicable to all facets of life. You have the opportunity to explore your other passions that can lead to careers or lifelong hobbies. I promise you that those aspects of the College that some may deem weaknesses are actually its strengths. Being all-male provides a common base upon which a brotherhood is built; being rural gives room to grow, explore and make mistakes in a forgiving environment; being Division III showcases students committed to living a rigorous, healthy lifestyle who compete for the sheer love of the game.

The liberal arts education at Hampden-Sydney is top-notch; however, I still cannot recommend it to anyone. From the moment you matriculate to the moment you walk across the stage during Commencement, you exist in a weird bubble. Once you exit this bubble, finding gainful employment is often a monumental task, because Hampden-Sydney does an abysmal job of preparing you for a career. Statistically speaking, Hampden-Sydney places near the bottom of the list (roughly 3000th place) of colleges for early career salaries. Most students wind up finding themselves in MLM schemes or other crappy sales jobs after graduation (roughly 60% according to LinkedIn). Graduate school is hardly an option either, since Hampden-Sydney has one of the lowest rates of grade inflation in the entire American tertiary education system. Simply put, H-SC graduates cannot compete with those from other colleges in graduate school admissions. Do yourself a favor and stay far away from this third-rate institution.

During my undergraduate years, I attended a politically conservative institution in rural Virginia. Hampden-Sydney was a unique experience. To spend four years in a rural, all-male, southern, white school, you must either love the culture that these characteristics bring or be offered a scholarship. Sydney's an institution does not stand for much outside of the white male perspective, and as a black man, I learned this pretty quickly. I found myself negotiating space, both literal and ideological. However, the academic environments I found with professors were phenomenal. The support I found in my professors truly pushed me to be the best student I could be. The resources and alumni connections were also fantastic.

Hampden-Sydney is the most transformational coming of age experience for men. It is one of the only colleges in this country that is intently focused on refining the character of its students with the goal of creating national and global leaders in any field of professional and personal endeavor. There is no college in this country that better forms good men and good citizens.
They have showed me the campus and it’s very good , this college is not that far but not to close either

My favorite aspect of Hampden-Sydney college is the close, tight-knit community that shares the value of building brotherhood throughout the college and maintaining a higher standard for the students through the Honor Code.
Nothing better than the brotherhood. Plenty of women on the weekends, but always business before pleasure.
It's the best decision a young man can ever make. The benefits of the Hampden-Sydney education and brotherhood are unmatched, and its positive influence will be with you long after graduation.

Truly an incredible experience and the most transformational college experience for men. You will be taught how to think, not what to think, unlike most colleges and universities in this country. A bastion of civil discourse and freedom of thought and expression. This college values individual growth, character refinement, moral development, leadership, social skills, and community. Highly recommend to anyone looking for a brotherhood that will last a lifetime.

Selecting Hampden-Sydney College was unequivocally the greatest decision I've ever made. Despite my childhood conviction that the University of Tennessee was my destined path, a single tour of Hampden-Sydney College shattered that belief, reshaping my entire perspective in an instant. If given the chance to rewind time, I would, without hesitation, choose Hampden-Sydney College once more. Its transformative impact on my life is unparalleled, and the opportunities it has afforded me are beyond measure.
While the journey to acclimatization required patience and perseverance, it pales in comparison to the boundless rewards I've reaped since finding my place here. Hampden-Sydney College isn't just a school; it's a life-long brotherhood. For anyone facing a similar decision, let me be unequivocal: Choose Hampden-Sydney College. Trust me; you won't regret it.

School is great but some people in the administration are not helpful. Dean of Greek life and students are extremely helpful along with most in OSA. Business office, career center, and other administration departments are not helpful toward students at all. Professors are extremely helpful as well.

Fantastic college that made me the man I am today. Heavy emphasis on character development. Go there to be a part of the community and get involved in everything you can. This place educates the whole person: academics, morals, socialness, etc.
Best college ever! Hampden Sydney is the perfect place for anyone who really wants to get the most out of their college.

My time at Hampden-Sydney College was nothing short of extraordinary. The academic rigor pushed me to new heights, offering challenges that were always met with rewarding outcomes. The faculty and staff embodied an unwavering pride in the institution, fostering an environment where learning and growth were paramount. What truly made my experience unforgettable was the unparalleled sense of camaraderie among students—a genuine brotherhood that permeated every aspect of campus life. And amidst the academic pursuits, the vibrant party scene added an exciting dimension to the college experience. Even in the quaint town of Farmville, there was a comforting, homey feeling that made every moment memorable. Hampden-Sydney College truly became my second home, where I thrived academically, socially, and personally.

Do not waste your time going to this school. The school prices itself on its “honor code” but lies to prospective students about the integrity of this school. Not only is Hampden-Sydney mediocre, but the admissions office prides itself on false advertising. No, this school isn’t that hard. No, we don’t have good parties. The professors are great aside from the Rhetoric department, the atmosphere is nice, but it is all stained by the ever-so-growing liberal presence on campus in the administration and beyond. Hampden-Sydney is NOT what it used to be and is a mediocre liberal arts college AT BEST.

ai_summary: Hanover College offers a small , close-knit community that fosters personal connections between students and professors, which many reviews highlight as a significant strength. The academic environment is described as academically rigorous , with small class sizes that allow for personalized attention , though some students have noted that the overall academic rigor can vary by department. Student life includes opportunities for involvement in clubs, athletics, and Greek life, although the local area and housing conditions have drawn criticism, particularly for upperclassmen. The dining options are widely regarded as lacking quality and variety . Reviews indicate that Hanover is less diverse socially and culturally , and some students have expressed dissatisfaction with administrative responsiveness to concerns. Overall, Hanover College is seen as a place where many students can thrive academically and socially, but it does come with challenges related to its size and student services.

reviews.text: I loved every minute of being at Hanover, as a senior now looking back it has been the craziest experinece but I would not have done it anywhere else than here. The faculty and staff are exceptional in what they do and help you through your college experience. As well as the community here, going so far away from home I needed to find somewhere that would feel like home and Hanover has done just that!

I love Hanover College and could never imagine being anywhere else. The academics are top tier and there are so many different classes to take. The school is big enough that I can still meet new people, but small enough that I can enjoy my classes and know I'll know someone in the class. The campus is beautiful and downtown Madison is a great place to visit.

I love how open to feedback our administration is. They are willing to attend weekly meetings with our student council organization on campus to discuss policy and the general student experience.
Tutoring, career services, housing, dining, and close relationships with professors are all very unique and catered to individual students.
Employment on campus is accessible and provides a multitude of opportunities in Res Life, dining services, student success services, childcare, and discipline specific roles.

I had multiple children choose Hanover as their college and all of them had unique, wonderful experiences. They grew in their subject area as well as in their leadership and communication skills. All are very successful in their chosen fields and utilizing skills learned at Hanover.
Hanover is a warm, welcoming place! It's a beautiful campus filled with great people committed to the success of everyone.
