I am a senior at Hanover College and I have loved every minute of it. The community is really what sets Hanover apart. The students, faculty, and professors are some of the kindest and most welcoming people you will meet. Hanover is not a "suitcase school" where students pack up and go home every weekend. Instead, campus buzzes throughout the week about what tailgate, Student Life event, or mixer we should all attend together. Academics are unmatched at Hanover as well. One-on-one time with professors and advisors is common and welcomed. I personally have many of my professors' cell-phone numbers to text them anytime I need help with a problem, study set, or lab. Many of our professors go above and beyond, bringing us treats to class or inviting us into their homes for "family dinner" or an end-of-the-year bonfire. I also believe Hanover has succeeded in setting me up for graduate school. I will miss every minute of my time spent at Hanover College.
Overall experience was great. People are awesome, solid academics in most areas, dorms are pretty good, food could be better but not terrible.

I have thoroughly enjoyed my time at Hanover College. The campus life, academics, community, and people all made the place feel like my second home. I have met some of my best friends through Hanover. I have learned and tried new things that I never thought I would. It challenged me to be the best version of myself. Academically I have succeeded through the small classes and strong relationships with my professors.

My overall experience at Hanover has been great. I do love the beauty of the campus in all seasons of the year. Having a campus right by the Ohio River makes Hanover stick out from all colleges I visited. The campus life is always active. There is always an event going on with that being speakers, meetings, informative presentations, parties, involvement activities, social/academic clubs, and more. Overall it has been a great experience for me. But something I would like to see change is housing upgrades. I feel like the main issue is the housing on the campus. Something is always wrong with there not being hot water, a/c goes out frequently, heat goes out frequently, fire alarms set off by nothing at 2AM, and basic room upgrades. With these changes I think the campus would get more students to decide on Hanover College as there new home.

This college is a joke! If you're living off campus you will be judge and look at differently then students that actually go there. The professors DO NOT care about how you do in there class or even care to help you. They would cancel class for the dumbest reason as well! Study groups are changed 15min before the actually meeting time! Which sucks if you leave almost 40min out! It doesn't matter if you even try to get help for testing or assignment they literally will tell you to have your doctor LIE about having an anxiety disorder or something else. Just to have an excuse! They will also tell you that its okay to have a D as a passing grade! Coming from someone that was an all A's honor roll with an Academic Honors Diploma. I was told that the classes were small size with only 20 per class but really it was 60!!! Nearly ¾ of us failed the classes in the science department. Also, when you do try to leave this college to get away from them they WILL hold your money from FAFSA and lie!

I experienced racism like I never have before in my life at this school and the administration did nothing. In fact, I believe they both aided and covered up for the perpetrators of each incident. I transferred out to IU-Bloomington shortly after my first year here and had the time of my life. If you’re a minority, a woman, or both, this is not the school for you. Admissions talks up a big game but they cannot deliver nor are they willing to work to keep you safe. I saw women preyed upon by professors, racist incidents happen in Madison, and assaults occur at frat houses and nothing was ever done. Hanover is one of the worst liberal arts colleges in America and a degree from Hanover isn’t worth the paper it’s printed on.
Hanover college is an amazing school the only thing about the school for me is that it’s expensive and i can’t afford it. I truly love this school and everything it has to offer
My time at Hanover was the best 4 years of my life. I made many lasting friendships, and received an undergrad degree to set me up for success in grad school.

Hanover is a place that has and still continues to surprise me. Staff are genuinely interested in students and will help where and when it is needed/noticed. The connections not just in school but out of school are true and genuine. There are many opportunities that Hanover isn't afraid to let their students know about and advocate to participate in what seeks out to the students. The diversity is improving but majority it is one specific group. The academics can be challenging but they set the reality for the real world.

! This program has already dismissed five Black women from the 2025 cohort. Multiple directors and faculty members have resigned from their positions, citing the racism, bias, and systemic unfairness within the administration. Currently, Hanover is facing a lawsuit for systemic racism and sexism within the program. There are two directors in particular who have openly expressed that Black students make them uncomfortable through both words and actions.

I like how readily available opportunities to work with real companies or do research were. I participated in 3 research labs and worked for two FAANG companies all through Harvey Mudd’s research and clinic programs.
A very calm and relaxing campus experience. The privilege of having multiple campus connected allows for students to have the opportunity of creating new connections.

I am liking my experience so far. The classes are very challenging but not more so than I expected, I adore some of my professors and am terrified of others, and the best part is definitely the food (dining hall is ranked 7th in the nation). I've only done my first semester so far, which is pass/no pass for freshmen, which I think is both a blessing and a curse -- I'm not as stressed about classes, but at the same time, I tend to think I can procrastinate more which then causes stress. As far as the first few core classes go, it's a bit difficult to enjoy them all the time, but I do find them interesting. I can't say I'm loving my experience, though, because for the first 2 years I can only take the core, required classes, which don't really consist of classes I truly want to be taking. Looking at the class catalogs is torture knowing I have to wait to get there, but considering I'm stuck learning the basics right now, I think it could be going a lot worse.
Overall, I was pleased with it. The dorms were nice, although the rats were a nuisance. However, once you get to know them, the rats become your best friends.
Harvey Mudd is a really nice school but the workload is real! The profs, students and staff here are all really nice!
I genuinely had a horrible time here. Offered almost no support. There are many great liberal arts colleges in the world, this is not one of them
Overall I had a really great experience at Mudd. The comments about workload are definitely true - you get pushed hard, but there is no shortage of support from faculty/staff and other students.
I liked the fact that we can also pursue individualized majors. The overall environment as part of The Claremont Colleges altogether makes it so unique and amazing.

At Harvey Mudd, I was a physics-math major, which meant a lot of my time was spent doing academics. Overall, I definitely enjoyed my time and was able to connect with a good amount of students and a few teachers. The food is fire and easily beats out the other surrounding schools. I would like to see the party life improve. I'm not the biggest partier, but there were only a handful of weekends where I found myself having fun at a party. I would also like to see the school grow in size and volume.
Harvey mudd college is the perfect school for me. I'm sure happy I go there. Go Stags and Nas!!!!!!!

Harvey Mudd was a great place for me to work in labs. I learned about Material Science and have decided to continue on this path at the graduate school level. The experience of studying the properties of various materials has been fascinating. Material Science has an incredibly wide range of applications from the clothes we wear, the buildings we inhabit, the gadgets we use , all the way to the rockets that take us to outer space. The Harvey Mudd professors were great mentors.

Black mold. Everywhere. Every single dorm on campus has instances of black mold and a majority of inner dorm rooms have an infestation. I have been housed in one of the newer rooms in the newest dorm room on campus and I was bed ridden from mold poisoning. I was coughing up blood, lethargic, and severely asthmatic. My roommate also has similar symptoms as she is currently still staying there. When I contacted facilities and maintenance they cleaned the uprise of the mold but not the source of the problem as multiple reoccurrences of the mold reared its head. I am concerned and bewildered with this problem. I thought going to such a small and academic college would mean that the administration would care about the safety of their students but I guess I was wrong. If you have health issues or are immune compromised. Do not come here unless you plan to have housing accommodations off campus. This has been awful.

This is an awful, miserable, heartbreaking, mind numbing place. Workloads are impossibly high, students throw around slang for sleep deprivation, and the freshman are up to their necks in problem sets come Saturday night. Also the entire money angle of this college is wrapped up in the military industrial complex (read: defense industry) and so you can kiss those after-graduation job prospects goodbye if you’re not interested in building bombs for empire. Do not go to this college.

I'm an incoming freshman! I was accepted to the Class of 2028 and I'll likely be studying either engineering or math+compsci. I attended Admitted Student Day 2 weeks ago on campus and the current students as well as the professors are extremely kind, humble, knowledgeable, and always willing to help.
The academic program and courses were very challenging, but there is a great sense of collaborative community.

ai_summary: Haverford College offers a unique blend of rigorous academics and a close-knit community , making it a distinctive liberal arts experience. Many reviews highlight the supportive faculty and beneficial relationships with professors, contributing to personalized education in small class settings. The Quaker values and Honor Code imbue a sense of respect and collaboration among students, though some express a perceived divide between athletes and non-athletes and concerns about social engagement , especially for those not involved in sports. The campus is celebrated for its beauty, but opinions on the social scene vary, with some students finding it limited or heavily centered around drinking. Dining is frequently praised, particularly for its quality and staff. While financial aid options are noted as excellent, some reviews suggest that administrative support could be improved, particularly for marginalized students. Overall, Haverford is described as a nurturing environment with ample opportunities for intellectual growth, but it may not suit everyone, particularly those seeking a more vibrant social life.

reviews.text: I love Haverford! It's a super intentional community where people really care about each other and the vast majority of people there (students, faculty, staff, admin) are incredibly kind. I love the Honor Code and Haverford's emphasis on student agency, and have always felt academically challenged while also supported holistically. The only downside is the dining, but they're always open to feedback!

My experience was mixed and I ultimately transferred out after one year. BUT what I will say really stands/has stood out to me is how kind students are and much the campus trusts them and readily engages with them for feedback. College students (validly) like to complain about not feeling listened to by their administration, but in the grand scheme of administrations, Haverford is very receptive by comparison. When I was there a few years ago, the president held open hours to meet with students and provide snacks once a week. The community is also very tight knit—for better or worse, I knew practically everyone in my year before leaving and made some amazing friends who I have stayed in touch with. Academics and local area were somewhat lacking, but the community is very special.
I had a wonderful experience. Amazing professors, friends, classes and incredible opportunities to learn.

Haverford has seemed more silent than I imagined—not in a boring way, but in a pensive one. Things move at a deliberate pace. There is a pause before people speak. People listen. You feel the culture of care in small moments—a professor lingering after class, someone holding space for an idea that’s still forming.
Academically, intellectual curiosity is taken seriously. Classes are small enough that you cannot disappear, and interdisciplinary thinking is natural. My interests in psychology and neuroscience are constantly pushed into ethical and philosophical questions. It feels less like memorizing and more like a shift in perspective.
The Honor Code shapes everything—open doors, unproctored exams, student-run processes. It’s empowering, but it asks you to be accountable academically and interpersonally.
If anything, I would expand the social mixing across years and interests. Haverford is quiet, intentional, and trusting—and that trust changes you.
My first semester at Haverford was amazing. The people are great, the professors are amazing and incredibly accessible, and my experience on an athletic team has been very good. Highly recommend!

I graduated in 2002. I had a good experience overall. One thing that college provided me was a good place for me to question/undo a lot of the notions of traditional success that I learned as a kid. I think I’ve found a much more fulfilling and authentic life because of this work. Haverford offered the kind of classes and interactions that I needed at that time in my life.

Haverford was the best four years of my life. The focus on student agency, student organizations and clubs, and the collaborative nature of the school made my experience what it was. The people that I met at Haverford were kind, friendly, curious, and so smart. I felt supported academically, socially, and extracurricularly. If you are a little quirky, curious, and interested in a tight-knit community - Haverford could be a great fit.

I can confidently say that Haverford has been 4 of the best years of my life. If you're looking for a college that has a strong, caring community, a robust alumni network, and a beautiful campus, I cannot recommend Haverford enough. The students here foster a collaborative academic environment that is unique and refreshing. Sure, there could be more parties, but who goes to a small liberal arts college to party? Haverford has incredible traditions, the best profs, and really cares about its students.

Haverford was amazing—small classes, awesome professors that have a 1:1 relationship with you / care for your research, and a tight-knit community and alumni network after college. Made great friends and grew in ways I never expected.

Haverford was a disappointing experience. For as much as it touts its inclusive environment and tightly knit community, there were many including myself that felt out of place socially and were dead set on transferring. That's absolutely not to say that there aren't some great people here, but the general feel of being ignored by many of your classmates for no reason despite trying to be friendly is a difficult one to take. I had a uniquely unfortunate experience in meeting some professors that also had atrociously bad attitudes/teaching (although I would say this doesn't apply to everyone). Drinking is almost a must to feel truly included here, and honestly, if more people let down their barriers and were their genuine, fun-loving selves like when they're completely drunk, perhaps the experience would have been more enjoyable. On a positive note, the campus is pretty and the Haverford name carries well for some graduate schools. The genuine experience, however? A letdown.
Horrible jew-hating admin; refuse to address safety concerns of jewish students and those who disagree with the anti-jewish sentiment festering here.

Great experience here! There are all sorts of different people here, and there's space for everyone to find their people. Everyone encourages each other to learn a lot and engage with the community deeply.

Small classes and community, very safe campus. There is lots of opportunity to build personal connections with professors, and get individual attention. Most people are smart, motivated, and generally courteous of others. The food options on and off-campus are bad though.

The Bi-Co and Tri-Co connections with Bryn Mawr and Swarthmore allow for more diverse course offerings while keeping a small college atmosphere. It is easy to find extracurricular opportunities. The school’s administration, however, is a little less progressive than student activist groups would like it to be, and they do not value student opinion and agency to the extent that I was led to believe.

Our college is a great college and academically fantastic. Although there are some problem in campus, housing and campus food and the res life is pretty mean. The food is pretty sad, but everything else is pretty good.

ai_summary: Hillsdale College provides a rigorous liberal arts education that emphasizes classical texts and western heritage. Students frequently comment on the strong academic rigor , describing it as challenging yet rewarding , often requiring long hours of study. The faculty is highly praised for their approachability and investment in students' personal and academic success. The campus fosters a tight-knit community where lasting friendships are formed. Although student life is vibrant with activities organized by various clubs and Greek life, some reviews note a perception of limited diversity in thought and demographics. The location is rural, which some find limiting in terms of entertainment options, but others appreciate the sense of safety and camaraderie it fosters. While the college is known for its conservative values, many students find their experience fulfilling, provided they align with the school's ethos. Overall, Hillsdale College is recognized as a unique institution that offers a distinctive educational experience centered around personal growth and intellectual development.

reviews.text: Hillsdale College is amazing in every way, and I cannot wait to be a college freshman as of the Fall Semester 2026.

Hillsdale has been absolutely amazing. From the classes to the student events, I have enjoyed every bit of it so far! It's a little annoying with a lot of construction on campus at the moment, but it will be beautiful upon completion!

save your money. So slanted toward the Fascist/Conservative point of view they cannot be taken seriously. I doubt many employers would consider this a viable degree. Shop around, find a more widely accepted school.

Hillsdale College was my biggest regret in my college experience. They have conservative, antiquated values, stereotyping of women, limiting intellectual discussion and free thought. I do not recommend.

A beautiful campus full of rich history. Professors care for students and are involved in there sucess. The people on campus to the locals make it a friendly, caring environment; a home away from home.

I went to Hillsdale College during the early 2010's and I had the absolute best experience possible. The classes were far more challenging than high school, but the level of students, even most of the athletes honestly as well, were so high. Very common for the professors to be some of the best in their respective fields and I felt as though I would have done myself a disservice going to undergraduate school anywhere else. I ended up loving the small town of Hillsdale the school was settled in, and being able to walk around the entire campus quickly from one class, to lunch, to the sports complex area was very convenient.
The campus is small but very well maintained and the change of seasons there offered a wholly different feel to the campus every time of year. Over all, I think anyone considering Hillsdale should give it real consideration. I grew to love the campus, it's staff, students, and faculty more and more every year I attended and still think very fondly of my time there.

Hillsdale is great, except for three key areas: 1. There are professors that are unnecessarily difficult and will torpedo your GPA if you take them and 2. The social scene lacks because you end up seeing the same 30-40 at the same parties every weekend and lastly 3. The college and surrounding area get really grey and depressing from November to March. Other than those three things its wonderful.

Hillsdale is an incredible school. The professors want you to succeed, the campus is beautiful (although it is currently under construction), the students and friendly and nice, and you will grow immensely. However, that growth stems from extreme challenge. If you are not looking to be pushed and challenged to your max in every way, Hillsdale is not for you. I would definitely recommend the school to anyone who is looking for an education that will actually develop and shape you, rather than just achieve a piece of paper stating completion.
Gross and clearly Republican. Sends surveys which a) ask 3 questions in 1 and and force you to answer to confirm their Republican agenda, and b) are uneducated.

Hillsdale College presents itself as a bastion of classical education and free thought, but in reality, it operates with a rigid ideological agenda that prioritizes conservative politics over genuine academic inquiry. While the college prides itself on refusing federal funding, this decision has led to a lack of financial aid options for many students, making it inaccessible to those who aren’t wealthy or willing to accept a heavy financial burden. The administration and curriculum push a one-sided worldview, discouraging dissenting perspectives and fostering an insular campus culture. Diversity—both in thought and demographics—is notably lacking, as the school has a reputation for being unwelcoming to those who do not fit its ideological mold. Faculty and leadership openly align with partisan politics, making the institution feel more like a training ground for ideological conformity than a place for well-rounded intellectual growth.

I’m a sophomore who chose to attend Hillsdale without a campus visit. I go here because of the program. I wasn’t overly concerned with the food or the dorm amenities. I was ranked high in my diverse and private high school near a major city. If you are not a top high school student who enjoys a good academic challenge I would imagine you wouldn’t be happy here. I love it. It is very hard, demanding and worthwhile. The students are largely very high quality with a strong family background. Many students attend some type of church or worship each week. I am on a premed track and the science courses are practical based and very difficult which I love. My professors have been great and I try to visit office hours to get to know them better. The school has a heavy history and English major population. The school is all I hoped for in a college academically and socially so far.

We are receiving a rigorous and thorough liberal arts education with a strong emphasis on western heritage and the classics. The professors are excellent and accessible. You will live their motto “ Strength rejoices in the challenge”. The students are genuine and the friends you wish every human could have. It is in rural Michigan and I was worried there would be nothing to do, however, the student activities board and clubs offer a multitude of things to do every week and you’re not far from bigger cities. I highly recommend this college and have zero regrets attending. It is more diverse in thought than many will have you believe.

Hillsdale College is an excellent liberal arts college and especially great for those interested in studying the humanities from a perspective that pays proper recognition to the western tradition. Many professors are highly respected in their fields. The general rigor of the core curriculum and many courses can be daunting, but it's manageable with appropriate diligence and time-management. The value of a Hillsdale education depends largely on what you make of it. Striking a balance between study and socializing is a crucial skill that will take some time to master at Hillsdale, but one that is worth aiming to perfect as the relationships and communities you choose to involve yourself in can comprise a large portion of your moral growth and intellectual development. The "work hard, play hard" spirit and off-campus shenanigans have sadly been on a gradual decline due to a narrowing acceptance rate and administrative crackdown. Greek life can be your friend. Again, strike a balance.

Started taking an online course about Economics. Within the first lesson, the right wing libertarian agenda was clear. They don't even attempt to put forth an objective curriculum, it was all very much opinion based.

The free history courses that Hillsdale College provides online were very beneficial to me. I learned so much about the foundations of America as a nation and what it means to be a citizen in today’s world and culture. The professors were all very kind people on the video lectures and were available to call if I had any further questions. Even though I was not a campus student, Hillsdale provided everything I could possibly need for my education.

ai_summary: Hobart and William Smith Colleges offers a close-knit academic environment with small class sizes where professors are highly engaged and supportive of students, fostering strong relationships and personal growth. The campus boasts beautiful surroundings on Seneca Lake, contributing to a vibrant community atmosphere . Many students appreciate the liberal arts curriculum that encourages interdisciplinary exploration , although some express a desire for a greater diversity of course offerings and improvements in student services, especially relating to mental health and dining options. The social scene can be limiting, often characterized by cliquishness or a focus on athletics, with nightlife described as less than lively, particularly in the context of a small town like Geneva. While career services and networking opportunities are frequently praised, concerns exist about the administration's responsiveness to student needs. Overall, the experience is marked by a balance of academic rigor , community engagement, and the challenges posed by campus culture and location.

reviews.text: HWS was a great experience. The departments I studied in had strong faculty leading their fields and were well-connected. I was able to and was encouraged to pursue multiple interests. The study abroad office was incredibly helpful and had many different types of programs around the world for different lengths of time that I was able to take advantage of.

I loved being a student at HWS—I feel like I got the best balance of academics, athletics, and social. I had lots of individual support and attention from professors, and I felt like they were willing to go out of their way to help me make my dreams a reality. Everything felt very individualized—you're able to get that level of attention at such a small school. And HWS felt like a community—everyone knows everyone, for better or for worse! Despite it's small size, students at HWS know how to have a good time. There might only be one party on a Saturday night, but the ENTIRE campus is at that party. The entire campus wants to see you succeed, and is going to help you do so.

I have overall greatly enjoyed my experience at Hobart and William Smith. I am a student-athlete here and my resources here as a division three athlete are fantastic, and significantly better than most of my friends at other schools. Everyone here is super nice, and the professors are very supportive of everyone.

ai_summary: Hope College offers a tight-knit and supportive community where many students appreciate the close relationships they form with professors and peers. Reviewers often note the quality of education , highlighting engaging classes , personalized attention from faculty, and ample academic resources, such as networking opportunities and research experiences. Student life is described as welcoming, with plenty of activities, access to downtown Holland, and proximity to the beach for leisure. However, some students mention a lack of diversity and a feeling of isolation, particularly for those from different backgrounds or those who do not align with the predominant culture. Dining options are frequently criticized, and while athletic programs and campus activities are valued, many note that the social scene may not be as vibrant as larger universities. Overall, while Hope College has its strengths in community and academics, experiences can vary widely based on personal background and expectations.

reviews.text: I loved Hope College; I found my experiences there to be some of the best of my life, not just because of the time frame or the people, but from the academics and the campus. The campus is beautiful in every season, and it's close to the beach, which is nice. The academics were good, more rigorous than I expected due to the school's high acceptance rate. I studied Pre-law and found almost every professor to be not only a good teacher but also a seemingly good person to want to learn from. Although there were professors who were disorganized, they were still engaging and knowledgeable.

Hope college is a good college, they invest back in the students, and each student feels seen and heard there. Small class sizes mean a chance to know your professors. Professors care for more than just their research and invest in the students and classes. You will get to know a lot of people who you will see everyday as you walk around. Uptown is very nice and walkable. Unsafe situations are unheard of and campus safety is quick and prepared to respond appropriately. Lots of churches nearby with lot of opportunity for faith to be grown. Research is really open to anyone that truly wants it and academic opportunities and extracurriculars are readily available. Lots of guest speakers and events go on and are free for students.
I love Hope College! I love the small town vibe and the small college feel. The professors are extremely personable and so helpful. I would highly recommend Hope to anyone!
It's been good so far! I've been very successful with meeting new people and making friends. The professors are all very friendly and accessible, and all the faculty are very helpful!

Truly an enjoyable experience, I've had no real complaints. The only minor gripe I've had with the school is that some of the professors aren't exactly the most available, but the reasons why are understandable.

I love Hope for the academic and social opportunities it has given me, and for the amazing professors I have had throughout my time at here. Classes are rigorous yet interesting, and really push me to be the best student I possible can, while there are many clubs and groups that have formed my overall experience as a college student. Work opportunities on campus have also been amazing, and it's very easy to get involved on campus in a variety of ways. Hope could work not only on gaining more diversity, but also supporting it - it's one thing to get students of varying backgrounds into the college, and it's quite another to keep students there and help them feel genuinely wanted.

I am a TCK (Third Culture Kid) grew up overseas. I am impressed with how well Hope College takes care of their international students and TCK. From the beginning with the international/TCK students orientation; to organise activities for us to connect with each other; to arranging grocery shopping trip for us; to connect us with a local family… the school takes care of us well.
The school campus is beautiful and with a great layout. I can easily reach any corner of the campus on foot, or more efficiently with a bike. I also feel very safe on campus. I enjoy the smaller size class very much which give students a lot more opportunity to connect with their professors and foster continuous dialogue.
We have a very young and energetic president who is friendly and love to connect with students as well.
Most importantly I love the fact that Christian values is integrated into all area of our campus life.
I love Hope so much! Hope College sticks true to their christain aspirations, welcoming, transformational, and faithful.

Hope college feels like an extension of home. From day one they welcome you in with enthusiasm Professors and administrators have gone above and beyond to help with support I’ve needed. So thankful I chose Hope College
Absolutely beautiful campus with great staff and students. Everyone is so friendly and helpful. The campus looks amazing like a postcard.

I love the campus environment that wants students to ask hard questions and gives us space to search. I would love to see a emphasis on civil discourse and discussion, because we have a good basis by which to facilitate it.

I enjoyed my time on campus and the spaces that I chose to be in. The professors of color do their best and support the activities of the students of color. This helped and showed that there were others who could relate to me and my friends. I enjoyed the surrounding others with walks and skateboarding whenever I was free. I would love there to be more diversity, more spaces for students of color, more opportunities to connect with the board of trustees.

I am a rising junior at Hope College, and I've loved everyone I've met on campus. It has so much to offer, from student organizations to sports and academics. I feel safe and encouraged by the staff, and every teacher I've had has been willing to help me with whatever I need. I honestly have no major complaints about Hope College.

Hope College has been an amazing experience for me. I have received so many amazing opportunities as a student. Since it is a relatively small college, it has allowed me to connect with most of my professors.

Overall haven't been super impressed with Hope, supposed to have price locked tuition and instead have dealt with price hikes, dorms have lots of mold, food makes me sick frequently and it feels a lot like a bait and switch from what the tours and promises were prior to actual enrollment. The rules are strict and professors are a pain about missing class despite sickness or family emergencies which feels like i'm back in high school but with a lot more alcohol, keeping in mind this is on a supposedly dry campus. If I could go back I would probably choose a different college but I do like the people and have found a good community there, but probably wouldn't recommend it for incoming freshmen.

ai_summary: Illinois College is often praised for its welcoming and tight-knit community , where the small campus size allows for meaningful relationships between students and professors. Many reviews highlight the dedication of faculty, who are described as caring and willing to assist students both academically and personally. However, there are some concerns regarding administration's handling of student welfare, particularly in relation to cuts in certain academic programs and overall communication. The college offers a diverse atmosphere and a variety of extracurricular activities, though some students express dissatisfaction with campus food options and limited social life due to the small town setting. Tuition is noted as a concern for some, although financial aid options are available. Overall, Illinois College provides a supportive environment for students, especially those looking for a smaller college experience with close faculty interaction.

reviews.text: I love it here at IC. I have truly found my people. Our professors are absolutely amazing and I feel seen at all times. At IC youre cared for and helped to get your degree.

My experience at Illinois College was great. There are a ton of opportunities for experience outside the classroom, such as internships and traveling aboard. IC also provides a lot of resources for students whether social, academic, or for your well-being. IC is a great place for connections and to feel seen as a student.

Illinois College is like a home away from home. Everyone on campus makes you feel so included in all type of different events. There is always something to do on the weekends from sporting events, different tournaments ran by student organization, to art and craft nights.

I have been a student at Illinois College for two years now. Attending Illinois College was my first experience of the Midwest and overall a big change which can be scary. As a freshman, I quickly felt right at home. From being involved in athletics and on campus jobs, I was surrounded by a new family. At Illinois College, the small class sizes allow you to have a connection with your professors who are always available to help. At Illinois College there is something for everyone!

Illinois college has been a wonderful experience so far for me ranging from campus life to professors and even resources. If your going through anything or struggling thetr are plenty of resources for anything and anyone.

I love IC it become my home more and more everyday. The professors here on campus want you to succeed as much as possible. They reach out if they think you might need a little they want you to come talk to them to help you suceed as much as you can. I have met some of my best friends here and couldnt imagine goign somewhere else.

Going to Illinois College has been one of the best decisions I could have made. The different extracurriculars, organizations and clubs I was able to join gave me a sense of belonging and made it easy to be myself here on campus. I love the campus environment and that you really get to know everyone really well and it doesn't feel as if we are just a bunch of students going to school together but you can really feel the love and supportiveness on our campus. I love how our campus comes together to support our athletics and show that even though we are a small school we have such an endless amount of opportunities here at Illinois College in our future endeavors and careers.

I love Illinois College because it has given me so many opportunities to be successful in my career. They have opportunities like academic coaches that allow help with study strategies and how to be successful in college. The athletic events here are so much fun and the community is great and extremally supportive of IC. I would recommend this school to everyone!

Going to Illinois College has been the best decision for both my personal life and professional career! Everyone is so friendly, the professors are amazing and love to interact with students, and there's so many opportunities to get involved and build a community!

Illinois College has become my home away from home! I absolutely love the community and the warming space this place brings. I have met some of my best friends here. Along with many memories and friendships that will last a life time.

Overall the college itself and the campus itself is very comforting and safe. When walking onto campus, no matter who you are, it's very homey. Not to mention the President of the school is well known and extremely personable. She takes time to understand and know each student. What the college stands for is shown within its professors and students.

Illinois College is great, but there is a lack of diversity. The surrounding town of Jacksonville is also unsafe for diversity due to the residents beliefs. Nevertheless, the school as a whole has great academics and athletics considering they are Division three.

It seems to be a great college with a lot of small problems between campus and professors. It also try's to hid that it is racist even though they are recruiting a wide range of students to go there it is mostly white students from out of Illinois.

They make you take a bunch of BS classes(the "Blueprint.") The blueprint includes psych 101, eng 101, 2 semesters of foreign language, a "literacy and information" course, a "freshman seminar," and cultural literacy courses. In addition to these, you have to sit through 20 convocations that are basically DEI seminars. Fieldhouse is not properly taken care of. I had a fieldhouse at my high school and they actually used air filtration and cleaned it with a carpet extractor to keep it nice, but the fieldhouse here smells weird and there's always visible dirt and crap on the floor. It's also not properly ventilated and it's easy to become overheated during a workout. The hot tub and sauna are not even hot and the pool is unsanitary.Bad facilities. The cafeteria has limited hours and a limited rotation of meal options. The dorm is a shoebox. The "literary societies" are the definition of "peaked in high school." It seems like they bully/exclude people who make them feel insecure.
I love the professors here and the community makes me feel at home. There are so many resources available for the students which helps a ton.

ai_summary: Ithaca College strikes a balance between being a medium-sized school and offering a vibrant college town atmosphere , which many students appreciate for its accessibility and opportunities. The academic experience is often highlighted positively, with small class sizes allowing for meaningful connections with professors , particularly in creative fields such as music, theater, and health programs. However, there are notable criticisms regarding the campus facilities, dining options, and financial management; many students feel the tuition does not correspond to the quality of services provided, especially concerning the aging campus infrastructure and inconsistent food quality. While the community is described as friendly and inclusive, issues with diversity and support for students with disabilities have been mentioned. Overall, students report a mixture of satisfaction and frustration, with a strong emphasis on the quality of education offered versus the limitations of the college's administration and resources.

reviews.text: Don’t do it! Too much money! Go to a star school. The value of an education is not worth this hefty expense. You will be paying back college loans forever. Go anywhere else

The best thing about Ithaca College (IC) are the caring professors and the robust academic classes. The professors are supportive. I find the classes challenging and professors expect high quality work from us.
The food is decent and serve a variety of foods. They offer a healthy salad bar and vegetarian options. IC has recently changed food vendor. Two main dining halls- Terrace and Campus Center dining hall.
Housing- They are working on this and currently renovating the Lower Quads and Upper Quads. The Lower and Upper Quads are older buildings and bathrooms are shared, but the rooms are larger than the Towers.
No party scene or Greek life. Best way to make friends is to join clubs and initiate conversations. IC needs to improve on creating more social spaces and activities for students to connect.
IC is dealing with a budget deficit and cutting back on things. You can read the school's online paper- The Ithacan - to learn more.

My overall experience at Ithaca College was great. Classes are small and professors truly care about the success of their students. The students in the Park School of Communications were largely dedicated to their work and were some of the hardest working people I have ever met. The school makes a strong effort to help students adjust, but if you are starting in the spring semester some organizations do not accept new students until fall. Campus food is okay. Housing is generally good, but students with accessibility concerns should speak to the housing department early, as not all buildings are ADA compliant.

A really awesome local band scene, cool surrounding natural areas including a really fleshed out and well maintained natural lands on campus, and strong engaging professors. I'm a Sociology student with minors in Art, ENVS, and Religious Studies and I could not be happier with the programs and professors I've gotten the pleasure of being around. Just by being engaged I have been given so many opportunities funded my departments, chances to display my work and research, book they knew I needed to read, jobs within the school, and recommendations to other staff. I have never had a professor I didn't believe was genuinely brilliant and attentive and every class I've been in has been intimate enough to feel like community. Ithaca does have it's flaws, particularly it's failures to properly allocate funds to the places that need it. I love the departments I'm about of and I wish such capable staff received better funding.

Before we start I have a degree in Television Radio.
I usually don't write reviews however as a previous reviewer stated “what was it all for” is the same thing I have been asking myself. To be honest I did not enjoy my time at Ithaca.
I feel like the community of Ithaca was a very if you don't succeed its your fault community. What I mean by that is if you had a negative experience you must have not put any effort in. I graduated with a cum laude in Television Radio. I did ICTV club, some other clubs and went to a ton of campus events. So I did put effort in, however socially it felt like it was never reciprocated by anyone. Impossible to break into friend groups and cliquey clubs.
Another issue was that networking was pushed but no one seemed open to it again no reciprocity.
I would at the very least not recommend getting a major in Television Radio or film at Ithaca. Considering networking is very important in theses industries and you will not be in LA, NY or Georgia.

Ithaca College has been a great experience so far for me personally. I've met tons of amazing people there and I love the beautiful surroundings of Upstate New York. I feel very much at home and comfortable there, which is important to me considering my actual home in Massachusetts is 8 hours away driving. I have found classes and activities and clubs and a work-study job that all fit my interests and personality just right. Freshman year was a bit of an adjustment for sure, but I couldn't be happier in my second year. I've decided to major in Environmental Studies, which is a strong reflection of my recent jobs in farming and conservation work. I plan on studying abroad next year as well. I'm very excited to see where the next few years take me at Ithaca. I highly recommend this school.

Beautiful area. Great local events. The one star is for the area. God awful school. I'm hoping people see this, because these people do not care about their students. They tell you they do, but I have never been so mistreated by an institution in my life. The president and vice president are taking out a budget deficit on students by stripping quality of life bit by bit and abusing student staff. No sympathy for lower income students. HORRIBLE to transfer to. Inaccessible layout. Tolerant of racism outside of POC run spaces. Do not come here. Just don't.

They consider IC to be an individualized experience because of the bonds you create with your professor. That is the only thing you will get at this college if you are not here for athletics. There is no reason to be here because you can't even do the work assigned to you because the wifi never works. I have never heard of someone who goes to this college and doesn't have stomach issues. Not only that but about 86% of the population is clinically depressed, when you come here expect to join the bunch.

My college expirience so far has been very cool. I met a lot of new people, I’ve learned a lot about this campus. The only problem is I just don’t feel that much at home here yet I still have to find my groove

Horrible WiFi. Horrible food. Horrible campus life. Too expensive for them to be taking away convenient features from the students year by year. Use their money not to bring out of date features up to date, or get better quality dining hall food, but to deck out le Jerome Cornish’s house and bulletproof her car. Parking options are horrible, the parking permit is daylight robbery. Don’t come here there are several better options.
Amazing area to see scenery, great downtown. Classes seem well organized so far and professors are open and helpful
A beautiful place that includes amazing diversity which will make any student feel at home there. You definitely will be able to find a group there.

My experience in the science programs have been amazing. The professors really care and take time explaining in office hours. There is always an opportunity for extra credit and bringing up your grade as long as you are willing to do the extra work. I truly have no complaints about my academic experiences. In terms of daily living, lower quad and upper quads are conveniently located by classes and dining halls. Any problems with housing were the normal ones you'd expect due to living with new people, never from any faculty mistakes or unprofessionalism from my experience. Though the school is a PWI, it is a very welcoming and inclusive school for all genders, sexualities, and races. There are various clubs and activities on campus like the Asian American Association, Black Student Union, Caribbean Student Union and so much more to feel included and represented in school. The only thing I'd like to see changed are more food options in one of the main dining halls, Campus dining.
I had a great freshman year. I love my program (Sound Recording Technology)! I love the people in it and the professors. I always felt supported and seen.

As a student-athlete at Ithaca College, I had a college experience that not many others get to experience. I arrived on campus with a group of built-in friends, and when I learned I might have to transfer, that was the thing I knew I would miss the most. While Ithaca College may give you food poisoning or snow so bad the roads aren't safe, you will always find your people here, and that's something I'm not ready to say goodbye to.

ai_summary: Juniata College is known for its customizable academic programs , allowing students to create their own majors and engaging in hands-on learning through study abroad and research opportunities. The small class sizes foster close relationships with professors, who are often described as caring and supportive . Student life tends to be intimate and close-knit , with plenty of campus activities and traditions to participate in, though some students feel the social scene can be limited, particularly in the surrounding small town. While many appreciate the sense of community and the educational rigor , there are complaints about the quality of food, outdated facilities, and administrative issues. Concerns about safety, diversity, and the institution's response to student needs also emerge. Overall, Juniata offers a unique environment for students seeking a personalized education in a supportive community, but the college experience is variable depending on personal expectations and circumstances.

reviews.text: I loved my Juniata experience! To me, Juniata is a home away from home. The staff and faculty at Juniata are very supportive and welcoming. Professors nurture their students while pushing them to achieve their fullest potential. It's a place where people know you by name and always say hello, and where everyone belongs. There is always something happening on campus, ranging from traditions like Mountain Day to cultural dinners hosted by student-led organizations to coffee and donuts on the quad (sometimes with special guests, like the president's golden retrievers). Juniata taught me how to think critically while maintaining an open mind. Juniata is not just a college; it's a community that focuses on empathy and prioritizes curiosity and growth. If I had to do my college experience all over again, I would always choose Juniata College.

Juniata College is an excellent 4-year school. The academics here are great; the professors are super helpful and provide a superb learning experience. There are also great networking opportunities through the college for finding internships and preparing for a career after college. The freshmen dorms aren't the best, the bathrooms particularly, but the rooms themselves are nice.

At Juniata College, there’s a cozy environment. Because the school has a few students, there are actual bonds formed there, as opposed to university guidelines being followed. The warm climate is also brought about by the faculty members who nurture students. This is a very laid-back environment and is focused on having fun in the natural beauty of the Allegheny Mountains and the charming character of Huntingdon. Everything happens at a slower pace in this environment, with all social events taking place either outdoors around Raystown Lake or on a blanket spread out in the campus quad. One of the best examples of this easy-going environment is Mountain Day, which is an unscheduled holiday in which all classes are cancelled so that students can all go to the lake to enjoy a picnic and a nap.
As a possible incoming student i have loved the process of applying to Juniata, exploring programs and finding new paths to lead my future forward.

What I like most about Juniata is the community and the great many clubs that are available. I like how Juniata is situated in the mountains and that it's near the Flag Pole Hill trail system. If you have a car, there's some stunning trails within a 30-minute drive. I will say, there's not much to do in town. There is the Clifton movie theater, some great restaurants like OIP, and some fun events at Standing Stone Coffee, but that's about it. Many people complain about the food but even as a vegetarian, I find the food to be pretty good actually, and the free Pick 3 option - where you get a drink, a side and an entree - is great if you want to quickly grab some food between classes.

My first few days at Juniata have been wonderful. I already have several friends that I met during orientation and summer activities. There is plenty of opportunity to hang out with friends or to keep up with course work. Juniata is a diverse college, with many of the students being from different countries. So far, my experience at Juniata has been pleasant.

At Juniata, it's very easy to find your group of people because of the fact that it is such a small school. You can be involved in so many different clubs and non-academic activities while still keeping up with coursework because most of the professors care about both the emotional well-being of their students along with their academic success. As a student, I have found comfort in Juniata because of the acceptance of the students, the support of professors, and the surrounding town that provides good places to eat and go with friends.

My overall experience at Juniata College as a rising sophomore has been positive and enriching. The small class sizes have allowed for meaningful interactions with professors who are not only knowledgeable but also genuinely invested in students’ success. The tight-knit community has made it easier to form lasting friendships and feel a sense of belonging on campus. I’ve had opportunities to explore different academic interests thanks to the flexible Program of Emphasis system. The campus itself is beautiful and peaceful, which makes it a great environment for studying and personal growth. I’ve also enjoyed getting involved in clubs and events, which have helped me grow socially and professionally. While there have been challenges, especially adjusting to college life in the first year, the support systems available—like academic advising, counseling services, and peer mentors—have helped me manage them.

You hear so many stories about how their alumni end up returning to Huntingdon to live or Juniata to work, and how many met significant others there or their kids end up attending. That shows the connections people make to each other and the school.
Professors are also very invested in helping students find what they are meant to do. Career services gets to know student’s stories and will really try to find meaningful experiences to better prepare you for work after graduation so you land a job you really love.
Juniata students are part of a close knit community, and we all see each other as family. Everyone is kind and helpful

I did like that it was a small community, because the friends I made I can call real close friends. There may not be much to do around the area, but its fun if you make it fun. I will say academic life will get you prepared. The professors are nice, and because it is a small campus they get to know you and they will push you to succeed. However, one thing I would like to see is to continue to advocate for the Office of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion. The events sponsored by them are to help advocate for underrepresented groups, and I want them to continue as much as possible.

Students are super welcoming and athletic teams really support each other even during their own seasons which is nice. The gluten free options are much better than other campuses I’ve visited. Juniata has so many awesome traditions which really make it unique, so if you visit make sure you ask all about them when the tour hits the main dining room. A bunch are listed on the mural when you walk in the building.
The campus and town are beautiful, professors are very nice and easy to reach. Typically smaller class sizes, nice dorms.

Juniata college is a very customizable school. Here, you can create your own major and study specifically what you are interested in. The faculty and staff is extremely accommodating , helpful, and available. Juniata as well is a very hands on school that encourages study abroad, work/study programs, and mentorship and research opportunities.

As a sophomore going to Juniata College, I would say that this campus is pretty unique. We have POE's (point of emphasis) instead of majors. There are a variety of POE's to choose from and students also have the ability to alter their course so it fits the best for themselves. The campus itself is very beautiful, especially in the fall when the leaves are changing. The campus is small so it is easy to get from building to building in short amounts of time. Our library was just renovated and is a great place to sit down and complete work. In general, Huntingdon is a town with very little to do. This is the main downside of the area, as there are barely any places to eat and we are about 45 minutes from larger areas like Altoona or State college. If the college could change in some way, I would suggest including more food options, as we do have the cafeteria and mini cafe called Fisher's, it would still be great to see more options on campus for students.

ai_summary: Kalamazoo College offers a close-knit , supportive environment characterized by small class sizes and dedicated professors who prioritize student success. Reviewers consistently highlight the unique K-Plan curriculum , which allows for personalized academic paths and encourages exploration. The college's vibrant campus community , with its diverse student body and opportunities for engagement in clubs and activities, enhances the college experience. While students appreciate the rich academic offerings and the rigorous coursework , some express concerns about the accessibility of campus facilities, food quality, and high tuition costs. Overall, Kalamazoo College is seen as a solid choice for those seeking a liberal arts education in a welcoming and intellectually stimulating atmosphere.

reviews.text: The academics are good and the small size of the college allows for you to get to know your professors. However, unless you have a car or take public transportation you can't do too much in the area. There is a cafe, a library, and a game room that keeps things interesting. The party scene is alright since there's lots of student athletes there and they like to celebrate after their games. Overall, it's a nice college where you get to know your fellow students and your professors.

this school has been fantastic. they have the best study abroad program in the country and it is so immersive. student life is actually very lively, join a club and don't be afraid to show up to things. the professors are extremely knowledgeable and fantastic resources as they truly take the time to get to know and support you.

I appreciated the ability to take many different classes as well as participating in campus activities and volunteer work for the community. A majority of the classes are fast-paced and a lot of work, but worth in if you like the challenge. A lot of students get accepted but we usually see a fair amount of freshmen drop out once they realize how much work is required/expected. I appreciated a majority of the professors, they truly care about you as a person and not just a student.

When I first came to Kalamazoo College, the experience was completely new for me. Coming from Austin, Texas, I had never really left the state, so moving so far was both relieving and empowering. It gave me a sense of freedom I hadn’t felt before and allowed me to grow as a person. Over time, I noticed areas where the school could improve. While there is diversity on campus, it doesn’t always foster true inclusivity. Diversity alone is not the same as supporting students of color or recognizing the cultural importance of our experiences. The school could do better in prioritizing the well-being of Black and brown students. Another issue is dining—the food is often poor and makes daily life harder than it should be. Still, I valued the chance to study abroad, which helped me connect with my identity and indigeneity in new ways. Overall, my time here has been both challenging and rewarding, but I believe the school must do more to support its students.

I really enjoy the professors and faculty. I was lucky enough to get set up with a great advisor who has made my time at Kalamazoo fun and worth so much. I would also recommend reaching out to your sources if you need help they are their to help you with any concerns you may have.

Kalamazoo College allows you to explore different options as they have different requirements that are made for you to be successful while they are different from a normal college or university, they have opportunities that you can't find anywhere else. They have great programs and opportunities that push you toward your goals whether it be studying abroad, internships, or being a part of the community Kalamazoo College wants for every student to succeed with it being a small school there is great community, and no one is ever forgotten. Your voice will be heard, as I was a shy kid coming here has allowed me to open up see that I am valued, I have had many internships and now will be studying abroad in Japan. While a small school doesn't have many options in majors and minors as well as the capability of what events or classes we may choose from which I believe can be changed. I believe that it allows you to figure out who you want to be and do in the world beyond college.

I liked that they have a diverse campus with lots of international students as well. Small school and a small campus so you get to know your classmates and professors very well. Great D3 athletic teams and lots of other fun campus activities. Kalamazoo as a town is a bit unsafe with homeless population and not much to do in the immediate area.

What I love about Kalamazoo College is that everyone who works here truly prioritizes student development and success, especially the professors. You might have some professors that are a little difficult to read/figure out, but they truly do want you to be successful. Even my least favorite professor I have had in my time here so far was very accommodating for office hours to make sure I was understanding the material and assignments. I am an athlete here and that has also truly been the best experience so far. I definitely would recommend this place if you want to participate in athletics. The food here is relatively pretty good, sometimes the cafeteria has bad days and is not great, but that is usually only on weekends (Sunday night dinners specifically) when most students are off campus, just be weary of that. Otherwise, there are good amounts of food options during the week. The dorms are also just fine, because the buildings are older not every room is the same size so be aware.

If you plan on majoring in anything STEM, do NOT go here. I cannot honestly recommend this school to anyone interested in the sciences. The quarter system is definitely appealing, but it is not helpful for someone who wants to take chemistry, physics, biology, etc. These classes contain a lot of content, and the quarter systems require the same amount of content that would be taught in a semester at other colleges to be smushed into a ten-week period. Obviously, this applies to all disciplines and not just the sciences, but in my experience, the struggle is much more present in STEM classes. The food and internet also suck. We literally didn't have wifi for almost five days during the first few weeks of this term. The biology department was completely overhauled, which screwed over anyone in my class. The only upside to this school is the ability to study abroad and that it is accessible regardless of degree. Besides that, nothing positive comes to mind. Overpriced school.

The campus is small so everything is pretty available in a short walk. My favorite place is Hicks center since that is where I usually hang out with friends and where some fun events happen throughout the trimester.

Kalamazoo is great to meet new people and the community that you build really helps you get a feel of how different everyone is. The housing at K is not the best but the RA's really make up for it because they are super kind and incredibly resourceful. It is a very fast paced school with 10 weeks of nonstop work but with only 3 classes per trimester is helps balance out your school life and your personal life. The professors are some of the kindest people you'll meet who are incredibly understanding and really do love their job. They consistently hold office hours to make sure that you are understanding the topics in which are covered in the class. They treat you as equals and as long as you are putting in effort they are willing to help you out when you need the help with.

Amazing professors that are passionate about their subjects and work hard to make their classes accessible for students. That being said. The administration are the most inept and condescending people I have met in my life. As a freshman I took a leave of one term because I had long COVID. Instead of accommodating this (during the height of COVID) they consistently suggested that I transfer to WMU. I received countless emails (and several letters to my parents home) warning that my financial aid would be cut off because I had not completed all the credits I signed up for. I had a 3.8 GPA but was given scholarship money and I honestly feel that they tried to push me out to save themselves the trouble. I had to do a 7 month appeal to be 'allowed' to stay. I know several people who were pushed out b/c of their own health issues. K pretends to be very diverse and welcoming, but is incredibly un-accommodating and intolerant of peoples medical//emotional needs. It is not worth the money.

Kalamazoo College is a very unique, small liberal arts school with plenty to offer. The K Plan allows for exploration within your academics prior to solidifying a path of study, assuring you receive full opportunities to discover what you truly enjoy. Class sizes are small which promise close relationships with professors who only want you to succeed. The campus is tucked next to another university and a quaint neighborhood which gives lots to do and see. Overall, K offers everything within a small campus!

I love Kalamazoo!!!! Best college ever. This college has the best professors and the best scenery and some of the best food. Ive always been a huge basketball fan and since Kalamazoo is in between Chicago and Detroit I can go watch games from time to time which is AMAZINGG.

I don’t go to Kalamazoo college yet. I am a high school student and I’m interested in going to Kalamazoo. My brother goes here, so know some people here and I enjoy campus. I’ve been up there a lot for football camps and I’ve talked to Coach Whitehead about playing on the football team this upcoming year with my brother. It would be a dream for me.

ai_summary: Kenyon College is often described as a beautiful , tight-knit community that offers a unique college experience in a serene, rural setting. Many students appreciate the rigorous academics and the dedication of professors who are accessible and genuinely invested in student success. The vibrant campus life includes numerous extracurricular activities, though some note that the social scene can feel cliquey or isolating, especially for those who don’t engage in typical party culture. While student support services, including accessibility resources, were cited as lacking by some, others found it a supportive environment ideal for intellectual growth. However, concerns about high tuition, housing quality, and administration responsiveness were prevalent, with several reviews highlighting frustrations regarding dining options and lack of diverse representation among students. Overall, while Kenyon offers a robust educational experience, students express mixed feelings about the balance of academic rigor and student life challenges.

reviews.text: The campus life is actually abhorrent. Everyone here is extremely cliquey, quirky, and very rich, which was just the worst possible combination for me. It feels downright impossible to have a conversation with a "normal" person. It's also very isolated, so if you don't find your friend group, then you're pretty much screwed and will have no life whatsoever outside of academics. I cannot stress enough just how much I can't wait to get the hell out of here.
Updated showers in dorms. More for students who choose not to participate in "party scene". Thankful for athletics. Academics challenging and engaging.

I graduated from Kenyon several years ago. It was my first choice school and I applied ED. In hindsight, I wish I had explored additional schools, as Kenyon did not live up to the hype. The dorms and food leave a lot to be desired, and the faculty is a mix. While there are some really good, committed faculty, others are substandard and should not be in this type of small school environment, where I bad teacher can ruin a department. I really enjoyed campus life and the friends I got to know. Now that I am out of school and comparing notes with co-workers and friends, I realize that I missed a lot by going to such a small, remote school.

In one word, Kenyon is thoughtful. Professors are experts in their field and truly care about their students, campus life and activities are thoughtfully curated, and the campus is carefully designed and maintained within the existing rural landscape. This is not a suitcase school; in fact, it's pretty easy to not leave Gambier for weeks. It's an isolated campus for sure, but if you're looking for somewhere where you can focus and fully immerse in a small community, this is the place. There's an incredible amount of distinctly different housing options, my only complaint is the one dining hall that does not have great hours and the options aren't always great.
Kenyon's (world-class & criminally underrated) physics program changed my life, though — incredibly well-funded, AMAZING professors, the support for women and minorities was incredible; my graduating class was 60% non-male and I had opportunities to travel and do real research — and I would not trade my experience for the world.

Overall, if I could take back my enrollment choices I'd probably not attend here. While the campus and faculty members are nice and the scholarships/career centers are incredible (which is the sole reason why I'm still enrolled) a lot of the students are incredibly catty, vindictive, and if you're not in their friend groups then they tend to be very icy towards you. The professors are also very nice and well-intentioned but sometimes they can be very scatterbrained when it comes to assignments and you will have to put in a lot of work outside class to maintain good grades. That being said, the career center is awesome and there are many campus jobs and research opportunities though some of them can be very competitive. Overall, I'd say if you're like me and you're a first generation, low-income neurodivergent student I likely wouldn't enroll here because campus culture tends to be hostile towards those demographics. But if none of these things apply to you then it may be well worth it.

Attending Kenyon for undergrad was truly the academic experience of a lifetime. The professors at Kenyon care so much about their students, teaching, and the community at large. After Kenyon, I attended a highly-regarded mid-sized school on the east coast, but the quality of the teaching, the courses, and the overall experience was not nearly at the caliber of Kenyon. I know a lot of prospective students have hesitations about attending a college in such a rural area or in the Midwest, but I can't stress enough how lively, supportive, intellectual, and exciting the Kenyon student body and community is. Being in this sort of small, inclusive, and supportive community in my late teens/early 20s helped me grow into the most confident and authentic version of myself, and I'm endlessly grateful that I had the chance to attend such an incredible school.

I can safely say that attending Kenyon College is one of the best decisions I have ever made. The academics are strong, the professors are wonderful (what they say is true - professors will invite you to their houses for a meal!), the campus is stunning, and the people there are just the best. I think the biggest thing to consider when thinking bout Kenyon is that in order to love it and thrive, you have to really want to be there, and be committed to making the most out of your time there.
The biggest concern students often raise about Kenyon is its location. This was my concern as well when applying. Someone told me once that you can always live in a Chicago, or a New York, or an LA, but you only get a Gambier once, and I believe that is true in every way. Knox County is small but it is vibrant and it's easy to explore and meet people from the area if you make the effort. Living rurally taught me so much.
I cannot recommend Kenyon enough, I miss it everyday. Go owls!

Very safe environment, can walk alone at night without any worries. Academics are 10/10, professors and staff are very intentional and teach with genuine care. Social life is strong; as someone coming from a bigger city I have never felt like I have had nothing to do. As for the party scene, there are things hosted every weekend, however it is usually greek life or athletic teams. Everyone is welcome, but you have to be in the know.

I really enjoyed the campus itself, it is incredibly beautiful, but there wasn't much to do within walking distance of the campus. I really felt that I needed to be able to get to Mount Vernon in order to do anything beyond eat or go to the bookstore or library. But the walking trails and nature on the campus itself were phenomenal, and I greatly enjoyed the scenery there.

I’m a proud alum and have returned for a few reunions. My fraternity kept me from feeling lonely and isolated and it’s sad to see fraternities have been abolished. Yes indeed a charming magical place, wonderful memories.

My experience was absolutely wonderful. Professors, even the toughest graders, truly cared and supported me. The student community was kind, ambitious, and delightfully eccentric. I joined a club and made amazing friends. With so many exciting courses in Environmental Studies and Biology, choosing was both fun and challenging. The campus is nestled in nature and offers peaceful trails and a river where we swam during the warmer months.
Laundry could get hectic on weekends since some dorms shared a room, but weekday loads were fine. Food options were healthy and diverse, and chefs were always helpful with people's dietary restrictions.
I was pushed to grow, met incredible people, and felt right at home in this small, off-the-beaten-path school. If I could do it all again, I would choose it in a heartbeat. Strong rec letters and my classes opened doors to great internships and my dream STEM grad program. Hope this helps someone!

To put it simply: if you are low-income or 'not rich' like most of the students here, I wouldn't consider Kenyon. The financial aid office is incredibly unhelpful and it is evident they aren't used to having students who cannot pay full tuition. The school has few donors, and as such they've been raising the tuition every year. I have been pulling out multiple loans during my freshman year just to afford this place.
The campus is INCREDIBLY isolated, and if you don't have a car- you're basically stuck. I had a friend who got an allergic reaction and needed to go to the hospital. She ended up having to pay for an ambulance because our health center closes at 4 pm and is closed on the weekends. The school did nothing to support the expenses.
In all - the academics here are very good, but if you're not the typical rich kid applicant, be wary of the extreme lack of support and resources for low-income kids.

I love Kenyon. It is such a beautiful campus, and there is a really great sense of community. Professors actually care to get to know their students and help them one-on-one, and there's a lot of great opportunities!
Going to Kenyon was one of the best decisions I have ever made. It is a quiet close knit community that really lets you throw yourself into your interests.
Kenyon was good but I wish there had been a little more science on campus because it seemed like it was all humanities

ai_summary: At Knox College, students frequently highlight the strong sense of community and personal connections with professors, which create a supportive and intimate learning environment . The small class sizes enable faculty to engage closely with students, fostering meaningful relationships that enhance the educational experience. Academically, many find the courses challenging yet rewarding , with diverse opportunities for involvement like study abroad and extracurricular activities.
