However, there are concerns about the campus climate, particularly relating to issues of diversity, student safety, and the administration's responsiveness to student issues. While some students enjoy the close-knit atmosphere and the unique opportunities it presents, others express dissatisfaction with the location and limitations of campus life, indicating that the social scene can be subdued in the small town of Galesburg. Additionally, while reviews recognize the quality of academics and faculty support, frustrations arise over housing conditions and limited resources for managing student health and well-being. Overall, Knox College presents a unique college experience characterized by community, academic rigor, and personal growth, but it grapples with challenges related to social culture and infrastructure.

reviews.text: Good school if you're still trying to figure out what you want to study. There's not much around town

It was a very fun and diverse college. Meeting new people from all types of backgrounds and interests makes communicating more fun. If you want to experience great academics and overall new friendships, then Knox is the place for you.

When they say Knox College is a small liberal arts college, they mean it. The town of Galesburg, sorry city, can be called cute but unless you have a car, there isn't a lot of places you can go outside of the college. As the seniors and juniors recommended, use Knox's study abroad program to get out of Galesburg, Illinois. The housing is expected for a college campus. Your roommate would be chosen almost at random based on a "buzzfeed quiz" as the staff call it. If you don't vibe with your roommate, like me, it is very hard to change rooms.

Knox is... okay. Their marketing and PR are incredible compared to what you actually get. I'm a transfer student here to study Art as I am getting significant financial aid, which was my deciding factor. In my opinion, their art department is hardly up to standard with other universities. There is basically one professor for all of Art History. Other friends of mine have been promised programs that don't exist, and/or their credits they came with were totally lost, prolonging their degree. Their programs are not equal, but they really push to make you think they are. Even the programs they do push aren't always great. If I were here studying something else, like environmental science, psych, or creative writing, it'd maybe be better. There is an odd sort of cult mentality that surrounds Knox, and they're predatory towards low-income and international students. I love the social aspects, as my dorm and suitemates are great, and the food is generally good. All depends on what you study.

I've learned so many different things while attending Knox College, and the biggest thing that I've learned is diversity. I've spent all of my life surrounded by people at my schools who look just like me but at Knox it's a different experience, I've learned to create new bonds and resources with people who don't look like me.

I enjoy the community feeling of the campus. All of the professors are open to help and are predominantly supportive. The housing could be better although renovations are already in place. Food could also be upgraded but they have many options.

My time at Knox College has been a rewarding academic experience. The professors are passionate and approachable, and the small class sizes really allow for deep discussions and personal attention. Academically, Knox is top-notch, I’ve felt challenged and supported throughout my journey.
Campus life is vibrant, with a strong sense of community. There’s always something happening, from student-run events to clubs and traditions that make campus feel alive. The food is not definitely it has enough variety to get by.
The one area that could really use work is the surrounding town. Galesburg doesn’t offer much in terms of entertainment, dining, or shopping, and without a car, it can feel isolating. I’d love to see more collaboration between the college and local businesses to give students better off-campus options.

Academics and professors are definitely too good. Students are fraustrated with campus life / housing because the system is so disorganized. Compared to other liberal arts college and even other big universities, campus food has improved so much that it should be rated higher.

I honestly don't mind the college that much, however, there are multiple factors that could be worked on. For example, the financial aid committee for International Students is HORRIBLE. You tell them you have financial changes and cannot afford the 24k you are paying and they tell you to solve that on your own. Moreover, the arts students that go here basically go here for free and just smoke weed, hang around all day, they don't care to really study and just come here because it's cheap. The professors are good, great class sizes and coursework is well manageable.

Knox College is a great example of a small liberal arts education. Most classes are less than 20 people and you will have every opportunity to contact your professors. The food is surprisingly satisfactory most of the time. I would like to see a larger art history department. Not the college to choose if you plan to leave campus as Galesburg is quite small.

Incoming freshman at knox here. Heard really good things about the college as my best friend studies there. The staff there has been very cooperative in solving any issues of mine and are very generous with their aid. Do check out knox parire fire at Instagram their athletic club is super active. Can't wait to go this fall.

It's mid....academically, socially, geographically.
It displays the usual mono-cultural of the "modern" American University. Anticipate that absolutely every intellectual exploration will be pushed through the racial identity rubric. So, it obviously became trite, boring, and unstimulating after a while. And I say that as a person of color. In fact, the only members of the student body who are challenged, and thus grow and get their tuition money's worth, are the conservative ones who grow a thick skin and the ability to dissect the fusillade of woke venom thrown their way.
(Some of)The faculty are barely more mature than the students. My friends and I would surreptitiously roll our eyes at their attempts to out-do each other in their white guilt upon our behalf. Fun at first, but it became repulsive after about a month of it.

I liked that Knox is such a close-knit community where my goals align with a lot of other students here. Everyone wants to do well in life and everyone wants to see others do well in life. It truly is such a supportive community where people genuinely care about one another, from staff to coaches to students.

Knox was my first choice school as I loved the diversity on campus. There are so many international students! Galesburg itself is a very small, quiet town with not a whole lot to do. The school makes up for this though by offering tons of fun activities and events on a weekly basis for students to attend. Because it is such a small school, everyone knows everyone. This is a good and bad thing, because if there is drama then everybody knows about it. The food is decent, with a pretty wide variety in what they offer on a daily basis. The campus coffee shop is also a nice perk, as well as the convenience store in one of the dormitories. At Knox you are required to live on campus all four years, and if you are in a dorm with no kitchen it can be inconvenient. That is the biggest change I would like to see at Knox, every dorm should be equipped with a kitchen for students to use.
I love the diversity. I also love how there are many social clubs and activities that I can get involved in. The academics are great as well, thus far.

ai_summary: Lafayette College offers a unique environment with its small campus, allowing for close relationships between students and professors, which many students appreciate. The academic rigor is highlighted, particularly in STEM fields, with faculty being supportive and accessible. However, the social life can be challenging, often revolving around Greek life and athletics, which some students feel leads to cliquishness and exclusion . Issues with campus facilities, especially dining options and housing conditions, were frequently noted as significant drawbacks . While there are many opportunities for involvement and personal growth, such as research and various clubs, some students feel that the college could improve in areas related to diversity and student services. Overall, while Lafayette provides a strong academic foundation, students should be prepared for a socially demanding environment and some infrastructural challenges.

reviews.text: It’s been overall good. There are lighting issues along with not enough washer and dryers for the dorms. The food plan is overall expensive for what they offer and the dorms leave a lot to be desired. Overall great academic wise.

I have loved my experience at Lafayette. Staff are incredibly helpful and want to see their students succeed, and the students are all kind and intelligent people. There is a diverse range of organizations and clubs that students can join to pursue their interests and make connections, as well as many opportunities on campus to give back through community service. The main issue I have is the food. The food services could be improved on, there could be more options, specifically for different allergies, as well as improving the quality of the food. Beyond that, I only have good things to say about Lafayette, I am loving my 4 years there and I know I made the right decision in my college search.

It was the perfect place to continue my education as a high achieving student who liked to be involved in seemingly unrelated things. I was able to participate in the theater community, study robotics engineering with undergraduate research, and compete in D1 athletics. Anything that their might be lacking such as the immediate area of things to do or lack on "Ivy prestige" is more than made up by the forever family community that you can make here from both your peers and professors alike.

As a current student at Lafayette College, I am proud to say that I have enjoyed my experiences on campus so far. From academics to student-life, I do not regret my decision of committing to this school. One enjoyable aspect of this school involves its classes and its class sizes. With most classes averaging around 20 students, I am able to connect with my peers and professors on a personal level; further boosting my education and involvement.
I loved my time at Lafayette, it was definitely the right school for me. I made friends that I still keep in touch with post college.

As a Pakistani female ECE student at Lafayette College, I thrive in hands-on labs, global learning, and a close-knit campus. I’m part of IEEE and the Investment Club, where innovation meets leadership and global ambition

Coming from Islamabad, I found a familiar warmth in Easton, both are green, calm, and welcoming. Being at Lafayette during its historic 200th anniversary celebrations this year has been truly special. My professors, especially in ECE and FYS on Global Justice, have shaped the way I think and see the world. Their personal attention made learning feel meaningful. As Pards enter their third century, I feel proud to be part of a community that values both tradition and bold ideas

I really feel like Lafayette is a hidden gem amongst college applicants. I had an amazing experience here, had excellent professors, and made life long friends (in and outside of greek life). I think the school has worked really hard to make campus life fun and provide many opportunities for involvement. Most of the administrators really care about the students and are committed to making your college experience the best that they can. If you are someone who likes a good balance between academics and your social life then Lafayette is a good fit for you. I always felt like we were a "work hard play hard" school. My friends and I would go the library to get all our work done together so that we could go out and have fun that night. Academics were always a priority!

I've loved my experience at Lafayette so far! The people are so nice: the students are such kind people, and the professors and faculty are so helpful and genuinely want to see you succeed! Social life has been great for me; definitely get involved in clubs and activities freshman year, and I would highly recommend rushing as a sophomore if you are interested in a party scene beyond off campus bars. The city of Easton is small, but it's quaint and there's some hidden gems to discover for sure. It's very walkable which is nice. The food on campus has definitely had its issues while I've been a student, but the college has done a fairly good job at listening to complaints and improving based upon the student feedback. Overall Lafayette is one of my favorite places on earth and I can't imagine having gone anywhere else!

Lafayette College is a beautiful, historical college with good academics and an eventful student life. Proximity to Easton is nice, especially when there are festivals and warm weather. Food could be much improved, but sometimes really good.

As a current Lafayette student, I am not just going to hype up my college because I go here, I truly love it at Lafayette College. The dining halls aren't the best and I wish they could have better food options, but the campus itself is incredible. Easton is beautiful and has incredible places to visit for whatever you want. The campus life is great and the athletics are super fun to watch and be apart of.
Awful, hated it, don't waste your money go somewhere else. You payin Maybach prices for a honda civic vibe. Personally, I didn't like anyone here, the academics were rather well.

Ate the caf food once — woke up talkin’ to God and a janitor named Earl. $87K tuition and they still feedin’ us mystery meat like it’s a side quest. My dorm got so much mildew, it got its own student ID. Dorm showers be so cold I started prayin’ in tongues every morning.

Loved it! If you are looking for a beautiful, lively campus with outstanding academics and a variety of resources to set you up for success after college, Lafayette is the place. It’s a small school, which means your professors actually know you personally and are more than willing to help and support you. The campus is historic and beautiful, but they are always making improvements to keep it safe and clean. The community is welcoming and there is a place for everyone at Laf. Truly no place like it, and I miss it everyday!

Campus food is atrocious and there is a mouse infestation in multiple buildings. Most students find mold on their food at least once in their time here. Getting food poisoning is also not a rare occurrence. Professors are all pretty great and kind people who love to help. The main academic buildings are in fairly good condition, especially the newer STEM and Econ buildings which have a ton of study space. I’m not active in the Greek system or party scene, but I’ve heard good things about it. Downtown Easton is a basic PA town, but it is quite quaint and has good food spots. Lafayette’s food is so bad it’s not even funny. They have disgusting dining halls and cafes/restaurants (etc.). The decor in Gilbert’s Cafe and Cinco’s (restaurant owned by Lafayette) is so ugly and affects the experience. They don’t have a real Starbucks or Chipotle or anything nearby. Gilbert’s serves Starbucks but it is usually gross and cold or stale. Tuition is now 87k and it is not worth it.

ai_summary: Lake Forest College has a mix of positive and negative experiences expressed in student reviews. Academically, many students appreciate the small class sizes and the accessibility of professors , noting their dedication and support for students. The college is situated in a picturesque area near Lake Michigan and not far from Chicago, which appeals to those seeking urban opportunities. However, several reviews highlight significant concerns about the campus facilities, including outdated housing with overcrowding issues and inconsistent dining options , often described as lacking quality and variety. The social scene seems limited, especially for non-athletes, with many finding the campus life too small and cliquish. The administrative support and student services receive mixed reviews, with some praising the helpfulness of faculty and staff, while others criticize inefficiencies within the administration. Overall, while the academic resources and personal connections with professors are often commended, the everyday student experience at Lake Forest College poses challenges that need careful consideration for prospective students.

reviews.text: If you’re like me and wanting to go to an out of state school near a huge city to get a unique experience, don’t. Pick the big state school that’s more affordable. You can be a great person and still have many people dislike you. If there’s an opportunity to be in a big environment- take it. I consider myself a well rounded person and had a hard time connecting with others. Athletes are too cliquey, and internationals are too full of themselves. Hard for those in between to find their place. It’s very isolating and boring for many, and the town hates the college. Residence life SUCKS, the housing options are buns for a school with so much money. I got a great education, but definitely wish I chose a bigger school to find my people. It’s so expensive around here and not reasonable at all. Go have fun at a bigger school while maintaining a good education. It’s so important to be in a well rounded community and unfortunately lake forest is not that.

Was a student athlete at Lake Forest and decided to transfer out after only one semester. An unpleasant experience I had during the orientation week kept me from enjoying and liking the school. It is overall a good school with caring and knowledgeable professors and friendly students. The freshmen dorms desperately need some updates though.
My experience at Lake Forest College has been wonderful. The only thing I will recommend and regret not doing is talking and getting to know my peers.

It set me up very well for the future as it has a great career advancement center. The teachers also have a passion for teaching, which made learning easier. I played football at the school and we have good athletics all the way around, both women’s and men’s. I overall had a great experience at the college academically. If you are looking to party this is not the school for you.
The professors are great and they get to know you pretty well. Class options aren’t much if it’s not a major where a lot of students are in.
I loved my time at Lake Forest. The school is a community and the professors want to see you succeed. You get to know professors and classmates on a personal level.

If you are someone who will have to utilize disability services at all, DO NOT GO HERE! Unless you are a white man who is here on a sports scholarship truly the school does not care about you. The Disability services here are underfunded and the staff is belittling towards anyone who does not have a physical disability, or a disability you can "see". Anything you see about the school "priding itself on its diversity" is a load of bull. It's not worth the high price tag if you want to use any student services including the Health and Wellness Center. Appointments are hard to get, the care is subpar, and Residence Life is run by a staff of people who also do not give a flying fart about their students. Truly anything regarding student life is a ton of bureaucracy that will get you no where. Frankly I am not sure where my tuition dollars are going because they are definitely not helping Student Life.

The life in Lake Forest College is very fun. The scenery is beautiful, I have seen many different animals roaming around. The food is great and there are so many different spots you can go to, to get things from coffee to fruit shops. I don’t go to parties so I can’t say anything about them but I heard people are having some fun. The school is so diverse, there are so many different types of people here and everyone is so nice and helpful. There are many different things that lake forest has that I can keep going on and on about how great it is.

Even though I've only been at Lake Forest College for a month now, I can definitely see how professors do their best to help their students achieve, getting to meet with students from all over the world, and have fun activities that bring all the people together.

I am a commuter student at Lake Forest College, so some of what I experience might not be what my peers who live on campus do. Since coming into my last year, I think I have a pretty good understanding of how the school it is, and in my opinion, it is a great undergraduate college. Academically and as a community it is very welcoming and understanding. We get a lot of diverse students from all over, and local students like me, so it makes it a place where there are people from all over that you get to see and hear from each other. Since I'm on the pre-med track, I have been in touch with many STEM and science professionals here, and they have been a great help for my academic/future career, along with my advisors and the career advancement center that we have. Overall, even as a commuter student, I think it is pretty easy to get involved with the school and its activities if you want to. My only complaint is the limited parking, but it is a very beautiful campus that makes up for it.

I am going to give a brutally honest review, and no I am not exaggerating any of this. In all honesty, my experience has been quite horrible. Freshman year was awesome and since then the school has gone completely downhill. There are so many things wrong I don't know where to start. I have been required to take more diversity classes to obtain my business major than I have actual business courses, I don't know anything except how to follow the civil rights act. There is not NEARLY enough parking for students on campus, many of my friends skip class because there's not any parking spots within a mile. They are holding graduation on the dorms back lawn??? And SHUTTING DOWN a parking lot in order to do so. They suspend students that are suspected of violating the rule book without any evidence, and give the students nothing in return when they are found not responsible. The housing is EXTREMELY overcrowded, they have started housing students in the LOUNGES. Not joking about any of this!

Lake Forest College is one of the greatest running scams in modern history. If you are not on full scholarship or near it, I would not advise attending this institution. The ROI is nowhere near worth the price of admission. The campus is rundown with subpar housing accommodations. 0 singles available to students, seniors are being placed in doubles and triples and even quads with little to no privacy. The food is decent but some days inedible. The parking restrictions which you pay $500+ for a pass ends up having you not have close access to your car every single weekend, including the entire last month. They’re making us seniors graduate on the lawn outside of the one nice dorm? Is this middle school? Maybe with better administrators this school would thrive but for now I’d spend your money elsewhere.

LFC was a great school to attend. The professors and academics were challenging but really gave me a such a well-rounded education. The location is beautiful right by the lake and a beach and downtown is very cute but generally expensive. There is a party scene but it is not much compared to larger schools.

My experience at Lake Forest College has been an up and down ride so far. I would first say that I have met a multitude of great people through the school and have made a great group of friends. The classes and academics are solid and most professors seem to care, although there are some classes and professors that are not of the standard you'd expect for the price you are paying. Furthermore, the social life is not great. I have a great group of friends but the most exciting thing to do on campus is sleep. The dorms are horrendous and your best living situation is a double room as a senior. Ridiculous. Athletics are okay, but there is no school spirit, no one goes to games except sometimes football, but it is hard to support a program run by those who lack character and cover for others but I digress. If you have a lot of scholarship and are not looking for a college experience, I would recommend LFC. Do not go if you are paying more than $1000 a semester.

This is a good institution for those looking to waste their money and waste 3-5 years of their life. The school has a quality location located just north of Chicago, and the internships student get (some through the help of the school, many not) are also solid, and lastly a high standard of safety just because the community is fantastic surrounding the college. Other than this, the college is subpar. The dining hall serves quality meals usually best on breakfast and worst at lunch. Dinner is hit or miss, but so is every cafeteria. The campus life and residence halls are god awful, something straight out of a scary summer camp movie. The residence life department as well is a joke. It basically comes down to: Is it better than no school? Maybe. Is it worth the price tag? Not even close. It should be looked into how much this school charges for the subpar facilities, education, and overall experience. I feel like I just wasted three years of my life.

ai_summary: Lawrence University offers a unique blend of strong academics and a close-knit community environment. Many reviews praise the commitment of professors, highlighting their accessibility and genuine interest in students' success, which is enhanced by small class sizes . The liberal arts focus allows for a diverse range of classes and the opportunity to design one's own major, appealing to students with varied interests. However, there are critiques regarding campus organization, and some feel the administration could improve in areas such as inclusivity and affordability. Student life includes an array of clubs and activities, but the general social scene may lack the vibrancy found at larger institutions. While the campus and surrounding area are generally viewed positively, concerns about food quality, housing, and the "busy culture" add complexity to the experience. Overall, Lawrence is seen as a nurturing space for motivated students who appreciate both academic rigor and community support.

reviews.text: Attending Lawrence University is the biggest regret of my life. I would have been better off not going to college at all. It is an ultra-competitive environment, which demotivates some students. I have no lasting friendships from this college. If you attend a reunion, there is so much pressure to contribute, that it brings up negative memories of the competitive environment, as each class announces at the convocation how much money they raised. There were only 3 or 4 professors there I liked, and I graduated from the school. If only I had gone to a large, state school, I would have been a lot happier, would have received a better education, and most importantly, would have had some level of mentorship, which was entirely lacking in my Lawrence University education.

Moving halfway across the country and attending Lawrence was the best decision I've ever made. I was challenged in all the right ways by empathetic, passionate, expert professors. I grew and learned so much here.
While I wasn't in the Conservatory, I chose Lawrence because I wanted to study STEM at a high level AND be around elite musicians. These are the some of BEST music ensembles in the nation.
There's a very intellectual vibe to LU, and it's a bit on the preppy side, but with incredibly thoughtful, caring students overall. Diversity has been improving over the past 10-15 years. Super LGBTQIA+ friendly.
They are investing in new buildings and majors which is very cool, but the new President has also been firing any staff or faculty that disagrees with her and forcing them to sign non-disclosure agreements to hide it all which is highly concerning. Institutions survive people though. It's an incredible school - no school is perfect, but you will definitely not regret going here.

Lawrence is for those who love small class sized and dedicated professors. there is not much in the way of any parties or night life aside from a few frat house parties if that is what you are into. they do award good aid and scholarships.

LU offers small sizes classes which allows you to connect with professor and classmates better. People are friendly and kind. You can expect to grow a lot from the connections you had with your peers. However, if you are into STEMs it might not be a good fit as there are very limited faculty and courses available. Humanities are fine and you can do independent study. It is a small campus but in general you got everything you need here.

Lawrence University is a tight-knit community for anyone wants a friendly exclusive campus. The campus naturally make you bond strongly with your friends, roommates, professors and the Appleton community. With classes less than 30 people, you won't feel left out or ignore by your professors.

Lawrence University has great professors, but major issues with management, living conditions, and student safety. Dorms like Brokaw Hall suffer from mold, leaks, and even a spider infestation, making them unsafe. A Title IX lawsuit was filed alleging the university ignored sexual assault complaints and in other cases mishandled sensitive information, leaking victims’ reports. A stranger entered Trever Hall and secretly recorded students in the bathroom, and the university's response was inadequate. Campus security is inconsistent, with some officers friendly, but others harass students. Dining services, provided by Bon Appétit, are bland and repetitive, with limited healthy options. The university’s outdated curriculum, failure to address these concerns, and mishandling of student safety make it hard to recommend. Despite these issues, I made great friends and had supportive professors.

I'm a voice student in the con. I've enjoyed my classes a lot; I liked their first year studies class, even though I'm very glad I only had to do it for one term. as someone who likes trying new foods and doesn't have dietary restrictions, I actually really like the food here. I have felt supported and cared for by all my professors and my academic advisor; they really want you to feel flexible with scheduling your classes and commitments. The faculty are generally really cool and I love being around them. There's so much beautiful music to do and see all the time. I like Appleton a lot; there are so many cute shops to walk to next to campus. I feel like I made the right choice, but idk how I'm gonna pay it off tbh.

I enjoy my time here. I wish some aspects were different, but overall, this is a good university. There are engaged professors who facilitate good classes which is the most important factor in college for me.

I love how tight knit the campus community is. As the school is small, it becomes easier to create close connections through classes and clubs. Though this smallness can sometimes feel strange when compared to other schools, it is ultimately a plus.

I love the people and campus climate. The only bad part about campus is the dining scene. I have a gluten allergy, and they don't have much for me. I tend to make my own food, but I'm required to be on the meal plan. The housing is sufficient, they save the best dorms for freshmen and seniors. The campus is beautiful though. I'm also a Christian and despite the liberal views of the campus, we do have a Christian community club here. The professors are tough and grade like you're in grad school, but looking at the big picture it's worth it.
It is impossible to work with Housing. They will take your roommate away from you and ghost you over email.
Very strong academics, Great professors, a lot of opportunities to do. I totally recommend this school.

Lawrence is a really nice community in a pleasantly small town. The academics are challenging but the professors are overall great. They are caring and always willing to work with students on understanding. I'm a creative writing major and really like almost every professor I've had.
The social life is okay, not a party school and there are lots of campus-sponsored, non-party events available. I wish more students attended those, like game nights and movie nights. Food is pretty good for cafeteria food. I'm a vegetarian and they always have plenty of options for me. The town is really close and has plenty of places to hang out and get coffee but we have to take a bus to get to the mall. Not a lot of parking on campus if you have your own car. Overall if you think your people are here, they probably are. It's a unique and very diverse environment where everyone fits in.

Hello! My name is Rebecca Brojanac and I'm currently a freshman at Lawrence University in Appleton Wisconsin. From week one, I loved it here! The education at Lawrence is completely different from all the other schools that I had toured or had class observations in. Unlike a normal lecture hall, that can be packed with over 200 kids given the subject, Lawrence University values intimate connects with your peers and community members. Classes are no more 25 people, so you are very close with your classmates and your professor. In class, if you raise your hand to ask a question you will always be given an answer directly then and there by the professor. Often times, the school will give you opportunities to connect with the community members on day trips called 'EPIC group experiences'. My personal EPIC group experience was going thrifting around Appleton and Fox Cities. That day was really nice because the school gave each person in the group 15 dollars to spend on them!

Lawrence University sits on a very gorgeous part of town with wonderful sunrises/sunsets, amazing trails with beautiful sights but it will occasionally smell due to fertilizer mostly. The community is predominantly white though they make efforts to be inclusive and diverse with a a plethora of student-led clubs and organizations that help you find a place to fit in. There are also large communities of international students making cross-cultural learning both easy and friendly. For the most part people are friendly and you will be safe as long as you make smart decisions. The professors are also amazing and its a very large part in the school's success, they are friendly, incredibly informative, and due to small class sizes, they are able to connect with students more personally and help them more efficiently. You will form a deep bond with the professors and they will want for nothing but your success and will always encourage you.

ai_summary: Lewis & Clark College offers a unique college experience characterized by its beautiful campus , dedicated professors, and strong academic programs. Many students appreciate the small class sizes and the personal relationships they can develop with faculty, noting that professors are engaged and supportive . However, some reviews highlight challenges with the social scene, describing it as cliquey and sometimes isolating, making it difficult for students to form meaningful connections. While the location provides access to Portland and outdoor activities, students mention that the campus feels isolated at times, particularly for those without cars. The tuition costs are a common concern, as many find it expensive given the limited variety of majors and experiences available. The food quality also receives mixed reviews, with some students describing it as poor. Overall, while many students enjoy the academic environment and the aesthetic of the campus, there are concerns about community, pricing, and living conditions.

reviews.text: Student life is generally pretty cliquey. Class sizes, especially for higher-level classes, are small, and it’s easy to connect with professors. The campus is beautiful year-round.

My youngest child graduated from L&C in 2025. He started his studies unsure what he wanted to pursue. I was very impressed with how the professors supported and encouraged my child to explore. He developed so much as a young adult, gaining self confidence through his study abroad experience and his academic achievements. One of his professors, who knew my student was super interested in all things Frankenstein (and had done a ton of independent studies on this subject), asked him to lead the class discussion when the class was covering Frankenstein. This wasn't an assignment. He was the teacher for the day! My kid was so proud of himself to have been identified as knowledgeable on the topic and was asked to do this. This is just one example of many in which I heard my student talk about how amazing the professors are.
Lastly, I will add, that the location of Lewis & Clark is amazing. Safe, beautiful, and near enough to downtown to give students internship opportunities.

There is a very obvious divide between a lot of the varsity athletes/recruits and the other students, but the overall community tends to be very welcoming and nice. Surrounded by natural beauty but close enough access to downtown Portland is a very unique and exciting experience.

Your mileage will vary depending on which department your degree is with and how close you are to the Portland stereotype.
Do research on the professors in your department before attending, as you will likely be taking classes with them for all four years. Many professors here are well-connected in their field and truly care about your success, to the point where it outweighs the odd social life.
The students are cliquey, but I think it’s because the average student here is just much shyer than usual. Some students don’t know how to react if a stranger says hello to them. They just stare back at you. I noticed that the kids who dress very artsy Bay Area or Portland tend to form big friend groups and travel in packs everywhere. It’s like 2020 alt TikTok in real life. If that is you, this is your place.
I was able to find my own diverse non-alt friend group through uniting mutual friends. It’s harder than at other schools, but it’s not impossible. Being outgoing is a cheat code.

I attended L&C for two years. I had a tough time at this school; the student body is very, very cliquey. Which is so painfully ironic considering L&C advertises itself/its student population as an accepting place? I met a few great friends, but other than that, I didn't fit in here. That being said, the professors here are excellent and were one of my few positive experiences here. Overall, I had high hopes for L&C, but this was not the right place for me.

I love Lewis & Clark. The environment is so welcoming, and I have no regrets attending this school. I came in as a transfer student nervous to meet new people/experience new things, but it has all worked out! Met people who will be a part of my life for a long time.
I adored the professors and the different classes offered with an interdisciplinary approach and overlap. The food wasn't the best and it was not diverse.

I was raped week one. After 1.5 years of reporting process, he was suspended after admitting to raping me. The experience was a nightmare. The administration and faculty were awful about it. Then I studied abroad senior year. I was raped by my host brother. 4 other women in my program were raped by host brothers. The school did absolutely nothing about it. They gave me a hotline number and shut me down. Almost a decade later, I’m struggling to recover from this experience of Lewis and Clark. My mental health was destroyed. I tried to get support and was shut down. If I ever manage to feel more stable and actually make a live-able income, I will do all I can to hold them accountable. This school ruined my life.
The college is extremely segregated. There is little to no diversity and very social group divided. The professors and the landscape is probably the only thing going for it.

Food is ridiculously bad and often dangerous and you can't walk anywhere to buy lunch or dinner since the campus is isolated. You could drive into Sellwood or downtown Portland, but first years were not allowed cars on campus and parking is VERY limited. The bus goes to Sellwood on weekends only and takes inconvenient breaks. I did not make a single friend in my time here and I promise I tried. It is, indeed, very cliquey and hard to meet your people. Stupid expensive as well. My professors were kind and passionate but none of the classes I took (all non-STEM) were academically rigorous. I took classes in subjects I was interested in but was still bored because all of the classes were discussion based and surface level. Very "how does this make you feel?". I really wanted this school to be the one for me but instead of finding community and getting a degree, I doubled my Zoloft prescription and dropped out after one semester.

I love LC. I’m a junior here rn and this place is Portland embodied in a school. This school is full of very unique, wonderful humans. Students: mostly women, LOTS of trans/non-binary, seriously 85%+ of the school is LGBTQ+. Wonderfully accepting place of everyone who is a kind good hearted person. That being said, if u are a bigot, if u are mean, if u judge—stay tf out of our school. You will regret coming here if you’re not accepting of the unique backgrounds this campus has to offer. The abroad programs are fantastic, scholarships are solid (though the school is fairly underfunded). The food is nice though plenty still complain: extensive vegan options and limited entree stuff, but an outlandishly good dessert/bakery section for all 3 meals of the day. The campus is absolutely beautiful (ranked ~7th US). Party scene is great, and though no one pressures you to smoke, but there’s a LOT of weed on campus. Academics and dorms are both pretty solid. Overall just a great people/school!
I met great people at this school with a variety of interests. The study abroad programs are a great opportunity. I enjoyed some classes but was not challenged by all of them.

Never felt fully felt fulfilled in any part of the school everything was just mediocre. The teachers can be good but never amazing. It seems like the school is just chasing its own tail. The school is Never committed to the student success, always more about politics or the perception. The people are very cliquey and judgmental. You have to watch what you say and who you befriend or you will be judged. If you don’t absolutely fit in don’t go here

The college is very isolated from everything. Classes and professors aren’t great. They have a small selection of classes. I’ve known a number of people who’ve struggled to get the classes they need to graduate on time.

I came to Lewis and Clark based off of recommendations from many people saying it was a really good school overall. It has barley been one semester and I am already planning on transferring. The value for money is crazy bad. Classes here are very hard to get into, they are also very small (15-40 students), however, the professors are very nice and supportive, they definitely want you to succeed. There are 2 dinning halls here, one serves pizza and chicken while the other serves a different meal everyday. Both of the dinning hall's food is horrible: undercooked chicken, undercooked rice, salty sauces, low variety, and overall really bad. I have heard people throwing up in the bathrooms right outside the dinning hall many times. It's honestly kind of amazing how they are even allowed to cook and serve food here. The dorms suck, I was put in a quad at the last minute without notice, no air conditioning, no space, and you need an accommodation to get single rooms. Research before coming!!

ai_summary: Linfield University offers a small college experience with an intimate academic environment , which many students appreciate for its small class sizes and the personal attention from professors. Reviewers frequently mention the supportive faculty who are approachable and dedicated to student success, fostering a close-knit community feel . However, some students find campus life challenging, particularly regarding social integration and activities, citing a cliquish atmosphere and limited options for off-campus engagement. The beauty of the campus and its community spirit receive positive remarks, although there are criticisms about the quality of food and outdated facilities. Tuition costs and living expenses are considered high, and some reviews indicate concerns about the university's administration and focus on athletics over other programs. Overall, Linfield is seen as a place to grow personally and academically, but with a mix of ups and downs in the overall college experience.

reviews.text: Linfield University has a strong team of staffs and professors in all departments and offices that provides many supports to students. Over my years at Linfield, I was able to see staffs working hard for providing helps to students in varies forms. Able to connect with staffs, peers and professors closely is a comment fact here as our class size is small and concentrated. I can't think of anything for change as there are reasons behind why Linfield do things the way it does. I am looking forward to see other opinions though!

The experience was very good; I had a fun time there. One thing was the price; I should have loaded myself with scholarships. I did wrestling as an extracurricular activity. I'm proud to say I attended, and the environment was quiet and accommodating. The dorms were not the best, but I had a very good roommate, so no complaints there. The food was on and off, good and sometimes questionable. At the end of all of that, I am finding myself wanting to return to Linfield College, which is why I am applying for this scholarship. I think I will be more prepared this time.

Been at Linfield for 4 years and I feel there are some great things about it but also some things that can be improved. Greek life (Sorority) is extremely toxic. I feel there needs to be changes to that but nothing will ever change. However, academics is really great if you communicate with professors. I have not encountered a professor that wasn't understanding and easy to contact. Looking back on if I would pick this school again, probably not since my experience with fellow students was awful. If it was based solely on professors I would pick it again.

ai_summary: Luther College offers a beautiful campus situated in Decorah, Iowa, where students generally appreciate the friendly and supportive community , as well as the caring professors who foster strong connections through small class sizes. The college provides meaningful academic experiences with a range of majors, notably in music and nursing, and has numerous opportunities for international study and involvement in extracurricular activities. However, some students express concerns regarding the limited social scene , issues with food quality, and the lack of diversity within the student body. While many find Luther to be a great fit for those focused on academics and community, others feel the campus can be isolating and limiting in terms of outside engagement, leaving a desire for more vibrant student life activities .

reviews.text: It was really good. I love the college and its environment. everything there is so good and the professors are really well qualified
The Luther community stand out as the unique and best part of my time at Luther. On campus and in the town of Decorah, I feel part of a community that cares about each other.
Luther has an amazing academic policy that aims to not only educate students but help them build morals and beliefs that they can focus their careers on in the future.

I have absolutely loved my time at Luther. I wouldn’t change a thing. This place couldn’t be located in a better spot the town itself and all the people in town make this place feel like home. Something I would change is Whippy Dip needs to be open year round instead of closing in October!

Personally, Luther college is a great place to be if as a student, you are not into too much outside attractions and would like to just focus on your degree. I really like the location of the campus as it is in a Village (Decorah) and you just have the strick minimum/required places (stores/restaurants/bowling) and a lot of beautiful area around.
I wish maybe they could help students to know more about scholarship( if any) and opportinuty related to their chosen majors.

I just finished my first year at Luther and I feel very mixed about this school. On one hand, people are so kind and I have many friends. The area is so damn beautiful. Classes were good and professors cared about their students. However, this school is not what it used to be. Campus itself was dead nearly the entirety of the year. Many clubs/organizations are dying or gone and there isn’t much to do other than homework.
For some people, the small campus life works. But Luther is far too small and uninvolved— enrollment has been suffering and resources for students, on and off-campus, are extremely limiting. I imagine as enrollment comes back so will some of this energy, but at the moment Luther is incredibly depressing and isolating. There is so little room for growth as a person and as a student.

It's very pretty in Decorah, and Luther capitalizes on that with frequent hikes and other outdoor activities through the rec services. The food could definitely be better, but it's not awful. The professors are very nice, and so are the administrative people I've talked to.
I think Luther College is a great college, but it is lacking in its night life. Having a more fun weekend experience could help bring up enrollment and enjoyment of current students

Honest review: My overall experience at Luther was mixed. I was there for a year before transferring. The great things from my experience with Luther are the academics, professors, people, and the diversity. It was cool having mostly engaging classes with great professors. The people there are also very nice. It was also cool meeting people from all around the world. The only negative in the academic aspect is if you are in music but aren’t a music major, the music department will treat you like a music major even if you aren’t, I found that frustrating. Onto the cons, with my experience, the student life aspects and everything that wasn’t about academics wasn’t great for me. The student life experience isn’t that good here in my opinion because it’s so quiet and small. The only thing to do is watch jazz. That was pretty disappointing. There wasn’t much to do on campus or in town. Good academics, professors and people, but for me it didn’t have a good student life experience.
Overall pretty good school lots of things could make it better like food and overall attitude. People are pretty nice.

Luther College is an amazing to choose for college . Luther is located in very small city in Iowa, this amazing because everyone knows everyone. The school has amazing support and guidance, from the president to the students. I am enjoying my experience thanks to my peers and professors. The campus life is amazing we are always having different events with different student organizations. The only thing that need improvement/ change is the cafe.

Luther’s environment is just beautiful. I love the little town of Decorah. The professors are great so hard working and caring. Everyone loves the athletics even the town comes and supports the teams. This school does a great job at educating.

I have made good friends and have had good experiences. Classes are challenging but professors are helpful. I appreciate the availability of many international opportunities. Students can travel during J Term, for a semester or in the summer.
I think Luther is a great school. The professors are really nice. There are a lot of strong political views to watch out for.
I love Luther and Decorah a lot. The whole area is beautiful and the Luther community is amazing. All of my professors are very supportive and are always there to help.

ai_summary: Lycoming College offers a close-knit community atmosphere , which many students appreciate for its personalized education and approachable faculty. The small class sizes foster strong relationships between students and professors, enhancing the overall learning experience. While students enjoy diverse extracurricular activities, including sports and clubs, opinions about campus life vary significantly; some find it engaging and vibrant , while others feel it lacks excitement. Dining options receive mixed reviews, with several students highlighting dissatisfaction with the quality of food. Despite its challenges, students value the supportive nature of the campus community, the beauty of the surroundings, and the breadth of academic opportunities that prepare them for future careers. Overall, Lycoming stands out for its intimate campus environment and committed faculty, making it a unique choice for those seeking a smaller liberal arts education.

reviews.text: It's a great rigorous institution of higher education, but it could use more modernization and diversity than it currently has. Since it's in a small town, it currently doesn't provide the space to go but it does offer a great educational environment that you don't get at a bigger school. The staff is very connected and it's easy to get the assistance you need.

There’s barely any student life including Greek life and parties are non existent. It’s the kind of college for people that want a high school feel of college, but if that’s not what you’d like, I wouldn’t advise going here. The food is pretty abysmal and the options are not sufficient at all. A lot can be improved on.

I'm new to both Lycoming and the Williamsport area. People and professors are nice. They do their best to help in anyway possible.
There is lots of walking from building to building. The stairs in the Academic Center is the hardest. The elevator takes a long time to close. The walls are carpet.
Since I'm rooming in skeath hall 1st floor, I can only say that it's nice. There's a few times the bathrooms would be messy. But it's not that bad.
Dining hall food is good for the most part. Rice could use more salt. Chicken is a bit dry. Some foods you can trust others you can't.
So far this College is good. Nothing bad to say. Its a small campus so you'll run into the president and other administrators. Everyone is nice and the views are nice.
Lycoming is a small school, easy to get to know other people and make friends. The dinning hall could be a little better. Overall a good school.

Lycoming is a wonderful college for those looking for a calm and chill environment. The professors are very accessible and understanding of any issues you may have and always willing to work with you. I highly recommend if your looking to further your education.

I wanted to express my heartfelt gratitude for the remarkable education and experiences I received at Lycoming College. With a triple major, I have enjoyed a 45-year awesome career in financial services.
The outstanding education & practical experience I received were instrumental in allowing me to excel in various roles, including Retail, Commercial Banking, Payment Solutions, Risk, Operations, Sales, Government, US Mortgage, Distributions, and more. My journey has taken me to many markets, from Toronto to Miami, leading key markets in NJ/NY/PA, DC, and FL. I've had the pleasure of developing new markets, executing key strategies.
My time at Lycoming College was enriched by my involvement in various activities. As the president of Theta Chi Fraternity, I had the opportunity to lead and make a difference. I also held some fantastic on-campus part-time jobs that contributed to my overall experience. Between football, rugby, and track, I gained life skills.
Thank you, Lycoming College.

I went to Lycoming College as a Criminology and Psychology major. The programs for both majors were good, especially with Criminology. You would have classes with probation officers, police officers, and judges. They also had trips to penitentiaries, which were extremely helpful and closely related to course materials. The staff and faculty were very supportive and would do their best to help you achieve your goals. The overall experience was enjoyable.

I very much enjoyed my 4 years at Lycoming . The smaller size of the college allowed for more personalization with my professors. I also feel that a liberal arts education made me a more well-rounded student and person. I was involved in 2 sports at Lycoming and appreciated the support for athletics from the college. I was also involved with the Greek scene at Lycoming and felt it added to my experience. I had plenty of friends that were not part of the Greek scene. Lycoming did prepare me for the business world once I graduated and contributed to my success over the years. I still stay in touch with a good number of the people I went to Lycoming with.

The opportunity to participate in a liberal arts education greatly benefited me in my career and in life. Lycoming College helped prepared me for a highly successful pharmaceutical career and later a highly successful leadership role in "face-to-face marketing", as VP and GM. My Genetics professor was also my Senior Advisor who helped me develop a special internship at Williamsport Hospital to gain insight and perspective in various potential healthcare careers. Through the internship, I met two Pharm D's who published a magazine, US Pharmacist. They had connections with major pharmaceutical companies and I had (3) job offers before graduation. I was also member of Theta Chi, a national fraternity, a 4-year letterman and captain of the wrestling team while attending Lycoming. Over 40 years have past since my introduction to Lycoming College and lifelong friendships with many fraternity brothers have expanded to include our spouses and our children. A Lycoming experience lasts forever!

I attended Lycoming College, and its focus on Liberal Arts gave me the skills I needed to build a very successful career. The ability to read, write, and think deductively set me up for success in business school and in the workplace. Lycoming is a special place, and every student is given the time needed to grow and develop.

I had real issues with this college.There was no quiet place to study despite the student council addressing this for years. The college gave us no help looking for jobs when we were about to graduate. But they wasted no time trying to get us to donate money as soon as we graduated. They were also very quick to assess and collect fines. Very little nice to say about the school.

My college experience for the first year was pretty eventful. I was in a position where I couldn't go my full first year but despite that I had amazing professors who provided information daily and worked with me and my schedule as long as it was reasonable. The campus staff is always willing to help and truly takes an interest on your goals and they do the best they can to guide you where you want to go in life.

Lycoming College is a good college because its small, supportive community and personalized learning environment allow students to build close relationships with dedicated faculty, ensuring they receive tailored guidance and ample hands-on opportunities that prepare them well for their future.
Outstanding overall experience. Great athletic programs and facilities, top notch academics and good food!

Lycoming College is a wonderful college – where you… get a great personalized education; are not a number in the classroom; get to know the professors and administration and staff, beyond getting to know your classmates; enjoy a beautiful campus; can play a varsity or intramural sport; can join clubs and organizations. I highly recommend it! I still go back and visit annually.

ai_summary: At Macalester College, students consistently highlight the strong academic experience characterized by small class sizes and engaged professors who provide significant support. Many reviews emphasize the dedication of faculty to student learning and the availability of research opportunities, contributing to a rich educational environment. However, students often mention challenges with the food quality and the limited social scene , especially for those without cars. The campus is described as beautiful and conveniently located near the Twin Cities, allowing for additional experiences outside of school. Some students express concerns about the homogeneity of the student body and the campus climate regarding political diversity, suggesting that it skews very liberal and may not foster a welcoming environment for differing viewpoints. Overall, it’s recognized as a close-knit community with a notable emphasis on inclusivity and personal growth, though some students feel the administration could improve support for all identities and social dynamics on campus.

reviews.text: Macalester was an amazing and singular experience. Both my majors have served me well, I value the relationships with professors I retain, and I have made lifelong friends. The most important thing I learned at Macalester was critical thinking - you won’t get out of there without learning how to do that properly, and the world needs more of that.

I loved my time at Mac. I made great friends and never felt as though the social scene was lacking. While I felt as though I got a solid education, there was somewhat an issue for my department in terms of replacing faculty which lead to larger class sizes and a lack of courses. Additionally, the physical accessibility of campus is lacking -- and the office of disability resources is more geared towards those who only need testing accommodations. But, those flaws aside, I loved Mac and highly recommend it to all!

I had an amazing experience at Macalester. I had professors who deeply cared about my learning and viewed students as "knowledge producers, not just knowledge consumers." What had the biggest impact on me was that it was a small liberal arts college in a big city. A lot of my classes actively engaged in the local community - going to museums, theater, volunteering or working with local organizations. A ton of my learning thus took place outside of the classroom, or translating "theory into practice."
Overall, Macalester changed my life for the better and I am still very close to many Macalester alumni, staff, and faculty even a decade later.

As an international student, Macalester College was the experience of my life and led me to higher academic achievement (PhD) and a fabulous career in International development and economics. Macalester College opened my eyes to a whole new world of scholarship and community, intellectual curiosity, and an engagement with a very supportive faculty and staff. In addition to life on campus, the surrounding St Paul and Minneapolis communities exposed me to realities of community engagement and real Life in America. The size of the student body, the high quality of faculty, and the urban community, made Macalester College the most important investment of a lifetime.

Macalester is the ideal school. Metro-heavy area, safe campus, and rigorous academics. That is, to those looking outward, in. It is very easy to get trapped in the “Macalester bubble,” interacting with the same people and seemingly having little room to maneuver. I’ve found myself stuck there now. With a population of just over 2,000 students, your world may seem small. However, I’ve realized that the mind is what creates reality. Opportunities are there, and easily accessible; you need to want to find them. Not once have I had a negative experience with a professor, nor have I questioned my worth in class. Unfortunately, I seek validation, and professors are where that validation comes from, at least for me. Having said that, 1:1 communication is integral to my learning, "networking" which gets thrown around so frequently, is easy, solely because of the care of each professor chosen into their role. My view of Macalester had changed from one full of limits to one of opportunity.

Life-changing experience. The ability that I had to meet people all around the world on the classroom and on the athletic fields. Many of these people are lifelong friends who continue to challenge me like they did 15 years ago. Mac is a top liberal arts college with a small field, but in a big city. That is a true rarity today
Not very diverse and very unhelpful for disabled students/not ADA compliant. But good and interesting classes, professors, staff, faculty, and students.

One of the things I value most about Macalester is how professors genuinely care about students as people, not just as academic performers. I have especially appreciated the close-knit learning environment. Small class sizes have allowed me to form strong relationships with professors and classmates, creating space for discussion, vulnerability, and collaboration.
Something I'd like to see change, I believe more support is needed for first-generation, low-income, and immigrant students beyond orientation programming. Many students quietly navigate financial stress, family responsibilities, and cultural pressure without consistent institutional support. I would like to see expanded mentorship programs, more culturally responsive advising, and increased mental health resources that are accessible and culturally informed.

Macalester is one of the friendliest schools I've ever been to. People check in with each other. Faculty and coordinators actually care about students. People are willing to talk about the things that matter, even when not everyone agrees. Community really matters here.

I loved my experiences at Macalester. I had incredible professors, loved the campus and St Paul location and appreciated interacting with so many international students. I graduated many years ago but continue to be active at the College. There many new and exciting programs available today including entrepreneurship. Facilities have only gotten better and they are continuing to improve with a new dorm under construction.

I've really enjoyed my time at Macalester College. It is a top tier academic experience with a great diversity of people for around the world. I like the many different opportunities Mac offers with their standing within the academic community, as well as the tight knit community that goes to school there. One thing I would like to change is the quality of food. The ingredients they use are high quality and responsibly sourced, but what they choose to make with those ingredients is not the best.

I am very grateful for my experience at Macalester. Academically, I had the chance to create an interdisciplinary major and formed close relationships with the professors who advised me. Socially, I enjoyed the smaller community at Mac and made great friends through multiple clubs and by living in special interest housing (veggie co-op). While my experience at Macalester wasn't perfect, I know my time at Mac shaped me as a person and helped me learn more about the world and myself.

Macalester is an absolute gem. Fun, but also serious. Rigorous but also open and friendly. Small (and the benefits of a tight-knit community) but also part of a large Twin Cities ecosystem. I can't speak highly enough of my experience at Mac. It quite literally changed my life. I recommend the school to everyone I speak to. I was able to develop incredibly close relationships with my classmates, my professors, and members of the administration. And Macalester is unique among elite liberal arts colleges in its proximity to a vibrant and dynamic city (cities in this case). I was able to both volunteer and find internships that would have been unavailable to me if I was at a school in a rural area. I LOVE Macalester!
Macalester nurtured my interest in the Latino community and gave me opportunities to volunteer and plant seeds for a future career in the sector.
Raw food in the Cafe, Professors who don't care about students ' well-being, athletic department that is okay. Big party school for the weekends and after games.

ai_summary: Marymount Manhattan College offers a unique experience primarily focused on performing arts, and many students appreciate the small class sizes that foster personal connections with professors. The college's location in New York City allows access to numerous internships and cultural experiences, making it attractive for those in arts-related fields. However, reviews indicate significant challenges with tuition costs, campus facilities, and a lack of racial diversity, leading to feelings of exclusion among some students, especially those not in the arts. The social scene is often described as cliquish and reminiscent of high school, which can impact the overall student life experience. While some find a supportive and nurturing environment, others express concerns about the academic rigor and the prioritization of performing arts over other majors.

reviews.text: My experience at MMC has been very positive overall. The community of staff and students is very close knit and feels like a family. There are robust resources for disabled students, counseling and wellness services, as well as the intercultural center offering support for minorities. Most staff is helpful and can offer lots of support. Most students are friendly and it is easy to make friends. The only con is the severe lack of funding which leads to run down buildings and unequipped facilities, particularly in the sciences.
The teachers are very well versed in what they do and know what they are talking about. The city is your campus so you have any resource imaginable right at your finger tips.

Really mediocre school, professors do not put in effort into teaching the classes or engaging the students. The preforming arts/dance program is strong from what I have heard but everything else is neglected. The school does not have a lot of school spirit and I would not reccomend this school unless you are looking to be friends/be around a very specific kind of person (Art liking, etc.) I feel like people would be better suited going to a CUNY since this school is extremely overpriced for what it offers.
So far loving it! Although, as a transfer, dealing with advisement has been difficult as they can take a while to actually process your documents.

My experience at Marymount Manhattan College has been a journey of personal and academic growth. One of the things I really appreciate about MMC is the small class sizes, which allow for more direct interaction with professors.
there are areas where I believe MMC can improve. One of the major issues is the lack of campus facilities compared to larger universities the college is very much a commuter school feel, and sometimes it feels like student life and community engagement could be stronger. More resources for career development, mental health support, and extracurricular activities would enhance the student experience. Additionally, tuition costs are high, and financial aid options could be expanded.
Overall, MMC is a great place for students who are self-motivated, thrive in a smaller, tight-knit academic setting. With some improvements in student resources and campus life, it could offer an even more well-rounded college experience.

Marymount is a lovely place with very kind and encouraging people. The location and campus are beautiful. We will be merging with Northeastern so this change may be intimidating but I’m excited to see what doors it opens for us students.

I participated in Marymount Manhattan's in-prison college program at both Bedford Hills and Taconic Correctional Facilities. I graduated with my bachelor's in politics and human rights in June 2022. I was valedictorian. My educational journey with Marymount changed my life. Because of the education I was afforded with this institution, I am now pursuing my master's in social work, while working full time at a job I love.

i transferred to marymount my sophomore year of college. when i first toured the campus i fell in love with it and the idea of the city life, but that was strictly all it was… an IDEA. when i actually got to the school i came to learn that some professors are way too strict and tbh rude, don’t get me wrong there were some gems but a lot of them were too outdated to be teaching. overall the biggest problem at this school is they are MONEY HUNGRY. they will find any and everyway to keep you from leaving and continue to give them money, tbh the school is a total scam. cost my dad thousands of dollars just for them to tell me at the end of my senior year that i all of a sudden “needed 10 more credits” when it was never told to me previously and i was ok to graduate.
Final thought: go somewhere else. They also skimp you out of scholarship money and give you almost nothing, you’ll be taking out loans. GO SOMEWHERE ELSE

I enjoy Marymount a lot. New York City is your campus which is really fun because you have a lot of freedom outside of just going to school. The area surrounding the dorms are mostly safe depending on the time of day, and there is a doorman in both form buildings. I will say that we have to pay for laundry in the dorms which I think is unreasonable because we pay so much to live in the dorms. Also there are no meal swipes we have to pay for our food from the dining hall whether it’s from the money the school provides or out of pocket. The diversity could be higher as well. I will say POC are the minority and that is an issue because we need more inclusivity in these spaces especially in the performing arts area.

I really love this school. I can see how it’s not meant for everyone but it definitely is where I belong. I am a musical theater major here, though, and I know that we kind of have priority over other majors.

I have had a blast at MMC, genuinely an amazing place where I can be indulged in the artistic side of life. This was one of my staples when picking a college and for me it made life very more easy going seeing like minded people with like minded hobbies of creative lifestyles where I can truly express my imagination in a vivid and meaningful manner.
I love the courses I have taken. Most of my professor's checked in with me and the rest of the class.But I would not one class again with a certain professor.
Its not a very traditional college, but if you are seeking arts in NY, its the right college for you.
I love having New York City as my campus and the opportunities it provides. My college is small and that is helpful as I know my professors and classmates.
I love that the professors have a very personal approach; they want their students to succeed, inside and outside of school

ai_summary: Massachusetts College of Liberal Arts (MCLA) provides a closely-knit community atmosphere where students appreciate smaller class sizes that facilitate stronger relationships with professors. The faculty is often highlighted for their dedication and support, which many believe plays a significant role in student success. Students enjoy a variety of academic opportunities and extracurricular activities, although there are concerns regarding the limited food options and, at times, the engagement level of the student body. The campus also benefits from its scenic location in the Berkshires , fostering a calm and beautiful backdrop for studies and outdoor activities. Overall, MCLA stands out to students seeking a personal education experience , albeit with some room for improvement in areas like campus life engagement and facilities.

reviews.text: It's not a bad school at all; it's just incredibly small, and it can be difficult to make friends because of that. People here can be very cliquey without even realizing it. The food is alright, but if you don't like what they make a lot of the time, you kind of end up just eating burgers and pizza every day. The dorms are nice, because it's such a small school, so are the dorms as well, which makes them a lot more homey and personal. In Berkshire Towers you only share a bathroom with the 4 other people in your suite, so it's good for anyone with a fear of public bathrooms.

I came here to pursue my dream of being a student-athlete and playing Division III hockey. My experience at this college has been great. As an international student, I’ve been able to fit in well and feel at home here.
It is OK I definitely if I had chosen differently would have stayed in the Boston area instead because of the costs and quality.

I had a great academic experience at MCLA, in large part due to the Interdisciplinary Major option (which allows students to build a custom major if the school does not offer a particular department due to the school’s small size). This allowed me to pursue what I was interested in, regardless of the limitations of what other departments would require. Additionally, while some may find the somewhat remote area a bore, the beautiful forested scenery was amazing and I greatly enjoyed being surrounded by nature. While there have been several administrative staff shake ups since my graduation, the professors I encountered there were all well versed in their respective subjects and the student services staff were infinitely helpful with any questions I had. I also highly recommend the school's work-study program, as more than any other job option it understands having to schedule employment around a class schedule. The cafeteria had a good variety, though the quality could be improved.

My experience at the Massachusetts College of Liberal Arts (MCLA) was academically enrichenimg. I had great professors who provided top.notch instruction in a wide array of subjects. This gave me the full well rounded liberal arts education that has helped.me.become a dynamic person professionally. MCLA early was nearly permanently shut down 25 years ago during my days there. I smile at the tremendous growth and metamorphisis the campus has undergone since my graduation in May 2002.

The professors at MCLA are sincerely second to none. Their drive, dedication and desire to make sure each student is on the right path of discovery is incredible. Their mission goes beyond just teaching and include ensure the overall long term well being and success fo each MCLA student they work with.
It is by no means a perfect school, but I have never once doubted there were people behind me who wanted me to succeed and to help me succeed at every step along the way.

As an MBA graduate of MCLA, I couldn’t be more satisfied with the experience. The program offers incredible value—top-tier instruction, small class sizes, and meaningful access to professors who truly care about your growth. The flexibility of the hybrid format made it manageable for working professionals, and the in-person sessions at the Berkshire Innovation Center were engaging and energizing. MCLA may be a small school, but it delivers big results. The curriculum blends practical business skills with strategic thinking, leadership development, and real-world application.
