I also gained lasting friendships and a strong professional network. What sets MCLA apart is its location in the Berkshires—an inspiring, innovative region that fosters community and entrepreneurship. Whether you're advancing your career or launching something new, this program equips you with the tools, confidence, and support to make it happen. Highly recommended for anyone ready to take the next step.

Growing up in Berkshire County, the last thing I wanted to do was attend a school so close to home. My decision came down to cost, and MCLA seemed to be the best fit financially. I am not exaggerating when I say that this college changed my life. The quality of education I received at MCLA is something I feel no other college could have matched. Classes are small, professors care about what they are teaching and want to form a meaningful relationship with their students. I built lifelong connections, not only with my fellow students, but with my professors as well. I met my wife here, and we ended up inviting dozens of our classmates and multiple professors to our wedding to celebrate with us. This college made me fall back in love with Berkshire County, and I can say, as a three-year North Adams resident and proud MCLA alum, that I don't regret my decision to make MCLA my home for those four years.

MCLA is a small hidden gem if a student makes the most of their experience here. There are so many opportunities for students to succeed and excel academically, in co-curriculars, in jobs and clubs, and in leadership. The faculty and staff are some of the kindest people that I've gotten the opportunity to work with.

Ad a parent who works full time MCLA gave me the option to do my courses and complete my degree in a way that fits my schedule. Not only were the professors and staff knowledgeable, they are also understanding to the needs of their students. This truly came in handy when I had 2 surgeries in a short time frame and lost a loved one. They truly cared about me as a person, not just another student. They also helped me believe in me, my success, and my future when I didn't believe in myself. Thank you MCLA for helping me achieve my goals and keep moving forward with my future.

It was helpful to have resources on campus (DRC, Trio, Tutoring Center) to help me navigate and get through my first academic year at MCLA. Faculty were open to questions and very willing to help whenever needed. Overall, MCLA has been a very welcoming environment.
I love the professors here at MCLA they are extremely understanding and really want you to succeed. The classes are small which helps you build relationships with professors.

Graduated in 2021 with a bachelor's in psychology. Obviously, my junior and senior year were during COVID, so I can't really judge those based on normal standards, but I really truly loved the professors here so much. When I was having personal problems and falling behind on work one semester, I had 3 different professors reach out to ME to ask if I was okay and what was going on. Also, the undergraduate research opportunities here are kind of crazy considering how small it is. When I went to get my master's degree, I found I was more prepared for research than most other students in my class. Plus, North Adams is a cute little town with a great art scene.

MCLA has offered me so much has a non traditional student. I was able to receive my BA in business and am now working on my masters all while being a married mom of 2 working full time. The school has been so accommodating.

ai_summary: At Meredith College, students appreciate the strong sense of community and support that comes from being in an all-women's environment. Many reviews highlight the dedication of professors and the personalized attention that small class sizes allow, which enhances the academic experience. Students often feel empowered and connected through unique traditions and events that foster a sense of sisterhood . While the campus is praised for being safe and picturesque, some reviews note issues with dining options and a need for improved diversity and inclusion efforts. Overall, Meredith College stands out for its commitment to women’s education , academic rigor, and creating a supportive learning atmosphere, though potential students should be mindful of the costs associated with attending.

reviews.text: My experience at Meredith has been superb! I love my small classes, as I get direct access to my professors who are willing to move mountains to help me succeed in their classes. I have also gotten so many opportunities because of the resources they share with us. I also love how there are so many clubs on campus and a LOT of things to get involved in! There are always events where you can go talk to people or just grab a treat and make your day better. The small community here at Meredith makes it much easier to find your people and do life with them.

Meredith College is truly such an incredible school that has helped shake me into the person I am today. I chose Meredith for the small campus size, intimate classrooms, and the community feel. Since coming to Meredith, I have found such a great community and a true sisterhood with my fellow peers. I have had numerous opportunities to become more involved on campus and to get to know my fellow Meredith Angels on a depot level. The intimate classrooms have helped me to feel more comfortable speaking out and has helped to foster a closer relationship with my professors. I am able to have lots of one-on-one time with my professors and I am able to better understand the material within the course. Also, I love how I can walk to class in around 10 minutes and then I have to take no other form of transportation to arrive at my building. Overall, Meredith is such a warm and welcoming environment that I would recommend to anyone.

When it comes to academics, professors are very understanding and willing to help you better understand the topics discussed. The school offers many opportunities career-wise and experience-wise, so you get a better sense of the field you are pursuing. The dining hall doesn't appear sanitary, and the food isn't up to par. There are other places to get food on campus, but access is limited. The campus is beautiful. Plants on campus are well kept. Without considering finances, I would choose Meredith again.

I think Meredith is a rlly great place with a GORGEOUS campus, professors and staff who seem like they want nothing more than for you to succeed, SO many great support systems (therapists, coaches, some professors, advisors, tutors, friends), and a welcoming community. However, there are also many negatives as well. The food, for one, is TERRIBLE. I don’t understand why they haven’t even attempted to improve the quality all this time, especially for students who don’t have many other options. I’m a commuter, so I’ve only had it like two times, but I’ve also heard stories from other students as well. The dorms aren’t that great either—which is why I decided to commute. And speaking of, I feel like commuter student life could be much improved. I remember during orientation week, I was going THROUGH it. I had nowhere to be during the long wait times between required events and didn’t know anyone, so I often felt incredibly lonely and aimless. I feel like there could also be more clubs

I've been at Meredith College for a few weeks now, I started as a freshman not too long ago and it was a very good experience, everyone was so nice and welcoming, not to mention how friendly they were.

I have loved my time at Meredith so far! We have an amazing support system and an amazing team. The dining hall doesn't have the best food, but it is pretty good overall, and we also have a cafe with really good food. The biology department and psychology department are my favorites, but all departments have amazing professors. 10/10 recommend!

As a sophomore majoring in Biology at Meredith College in Raleigh, North Carolina, my academic journey has been both challenging and rewarding. The Biology program offers a comprehensive curriculum, blending lectures with hands-on lab experiences. My favorite course so far has been General Biology, where I had the opportunity to learn about most if not all aspects of biology. Meredith has such a beautiful campus, with plenty of study spots and scenic views, creating a perfect environment for study and collaboration. Living in the dorms has been the best experience for me as I met the most amazing girls who have become my lifelong best friends. Overall, my experience at Meredith College has solidified my passion for biology and prepared me for a future in healthcare or research.

Meredith is an amazing fit for international students like me, offering incredible academic programs that really stand out, especially their honors program. With small class sizes, I know I'll get to know my professors really well and get the personalized attention I need to succeed.

I over all had a good time and experience at Meredith College, but like all places it has its ups and downs. I'll start with what I enjoyed during my time there. The colleges campus landscaping is so beautiful and well kept, the campus itself is gated and on the smaller side (can cross entire campus in 20 minutes), the professors are mostly all amazing and truly knowledgeable in their field. The downsides/ things that I disliked were the academic and dorm buildings are all (minus the two new ones) extremely old and underkept, the food options on campus are sad to say the least and the dining hall is only ever good when parents or events happen on campus, there is a disconnect between the students and the college executives (they don't listen to our needs and waste money), the cost of attendance/ tuition is honestly not worth what we get out of it.
I love the atmosphere and how much Meredith College tries their best to include everyone in clubs and after class activities. I love how they give out study abroad opportunities.

I have enjoyed my time so far at Meredith college. I love the active student and campus life with so many events happening every week. I would love to see more fun student spaces on campus so that we don’t have to travel off campus for something fun to do.

Merediths only really redeeming trait are the small class sizes and ability to contact professors relatively easily. Aside from that the social scene is terrible. It’s a very you either fit the mold or not, which also translates to how professors treat you. Classes are also never offered at times that allow you to do things like work outside of school. Most required courses are offered at varying times throughout the day which makes it impossible to keep a job and go to school. The only reason I’m still here is admin gave awful advice with pulling up grades after a health crisis my freshmen semester. I ended my first year with a gpa under a 1.0 since they told me not to retake failed classes, and have just recently recovered as a senior. Staying here though this has ruined my mental health and I wouldn’t suggest anyone go here.

I loved my first year at Meredith! The people are so kind and friendly, campus is beautiful, and there is so much to do in the surrounding area. The professors that I had were all very kind and willing to work with students, and go over ways to help students improve. The food wasn't 5 stars but there was a good variety while still having staple foods. I enjoyed all the courses that I took, as most of them were well organized and truly taught me things that have bettered me and my education. Some things I would like to see change are the dorms. Some of the dorms don't seem to have been updated since they were built, with bathrooms not having fans, windows being rusted over, the heaters and A/Cs consistently went out or would be overworking on one floor and not another.

From what I’ve had the pleasure of witnessing, It’s a great environment that would be good for any major. The campus is beautiful. The people and the professors and staff are super personable. It’s overall great.

Really good college, with great opportunities for certain majors. provides great resources if you actually take advantage of them, and the professors and advisors are really helpful. I wish there was more opportunities for more engagement form the community, it's hard to make friends but you can do it.

ai_summary: Middlebury College is recognized for its strong academic environment , where professors are enthusiastic, accessible, and committed to student success, contributing to engaging small classes that foster meaningful connections. Students often find the workload challenging yet rewarding, with many expressing appreciation for the rigorous learning experience and diverse course offerings. Student life is characterized by a tight-knit community where extracurricular activities thrive, although some note that the social scene can feel cliquey, and the campus lacks diversity. The picturesque rural setting offers opportunities for outdoor activities and a serene atmosphere, but the school’s remote location can be isolating for some. While student services, including health and academic support, are generally well-regarded, there are concerns about housing quality and dining options. Overall, Middlebury seems best suited for students who prioritize academics in a supportive community , appreciate natural surroundings, and are willing to navigate the challenges of a less diverse and somewhat affluent environment.

reviews.text: Middlebury College offers a uniquely immersive liberal arts experience defined by intellectual intensity and a strong sense of community. Classes are small and discussion driven, pushing students to think critically and speak confidently. The language programs are especially renowned, and the environmental studies department stands out for its interdisciplinary depth and real-world focus. Winters are long and cold, but campus traditions and close friendships make the atmosphere warm. Social life is active without feeling overwhelming. For students who value academics, outdoor adventure, and thoughtful peers, Middlebury provides a challenging yet deeply rewarding college experience.

At Middlebury College, I have enjoyed a great level of challenging academics in a variety of subjects, which is easily accessible through my liberal arts degree. I like having the options to explore my academic interests. People are very nice here, it is easy to find activities to do, and the college provides a lot of student support. The location is definitely a bit rural, but it is beautiful here and there's lots of access to nature as well as all the necessities right in town.

Middlebury College is a top liberal arts school in Vermont known for its small classes, rigorous academics, and outdoor-focused lifestyle. It excels in languages, environmental studies, and international programs, with nearly half of students studying abroad. The tight-knit community and supportive faculty create a collaborative, friendly atmosphere. Its rural setting offers stunning nature and a peaceful campus life, but limited nightlife. Overall, it’s ideal for students seeking academic excellence, close mentorship, and outdoor adventure.

My first year here at middlebury was a very unique experience there are so many aspects of life here that i didnt expect to enjoy as much as i have. From my january term course on DJ-ing, learning how to ski in the winter and just all of the really interesting classes I've taken, all in all it has been a very transformative experience.

Middlebury has been my home for the past three years. I have received unconditional support and guidance from professors, staff, and students on campus. There are over 200 student orgs on campus that can help students find their group! Parties here can be repetitive, but overall good. The Mill has at least three events every single week with at least two of them being live music. There are always things to do/to see but you need to know how to find out about them. If you are a first-gen/low-income student, Middlebury will always go out of their way to support you. From orientation to graduation, you matter. Students here come from a large variety of backgrounds, it takes a bit of learning and unlearning to truly understand your peers sometimes--but the perspective they bring in invaluable. If you want to be challenged academically but also personally, Midd is one of the best schools to go to!

I am a Junior at the College and I have had a lot of very unique experiences offered by the school. Students have a lot of flexibility to mix and match different areas of study, go abroad and have their credits counted, and develop as professionals. It's super easy to pursue a double major or major/minor at Midd, professors are super used to this so they can give you really useful advice. Students can go abroad for up to three semesters and have their credits counted for their degrees, so they still graduate in time. Additionally, the school has a lot of opportunities to travel in different settings, such as Alternative Breaks (MAlt), or with clubs and orgs (Debate, Mountain Club), etc.. There is a lot of support for students to develop their professional skills and access very coveted, enriching internship experiences.

I am going into my sophomore year at Middlebury and I absolutely love it here. The campus and surrounding areas are breath taking in the fall and winter. I also love how small the school is because you are able to build a really personal relationship with your professors and peers which in my experience has made the learning experience more enjoyable. It is also amazing how smart everyone is here. I do not feel a competitive atmosphere here whatsoever but instead I feel like we are able to collaborate and build off each other. Not only are the students smart but also the professors. I am a chemistry major and I had heard my whole life that Organic Chemistry would be the hardest class I would have to take but because my professor was so knowledgeable in her field, it was genuinely one of my easiest classes.

Middlebury College is renowned for its exceptional language programs, offering immersive and intensive study in a wide range of languages. The supportive faculty, global focus, and opportunities like study abroad and the renowned Language Schools make it an ideal place for language lovers to thrive.

There are actually capitalist all around campus and the networking for Wall Street jobs is exceptional. Who knew that would be the case when arriving in Vermont. I love it here and appreciate the network.

Every student is so passionate about whatever their interests are, which can be intimidating and inspiring. It's def not a big party school, but that's not why you go to a small school in VT. Students are always going on runs/bike rides, going to and putting on concerts, and engaging with their clubs-- of which there are so many. The professors are there to teach, and most of them are really excited to talk to their students and become mentors in any capacity. I had a difficult time adjusting to small college town social scene, which can be a bit cliquey (and because we were coming out of the pandemic shutdowns) but college is what you make of it and everyone can find incredible friends and experiences at midd.
Very good academics, cold and sad sometimes but incredible professors and students. Literally so white.
It's a beautiful campus in the middle of nowhere with rigorous academics and cold weather. I wouldn't have chosen a different school.

Most of the professors I've had so far are very passionate and knowledgeable about their field, and are always willing to get to know you and answer any questions. Living on campus is great, and even though Midd is located in a relatively rural area, there's always something to do on the weekends. I wish the Title IX Office was more active in helping students navigate issues such as sexual harassment, but most of the other service offices are helpful with other issues.
Amazing New England college with a good community and academics. Small class sizes are super personalized, and the campus is gorgeous.
There are amazing professors and classes at Middlebury. Vermont is a beautiful place for college. Being a Feb is the best thing that has happened to me.

ai_summary: Morehouse College stands out as a unique institution that fosters a deep sense of brotherhood and community among its students, which many describe as a transformative experience . The college is known for its strong academic reputation , with professors who are invested in student success and offer personalized support in small class settings. Students frequently highlight the vibrant campus life , enriched by opportunities for extracurricular involvement, mentorship, and leadership development. However, common criticisms include challenges related to housing conditions , the high cost of tuition, and issues with financial aid and administrative processes. The location in Atlanta provides students with access to cultural and social activities, but some feel that the surrounding area can pose safety concerns. Overall, Morehouse College is recognized for its commitment to empowering Black men and preparing them for leadership roles, making it a respected choice among prospective students.

reviews.text: I plan on attending Morehouse College Fall 2026 for my freshman year. Visiting campus during the spring time for the first time was an experience like no other, I just enjoy being around similar minded people
My experience was the true fight for equality for African American's and the true history of our people and the true facts we are still human beings of all races and ethnicities

I appreciate the opportunity to attend an HBCU where the focus is on excellence for black males. I have opportunities to network and learn more about different industries that I may be interested in when graduating.

Morehouse college is a school that stands strong by their mission. The education is guided towards empowering Black Men and spreading traditional teachings. The social life is amazing as Morehouse College is in the middle of Atlanta and is close to many other schools.

Morehouse College is a house first. A home to every Black boy who grew up questioning himself in comparison to his peers. A home that builds us up brick by brick so we may have the fortitude to go out in the would and continue building upon our house.

To be honest I wish I was more involved with school. There is a lot to do around campus. Join a club, play. avsporet, find a mentor, GET CLOSE WITH YOUR PROFESSORS! You will need them in the future and oit's nice to have a faculty member in your corner during some tough situations you may encounter.

Don’t listen to the negative reviews. This is a good school! It helped me mature socially and allowed me to get out my shell. I highly suggest studying abroad. Also every cool rapper showed up to perform when I was there! Drake, Lil Yachty, Young thug, lil baby, Gunna, T.I, Hxncho, 1017, etc. However, the surrounding area isn’t the safest and the campus food isn’t always good. Overall it’s a good school and if you apply yourself you will have good experience!

When I first arrived at Morehouse, I wasn't entirely sure where I fit in. I had questions about myself, about my purpose, and about whether I truly belonged in a place with such a powerful legacy. But over time, Morehouse has shown me something I didn't fully believe at first. I am capable of all things.
Morehouse is more than a college, it's a place that doesn't just educate, but uplifts, sharpens, and challenges you to rise into the best version of yourself. The faculty, the brotherhood, the history. Everything here pushes you to understand that excellence isn't optional, it's expected. Everyday I walk these grounds, I'm reminded that I'm standing on the shoulders of giants. Men like Martin Luther King who once sat in the same classrooms. It's not just history it's fuel. It drives me to want more, to do more and to be more. Morehouse believed in me before it was legal in all states for me to read. Being a Morehouse man is about striving, growing and leading with purpose

My experience at Morehouse College was deeply enriching. From the moment you step onto campus, you begin to grasp the historical contribution the school has made to the world. The social life, Alumni network, clubs, and events are all top of the line and contribute to a unique experience that you cannot find at any other college. The only issues I had with the school were with the dorms, which are outdated and frequently malfunction, and the food, which was inconsistent and limited in options. But overall, my experience at Morehouse has been great.

As in incoming Freshman, Morehouse isn’t just a college—it’s a calling. It challenges you to grow, to lead, and to represent something greater than yourself. It’s not perfect, but the value of the experience, the people, and the legacy far outweigh the challenges. I’m proud to be part of the House. I look forward to feeling the weight of its legacy and the pride that comes with being a Morehouse Man in the making.
Within the campus grounds, I will experience brotherhood & community, leadership development, cultural pride and faculty mentorship. The environment is one where I will constantly be encouraged to rise—academically, socially, and personally.

I enjoyed my experience at Morehouse College. Being a freshman at the time, it was a big transition from high school to college. As an African American, I was surrounded by my culture all the time, and the school covered important topics such as mental health, politics, physical health, friend groups, peer pressure, etc. What I like was the idea of independence and the ability to change myself. Things I would change are the school's responses to feedback given by students and even staff. Overall, I would say that Morehouse College is a great school to attend. Even if not for attendance as a college student, this school provides tools to prepare you for life outside of schooling and being ready for the real world.

Morehouse produces some of the most intelligent, well-rounded black men in the country, and the experience that Morehouse gives you truly prepares you for the real world. The professors are stern, but all of it comes from how much they care about their students, and the brothers that you meet at this school will become your family for life. There’s no better school in the world for a black man than Morehouse College.
the change I would like to see would be in the support for enrolling money wise and overall help. It was pretty overwhelming how strict they are but mess up as well.

I enjoy the connection to Black brotherhood that Morehouse offers. Through it's NSO week as soon as I touched down on campus, I felt the deeply rooted connection and likemindedness of even the person next to me, whom I didn't even know at the time.

Morehouse College offers a unique and transformative experience for its students, emphasizing not only academic excellence but also leadership development, community building, and a strong sense of brotherhood. It's a highly selective, historically Black college for men, known for its rigorous academic programs, vibrant campus life, and extensive alumni network.

ai_summary: Mount Holyoke College is known for its beautiful campus and a tight-knit, supportive community where students often form lasting friendships and develop close relationships with professors. Academically, the college offers rigorous coursework with small class sizes that foster engaging discussions , though some students note challenges in course availability and difficulty accessing desired classes. While many professors are described as knowledgeable and helpful, there are varying opinions about faculty support and responsiveness. Student life is vibrant with numerous clubs and activities, though some feel the campus could be more politically and culturally diverse. Dining options are generally appreciated, although there are occasional complaints about food quality. The college’s commitment to a welcoming environment for women and non-binary students is frequently acknowledged as a unique aspect of the Mount Holyoke experience, despite some concerns regarding housing and tuition increases.

reviews.text: If you are neurodivergent or disabled, you may have a difficult time with administration, peers, and professors. My first year as a transfer spring student was so difficult. I'd recommend not starting in the spring semester. There can be a lot of social clicks, but there are chances to meet others like you. Be prepared to advocate for yourself and have documentation for accommodations. You may have to fight the disability office to get what you need. I am graduating with a major I didn't want because of a lack of accommodations, teacher bias in the bio department, and a lack of advising support. I face a future I am uncertain of with a degree I didn't want. I found the bio department very lacking in terms of understanding both neurodivergence and disability. My classmates in STEM were also very rude and solely focused on themselves. I'm a humanities major now, which is frustrating as I came here for STEM. I did my best, but it has affected my mental health.

Loved Mount Holyoke. Graduating as a frances perkins scholar this may. I think this is one of the best colleges out there for people who identify as female and also for nontraditional students. Really great peaceful area to just focus on your classes and homework and so many different class options to decide from. I've had the best time at Mount Holyoke College and I'm excited to move on to grad school at WashU to get a masters in social work.

My overall impression of Mount Holyoke College is very positive. I really like how supportive the community feels and how comfortable students seem being themselves and sharing ideas in class. The academic environment stands out because classes appear discussion-based and professors are approachable and invested in their students. I also appreciate the college’s focus on leadership, confidence, and empowering women, which gives the school a strong sense of purpose. The campus itself is beautiful and peaceful, making it a great place to focus and grow. One change I would like to see is continued efforts to increase racial and cultural diversity so more students feel represented. I also think there could be more encouragement and organized opportunities for students to explore off campus, since the area is more suburban and not everyone may know what is nearby. Overall, Mount Holyoke seems like a welcoming place that supports academic and personal growth.

I overall loved my time at Mount Holyoke. One of my main frustrations was course selection, as until my Junior year, I was pretty much not able to take any classes I was interested in or needed for my major because they all had insanely long waitlists. I also had issues with the head coach of the Equestrian team, as she was not very accommodating of injuries, and many of her practices were unsafe for both horse and rider. However, I made lifelong friends, and when I was able to get into classes I wanted, they were mentally stimulating and interesting.

I absolutely loved my first semester at Mount Holyoke, being a girl from oak cliff it was truly amazing and a really great experience. I got to experience the seasons change which is one of the things that I loved, it brought me so much peace. The professors always created a really great warm welcome. I found communicating with professors being such a great reliance, as it was never like that in my original high school or early college program. I feel supported in this campus which makes me feel safe and not so afraid of pushing through to an experience and live a life I never thought I once would. While college was the goal I never thought it would be far away from home. I expected to always be sad but rather on the contrary I feel like everything was going to be okay as I felt like I was home away from home. It is a beautiful experience and place to live in and as a low income student I am very grateful to experience and live what I thought once was impossible.

MHC is very typical of stereotypical liberal arts and women's colleges. Lots of cute traditions and fun things to do if you are into that sort of thing. Being an athlete is fun and relatively low stakes. It is hard to feel lonely and there are a lot of really kind amazing people here. However, it can be pretty tough to be at a womens college as you do lose some diversity of thought, and MHC does lack some more concentrated majors, like archaeology and education, which can be made up for in graduate school but if you want a foot on the door MHC isn't the place for specialization (great if you don't know what you want to do yet). MHC is a tough adjustment for people who grew up in cities and is in a pretty barren part of the east coast. Parties are pretty bleh at MHC, UMass parties are not safe for women but Hampshire parties have a good rep. I am planning on transferring, however I do not regret the time I spent at MHC: I just need specialization and a city.

Mount Holyoke is truly the place for ambitious and open-minded students. The academics are top-tier, along with incredible professors who deeply care about the success of their students. There are many opportunities to get involved on campus and to find your people, but the surrounding area can get a bit confining at times because of its remote location. MHC strives to make sure everyone feels like they belong, whether it's in inclusive housing, programming for all types of interests, or allowing students to take classes in any subject area they find interesting. Come to MHC if you want to grow and find yourself, as well as your people, while focusing on your academic journey.

Campus Culture:
The campus culture is not ideologically neutral. MHC has a very dominant progressive/liberal moral framework, especially around gender, sexuality, race, and politics.Public alignment with these views is often socially expected, and disagreement, even when expressed cautiously or privately,can lead to social isolation.
Many students feel pressure to constantly demonstrate correct language, beliefs, and positions.This creates an environment of walking on eggshells
it can feel stifling for students who are religious, politically mixed, philosophically exploratory, or slow to adopt prevailing moral language.
Social Environment:
Social life is highly politicized, and moral judgments can carry real social consequences. Friendships and community often feel contingent on alignment rather than curiosity or patience. For some students, this creates a sense of belonging; for others, it creates anxiety and fragmentation.

I loved the welcoming community. Since I am from another country, it was important for me to feel comfortable and welcomed. And that is what the whole orientation team did good at. I love the amount of opportunities we have here to help us grow in our comfort zone. I only wish we had AC’s in our rooms. Some dorms have them but not mine and some other ones. I love to be in a cool room with a heavy blanket, which I fail to do because it’s warm. But even this goes to the back of my head when I think of how amazing these community and experiences are.

I love MHC. It is truly my home away from home. Almost everyone I have met here has been kind and accepting. My professors have been great too thus far. Staff in the dining hall are the best they are all so kind and friendly I love working with them. Even though MHC wasn’t my top choice, I am incredibly happy with my decision to come here. The people I have met here and the friends I have made have changed my life for the better. I could not say enough good things about this school to truly express how much I love it here.

I attended Mount Holyoke as an adult, enrolling at age 28, as what they call a Frances Perkins student. I chose it because of this support for nontraditional-age students. Otherwise I had a completely traditional experience there - I lived and worked on campus, was on the dining plan, and took a full-time course load.
MHC totally changed my life. It gave me space to safely grow intellectually. My professors cared about my goals and knew me individually. Mentorship from them encouraged me to pursue my PhD, which I am doing currently. Being there made me a more confident person. Every student there is driven, open-minded, and kind. I wasn’t particularly challenged academically - everyone at MHC is an overachiever. I never wrote a paper longer than ten pages. I met my lifelong best friends here and we remain close. It is truly a special place with tons of opportunity to get involved. The area is very quiet and isolated and support for smaller programs can be lacking.

Mount Holyoke College is a place where I really felt like I was well taken care of. I have a lot allergies and felt very well taken care of in the dining hall. MHC really is a great place to learn not just about things but learn how to learn. MHC let me try out so many different things and it felt like a playground for education. I really loved my time here and am so grateful for it.
I liked everything about my college from the amenities to the academics and professors. Maybe the only thing I’m not so happy about is how it’s very far away from most local shops
I have loved my time at Mount Holyoke so much! There is truly no other community like the one found here.

Overall Mount Holyoke is a lovely place. The academics are great, the campus community is great. There are things I would change about student life like housing and dining. That mostly has to do with over admission and general greed

ai_summary: Muhlenberg College is characterized by its small , close-knit community where students can build meaningful relationships with professors and peers. The faculty is frequently praised for their commitment to student success, offering a supportive and engaging academic environment . Students appreciate the liberal arts education , which promotes critical thinking and exposure to diverse perspectives, though some express concerns about the lack of diversity within the student body itself. Many reviews note a vibrant dining experience and a variety of extracurricular opportunities, but there are mixed feelings about campus life , particularly regarding the social scene. Critics mention that socialization often centers around athletics or theater, leaving other students feeling isolated. Additionally, housing and tuition costs receive negative feedback, with some students feeling that the financial value does not meet expectations . Overall, while Muhlenberg provides a nurturing setting for academic and personal growth, its social dynamics can be challenging for those outside the predominant groups.

reviews.text: The theatre program is great with engaging professors. Outside of that, I've found that the academics are lacking and I often don't feel challenged enough, however that could change as I'm only a freshman. Everyone here is super outgoing and motivated, being involved in multiple student organizations and clubs especially within the arts. I don't think it's too cliquey and I have plenty of friends outside of my major. There are a lot of privileged people here, but the diversity is definitely better than some other places. Allentown is an amazing historical city that people don't take advantage of because they think it's too ghetto. You can take semi-reliable public transport in and out of the city with an hour bus ride into New York. Food is okay, but definitely not 14th best in the country like they claim.

So far my experience at Muhlenberg has been great. Being a member of a sports team has definitely helped to get connected to other athletes. Several of my teammates have studied abroad and that has my interest. At this time I don’t know what I would change as I have only lived here for one semester.
I still am friends with poeple I wnent to college with and we are almost all employed in fields we love!

My daughter is now a senior and has had an incredibly positive experience at Muhlenberg. (Her mother also went in the 1990s!) She is a dance major and has had wonderful opportunities to dance in a diverse range of styles AND to choreograph. She danced in HS and has friends who went to conservatories in NYC and they are not getting nearly as many actual opportunities to perform. The Trexler Pavillion is absolutely incredible, one of the most beautiful dance and theater facilities at any small liberal arts college of this size I've ever seen. She has connected deeply with a great group of young women. They are in a sorority but it's not the toxic Greek Life scene as at many other schools. The housing has been fantastic and of course you probably know the food is considered among the best in the country. We got generous merit financial aid, making her college experience affordable. I know there are some negative reviews on here, please stay open and visit the beautiful campus!
I think Muhlenberg College needs to listen to their students more. I think it's an overall "safe space" but it definitely could be better.

Overall, Muhlenberg was a very decent university. The classes are decently challenging but do not require an intense amount of extra critical thinking. Classes do not require much time outside of the course itself. The campus is small but homey and the shops just around campus are great to visit. It is not as safe to go much farther but most Muhlenberg students stay around campus. It is certainly a small school and you will get to know most of the people you see on a regular basis. On campus housing is required for all four years which is frustrating but there are house and apartment options owned by the school. The social scene is not that great but if you look for it, there will be options to party.

Muhlenberg is a classic liberal arts school where students who are looking to excel in a number of fields can make meaningful connections with their peers and professors. While the school is best known for its theater program, which has a loud presence on campus, the real treasure this school hides is all of the talent in its smaller departments.
I love this school. its close to lots of activities which is fun and also the classes are really interesting.

I got a really good education. That's it. 'Berg taught me to think critically and to embrace the perspectives of those who aren't like me. I felt proud of my academic achievements and how I grew as a student and intellectual. Everything else, though, was awful. I never felt so lonely and socially isolated as I did during my four years at Muhlenberg. There was a sense of entitlement that swept through campus and permeated every social situation I found myself in. Navigating these situations was next to impossible, as the politics of the student body had to be memorized just as much as the stuff for your classes. I felt that I had to forcibly mold myself into what I thought a Muhlenberg student should be- and that was not a version of myself I was ever proud of. I was almost relieved when we all got sent home for COVID, because I got a break from feeling like such a phony in order to fit in. If you can fake it for 4 years, go for it. If not, go to a college that will accept you for you.

I had trouble getting adjusted to the campus culture at first but once I was able to I found a community that really helped me develop into a better version of myself. I had some amazing and super intelligent professors that were always willing to support me and my academic journey. The food is advertised as the number one dining hall in Pennsylvania and that was not my truth, but I do appreciate that it could have been worse.

I attended Muhlenberg College for my freshman year, and I made many friends and played on the tennis team. I enjoyed my experience, but was forced to withdraw after a raise in my tuition made it impossible for me to afford to attend. At Muhlenberg, I studied English and Film Studies, both of which I enjoyed, but wished there were more opportunities in those fields, both in class and outside.

Studying at Muhlenberg, I think, was kind of a mistake; the programs I ended graduating from were under-supported by the administration, and thus the quality of my education suffered. I really enjoyed my experiences with the Music Department, despite the chronic lack of support. The Theatre Department was a bit of a mess, and the fact that they do not offer Theatre minors shows they are more concerned with preserving their inflated budgets over the education of their students. The school placed undue emphasis on athletics; most students can and will go their entire 4 years without seeing a single football game, which calls into question as to why they get so much funding. HRL was a pain to work for, as they do not cover room & board as a part of your employment. Finally, the administrators and the board of trustees seem ludicrously disconnected from the campus; they focus so much time, money, and effort on fundraising than engaging with students and improving their experiences.

Muhlenberg was a complete culture shock but when you search and find your community you can make the best of your experience. I started off isolated from people and the campus community until joining certain communities and incoming emerging leaders student that allowed me to make easier connections.

Muhlenberg provides a safe space for students to be themselves and connect with others in the community. Professors are really great and you can tell they are on our side. This being said, the housing is not the greatest and it is pretty expensive. There is also no option for off campus housing as a full time student (unless you’re a commuter).

I attended Muhlenberg because I was expecting a tight-knit community which I was looking for. Unfortunately, as time went on, I learned through personal experiences that this school attracts narcissists which is a shame there are so many talented people on this campus.

ai_summary: Oberlin College offers a unique college experience characterized by a strong sense of community and a commitment to progressive values. Students appreciate the supportive and engaged professors , small class sizes, and diverse extracurricular activities, which foster both academic rigor and personal growth. However, concerns about administrative decisions—especially regarding housing shortages and the quality of dining services—are prevalent. While many students thrive in the vibrant, artistic atmosphere and express satisfaction with the collaborative environment , some express dissatisfaction with the administration's responsiveness to student needs and the overall quality of life on campus. Despite its challenges, Oberlin remains a cherished choice for students seeking a liberal arts education within a close-knit community.

reviews.text: Great academics and professors. Most of the programs are highly competitive and teach you a lot of valuable information and provide you with many opportunities.

This is just a heads up to any young ladies considering the voice major which is nationally marketed: I've been in a successful opera career for 20 years, despite my experience at Oberlin. This school's ratio of men to women makes it next to impossible for talented women to get the performance experience marketed by the department. You're lucky to perform a solo after two years of paying for these facilities. The imbalance also unnecessarily strains relationships between classmates in the major since the school will allow women to believe it's an issue of talent or work instead of overpopulation of the major. The ratio that they expect to make it in the career on any level is 1 out of 10. When I found out that the faculty was casual about this ratio, it was shocking. My graduate program accepted far fewer singers and voices that would never have stood out at Oberlin went on to major careers after receiving the coaching and attention they deserved and were paying for.

Oberlin college feels fairly isolated, decently far from Cleveland, but the community within the school seems fairly close. The town is nice. I don't love the dorm room quality. My professors respect their students and the students enjoy each other as well.
So far, so good. Most faculty (specifically not teachers, but places like the Center for Student Success) are nice. Food overall is good, average.

I've loved my time at Oberlin. I've learned so much about myself and the world from the people and professors I've met here. The best things about Oberlin are the community, the relatively open requirements for courses, and the small student-teacher ratio. I'm a theater major and love the open-ended approach to theater, which teaches you initiative while giving you a well-rounded theater education and challenging you about the social impact of theater. The community here is amazing and you're encouraged to be your own weird self, which I love. I feel safe and supported as a trans student here, and I admire the amount of dialogue that exists between the school and the students at the school.

One of the things I love most about Oberlin is the professors. Because of the small class sizes, they really get to know you and genuinely care about how you’re doing both academically and personally. It makes such a big difference and has helped me find a lot of cool opportunities. I also really appreciate how easy it is to explore different interests here. I didn’t feel pressured to figure everything out right away, and I’ve been able to try out so many things before deciding what I want to do. Plus, even though I’m not a music major, I’ve still been able to be part of the music scene, which is such a huge part of Oberlin life

Good academics, amazing professors. The classes are rigorous. They're also building up some programs with good degree return on investment (business, financial economics) with real success and strong placements (Goldman, JPM, UBS) This and CS is all this school really has to offer. Do not come here if you are low income and have other opportunities, this school is full of rich snobs. If you got bread you're fine, but idk why you would spend more than 20k a year for this school. The school food had improved since I've been there. My freshman year, you would literally find maggots in every meal (not even joking). Athletics teams are cliquey. The dorms are horrendous, you have to be ready to move everything you own in so your room has personality. Party scene is horrendous besides maybe 1 or 2 good parties a year. You run out of things to do in the town. Genuinely the only good thing is you're with other smart people who are open-minded- for the most part. It will be a tough 4 years.

As a transfer student, I really love the campus culture and the town here. I've met so many amazing people, and have founds clubs in philosophy, politics, and tennis that are really amazing communities!

I am a current student at Oberlin, and I can confirm our academic and student life is unlike any other. It is hard to separate Oberlin college from the Conservatory- both share and inherent connection to music which informs all aspects of campus life. Our house parties have jazz bands. Almost all campus events have some kind of live music. We have more music performances in a year that we do days (500 to be exact!). Academic life is competitive but supportive. Professor are passionate, almost all faculty in all departments are kind and helpful. The campus itself is influenced by a large population of students from big cities which, when coupled with Oberlin's native small-town charm, create a very unique campus environment. The community is the perfect dialectic of a homey atmosphere and a place where you never run out of fun things to do.

I just finished my first year at Oberlin and I absolutely loved my experience. Everyone was so friendly, the concerts and events were super fun (I really loved the pottery sale), and I met some of the most fabulous people. It was the best I could have ever asked for.
I wasn't all good, of course. Although I didn't have complaints about the quality of the dorm (I'm generally satisfied, but maybe it's because I've only spent one year here), I was not really satisfied with how the Office of Residential Life manages things. Sometimes it could be a little bit chaotic, which required me to send many many emails to solve easy problems. However, what made my Oberlin experience perfect for me was that there are things so great that some drawbacks didn't matter. The professors are super passionate, and they genuinely care about your interests and how you do inside and outside the class. I always went to office hours and we would talk about everything. Literally everything.

I'm going into my Senior year at Oberlin College. What I like to communicate is the theme of "not being stuck" at Oberlin. With housing, say you're with a roommate that you don't see eye to eye with, you can fill out a Room Change request form (not always a guarantee, but at least it's there). With the major, you don't have to declare until the end of your sophomore year, so you have that flexibility to pursue different interests and find what's right for you. In terms of campus, I have never felt stuck or suffocated on campus. I'm from a small town, so Oberlin was actually a step up in terms of how busy things were. However, if I do want to get off of campus, I can. There are different transportation services for students, but more simply you'll most likely be friends with someone with a car if you don't have one. This idea of not being stuck in different scenarios has made Oberlin very welcoming for me and is one thing I'd want prospective students to take away.

I had a great experience at Oberlin. Most of my professors really cared about me and cared about teaching, which is not something that is guaranteed at other schools sadly. This is speaking from experience as I did the 3+2 Engineering track and am now at a very research heavy school. The dining hall food is not great, the dorms are a bit sketchy at times, and the sports teams stink, but the people, the town, the atmosphere, the energy and the charm of Oberlin pull it all together. You'll definitely find your people here and learn a lot about yourself.

Oberlin’s campus is small but safe and tight-knit. There is a large variety of food options. Housing is very outdated and sometimes gross. The conservatory is an inspiring place, a rigorous but not toxic environment filled with passionate people.

I'm a parent of a rising senior at Oberlin, and I have one main criticism - Oberlin's housing is terrible.
Oberlin admitted about 50% too many students to my child's class (of 2026), which has created a housing shortage on campus. To make matters worse, Oberlin decided to reduce the number of students who are allowed to live off campus.
My child will now need to share a double for their senior year, and they won't be able to live near their friends. Sharing a double room is fine for first and second year students, but my child has been in a double their junior year, and now will be in one senior year.
Oberlin is doing this, I believe, to make money - students who live off campus do not pay room and board. So, the College is charging my family more so that my daughter can live in a double for her senior year. This is unfair, and terrible policy.
Students have been petitioning the administration to change it, but so far, their requests have been ignored.

I’ve been at Oberlin College for three years, and my experience has been overwhelmingly negative. The school consistently over-enrolls students without having enough housing, yet they refuse to grant off-campus approvals. This leaves students scrambling, stressed, and often without a place to stay, forcing them to spend extra money on overpriced, inadequate housing. The food is just as bad. The meal plan is expensive, and the quality is terrible. For a school that prides itself on being progressive, it does little to actually support students who are struggling financially. On top of that, the Title IX department is a complete mess. They fail to handle cases properly, and their lack of organization and accountability makes it clear that they don’t prioritize student safety or well-being. Socially, Oberlin is full of privileged, out-of-touch nepo babies who claim to care about social justice but don’t actually do anything meaningful. The performative activism here is exhausting.

ai_summary: Occidental College offers a unique liberal arts education in a vibrant Los Angeles setting , attracting students who appreciate close-knit academic and social communities . Many reviews highlight the strength of faculty-student relationships, with professors being approachable and genuinely invested in students' success. The small class sizes foster personalized learning experiences , though some students felt that not all professors were equally effective. While the academic programs are often praised, there are concerns about limited choices in majors and research opportunities, particularly in STEM fields. The campus atmosphere is described as supportive and involved, yet social life can be perceived as cliquey and less vibrant compared to larger universities. Many students enjoy the diverse extracurricular options and often participate in off-campus activities to supplement their college experience. However, critiques exist regarding administrative effectiveness and the high cost of tuition, which some feel does not align with the overall experience given the institution's size and resources. The dining options are frequently noted as a positive aspect of campus life, while concerns about accessibility and safety on campus have also been raised. Overall, students who thrive in intimate, engaging environments and seek strong faculty connections may find Occidental College a fitting choice.

reviews.text: not accessible at ALL if you break your leg here you're effed. very few elevators soo many stairs and if you call campus safety...they'll laugh in your face. Very nice campus and professors tho!

Had a great experience at Occidental before I transferred. Professors truly care about you and the discussion-based classes are great. I loved the sense of community. Many cool research opportunities on campus, and of course the weather is great and the food is amazing. That being said, it was ridiculously expensive and felt very cliquey in terms of social life which I did not love. Since it is a smaller school, your options are pretty limited, in terms of sports, jobs, clubs, social groups, etc. I would definitely recommend this school to someone who knows they can afford it all four years and who is passionate about the arts. If paying for it is going to be a reach, I recommend staying with your most affordable option. Often, large, in-state schools with tons of people, clubs, sports, and research opportunities can be just as incredible and academically fulfilling. I wish I had realized this sooner but I am still grateful I got to experience both. Good luck on the college search!

Occidental is anything but an environment conducive to school, social life, or anything meaningful for that matter. Its student body is comprised of people that walk around half-naked, smoking cigarettes, judging you from a mile away, all while simultaneously being some of the most elitist, entitled, and close-minded people you will meet. This school is branded as a rigorous liberal arts institution but I would argue it is anything but that. Many students and some faculty alike believe they know all, and that anything outside the California bubble is merely uneducated and frowned upon. News flash, not everyone is rich, lives in California, or indulges in the same overly lavish lifestyle as you. Other important points worth mentioning are that people do not stay on campus (because there is such a lack of community and on-campus programming) but rather venture off to insanely expensive restaurants, clubs, parties, you name it, so long as their social status and ego is being boosted.

In my experience at Occidental College, the school puts emphasis on inclusivity and growth in various environments. Whether it is in the classroom, within the world of athletics, or in the campus social scene, everyone maintains an open-to-grow mindset, welcoming students from all different backgrounds. In my first year, I deeply appreciated the opportunities I had to learn and grow from the diverse body of students, always meeting someone new hence adding a new layer of personal understanding for the world. I am eager to continue to expand the connections I have made thus far, as well as advancing my academic career within an open-minded college environment.

Oxy was amazing for my niche, but I may not recommend it for everyone. Generally there are so many amazing opportunities like undergraduate research, internships, field experience (campaign semester and UN program), but you have to choose to seek out these opportunities. The faculty are amazing, and though I found the courses to be pretty easy, they are still very interesting and engaging. Do not come here if you are looking for a traditional college party scene, to be honest the campus night life and 'school spirit' leave much to be desired, but there is still really good nightlife in the LA area to explore away from campus. Lastly, be very careful about coming here if you are recruited for a sport, there is a documented pattern of teams over recruiting athletes and then cutting many of them once they're already stuck at the school--take the things a coach tells you with a grain of salt. With that all said, I loved my time at Occidental, great people and great academics.

I studied Computer Science with minors in Classical Studies and Kinesiology. As a Liberal Arts school, much focus is placed on interdisciplinary studies, so I received a complete and well rounded coverage of topics outside of STEM and typical sciences. This complemented my educational and personal passions well.
My experience at Oxy was awesome! Loved my academic experience, being an athlete, and the overall care and connection in the Oxy community.

I've really enjoyed my time at Occidental College so far. The small class sizes make the professors accessible, and the professors are committed to helping students and making their learning experiences the best that they can be.

Occidental was a terrible college to attend. Other students would talk about me and were not friendly at all. I would have changed colleges but felt stuck once I got in. I had a roommate that bailed on me without notice to room with another student and got word she was talking trash about me. Professors were rude. I would work so hard and barely make Cs on my essays. One made me cry. The campus is tiny. Never made any friends and never felt supported. Had some trauma happen and nobody cared. Once you graduate, they track you. You could move a million times and they figure out where you live and ask for donations nonstop! Lots of druggies. If you don’t partake, you’re ridiculed. I didn’t partake and so I was considered the weirdo. I saw a puzzle outside one of the math professors office and commented that it looked hard and was curious how to solve. Her reply was so snarky, like you figure it out! Verdict is this was a horrible school. Don’t ruin your life with PTSD like I have now.

Being such a small school, Occidental gives me the opportunity to be supported by professors and develop relationships with them and peers in the classroom. Hard to get all required classes sometimes, food is great but dining is expensive if you eat often
The professors and courses offered at this college are incredible. I was able to make extremely helpful connections with faculty. The food is pretty good, and the dorms are nice enough.

Occidental College has been a great place to start my college career. The professors are genuinely invested in their courses and subjects respectfully. While one would expect professors to be less involved in class due to Oxy being a research-campus, I haven't seen this play out in class so far. Everyone is genuinely helpful, and there are many opportunities to take advantage of. Just be careful to be exploring off-campus options and activities, as it can be easy to feel stuck or 'stranded' out here.

I have visited and taken a tour of Occidental College and fell in love with it. The staff and professors were amiable, and helpful, and provided lots of useful information. The campus is beautiful and the school offers lots of great resources and activities for students.
I think Occidental College is a great place for getting hands on experience in the field of your choosing! The only complaint I have is how expensive it can be to go to Occidental.

It's a good school. It is pretty diverse and pretty politically left wing. I think most critiques that can be levied toward it also apply to most other private liberal arts colleges of similar sizes. Campus is pretty, but very difficult to navigate if you are a wheelchair user. Its hate speech and Title IX policies are much better than my high schools.

ai_summary: Ohio Wesleyan University (OWU) offers a close-knit , supportive environment due to its smaller campus size, which many students appreciate for fostering personal interactions with professors and peers. The faculty are frequently noted as dedicated and accessible, providing quality education and encouragement. However, critiques arise around administrative issues, particularly concerning the dining hall food quality , which has received negative remarks for being unhealthy and inconsistent, with reports of foodborne illnesses. Additionally, while OWU has vibrant opportunities for travel and internships, students express concerns about the lack of diversity in both the student body and course offerings. The campus's safety is also a point of contention, with some students reporting incidents that raise concerns about campus security . Overall, while students value the academic and community aspects of OWU, there are significant areas, particularly around administration and student services, that require improvement.

reviews.text: I like how small my class room sizes were in regards to student. The small size made interpersonal relationships with your professors possible and allowed students to build tangle relationships/mentorship with their professors.

My experience while sadly short was one I’ll take with me with the rest of my life. I loved the connections I made with professors and students alike and the amount of opportunities available there. I do wish there was better food or course but mainly for me specifically a more reliable source of mental health care.
I think the environment is very great in this University and the professors are very helpful which I liked.

There is nothing currently that I would like to see change with the university. It is a highly enriching environment with plenty of opportunity to advance one's career with a great community and support system.
I appreciate OWU’s professors, athletics, students, and school programs for their support and opportunities.

My overall experience with Ohio Wesleyan University has been good. I have made very good friends. I enjoy the acdemics I have had good professors and I made the deans list last semester and my goal is to make the deans list the rest of my time here at OWU. I enjoy my softball team and have formed a close bond with all of my teammates. I am especially close with my friend Emily on my softball team and we have already requested to live together next year along with her twin sister. All in all I am enjoying my time here and I am truly blessed.

The CS and Math department at OWU is very small but has great professors. The school has great resources for career development if you explore them (Travel learning, Connection Grants, etc..). Since it's a small college in a relatively quite empty town, there is not a lot to do here, however, I have made close friends and it feels easier to get to know people.

OWU is amazing for travel opportunities and research! The main issues with the school are administrative. Our dining hall is rated as having the worst private school food in the U.S. by the Princeton review and there have been multiple hospitalizations because of it while I’ve been on campus. Also our current President thinks he’s a tik tok influencer and I’m fairly certain he posted a thirst trap on an OWU affiliated instagram page, which is wild. OMSA does a lot of really great work though, and the travel opportunities are fantastic.

I have had an overall mixed time at Ohio Wesleyan University, I enjoyed the classes I took and the professors however I really did not enjoy the student life aspect. The student life was really geared towards a specific type of student which did not match overall with who I am. I would love to see an improved communication department and a speech and debate team added for the university.

So far, I've enjoyed my time at OWU. A lot of criticisms I've seen of this school I simply do not mind or have not experienced here yet but I did take some of them into consideration in writing my review of this school. The professors I have this semester have done a great job in describing and running through their topics and answering any questions I have about any problems I couldn't solve on homeworks and quizzes. I think very highly of the school and a lot of the students here seem very serious about their academics and want to achieve a dream job in their field one day. I hope the same for myself.
The professors and classes are great, but the Ancient Medival Renaissance courses, and English courses are shrinking. Also the food is usually pretty bad.

Ohio Weseleyan Univesity (OWU) is definitely not the college for everyone. There are many ups and downs when it comes to professors leaving, the dining hall food being sometimes inedible, and the inconsistency in faculty responses to issues such a financial aid, residential life, and other pressing issues. For those who are willing to advocate for themselves in such a small environment, OWU is great place. The professors are willing to meet with you to give extra attention when it comes to your courses, as long as you are willing to meet with them in compromise. The small campus leads to gossip and drama, but those who know how to navigate this somewhat hostile environment will definitely succeed. Study abroad programs and and Theory to Practice Grants allow students to work on the niche interests that they want to explore deeper.

Overall, the academics were alright. I felt somewhat challenged, I had a really good relationship with my professor/ advisor personally, but I know many who did not have good experiences. The campus food was HORRIBLE. Students were sent to the ER for food poisoning due to unchecked food regulations such as not taking temperatures of food to make ensure they are within the correct temperature ranges for consumption. Their options overall were bad as well, they had almost no healthy options. Their food sensitivity sections were contaminated and foods were mislabeled to be sensitive when they were not. Housing is a nightmare. Not only is the process flawed, but it’s expensive, especially for not having amenities. Campus safety has trouble keeping students safe and have proved their main priority is parking infractions. On many occasions, strangers have encroached on campus and sexually harassed students. They do little to prevent incidents and only act if/when a 2nd incident occurs.

I have loved my time at Ohio Wesleyan University. But I do not think this school is providing everything that they should. The food is consistently not edible and causes many students to get sick. The dorms are also not well-kept or nice at all. We are also forced to live on campus for all 4 years so we don't have any other options but to live in the dorms. I do think that the academics are really good and I love my professors and the small class sizes.

In my experience, this is a wonderful small school in a small town for those that don't care for big cities or partying. The professors are excellent, and the small class sizes make for a personal connection between students and experts. Many people here do multiple majors and love to challenge themselves. It's not very competitive, most people don't worry about others in that way. The food is not good, and the housing situations could certainly be better. The Wifi is iffy and inconsistent, and the tech support is not helpful. The sidewalks in Delaware are awful, which is something to take note of if you like skating.

ai_summary: Patrick Henry College offers a unique blend of rigorous academics and a strong, Christ-centered community, making it an appealing choice for students seeking both educational and spiritual growth. The professors are consistently described as caring and engaging, providing individualized attention to students. The small class sizes foster meaningful interactions, and the close-knit student body contributes to a supportive atmosphere where lasting friendships are formed. While the college emphasizes a strong classical education grounded in a biblical worldview, it also encourages critical thinking and spirited discussions. Although it may lack a vibrant party scene, students highlight the enjoyable opportunities for social engagement and deep conversations, often emphasizing a culture of friendship and mentorship . With its focus on academic excellence, faith, and community, Patrick Henry College is often likened to a "Christian Ivy League" that prepares students for future careers while fostering their personal faith journeys.

reviews.text: The professors are wonderful! They are engaging and quick to respond to questions. I received answers just hours after I emailed them. They are invested in each student and are willing to read your work and give comments before you submit it. Their library staff is amazing and is always ready to help. They have a huge variety of books both on campus and online. Their prices for textbooks are very competitive and it is easy to order the required textbooks from them.

Patrick Henry is an amazing school that truly cares about its students. The professors are incredible and caring, the other students are friendly, and the admissions team is amazing. This school is academically rigorous and spiritually strong. Patrick Henry is a college where anyone serious about their education will thrive.

Fabulous - no substantive improvements necessary. I have enjoyed this college immensely, and have no motive to transfer to another. I have particularly enjoyed the rigor of the academic program, the quality of the professors and the class instruction, and the intelligence and maturity of most of the student body.

As a junior at Patrick Henry College, I have had a great experience. The academics are challenging, but I have grown in my knowledge of my major and of the world. What I appreciate about PHC is how they combine their biblical worldview with their academic rigor. Every class we take at PHC begins from a Christian perspective. The professors at PHC help students to build their worldviews from that foundation. Because students and faculty have a strong focus on their faith, the Christ-centered community has also been a huge blessing in my growth as a Christian woman. I could walk up to anyone on campus to ask for prayer and they would be willing to pray over me. My friends love God and push me to love Him more. I could not ask for a better place to have spent these last three years.
Patrick Henry College challenged me to grow academically and spiritually. The school hosts a community of students, staff, and faculty dedicated to pursuing excellence and glorifying God.
Patrick Henry College is a place with many crazy, passionate, yet thoroughly loving people who have taught me so much.

I've so appreciated Patrick Henry's commitment to cultivating character in its students. It's equipped me to better tackle cultural issues and understand how my faith answers some of the most challenging questions life has to offer. Patrick Henry offers unique professor/student mentor relationships that further enabled me to interact with the material I absorbed from my courses and deepen my walk with the Lord. I've had the privilege to call many peers close friends and look forward to maintaining those relationships post-graduation. Most importantly, PHC has given me a deeper purpose for life and greater joy with which to serve the Lord.

The professors here are intentional and qualified. The community is exceptional. I highly recommend looking into Patrick Henry College as you search for a place to continue your education after high school.

I greatly appreciate the professors and academics at Patrick Henry College. The professors encourage me to do my best work, and they challenge me to explore new things and debate different topics. The academics are phenomenal because they allow me to study with an open mind and to learn things that I would not otherwise learn at another school.

The professors and students are very kind and polite. They expect much of the students and push us to succeed. It's a lot of work, and not for everyone, but it's great for those who like a bigger workload and being pushed to succeed.

Patrick Henry is obviously a very small college. For some that would be a downside. My experience is that this feature is one of its greatest strengths. Having a small, likeminded community is absolutely invaluable to the formation of Christian character, and I have found myself strengthen in my faith, challenged by my peers and mentors, and encouraged to pursue the Lord in excellence and love.

I love Patrick Henry College so much!! My mom graduated from there and she had such an amazing experience. She was in one of the first graduating classes, and she was the first public school student to attend. If you are looking for a tight-knit body of believers with high educational standards this is the place for you. They are like a small Christian Ivy-league school. They have beat Oxford and several US Ivy leagues in debate, and have many prestigious alumni. I have not met people that love the Lord more than these students. You will make so many life long friends here. Even though we live halfway across the country, my mom’s college friends are still our closest friends. We visit each other every year. I have attended teen leadership camps at PHC, and they were some of the best experiences I have ever had. I hope to attend as a student one day. I know that PHC isn’t for everyone, but if any of those things I mentioned are what you’re looking for, you’ll love it. ❤️

I had a great experience at Patrick Henry College! I attended from 2006-2010. I built lifelong friendships, learned from incredible professors, grew in my faith, and met my wife. Upon graduating, I became part of a robust alumni network that I rely on heavily now for finding employees for my small business. The Patrick Henry College "product" is a hard-working, excellence-seeking, God-honoring, others-loving, initiative-taking, well-read, well-spoken, clear-thinking, community-building servant leader in every area of their life.
