That's who I strive to be, and that's the sort of person I prize highly as an employer. My faith- and humanities-focused education has provided me with a firm foundation for my life and work, and I am eternally grateful to Patrick Henry College for that!

My experience at Patrick Henry College has been spiritually edifying and intellectually enriching. The professors are well qualified, share a wealth of knowledge from a solid biblical worldview, and show genuine care for each of their students. The coursework has pushed me to improve greatly in my academic skills.
I have also been greatly blessed by the student body. The community is very close-knit, and students are very serious about serving the Lord and intentional about encouraging one another.

Patrick Henry College is the perfect place to grow in both education and character. From the students, professors, and other personnel, everyone is very welcoming and there are a lot of ways to get plugged in. From student activities to watch parties in the longue, there is no shortage of opportunities to meet new people. Professors are also invested in the growth of individuals, academically and spiritually. Discipleship and mentoring are offered here for students who desire to grow in the Lord and want to learn from more mature and wise individuals. The food here is also pretty good, at least better than other places. Dormitories getting renovated and seeing a lot of improvement over time as well. Highly recommend!
Please, read the critical reviews, they'll encompass the bad, and common parts of the experience here.

Pitzer is one of the few schools that actually feels unique. The core values are not abstract concepts you read when looking at promotional material, rather they shape everyday student life. Students and faculty share a real commitment to learning, community impact, and supporting one another. On top of that, the campus itself is beautifully funky! There are also strong resources for students from many different backgrounds, which makes a meaningful difference in feeling supported here.
That said, Pitzer is still a PWI, and that brings real administrative and social challenges that are important to acknowledge. Overall, my experience has been complex but deeply positive at its core. If you are someone who cares about social responsibility and environmental sustainability, it is definitely worth looking into Pitzer.

I love my time at Pitzer. Though there are some drawbacks, every other school around the country also has their own issues as no college or university is ever perfect. Pitzer is the kind of school that offers a wide range of classes, really unique experiences that you can't get at other schools (taking classes at Norco's rehab center with incarcerated students or CASA - Critical Action and Social Advocacy), the shared resources with the rest of the consortium, and just honestly a really great group of students who are passionate, advocative, socially responsible, you get the gist. It's a good balance between academics and social life and for those that lean more on one or the other there is that as well. And out of the Claremont colleges, I find professors here the most understanding/caring towards their students. Students tend to connect better and foster genuine curiosity with their profs and amazing to see those blossom into changes on campus/surrounding communities.

Pitzer is amazing! Such a happy environment that fosters a tight-knit community. Academics are fantastic, especially in the Humanities department. Professors are easy to access, and all my profs so far have been very experienced in their subjects. There's always something to do on weekends, and the campus feels very lively at night. Dining food is delicous and we can eat at any of the 5C colleges, which is nice. Students are involved in multiple clubs and sports; as cliché as it sounds, they care about making the world a better place while in highschool. Students have very diverse majors at Pitzer, lots of STEM, lots of Humanities. LOVE this place.

I am in my second year at Pitzer College and it is the best decision I have ever made. The small environment ensures you have a strong community on campus. If Pitzer ever starts to feel too small there are 4 other distinct LAC right nextdoor and taking classes on other campuses is a great way to make connections and find professors who specialize in your interests (even if they are pretty niche!). The Pitzer Orientation Adventure program does a great job of helping new students make friends and explore the opportunites of the 5cs and LA county. My favorite thing about Pitzer is how passionate everyone around me is, it inspires me to try new things and make the most of my college experience!

I had the best time at Pitzer. There's so much I could say but I think the bottom line is that it's a school where people are generally very happy, academically motivated, and supported by the school. Academically the classes are engaging, with a real mix of different kinds of professors and teaching styles. You'll get very fun classes, as well as your standard intro courses. Socially people are outgoing and very friendly, with lots of friends across the 5Cs. Being part of the consortium is a lot of fun, especially because it gives it such a good party scene for a small school. Also shout-out a great career office and amazing on-campus employment opportunities.
I came from a very rigorous high school where I felt a lot of competition and pressure and Pitzer was nothing like that. I felt pushed to do well and grow but in a very friendly and collaborative way. Genuinely the best decision I ever made and I miss it so much!

it's full of bigots, also really nice people, but just scan through TSL (Claremont student newspaper) and filter for Pitzer, tire slashings, stalking/police impersonation, harassment. I don't know how this works given Pitzer's values but there are dishonest and surprising aspects of the school. The amount of people who try to say otherwise despite how obvious it is, makes it worse.

I love Pitzer!! I have met the most amazing friends that have changed my life. Love them so much lol. All my professors are very accomodating. Housing is great, but I marked it down because the housing selection process is awful, but ended up working out mostly for everyone I'm close with. The food should not be a B?? I am abroad right now & I miss our dining hall food!! Niche definitely needs to up it to an A+, especially as we have access to all 7 dining halls on campus with our meal plan!! My academics are great but whats incredible is access to classes at all 5Cs's. Though there are some classes at schools like CMC or Pomona that are more exclusive, Sophomore year I took only 3 at Pitzer, 3 at CMC and 2 at Scripps! It is so interesting to take classes at each college and get a different vibe from each. For example, learning Economics/Politics at CMC vs Scripps provides me with two very different perspectives. Btw, I do wish Pitzer had the same resources as CMC tho, but I love PZ!

I have loved my time at PZ. I know there's a preconceived notion that PZ students don't apply themselves academically when compared to other schools but after taking classes at the other 4C's and transferring from a top-10 university, I have to disagree. PZ classes have had some of the most lively discussions of all the campuses and people are incredibly interested in super-niche topics. I think more than the schools, PZ students tend to have more outside hobbies, and are especially outdoorsy which some people think means that we're hippies or disorganized but that hasn't been my experience. I think PZ as an institution strives to create well-rounded people rather than just good employees. The professors deeply care about their students and their lives. They're passionate about their respective fields and eager to help you. There are plenty of clubs and multiple parties each weekend. Pitzer truly is a college where you get out what you put in. If you use your resources, you succeed.

One of the most annoying things, I think someone in the reviews suggested as well, is that, whenever there's an issue it's kind of like blamed on something involving income status. So if there's like a hate issue, it's blamed on some students not having enough money, like it's worked into the reason for the issue somehow despite having nothing to do with what occurred. However, at Pitzer, it's not only an annoying thing, but I think how the school works. Financial aid students are like second class students here, and there's an imbalance in accountability probably because of the school's budget and reliance on tuition.

After taking a few classes across the four Claremont Colleges, it became clear to me why Pitzer’s academics are often ranked among the lowest. Many of my classmates were unwilling to put in the necessary effort; when professors set reasonable expectations, they complained rather than engaging with the material. This dynamic makes it harder for students who genuinely want to learn, as professors often lower their standards to accommodate them. In courses with students from other Claremont Colleges, the gap in academic preparation and willingness to take initiative was even more apparent. I wish I had chosen a more academically rigorous school and surrounded myself with more ambitious and hard working peers.

Pitzer has been a phenomenal fit for me. I love having the opportunity to connect with my professors and get the classes I want, and the consortium makes for a really fun social scene. I appreciate how Pitzer prioritizes quality of life things- there are tons of clubs that get you outside, the dorms are nice, food is excellent, and there are tons of supports available. I’d definitely recommend another school if you want a big party school or sports but Pitzer is otherwise lovely and I couldn’t imagine spending my undergrad years anywhere else.

I've had a great time at Pitzer. I LOVE the consortium, and being able to branch out/take classes at other schools. My one biggest complaint is the Limited Campus life and lack of school-specific community.

Pitzer connected me to a great community and allowed me to form great relationships with my professors. However it should be noted that though Pitzer touts themselves as a diverse and socially conscious school, they admit a shockingly low amount of black, indigenous and international students. They struggle to provide financial aid as well, which results in a cohort from a very similar financial demographic.

Pitzer is a very diverse school allowing students to explore all their interests. I also like the flexibility of using practical training as part of classroom learning. I searched for a summer internship with the local District Attorney's office and was able to turn it into an independent study class for credit! I also did school year internships in local elementary schools! Best of all, I have made life long friends...20+ years strong! Loved it!

ai_summary: Pomona College offers a distinctly vibrant and engaging college experience characterized by a supportive and intellectually stimulating community . Many reviews highlight small class sizes and the accessibility of professors, fostering strong mentorship and personal connections between students and faculty. The campus is described as beautiful and located in a desirable area, with the added benefit of being part of the Claremont College Consortium, which expands academic and social opportunities. While some students express frustration with career preparation and the administration, others appreciate the abundant resources for academic support , diversity initiatives, and student engagement in social and political issues. Yet, there are mixed feelings regarding the campus culture , with some describing it as elitist or insular. Ultimately, students feel that Pomona’s strengths in academics, unique community, and supportive environment make it a worthwhile choice, though prospective students should be aware of its limitations, particularly in areas like social life and certain academic opportunities.

reviews.text: Pomona College is a part of a College Consortium, so don't be worried about not having a life. Most professors at Pomona genuinely want their students to succeed. They don't want to make your life harder they simply want you to be able to learn and retain information. As for the party scene there are plenty of parties however I don't know how they are since I haven't attended a party. In terms of diversity, there is diversity and we have several affinity groups and spaces. The students here may not be the most empathetic or humble, but it could be worse. I'm sure you can find your little space here. Also, the housing department isn't very helpful here so keep that in mind when applying for housing and making changes throughout the year.

Really glad I got admitted and accepted Pomona. The people are really nice. The campus is beautiful and I really enjoy the small class size and being apart of the 5C. In my opinion pomona college doesn’t have the best dining out of the 5C, it’s in the middle. There is grade inflation and lots of events going on on campus. Some of the dorms don’t have AC so that’s disappointing. I like the sunny SoCal weather. Commute to LA is a little far but possible.

This was an incredible place for me to attend school. I recently came on to niche, as I want others to know excellent schools. This college shaped my entire future, healed me of wounds through learning, analyzing and not being afraid to ask questions that are beyond the scope of the material.

Overall, if you want to actually meet and know people on campus, this is the school to go to. The best part: Pomona provides me everything I need while the 5C’s provide me everything I want. Find your path and you’ll love it here.

Professors and classes are excellent if you are willing to put in the work to find the good classes and professors and working to build and maintain relationships. Some majors are definitely harder than others, like the math department but it is very good. Party scene is not very good, but good thing is it's connected to the other five colleges so it feels like a bigger school. Food at Pomona is bad, go to other school's dining halls which are pretty close by.

My experience at Pomona College was academically challenging, intellectually rewarding, and personally transformative. The small class sizes and close relationships with professors created a truly collaborative environment where I felt supported in both my academic and personal growth. The liberal arts approach helped me explore a wide range of disciplines, and I appreciated the freedom to design my own academic path.

Being a student at Pomona is like bowling with the bumper guards-- you still have to consistently be ready to try, but the school will do almost anything to help you get where you want to go. With amazing support programs, so much funding poured back into students and their quality of life, and an amazing location, I could not be more grateful to be a student there. The affinity groups and mentorship programs are incredible, and the classes offered require you to really develop critical thinking skills that really will help you no matter where you go post-grad. Great gym/athletic facilities even if the teams aren't so good, and amazing dining hall food. If you are ready to have a small college experience, being at Pomona as a part of the consortium is an opportunity like no other.

I love Pomona College. I am heavily involved in the chem and math departments here, and wow, the professors in both departments are incredible. I love them so much. I chose Pomona because I wanted to form valuable relationships with professors, and as a freshman, I already feel like I've made great connections with the profs here! They are genuinely so caring and kind. I think Pomona is awesome, and everyone is nice. The academics can be stressful— no doubt, but everyone is here to help! Also, I definitely don't feel the intense, cutthroat environment that is usual in STEM environments here. It's very supportive and collaborative. Yay to Pomona academics! I def recommend coming here for Chem and Math, unsure about other fields, but they're probably awesome too :) The worst thing about Pomona is probably the party scene here lmao. Def depressing ngl. Attended a few parties with just 30 or so people... but i don't really care about night life. great clubs and opportunities too!

I really like Pomona! It was my dream school and the only one I applied to, actually. I'm very glad I ended up coming here. The area is small, but with the consortium that Pomona is a part of, it feels way bigger. There is so much to do here; you can drive to the beach or to the ice capped mountains within 45 minutes. The teachers are so kind! They'll take you out to dinner, want to get to know you as a real person rather than just a student, and genuinely want you to learn and succeed in their class. You can't get that at a bigger school, really. Also, since the school is so small, there is more opportunities to get research experience in, or other experiences that you need (e.g. performance experience by being in plays or dance shows, etc.). The food is pretty okay and so are the dorms, but the best parts are definitely the people you meet here. I would not want to go elsewhere if I had to choose again.
I enjoyed my time at Pomona. Classes were well taught by the professors and the small class size helped get extra help whenever you needed it.
I loved Pomona. The campus were beautiful. The classes were interesting and challenging. There were myriad opportunities. I just wish it had been more diverse.

Loved it do much! Pomona is such a vibrant college full odlf students faculty and staff who are not only passionate about their subject/work but also about sharing that passion unconditionally woth others.
The administration is a little annoying to deal with, but I have met a lot of great people that I love very dearly.

As time passes, I am increasingly sure that Pomona was the perfect college for me. The professors are caring and passionate, the campus is beautiful, and most of all, each student is incredibly unique with motivations and hobbies that provide constant sources of awe. Our community is diverse in all senses of the word. Prior to college, I had heard from everyone that all Pomona students raved about the school, and this has proven true in my experience -- everyone is SO happy to be here. While no school is without its faults, Pomona's few weaknesses are easy to embrace in the grander scheme of its seemingly infinite benefits. I'm still meeting brilliant and wildly cool people everyday, but rarely do I encounter the egotistical personality that dominates Ivy League schools on the East Coast. COME TO POMONA!
There have been a lot of protests due to controversial political beliefs. There is change to be mad in who is representing pomona. The college itself is good.

I love the small class dynamic. This allows me to develop close relationships with my professor. This also allows me to get the help I need while in class. The only thing I would change is all-day dining. As of right now, we only have selected meal hours, and as a student-athlete, it is hard to make it to those times at some points in the week.

Principia has many positives and negatives. Classes are small, making it easy to get personal attention like a private tutor. The campus is beautiful, surrounded by woods and wildlife, offering a peaceful, relaxing environment. The school encourages you to do your best and provides unique opportunities. However, it can feel overly controlling, with too many restrictive rules that treat students like kids. Dining services struggle with repetitive, low-quality meals, poor options, and inconsistent dietary accommodations. Communication is often unclear or last-minute, and students are expected to accept new tasks or events with little notice. Professors may avoid rigorous coursework to keep students from dropping classes, especially in the performing arts. Frequent move-out requirements between semesters are inconvenient, and campus events receive uneven promotion or recognition.

Principia is a great community, beautiful campus, world class facilities (especially for a D3 school), but it's flaws are too evident. My experience has been great because Prin allows you to focus and if you have a good group of friends, you'll be alright.
But it's also true that the campus is way too isolated, you can't do literally nothing without a car (which really limits options when it comes down to jobs, internships, etc) and they babysit you all day as if you're 13. Also, the student body shrinks by the year and nobody seems to do anything to acknowledge that. The admissions team is a joke as well as most of the staff. And of course, if your student body is less than 300 people everything you do will be gossipped around the next day.
The religious side is way too exaggerated, you only have to take one Bible class in your four years and you're not required to go to church or do any of the activities, and the Christian Scientists only really work with those of the same religion

Principia is honestly a great place. I have had the opportunity to go here for the past 2 years and the experience so far is beyond what I expected. It is a beautiful campus but what I like most about it is the people. It might be a small campus but once you find your people, the campus opens up. There are opportunities to have fun everywhere but also great resources for you to become the person you want to be, especially academically. All in all, being here is a good experience, and I would encourage everyone to experience Principia!

Look, Principia is basically a gorgeous, high-end "bubble" on a cliff that feels more like a supervised retreat than a typical college. The architecture is stunning and the classes are so small you’re basically getting private tutoring, but the trade-off is a social life that feels a bit stunted. Between the strict "dry" campus rules, the isolation of being stuck out in Elsah, and a tiny student body where everyone knows your business by lunch, it can start to feel a lot like High School 2.0. It’s a great deal if you’re looking for a quiet, faith-based environment and a massive scholarship, but if you want any semblance of a traditional, independent "adult" college experience, the restrictions and the "middle school" vibe are going to get old fast.

Principia is great for the focus of character development, with a small community setting surrounded by people who truly care! It is a dry campus but constantly try’s to get students involved, the housing is monitored by RCs as well as RAs
The views and nature on and around the campus are amazing. The professors are great and care about the students. Everything is great, but the athletics and food could be better.

My overall experience with Principia College has been really good. I personally like the small classes that allow more 1-on-1 work with the teachers. Through my first year all of the teachers were there for me and I always felt like a good fit for the school even though I come from a very big Highschool in the city. There are a couple things that could be better though such as price and financial aid as there are some students are here for almost little to nothing, and I am struggling just to make the first payment even though I am having hardships at home.
The community is amazing and their arts and sports programs are outstanding. I am enjoying my time here.

Fantastic experience. Study abroad in Japan for a semester. Study abroad in Europe for a semester. Built and raced a solar-powered vehicle. I was able to participate in a sport, perform in the orchestra, and be a part of student government. I loved the small classes.

The classroom engagement was incredible, as a performing arts student I was able to participate in shows and classroom craft within the first week. I was given opportunities to explore a variety of subjects outside of the arts which was one of the best things about attending a liberal arts college. The campus is beautiful and I gained invaluable work and life experience.

It may sound trite, but I truly enjoyed my education! As a prospective engineering student, I had underestimated the importance of character education, social education, physical education, and writing. My Prin experience prepared a firm foundation for my career as a Technology and Acoustical Consultant.
Principia College provides an excellent liberal arts eduction, while also supporting young Christian Scientists as they embark on the world.

Principia College in an excellent school. I received a wonderful education. I learned to be an independent thinker and problem solver, which also making great friends and taking part in amazing experiences. I was able to travel to Europe for a semester, which was wonderful. What more could a student want out of a school? Prin is a fantastic school with highly dedicated faculty and staff members.

Principia College is an amazing place for any young adult to grow and pursue their goals! Faculty and Staff embrace the students and are able to give them significant attention in small, intimate classes. The campus is rural, 45 minutes outside of St. Louis, but beautifully located on the bluffs of the Mississippi River!
This is a great option for anyone looking for a small, spiritually minded, liberal arts college.

ai_summary: Randolph-Macon College is characterized by its close-knit community , which many students describe as feeling like a second home. The small class sizes foster personal connections with professors, who are noted for their genuine care in helping students succeed academically and professionally. Academically, the college offers a variety of majors and emphasizes a liberal arts education, with opportunities for hands-on experiences early in students' careers. Student life is vibrant, despite a limited party scene, with plenty of involvement opportunities, sports, and events on campus. However, there are consistent concerns regarding inadequate housing conditions and food quality , with many students expressing dissatisfaction about the cafeteria offerings. Overall, while students appreciate the supportive environment and academic rigor, they also highlight the need for improvements in dining and facilities to enhance the overall college experience.

reviews.text: I am a current student-athlete at Randolph-Macon College in Ashland, VA. Overall, my experience has felt like home, with the small student body and connections with all of the other sports. Apart from sports, my professors, small class sizes, and dormmates have makes RMC such a connectable school, making it easy to make friends and get to know people quickly and easily.

Overall my experience at RMC was okay. But, for anyone considering playing baseball there, as a baseball player who did play, I do not recommend playing for Ray Hedrick. He puts on a good act in the beginning. But he is not honest, motivational, or knowledgeable about baseball. Most players think he is bipolar. Things got so bad that players quit the team while I was there and I recently found out that 2 seniors quit the team this year because they didn't want to deal with him anymore. Talk to players before committing.

My experience at Randolph-Macon College has been great so far. I appreciate the strong sense of community and the way students are supported in their goals. It is encouraging to see that everyone is given the opportunity to succeed, whether academically or personally. There are always activities happening on campus, and it is easy to get involved if you choose to participate. The college also has a robust sports program that brings energy and school spirit to the community. If I could suggest one improvement, it would be the food options. Overall, my experience has been very positive.

I am currently enrolled at Randolph Macon. I have greatly enjoyed my time here and it has left a lasting impact on me as a person. I have learned important skills and habits that will help me in the future. Randolph Macon has something for everyone and is a great place to grow.
I had a wonderful experience at RMC. It was small enough of a school to provide individualized education and a wonderful professional relationship with the professors and staff.

So far, I've enjoyed my time at Randolph-Macon a lot! Not only is living on the campus a new experience for me, but the staff and students here are friendly, and I feel safe being on campus. Furthermore, there are a multitude of new experiences for me that broaden and feed into my interests. Notably, having a dorm room to myself feels peaceful since I don't have to worry about being incompatible with my roommate. One thing I would like to see the college improve on is their food. This is because the quality of the food is sacrificed in order to provide more/certain options. On the other hand, they still do well in cycling between the selections of food they provide. Aside from this, RMC does well with its school spirit and maintaining an enjoyable campus life.
Cute college in a small town. The school was good at having small things around campus for students to do.

My time at Randolph-Macon has been nothing short of positive. The small campus, mixed with the small class sizes is a great network into making deep connections with not only fellow students, but also professors. Outside of campus is the town of Ashland, within town, there is plenty to do. Local mom-pop shops, plenty of restaurant's, food stops, and so much more. Randolph-Macon also has a great athletic program. So many club sports, DIII teams, and intramurals definitely keep athletes and others alike busy. The campus is also in a very safe spot in Ashland, many places are walkable, and major highways can be avoided when going to and from campus. Overall, Randolph-Macon is a great school. Very easy to get to, in a great spot, and has plenty to do with a busheling student life.
I do like the Class size. Everyone is very friendly. The professors are willing to help you. The sports teams are very good at their craft
Randolph-Macon College is gorgeous, welcoming, and breath-taking. The moment that I stepped foot on campus I knew that it would be my home.

So far my experience at Randolph-Macon college has been like none other. I have cherished every moment I have spent there and cannot wait for the upcoming years. This school has everything I ever needed. It has exceptional athletics which is perfect for me as I am on the women’s soccer team, along with the great athletics, it has amazing facilities for those athletes to shine and work in. Apart from the athletics they have challenging academics that always make me strive to succeed and push myself to become a better person. I love my time at Randolph Macon and cannot wait to see what the future holds.
Great small school vibe. The professors are very engaging and the community is nice. Being a member of the baseball team makes me feel very valued to the school.

As a student at Randolph-Macon College, there was no diversity. Racial groups were very underrepresented on campus. The housing and dining hall options were terrible. There were mold in dorms, food options were scarce, and I always found myself just going to Richmond. Other than that, the people were friendly.
It’s been a great experience every time I’ve been up there, everyone is welcoming and I’m excited to have a new opportunity here.

As a student, I like to attend events like movie screenings, several honors events and concert events for my colleagues. I work at the school library and find myself helping with my school's literary magazine, The Stylus. Many of the professors are very helpful and willing to do as much as they can to ensure their students' success. The faculty and staff are also very kind, and I would recommend students with high discipline to attend this school.
Great academics and professors however the campus culture is not very inclusive and overall diversity of the student body both in race, identity and opinion is severely lacking.

Depending on the professor you’ll get a lot of leeway from due dates and grades. The professor are kind and are really good at teaching. It’s fun especially when you get to compete In competitions. Online is easy just have good communication with your teacher if you miss something or have a question some answer after a few minutes.

Dont go to this school if you enjoy having friends. Very academically focused students, which is great if you know exactly what you want to do. I went in as undecided and did not have the best time in classes. Very empty campus, super cliquey and almost no social activities that are accessible if you are not an upper classman with connections.

Reed College is a good school to learn about things in ways that are not about the mundane details, like the differences between CPU X and GPU Y, price differences, country of origin, et cetera. It offers routes towards "universal understanding". Routes is the wrong word to use. Otherwise, everyone and their mother would know. Many people, especially the normies and paranoid leadership, complain that liberal arts colleges don't offer the specifics and the connections to make it out in the real world.
About that... the governments and its cabal of contractors, such as Big Banks and non-profits, were and are in the way. They try to be when they're not called for.
This here only matters if you're an American. Hurdles in manufacturing, the manufacturing work goes to the normies. Software engineering was already well outsourced to other countries since 2010. Not everything is made in the USA. Not every leader is American. That's the big lesson in bridging university and profession.
This school is evil and hard but it feels super rewarding to go through. The professors are all super intelligent and you are bound to learn loads in any class you take.

I'm writing a review because I want to point out that the school motto "Communism, Atheism, Free Love," was meant as an ironic joke. At least that's my understanding--not the actual promotion of communism, atheism, and free love. Reedies can sometimes be joyless pedants and may not get this.
I wouldn't send my kids to Reed, but I do think it was a fantastic experience in many ways. But I was a student who probably should have had a more supportive academic environment. If you are independent and focused on learning, it's a great place. But it is an insular bubble.

I'm a recent alumni from Reed College. Overall I enjoyed myself at Reed College but I am on the fence as to whether I would do it again.
Upsides, easy for to get connected within my department and I am not sure I would have had the same academic and career development opportunities if I went to a larger school. The research was great for real-world practice and I really lucked out with my research advisor. I like the smaller class sizes, the small-knit community, traditions, and study abroad. I love the location in woodstock and the campus is beautiful.
Downsides, I am a woman of color and I felt very isolated here. It's a "white-liberal" school but found that a lot of people close to me were complacent with racism and not very self/aware with those matters. Drug culture is intense, never participated but still affected me. Kinda cliquey. Portland weather sucks. Reed isn't for everyone, low retention, common to struggle with your mental health for a period, and its a hard school.

Awesome professors. Workload can be hard to manage. There is a particular type of people who go to this school, and if you are not like them, it would be hard to find a friend group. But you can make friends anyway.
Reed College was a great school for focused academic work and close community. The symposium style courses were deeply engaging and the thesis requirement proved to be very valuable.

Reed has such a beautiful campus–truly something I will never forget! The only real issue I encountered was the lack of meaningful support in helping students find careers immediately after graduation. CLBR is notably unhelpful beyond a certain point when it comes to securing internships or jobs, although they're undoubtedly excellent at guiding students into graduate programs.
The college proudly emphasizes the difficulty of its coursework and practices grade deflation. This is all fine, BUT if they're going to do that, they absolutely must support students by clearly communicating to employers just how rigorous the curriculum is. Unfortunately, Reed completely fails in this regard.

Reed will never die. Do you want to go to a big school? Do you want to go to a dry school? If you answered no to either questions, Reed may be for you.
Jokes aside, I know a lot of people who went to a lot of different liberal arts colleges. Many of them have similar drawbacks, but absolutely none of them have what Reed has. Covid gave the school a bit of a stress test, but somehow the traditions and culture that have made Reed home to idiosyncratic beautifully stupid intellectuals since forever has made it through, in some shape or form. Its not worth parsing through the specifics, but the academics are HERE, the weirdos are HERE, and as far as I can tell, that won't change any time soon. You get out of Reed exactly what you put in, but if you take that dictate seriously you will receive an experience and education with no rival.
The administration will always be faceless and disingenuous, and the students will hopefully always push back. If you think Reed's for you, we need you.

Reed retaliates against students who speak out online. The administration has made it impossible for me to seek career services because I voiced concerns about SA at Reed.
Multiple administrators have been visiting my LinkedIn, so the idea that I'm experiencing anything but retaliation is risible. I started the group r/RecoveringFromReed/ on Reddit because I have had a negative ROI from Reed, and the school is actively retaliating against me.
I can't recommend attending Reed to anyone in good conscience. Unfortunately, I fell for the whole "life of the mind" schtick, even though I got into higher-ranked schools. Attending Reed was the worst mistake of my entire life, and I became a career advisor to help students avoid making similar decisions.
You might say, "It won't happen to me," but it may happen to you. Please think twice about attending Reed College; it is the most toxic environment I've ever seen, and I spent years in state party politics.

In the last couple years with the new administration, the Staff turnover rate has been massive. Career staff members who worked here for decades are resigning en masse every year in protest of manipulative and abusive stances being taken towards staff and students. In my major the matriculation rate was crazy low (under 3/10 I would estimate between students leaving my major for another/the college/academia entirely) And I'll be upfront because this needs to be said- the amount of SA that the college doesn't do anything about makes for a scary campus culture. I know many have had good experiences here, so I wont say don't come to Reed, but if I could have made a different choice I would have. If you do come to Reed, remember to get everything in an email so you have a paper trail, and remember that the college has your well being and success second after their reputation. It's probably like that all over, but its good to be aware of these things.
It was alright. Lots of nice trees I like trees a lot. It was alright. Lots of I need 100 characters
The community can be fairly eclectic and the academics are good, but nothing noteworthy. Only need-based aid means that you will end up paying “max” price and the same price as much better schools.

ai_summary: Rhodes College offers a distinct college experience characterized by its small campus size and close-knit community within the vibrant city of Memphis . Many students appreciate the small class sizes, which allow for personal relationships with professors who are often described as caring and engaged, enhancing the academic experience. While Rhodes is known for its rigorous liberal arts curriculum and various extracurricular opportunities, including involvement in student organizations and Greek life, there are mixed feelings about elements such as campus safety and dining options. Some students highlight a lack of diversity and occasionally cliquish social dynamics, particularly regarding Greek life. Overall, students value the support they receive academically and personally, while also expressing a desire for improvements in administrative responsiveness and campus facilities.

reviews.text: I have loved Rhodes! The city alongside the support Rhodes professors and and pre-professional department's have provide me are what got me into Med school without taking a gap year.

I loved Rhodes. The professors were great and I really enjoyed my classes. The food was sometimes hit or miss but overall fine. The environment was generally very good- nice people, welcoming, involved.

Rhodes is a fantastic school to gain a liberal arts education, while having a large city to explore in Memphis. Small class sizes make learning and connections with professor or other students easy. Everyone is able to find an extracurricular for them to connect each student to a community on campus.

So far, my experience here at Rhodes has been good. It is a rigorous school, so maintaining academics has definitely been a big part of my experience. Professors here are great; they are really interested in getting to know you on a personal level and helping you out in any way they can. They are available to meet at scheduled office hours, but they will also accommodate you if you need to meet at another time. Memphis and Rhodes have plenty of opportunities for students from just about every academic discipline, and it is not hard to get matched with things that interest you because of how small the school is. There is a lot of this happening on campus and around the city, so you are covered for the social aspect as well. You can meet people through classes, on campus, or at club events. There is always a friendly face, and people here are very welcoming.

I have really enjoyed my time here at Rhodes. I love the small class sizes, I usually have around 10-15 people in a class, they've helped me make friends and get to know my professors better. The professors here are wonderful and really want you to improve, I've had great opportunities offered to me because of their encouragement and recommendations. The architecture is stunning and the honor code makes me feel comfortable to leave my stuff anywhere and go walk around. I think the food and housing is pretty standard, housing gets better the longer you're here. The 3 year on campus living requirement can be a bit annoying for some people but I like that it means everyone is always around for parties and clubs. The housing gets better the further you get along. Being in a big city, safety is something you have to be smart about when going out for the night, but I have never felt unsafe on campus.

I'm a current sophomore at Rhodes who just declared a major in philosophy and I feel so supported by the staff in both the philosophy department and across the board in other departments from the classes I've taken thus far. The professors truly love their work and care for their students, with some going above and beyond to ensure students feel comfortable and confident in the content they're learning. I couldn't have asked for a better opportunity to learn from them and the amazing students here. The learning environment feels collaborative and driven and other students are always willing to help out where they can!
I'm a current senior, and I can say that I've really appreciated the liberal arts education and strong relationships with professors at Rhodes.

Wouldn’t change a thing about choosing Rhodes! While it has its ups and downs like any school, I truly think the liberal arts education I have received has prepared me to be more adaptable in my field once I graduate. Rhodes’ location in the heart of Memphis is also well situated for internship placement, making the world your oyster!

Rhodes is an amazing school! It is a great small liberal arts experience with a vibrant social life as well. Being in Memphis allows for great job and professional connections. The professors are very well-knowledgeable and personable. It is definitely a time I will look back with gratitude on forever!

I have loved my time here at Rhodes so far! I am a sophomore and have been having to much fun so far. It is so easy to get involved on campus and I have loved almost every single on of my professors. I really enjoy the small classes and being able to talk to my professor whenever I want. Memphis has also been a blast to explore!

I am a current senior at Rhodes College, and I am also a Memphis native. It has been wonderful being on campus, and there are so many ways to bond with people and professors on campus from events! The food is the basic cafeteria and can vary day per day, and there are two different dining halls. Rooms are basic doubles for freshmen, size depending on forming hall. If you want a place with great academia and environment, this would be your place!

I have greatly enjoyed my time at Rhodes. All of the classes I have taken have been helpful and I have had a great set of professors. Any time I have needed help or support I have been able to find it around me through students or professors. I have been given a chance to get involved and make my mark on the school making my time here feel useful.

Rhodes has been a great liberal arts experience for me. As a humanities major, all my professors are generally passionate about the subjects they teach, truly want to get to know and help their students, and accommodate their needs. The campus is beautiful and something I take pride in. I feel very safe on campus, as we have great security measures as well. Though the food isn't always the best, we definitely have a lot of variety to find something good every time.

I have been at Rhodes and so far my experience has been very positive! Becuase the school is so small it really does feel like one big community. Everyone here is so friendly and professors truly care about their students. Despite being in Memphis (although Memphis is greatly improving in safety), I feel very safe as the campus is completely gated and campus security takes their job very seriously.

If you like small schools this might be the school for you, however for me I felt trapped. I was constantly seeing the same people over and over again and it was honestly pretty cliquey. There wasn't any good parties and people were just overall very uptight.

ai_summary: At SUNY Purchase College, students often highlight the strong academic programs and dedicated professors , particularly in the arts and liberal studies. Many appreciate the school's open-minded and diverse community that fosters creativity and self-expression, making it a welcoming environment. However, there are consistent critiques regarding campus facilities, with several reviews mentioning outdated buildings and issues with housing quality. Social life seems to vary, with vibrant activities for artists but a perceived lack of engagement for those outside creative disciplines. Some students point out limited dining options and the disappointing quality of food as notable downsides. The proximity to New York City is frequently mentioned as a benefit, allowing students to easily explore the cultural offerings of the city . Despite some frustrations with administration and campus life, many find SUNY Purchase to be an enriching experience for personal and professional growth.

reviews.text:
Life changing school, great cultural and intellectual atmosphere. No legacy/donor dullards here!
Would be nice if this school had more of a connection to NYC, since so much talent in the NYC public permorming arts school.
I enjoy it a lot. Good size and area. Close, supportive, and friendly community. Food is iffy. Wayback is the best dorm. Wish it was cheaper since it’s a SUNY
Everything at Purchase is great. From student life to smaller events, I actually really enjoy the atmosphere of the school as well/
SUNY Purchase is a good and affordable school with a really good Art and Design program. The photography program is decent.

SUNY Purchase is a vibrant and creative campus where the cultural clubs truly set the tone for the fun. There’s always an event, showcase, or community gathering that makes the school feel lively and welcoming. The professors are skilled in their fields and genuinely invested in helping students grow in their area of study. The food is okay (nothing amazing, but enough variety) The campus is tucked away in the woods, you’ll definitely need to drive off campus or take the loop if you want more food options or things to do. Overall, an affordable and unique college experience for students interested in the arts or general studies.

The campus is generally decent if you find your community. However, it's not very diverse for poc, and in the art and design department, the courses could be more up-to-date and address topics that many other institutions cover. Still, you will grow to love the environment and the professors, and you will appreciate who you eventually become as an artist.

As a current Visual Art major this school has amazing professors that really push you forward and make you work hard when it comes to projects. As a commuter it’s relatively easy to have a compus life as staff and students are really nice and are open to having friends and colleagues.
SUNY purchase brutalizes their students. Do not attend unless you want a baton to the gut or a knee on your neck!

I generally enjoy my time at my school, there’s something new to explore and learn every day! I just wish there were more affordable food options or more options for those who cannot pay for food without needing to pay for a meal plan.

It’s very, very good. I like it a lot. The food is very good on some days, and the academics are great. I’m learning so much and how to apply it to my life. The dorms are very small, but I didn’t come to school for that. The people are so welcoming and helpful.

I Am having an enjoyable time at my college. Unfortunately though, I feel as though, financially I am extremely stressed. I would like to apply to be an RA. My father is unemployed and we recently my grandma passed away, creating a hardship in who would help take care of my younger sisters while my parents work their 9-5 to support the family. Babysitters, and daycares cost about way too much. And I am now the one carrying this guilt of wanting to go away for college. Why is claiming and education so expensive. I find myself crying and asking that question 20 times today, I have felt unmotivated but i must continue to apply for scholarships.
Purchase is an amazing school with an amazing student body. I love the community and the feeling of belonging since I have transferred

I love the diversity and the ability for people to be who they are without fear of judgement. Professors are amazing and kind; they make sure their students learn in and out of the classroom. Overall, I would choose this college over any other option every time.
I attended the EOP program and the experience was so warming they were very helpful and helped us understand things before school start.

I'm still a senior at Purchase, but overall I would say I've enjoyed this school more than my last college. Generally, the professors are kind and helpful. I found a great internship here and gained valuable experience from it. The art facilities are amazing. However, I wish there was better food, more academic and counseling help options for students, and more options for art classes.

ai_summary: At Saint Anselm College, students frequently mention a strong sense of community and support, making many feel at home while also challenging them academically. The professors are generally described as approachable and invested in student success, although some students note variability in teaching effectiveness and grading practices. Many appreciate the small class sizes that foster engagement but express concerns about housing issues and a lack of diversity on campus. While student life is active with numerous clubs and events, some find the social scene to be cliquish or reminiscent of high school. Overall, students feel the college offers a unique environment that encourages personal growth and close relationships, though opinions about the food and campus facilities vary significantly.

reviews.text: The liberal arts and Benedictine traditions the school is founded on really support the student experience. There are things for all students and as much as there is faculty and staff support, they let students take the lead and create great environments. The core curriculum can be tedious but there are always connections to be made.

This college promotes a unique experience, but falls short of its claims - especially given its cost. Student life is limited and insular, with minimal programming and little diversity. Student perspectives are often elevated over faculty and staff expertise, weakening mentorship and blurring authority. A culture of escalating complaints discourages resilience and accountability. Leadership development lacks clear distinction between students and professionals. The campus reflects a “this is how we’ve always done it” mindset, resistant to outside ideas. Paternalism is most evident in its treatment of Saint Anselm Abbey, where monks are elevated as princes and caricatures rather than modeled as humble servants. With questionable administrative credentials and a manufactured, exclusionary community, the college risks leaving students unprepared for professional realities. Families should consider alternatives carefully.
I plan to enroll at Saint Anselm in the fall of 2026 to study and play Ice Hockey. I am not currently enrolled anywhere. I am in New Jersy playing junior hockey in the NCDC.

A great community and wonderful education. I feel very connected to the school and the staff. It is a very small school but there are tons of opportunities. While housing is old and limited it allows you to make the most of any situation and make unforgettable memories with friends.

The college is known for its tight-knit and welcoming community. The campus culture is supportive and friendly. Everyone knows each others, always saying hi to everyone and known to open doors for each other. A sense of belonging. the campus always has events going on and run deep in traditions like the gingerbread house making competition, spring weekend and banner raising. Nearly all students live on campus, so it's always lively. The small size classes make it a better learning environment helping a close student-professor interaction and individualize attention.

Going from a homeschooled student from kindergarten through high school I was very nervous to go to college, but Saint Anselm College took all my worries away immediately because of the high quality student life and comforting campus. The clubs and recreational organizations really make the school as great as it is. If you are not a business or nursing major you may find the classes and professors outside of those departments are serviceable but leave a little to be desired. Overall Saint Anselm College is a great school, filled with great like-minded students all pursuing individual success in a great environment.

Coming from a small High School, I loved going into a small college and get the same experience. The food is okay in the cafe, but the Coffee shop is awesome. Dorms are nice and clean and enjoy my dorm experience. Professor as awesome and I am getting a great education.

Saint Anselm College for me was nothing short of extraordinary. It was a true hidden gem that feels like home from the moment you step on campus. The sense of community is unmatched; whether you're grabbing a coffee in Davison or walking to class, you're always greeted with warmth and genuine connection. The food? Absolutely phenomenal—far beyond your average college dining, with rotating menus and comfort meals that make every bite memorable. School spirit pulses through every athletic event, students, alumni, and faculty come together in a sea of blue and white to cheer with pride. And perhaps most remarkable of all is the faculty—professors who don’t just teach, but mentor, guide, and support you like family. Their care, patience, and dedication to student success elevate the entire academic experience and speaks to why I had such a great four years here. Saint Anselm is a 10/10 in every way—a tight-knit environment where you grow not just as a student, but as a person.

Saint Anselm College has truly changed my life in the best way possible. From the moment I arrived, I felt part of a close, supportive community that wants you to succeed and become your best self. The professors are incredible—they care about you as a student and person, and the small class sizes help build real relationships. The Core Curriculum challenges you to think deeply and grow beyond academics. The campus is full of students from all backgrounds, and the growing diversity makes the community more welcoming and vibrant. There are so many ways to get involved—through athletics, service, ministry, and clubs. One of the best parts of Saint A’s is the New Hampshire Institute of Politics, where you can meet leaders, attend events, and be part of national conversations. I’m proud to be an Anselmian—this place gave me lifelong friends, unforgettable memories, and the confidence to live with purpose and lead with heart.

St A's is the best school ever! From the staff to the students, everyone does their best to create a positive, welcoming community. The professors have been so supportive and helpful, despite my home life, and they have given me every opportunity to succeed. The student life is amazing. I LOVE ST A'S!!! BEST SCHOOL EVERRRR!!!!!!!!

The college experience at Saint Anselm College is unmatched. The connections and community that you are emersed into when you arrive on campus is amazing. There is always a place for you to go if you need help or need space. The professors and professional staff are amazing resources and are always there if you have a question or need help with things. 10/10 experience, best school ever!
Saint Anselm is an amazing school I'm now in my sophomore year, and I really love the campus and the people here, it is the home away from home.

As an alum of Saint Anselm College, I can confidently say that my time there was both transformative and deeply fulfilling. The close-knit community, grounded in Benedictine values, fostered strong personal and academic growth, while small class sizes and dedicated faculty made for an engaging and supportive learning environment. The campus itself is beautiful and peaceful, offering the perfect backdrop for reflection and connection. While the college could expand resources in areas like career services and diversity, the sense of purpose, integrity, and lifelong friendships I gained there continue to shape my life well beyond graduation.

Saint Anselm College is a place that prepares you for success not only in your career but also in life. I built a lot of meaningful connections with my time so far here an have really enjoyed my experience. GO HAWKS!!!
I have thoroughly enjoyed my experience at Saint Anselm College. I have met amazing people, professors, clubs, and more.

ai_summary: Saint Mary's College - Indiana is often praised for its tight-knit, supportive community and the unique all-women environment that promotes empowerment and confidence. Many reviews highlight the small class sizes , allowing for personalized interaction with professors who are described as caring and invested in student success. The partnership with the nearby University of Notre Dame enhances academic and social opportunities, such as attending events and taking classes across campuses. However, several reviews express concerns about the lack of diversity on campus and uneven experiences with professors; while some are exceptional, others are considered less effective. Additionally, dining options and the cost of tuition are frequently noted as areas needing improvement. Overall, students value the sense of belonging and leadership development at Saint Mary's, although some express mixed feelings about particular aspects of student life and administration.

reviews.text: The school heavily promotes the tri-campus experience, but the tri-campus community is little to none. We do have many opportunities involving attending sports events at Notre Dame but besides that it is very secluded. The academic advisors at the school are also pretty bad. Most of them don't seem to know what they are talking about and messed up my roommates academic route causing her to stay an extra semester.

St. Mary's is a great experience for people who are used to or want a small, close-knit community. For that reason, making friends is harder down the line, and the party and social scene is not the best.

I've had good experiences with the professors I've taken courses from, and I've also met some really great people who are close to me, like sisters. There are some issues with the elevators in some of the older dorms, such as breaking down or washing machines. There is a safety bus that transports students to and from Notre Dame, which is nice, so you don't have to walk alone in the dark, but sometimes campus security can be rude when you ask if they can send around a car to pick you up

ai_summary: Saint Michael's College stands out for its strong sense of community , with many reviews emphasizing the friendly atmosphere and the close-knit relationships among students, faculty, and staff. The small campus size and low student-to-teacher ratio foster personal connections , allowing professors to know their students by name and provide tailored support. Academically, many students appreciate the diverse course offerings and the passion of the faculty, though opinions on academic rigor vary. Student life is lively, with numerous extracurricular activities and a vibrant social scene , although some reviews noted a lack of diversity and engagement among the student body. While the campus and surrounding Burlington area are consistently praised for their beauty and opportunities for outdoor activities, concerns about food quality and administrative issues were also mentioned. Overall, Saint Michael's College is characterized as a welcoming and supportive environment ideal for those looking for an intimate college experience.

reviews.text:
As a 2022 graduate now working at a Fortune 100 company and earning a 6-figure salary, I can confidently say that choosing Saint Michael’s College was one of the best decisions I’ve made. I had an excellent all-around experience with the college. My professors were outstanding, I loved being in Vermont, and I was fortunate to be a member of the Nordic ski team. While it may sound cliché, the community at SMC truly is the difference. People really care, both about each other, and the school itself. It is easy to say that my time at Saint Mike’s shaped me into the person I am today.
One of the most meaningful parts of my time at SMC was working on the outreach team, calling alumni from across generations. It was really impactful to be able to connect with people who graduated 60+ years ago, but shared the same pride of being a Purple Knight. I look forward to the day when a SMC student calls me in 2080, and I get to tell them just how proud I am to be a Saint Michael’s College alumnus.
Great small community. Great to have a smaller number of students to interact with. Creates a family oriented atmosphere

I spent my freshman year at St Mike’s and it was one of the worst periods of my life. If you want to feel like you’re still in high school, want to be surrounded by privileged white kids, and want the only social activities to include getting black out drunk in the freezing cold standing around like a herd of sheep, this place is for you. Seriously though, this place isn’t even going to be open in five years, and should have shuttered its doors a long time ago. It’s a small student body and shrinking by the day. If you aren’t in the clique, you will be ostracized. Go literally anywhere else.
I loved transferring over from CCV to Saint Mikes. 10/10 recommend. I've been there two semesters now and I love it.

Saint Michael's was a fantastic liberal arts education and an excellent springboard to law school and my career as an attorney. It is impossible to describe in an online review how Saint Michael's magically felt like "home" instantly for so many incoming freshman and how much of an impact something like the Edmundite tradition can have as the years pass by. I wouldn't hesitate to send my children to Saint Michael's someday.

I enjoyed the small class sizes in my STEM courses which ranged from 30-5 students. There was also plenty of opportunity to take courses that generalized my educational opportunities. For example, I graduated with a B.S. in Biochemistry, but took political science, visual arts, and art history courses throughout my years at Saint Michaels. I would not recommend Saint Michael's for student looking for a visual arts education. The visual arts courses are taught on a separate campus about 1.5 miles away in run-down buildings that were unkempt by the school. The campus is very walkable and downtown Burlington (~10 mins away) provides a college town feel as there are many students who enjoy nightlife in the city.

Saint Michael's College is a great school. I've met a lot of amazing people so far and am excited to continue my journey over the next two years. All of the professors I've had are passionate about what they teach and truly care about each and every student, which is one benefit to a small school with small class sizes. My only issues are small things that are probably relatable to any college, the freshman dorms were not great but housing gets better as you move up. The dining options are limited and the food is not always the best. Otherwise, I think it's a decent enough school.
I absolutely loved my time at St. Mikes. The small on-campus community created such a closeness with my peers and lead to some lifelong friendships.

From being an undergraduate since 2020, to now being a first year graduate student at St Mikes, I have fallen in love with the campus and its people. Everyone cares about your well being, there are multiple resources for assistance whether it be for tutoring, medical assistance, counseling, and more. The students and staff are more than caring and welcoming, and I have never felt like I belonged more. One significant circumstance I'd like to mention is that when I'd come back from the library late at night, I was never concerned for my safety walking home in the dark here. I would love to continue my education here at Saint Michael's, since it is my second home.
I currently attend Saint Mikes, and I love the school so much. I have made many lifelong friends, enjoy the community area surrounding the campus, and love the campus group opportunities.

Since I've arrived last August I found myself to be quite content with my experience at Saint Mike's. The classes are quite enjoyable both good and bad. The professors are open-minded for the most part. The community is what really makes the campus flourish with positivity. I hope to continue my journey here at Saint Mike's and make new memories along the way!

I am so grateful that I chose Saint Michael's College to be my home these past four years. Professors here are genuinely interested in your success and are eager to create close bonds with you. The small and tight-knit community provides the perfect setting for you to make close friends and become acquainted with so many people on campus. It's nice to never worry about housing as a fully residential school, and the accommodations are impressive for such a small area. The dining hall is not the best, but it's part of the college experience. Covid put a strain on the community and many traditions, but the students here are passionate and hope to get many cherished traditions back up and running. Come to Saint Mike's!
I had a great time at Saint Michael’s College. The faculty/staff was awesome and was always there to help you.

I love the small school experience. You get to know so many people that makes the college experience that much more enjoyable. The community of Saint Michael’s is like no other. It connects students from all over to create the best 4 years you can imagine!

The school is really big on student engagement. I feel like I knew almost everyone and was highly involve in a good range of activities. Kind community. Has some bad staff in housing however which made LGBTQ housing a nightmare at times. But there was a really good LGBTQ club on campus.

ai_summary: Saint Vincent College in Pennsylvania offers a close-knit community with an emphasis on strong academic relationships and personalized support from faculty. Reviews highlight the professors’ dedication and the benefit of small class sizes, allowing for individualized attention and mentorship. The campus is described as beautiful and welcoming , although some students express dissatisfaction with its limited student life , food options, and dorm conditions. While many appreciate the number of clubs and extracurricular activities available, others mention that the campus can feel quiet on weekends, with limited social opportunities. The college’s commitment to student success is frequently noted, alongside concerns about the administration’s responsiveness and academic resources for certain programs. Overall, Saint Vincent is seen as a place where students can thrive academically, but it may not offer the full college experience expected by some.

reviews.text: My experience has been very good overall. The services and community that Saint Vincent College provides are beneficial to all. The professors and other staff members are always kind and approachable. The campus itself is very nice, but could definitely use renovations to areas and buildings before others.

My school is a good place to learn and grow. I like that classes are small because it makes it easier to get to know teachers and ask for help when needed. There is a lot of support for students, including mental health support, which makes the environment feel caring and welcoming. Our sports teams are close-knit and work hard together regardless of the results, showing strong teamwork and dedication. Even though it is a small Catholic school, there are clubs and events that keep students involved, and the social life is enjoyable. Overall, school spirit is strong, and it feels like a community where students truly support one another. Just diversity isn't as big but it is in the middle pf western PA so there isn't much to expect.

Saint Vincent is more than just a college, it becomes a home. From every aspect, Saint Vincent College is the perfect school. It is close to home but still around enough to be learning to live life on your own. The church is beautiful and always open and welcoming. The students, professors, and faculty have all been wonderful and always supportive.

ai_summary: At Sarah Lawrence College, the academic experience is often highlighted as both unique and engaging , largely due to the close relationships students have with their professors and the unconventional open curriculum that allows for personalized education. Many reviews praise the professors for their dedication and the quality of instruction, noting that small class sizes foster meaningful interactions . However, there is significant criticism regarding the food quality, living conditions, and the overall administration, with some students pointing out issues like ineffective student services and a lack of maintenance in dormitories. The campus atmosphere appears to cater to a specific type of student, often described as introverted and creatively inclined , which may lead to challenges in social integration. While the community is generally considered supportive and diverse, some feel that the actual inclusivity does not match the college's claims, leading to a perception of elitism among certain student groups. Overall, the experience seems to be highly dependent on individual initiative and the ability to navigate the school's unique characteristics.

reviews.text:
My experience with Sarah Lawrence College was on one of their study abroad programs, many years ago. That year, about half the students were from SLC, the rest were from other schools (Northwestern, Bates, Hamilton, etc.) I was struck by two things: how much the SLC students loved their school and how much more invested they were in their academic work than the rest of us were. They had a real passion for what they were studying, probably because they were used to having input into what they focused on.
In the years since, I have met a bunch of SLC grads and they have been some of the most interesting and intelligent people I have met (artists, poets, musicians).
As others have said, if SLC is a good fit, it will be phenomenal, like no other school in the country.

I chose SLC because of its homey environment and the opportunity for me to get to know everybody. While being at Sarah Lawrence, I try to make friends with whoever I can, so much so my friends always comment about me seeming to know everyone at the school.

Sarah Lawrence is a very particular type of school. If you're the type of student for it, you will love it. If not, you'll probably hate it. SLC's educational model is very independently-focused, with an emphasis on an individual program of education for each student and a lot of independent projects. This educational model was fantastic for me and I can't see myself having thrived at any other school - but I also know a lot of people who really wouldn't like it. The campus life is similarly niche - it's a very small school, which leads to a very tight-knit community. This can be really great, or can be very cliquey - your mileage may vary. The food is not good. Straight up. Admittedly, it's a bit better than it was a few years ago, but it's still not great. The party scene is pretty small, but I like a good small party so this never bothered me. Overall, if you read about SLC and it sounds interesting, you'll probably love it for its academics.

Sarah Lawrence College has a reputation for many things, but nice people does not happen to be one of them. Even the College's strongest proponents admit that it is an unfriendly campus. If you ask about parties, you are likely to get an answer such as "Well of course you can have parties. You merely need to invite your friends and you are welcome to have a party whenever you like."
Mental health concerns are pervasive. This needs to be take seriously. You will be surrounded by people who are not well at all. No one bothers to deny this. It is not healthy to be around people who are not well themselves. The academics are okay, but frankly they do not justify the tuition costs. Sarah Larwrence College has been floating on a reputation of past years that they no longer possess. Their rankings have plummeted after the Larry Ray scandal, where the administration permitted the father of a student to take up residence in the dorms and abuse students sexually, emotionally and financially.

Overall, my experience at Sarah Lawrence has been really positive. It’s a unique school, and while it’s not for everyone, it’s been a great fit for me in a lot of ways. The academics are definitely the highlight—professors here are amazing. They’re not just knowledgeable, but also genuinely invested in their students. Because the classes are so small, you actually get to know them, and they get to know you, which can make a big difference in how you learn.
Outside of class, there are a ton of clubs and activities to get involved in. People here are really passionate about what they do, and there’s always something happening.
Sarah Lawrence has a great community as well. It’s a small campus, which means you end up making a lot of connections. It’s the kind of place where networking feels more like making friends than anything formal.
Access to Manhattan is also a huge plus. You’re in a safe, pretty campus while still being able to go to the city easily.

My experience at this college has been deeply disappointing. The quality of the food is consistently poor, and when concerns are raised, the administration seems indifferent. Safety measures on campus are lacking, and student well-being does not appear to be a priority. The school focuses heavily on appearances, showcasing only the most appealing aspects to attract prospective students, while ignoring more serious underlying issues. I strongly encourage others to research carefully and consider alternative options.

Avoid this college. It allowed a father to live in the dorms, blackmailing students, and he is now in prison. Limited course choices. Deserted on weekends. I would never admit to attending, so I only mention the name of my graduate school experience (Harvard) instead. In college, had Unsupportive don. Due to my curiosity, hard work, commitment to child development, and skills, I attended Harvard graduate school, despite little support from the don.Studied anything I wanted at Harvard & MIT!!! Became a psychologist, author, board member of the Autism Society of America, director of a public speakers program, clinical psychologist, consultant.
Sarah Lawrence is a disaster. it is clearly in trouble, unable to hire more professors, nor offer a variety of courses, nor renovate or serve decent food. What a nightmare. Should be closed/ shut down. Any college allowing a parent to live in the dorms, extort money from students - later jailed for his actions - is not a credible institution.

The student faculty interaction is strong and encourages confidence in any field of study. The faculty of the school care about teaching material and making sure it is understood. Connected classes, strengthened student environments, confidence in learning are all factors that apply to Sarah Lawrence.
The school is known to have not the best food- and boy is that the case! Registration for courses is difficult to complete, but there is no lack of interesting classes

It is in all likelihood the only place where I would say straight white men will experience societal misandry against them, but also a place of flowing ideas and beautiful discourse. Idyllic and chaotic. A hodgepodge of any and all types of smart people from across the globe in close proximity to the greatest city in the world. If you are into a specific subculture or academic fixation, there is a place that both NYC as a strung together cadre of communities and SLC as a university will be your new place being. Are the security guards annoying? Sometimes. Is the food not the best? No, not necessarily. Is the side of campus bordering Yonkers kind of suspect? Yes, it is.
