However, SLC is a place more than the some of its parts. It’s the professors, the students, the library, the campus, the location, and so much more.
Its a great school with amazing professors and a very accepting and kind student body. However, it is way too expensive and no college is worth that amount of money.

Positives: phenomenal academics, professors are some of the best I've worked with, the unique conference system will give you a truly deep and broad education like no other. Academically, you simply can't do better than Sarah Lawrence
Negatives: SLC's housing and food situation is kind of notorious among the student body. The dorms are old and in need of maintenance that the administration is unwilling or unable to provide, and food options are limited and not super high quality.

Classes are insightful and engaging and the events run by students and the college itself are fun and provide a good break from studying. Food is generally good---but it is college food so it's best to keep expectations there. Classes are small so you are bound to run into someone you know no matter where you go and the academics are expansive, well-taught, and eye opening in a variety of ways.

Don't trust the B+ this school apparently got in academics. The academics here are incredible. When I was deciding where to go to college, that was a big worry for me, but the B+ was totally misleading, they should've been given an A+. Especially if you know where to go. If you surround yourself with smart people, if you pay attention to which professors are the best, and if you're not afraid of a class with a lot of reading, you will find some of the most incredible academics you could ever encounter. The professors here are great - they don't get paid enough, so they have to really love to teach in order to teach here, and they do. Class sizes tend to be small which means you can actually get to know your teachers and speak to them one on one, also the graduate program here isn't that big, so no teacher is focused on their graduate students and pawning off the undergrad teaching work to a TA that doesn't know what they're doing. Also, the food here is actually quite good!

Sarah Lawrence is very liberal and has a very impressive staff of professors. It is a strong stoner school with a student body that tends to complain about parties, so that scene is not very well developed.

I left Sarah Lawrence after my first semester. It could definitely work for a specific kind of person but it is not for everyone. The classes are all very unusual and niche which seems cool but in actuality can get kind of annoying if you want any sort of in depth education of a general topic. I took a course called Astronomy, which was a lecture class, expecting it to be about the basics or history of astronomy. Instead, most of our time was spent reenacting the trial of Galileo which was more about your acting than the actual theories of Galileo or about astronomy. Even in the Calc 1 course a lot of the assigned reading is about the history and philosophy behind math and not learning Calc. It is also HEAVILY writing based (papers/essays) which just doesn't work with me. Another issue that I had was that there is no access to grades so it is hard to assess how you are doing. Lastly, the dorms are really a lottery some are great and some are god awful so watch out.

The administration (IT, campus facilities, and other aspects) were very unhelpful. One of my friends experiences issues with school and they only helped her by sending her links to resources. When she began to consider transferring, they finally provided her with support.
One of the benefits of being in a consortium is the resources we have access to. I've had access to many resources including grants and money and extracurricular activities. However, most of these activities were not through Scripps. I feel that Scripps has the least amount of support and activities out of the 5Cs.
Additionally the diversity of the school is very lacking. There were less than 80 people of color in my incoming class. The affinity groups are not that active and don't get that much funding at Scripps, but I've had a lot of fun with the 5C affinity groups.

As a Scripps first-year and a student-athlete, I can honestly say that choosing Scripps was the best decision I’ve ever made. It’s changed me in such a positive way. I’ve already met people I know will be lifelong friends, and I’ve had great experiences with my professors. I also love getting to take classes across the 5Cs it’s an opportunity that doesn’t get talked about enough. If you’re considering an all-women’s college, this is the place for you. And if you’re worried about feeling “too isolated,” don’t be you’re surrounded by five other campuses, and the communities are extremely integrated. Some of my closest friends are from Scripps, and others are from the other colleges. The social scene is fun without being overwhelming, and there’s a really good balance overall. Scripps is an amazing school, and I’m genuinely grateful to be here.

I was excited to come to Scripps but was thoroughly disappointed. The faculty is great in most cases, if you're a humanities major - the administration and campus life is where Scripps falls short. The administration is financially unsupportive of faculty, punishes students who protest for causes like Palestine, but allows Zionist speakers on campus, and over-enrolls to the point of there being not enough on-campus housing for upperclassmen. The campus culture is very exclusive unless you're white, thin, and rich, and the parties mainly consist of wannabe-frat guys jumping in a circle while pushing girls out of the middle. The public transportation here is inadequate as well, and it takes almost 3 hours to get to downtown LA. Without a car, you're basically just stuck in a small desert town. Overall, the campus is beautiful, and the facilities are nice, but you're sacrificing a lot unless you're white, thin, rich and not politically inclined.

Scripps is okay, but it is not a welcoming or supportive place for students of color. The environment is extremely isolating, and the institution fails to provide meaningful support for POC. There is a severe housing crisis that forces many students to live off-campus for at least a year, often without adequate resources.
While the food is great and the academics are average on a national level, the social climate is where Scripps truly falls short. The culture is extremely cliquey, and unless you’re white and affluent, it’s hard to feel like you truly belong.
Unless you fit a very narrow mold, Scripps can feel like anything but a “second home.”

I’ve always loved the idea of going to a women's college because I think it provides a safe environment that would allow one to become more confident academically and as they transition into the workforce. While I did love the campus, classes, and even the extracurricular activities, what I really love about Scripps was the students. I admired the way students were unafraid to discuss their opinions openly, especially when the student-run coffeehouse, the Motley, was closed. I was pleasantly surprised to find out that most of the student body expressed their support for the affected students even though some of them lost their work study jobs. This experience showed me how Scripps builds a strong, supportive community.
One of the best women's colleges that centers its education on a core humanities and writing curriculum.
Overall I really enjoy Scripps because of the campus and the small class sizes. All of the professors that I have had at scripps have been super engaging and keep me interested.

Claremont Consortium is unlike any other college experience. Scripps has easily one of the most beautiful campuses in California and lovely amenities. There is a housing crisis right now so be aware you will not get all four years on campus u less extremely lucky.

Scripps seemed perfect—a women’s college in sunny California. But I quickly realized it didn’t meet my expectations.
Scripps is ~55% white, and diversity is lacking. Microaggressions are common, and I’ve witnessed outright racism. In my Core I class, seating naturally formed a racial divide.
The Core Curriculum is deeply flawed. Professors from unrelated fields teach the same syllabus with varying quality, and discussions feel like an echo chamber when many students share the same background (upper class, from LA).
Scripps is also outrageously expensive ($100K/year). The supposed tight-knit community is cliquey, and orientation felt superficial. Housing is unpredictable—you might be placed in co-ed, off-campus housing, further diminishing the women’s college experience. Academically, classes are hit or miss; my best course was at Pomona.
I’m considering transferring. Think carefully before committing.
Positives: Good food and writing department. LA is relatively accessible.

I loved attending Scripps. I truly connected with some of the most amazing people I have come across, and was closely mentored by talented professors. This campus hosts a wide array of opportunities for its undergraduates. Not to mention its welcoming, beautiful environment. Most of all, I really value being part of the 5Cs; it is a unique experience and the community is truly interwoven.
Scripps offers students a quality of life like no other. It is truly a paradise. Scripps prioritizes students’ mental health and wellbeing.

So far my experience at Scripps has been nothing short of extraordinary. I am currently in my Sophomore year at Scripps, and am surrounded by so many amazing people that make the community be so welcoming and supportive.

Attending Scripps College was one of the best experiences in my life. My freshman year dormmates became my best friends for life, even 15 years after graduation. If you are worried about attending a women's college, don't be! The campus boasts 4 other schools that are coed, so my social life was not limited whatsoever. It was actually really nice to have a few classes that were all women or mostly women, and I got to take as many classes at the other 4 schools as I wanted. I even got to do a major that wasn't offered at Scripps, but was offered at one of the others. They also had the nicest dorms I've ever seen and the most beautiful campus. I can't recommend this school more highly, it was perfect in so many ways.

Absolutely loved it. Brilliant, caring, eclectic community in a to-die-for setting nestled in the center of the Claremont consortium.
It’s a dream.

I have loved my Scripps experience so far, but it is absolutely fair to say that a certain type of person goes here. I wish there could be more diversity in the student body. I appreciate the academics, though, especially in the language department and Keck. Being apart of the 5Cs is a massive bonus, and is definitely one of the best things about this school.

school_name: Sewanee: The University of the South
ai_summary: Sewanee - The University of the South is characterized by its tight-knit community , where students and professors often know each other by name, fostering strong relationships and personalized learning experiences. While academics are noted as rigorous and many professors are dedicated and supportive, some students feel certain departments, particularly STEM fields, may lack in quality and opportunities. The campus itself is praised for its natural beauty and extensive outdoor activities, though the remote location raises concerns about a limited social scene and dining options. Greek life is prevalent, and while many find it inclusive, some note it can feel necessary for social engagement. Housing quality is a common complaint, with issues ranging from outdated facilities to a lack of air conditioning. Overall, Sewanee is viewed as an ideal fit for students seeking a beautiful, small campus with a strong emphasis on academics, though it may not suit everyone due to its unique social culture and remote setting.

reviews.text: Professors are great, and people are very helpful. However, its located in a rural area far away from everything else. Not much diversity either. Not really recommended for international students.

Sewanee has been my home away from home. I've found community here that is unmatched. I've grown both academically and socially in a safe and comforting environment. I could not ask for a better place. While the housing arrangements are not ideal, I do feel as though that is the very thing that brings charm. The church is GORGEOUS and open 24/7. There is also a new gym facility for non athletes and athletes alike that has up to date equipment and okay hours. The views here are unmatched though. If you visit, I would strongly suggest visiting Green's View and/or the Cross.

I put N/A for party scene because it's not really my thing but it is there for folks who want it. The campus is rural but is absolutely stunning! The buildings are beautiful, and All Saints Chapel is essentially an on-campus cathedral. The campus property has so many mountain overlooks and hiking trails and is wonderful if you are outdoorsey. While it is a Christian (Episcopalian) school, many students are not religious and there does not seem to be any pressure for students to have specific spiritual practices. That said, if you are Episcopalian, it is fantastic! There's an Episcopal convent nearby and an Episcopal seminary within the university along with lots of opportunities for student engagement at All Saints.

I am transferring out of Sewanee mid-way through my Sophomore year. I loved it at first in my freshman year fall semester. I made friends quickly, and felt a part of the school while being so far away from home. However, I was failed by the Greek system here twice. It is an 80% Greek school, and when you are a part of the 20% you feel like the biggest outsider. Do not come to Sewanee if you are generally social and don’t want to be in Greek life. There is no place for you, even if they say there is. It made me realize how small the bubble is and how difficult it is living in a rural area. I felt trapped in the small bubble. I don’t have a car on campus, so I was stuck every weekend and miserable. Even without being in Greek life, I became involved in clubs and even made a varsity sport team. Yet, I still found myself feeling like an outsider and feeling trapped in the bubble. There is nothing to do or eat on the weekends except the same frat parties with the same people and music.

During my time at Sewanee I met so many people, was able to get so involved in campus life, and I learned so much about the world and myself. I was lucky enough to experience several of the off campus classes and activities that Sewanee offers, notably, the Western Geology trip to California to study geology, the Sewanee in France study abroad program, the Rio Grande canoe trip offered through the SOP, the subalpine forest inventory survey trip in Colorado, the Rocky Mountain Biological Laboratory partnership for a summer of funded research in Colorado, and so much more. I would not trade my experience at Sewanee for the whole world. I met the best friends, professors, employers, and alumni network who I can see myself keeping up with for a very long time. The Sewanee community and support always amazes me, and I know that wherever I am and whatever I am doing I can count on this school and the people who make up this school for anything I might need. I love Sewanee.

So far, my college experience and adjustment to Sewanee have been smooth and magical. The professors are extremely attentive, knowledgeable, and genuinely want their students to succeed. I find the small classes critical for academic success, as they make it easy to ask questions and to get to know both the faculty and classmates. In addition, I have felt supported by the advising and career services office, which has helped me plan my academic path and think about gaining experience in my intended field. The campus has a strong sense of community with countless opportunities to get involved through clubs, events, and research. In the short time I have been here, Sewanee has already provided me with both a strong education and a true sense of belonging.

There is truly a one-of-a-kind environment here at Sewanee. The academic, social, and natural environments come together to form the most unique and beloved community in the country. From challenging yet rewarding academics, to friendly and welcoming hearts, to the beauty of Mother Nature, there is a reason why our alumni are successful, our community dwells in unity, and our campus is the most beautiful in the country. Above all, Sewanee is for everyone, and whether you're into sports, outdoors, or research, there are thousands of ways to make it your own. Come see why Sewanee holds such a special place in all of our hearts, and get a taste of the true meaning of Ecce Quam Bonum.
Sewanee is such a unique college experience with a really great campus community. My four years at Sewanee have been amazing.

I have loved every minute of my time at Sewanee! I have met my lifelong friends and have made amazing memories! I enjoy the close knit community between students, professors, and community members. The classes are rigorous, but the professors are always willing to help you succeed. My Sewanee education has been great and I appreciate the fact that you gain knowledge beyond your major. Choosing to attend Sewanee was one of the best decisions I ever made!

Coming to Sewanee was the best decision I ever made. I was hesitant to attend a small school in the woods, and now, those are two of my favorite things about it. All of my professors know me by name, support me in all areas (not just in their class), and go above and beyond to ensure my success. While there are definitely some challenging professors and classes, they prepare you for life beyond Sewanee. Because my professors know me so well, I have received outstanding letters of recommendation. As for being in the woods, there is never a lack of fun things to do. Students create their own fun and use our beautiful campus to do so. From waterslides to camping to sports to social events, there is always something going on across campus. Greek life at Sewanee is incredibly unique compared to other schools, and it is more welcoming and inclusive because of it. Everyone is invited to everything, and I've never met students who want their peers to succeed as much as Sewanee students.

Thus far, I have absolutely loved my time here at Sewanee. This college is like no other in the sense that the community aspect is very present at all times. You don't have to know anyone well to strike up a conversation and chat for a while. You do not feel alone here. Sewanee has the ease of transition from High School to College down to a science. Additionally, the professors also engage in the Sewanee Community. Professors here make an effort to get to know each and every one of their students and will occasionally invite them to lunches and dinners on campus with other students. I have baby sat, worked for, ate with, and chatted with my professors. The small classroom sizes also help with getting to know your professors, too. People seldom fall through the cracks here because of the care able to be given by the professors.It doesn't hurt that Sewanee is rich in its natural beauty and the amazing Outing Program makes the outdoors accessible to all students. YSR forever!

I absolutely love attending school at Sewanee, everyone is wonderful and wants to see you succeed. The neighboring community is very kind as well and I have some friends that are alumni and have become my Sewanee family. Everyone can find their place here and I am so glad that I chose Sewanee.

I adore Sewanee. Everything about this place is so unique and full of charm. After only being here for one year as a student, I already feel great sadness forming when I think about leaving in three years. There is no place like Sewanee, and if you talk to anyone about their time here as a student, they will say the same. This place is so easy to fall in love with.

The small class size, strength of professors, world class campus, and the student-athlete lifestyle all contributed to my amazing experience on the Domain. The alumni relations are very strong which have been pointed out to me by individuals who didn't even attend the university. Sewanee is a hidden gem.

I've truly enjoyed my Sewanee experience. I've had the opportunity to meet so many incredible people and grow in ways I never anticipated. From a tight-knit campus community to small classes to a variety of ways to get involved, Sewanee strives to ensure the flourishing of every student's mind, body, and soul. Having spent four years here, Sewnaee has become a home away from home. Despite not being from the South, I never felt out of place. The campus community welcomed me with open arms and gave me space to thrive and grow. I am grateful for all I have learned and experienced by being on this beautiful mountain.

ai_summary: Skidmore College offers a unique environment that blends a strong academic focus with an intimate community feel . Many reviews highlight the excellence of the professors and the small class sizes, which foster close interactions and mentorship. Students appreciate the liberal arts curriculum, which allows for exploration and double majoring, but some find the social scene can feel cliquish, often dividing along lines of artsy versus athletic interests. The campus, set in scenic Saratoga Springs, is described as beautiful, though concerns about accessibility and diversity are prevalent, with a majority-white student body that can be isolating for some. Students enjoy the dining options but have mixed feelings about housing quality and maintenance. Overall, while Skidmore is noted for its academic opportunities and community spirit, it faces challenges related to social dynamics and inclusivity.

reviews.text: Skidmore has been a great, supportive environment! The professors are very accommodating and eager to see student succeed. Saratoga Springs is also an incredible college town.

Skidmore college has an amazing education studies department that prepares you to be an elementary school teacher with certification. The on campus apartments are amazing housing and very comfortable. The professors are really kind and understanding of various health concerns as well. The school is a PWI so there is a lack of diversity but it’s Upstate NY so diversity is fine for the area.

I have loved Skidmore. I chose this school because I needed to be held accountable and academically challenged, Skidmore has done both. Outside of that it has provided a wonderful environment for me to harbor relationships and have adventures on and off campus.

Skidmore has a very strong sense of community, which is one of the things I love about it. The class sizes are small, and professors will know your name. A diverse variety of courses and majors are offered, and our arts and sciences are both very strong. I love the balance here between nature and culture; we have so many artistic opportunities offered at all times, are very close to Albany, Boston, Montreal, and NYC, but we are also 20 minutes away from the Adirondacks, the biggest state park in the country. Since the school is so small it can be hard to branch out and meet new people after your first semester, so it really requires putting yourself out there.
The town of saratoga springs is great! Lots of good food and shops, and fun town events like farmers markets and chowderfest.

Skidmore is a really great undergraduate institution. They really want you to get the most out of the four years that you're here and set you up for success when you leave. Each area of study, humanities, STEM, and the social sciences have really strong programs as well as research opportunities that are so accessible to students. It's a really great school for people who want to try a few different areas of interest and like an interdisciplinary approach to academics.

I love Skidmore but there is a lot of work that still needs to be done with the administration. Students here are incredibly motivated and are not intimidated to see others doing well. They lift each other up.

I absolutely love Skidmore. It is a wonderful place for creative students whose interests don't align with only one subject or area of study. It certainly feels small at times, which has been great for making special connections with peers and professors, but would likely be difficult for those who like to meet different people all the time. I would highly recommend for students who don't mind that tight-knit feeling.

My experience at Skidmore College has been both academically engaging and creatively fulfilling. As a Business and English double major, I’ve developed strong analytical and creative skills through diverse coursework. Outside the classroom, being part of the Ad-Libs Improv Group and serving as Treasurer of the Wombats Ultimate Frisbee team has helped me grow as a leader and collaborator. What stands out most is the supportive, close-knit community—small class sizes make it easy to connect with professors and peers. Overall, Skidmore has allowed me to grow academically, professionally, and personally while exploring a wide range of interests.

As the parent of a young alum, I am so grateful for the experience my daughter, now in graduate school, had at Skidmore. She was challenged, supported, and engaged - in and out of the classroom, and loved so many of her professors. She got involved in theatre immediately and found a home there, as well as in her psychology major. She's still regularly in touch with multiple Skidmore friends in and beyond our area, and the research experience she got certainly helped with getting her first job and then with her successful graduate school applications. Skidmore was a life-changing experience for her!

There was little to no diversity among the students and many of the students do not respect people with different lifestyles than them. I ended up leaving Skidmore after one semester because of how awful the social environment was and many others in my class did the same after that first semester/year.

Super snobby and privileged, majority attended private boarding school for high school ......diversity is essentially non-existent.....professors and academics phenomenal....look around the parking lot, they are the ones driving older vehicles 😂
I had a great experience at Skidmore. I loved all my classes and I was able to study abroad for the full year which was amazing.

I do not think any one college or university is perfect - each is going to have their flaws. That being said, Skidmore has been a good fit for me. I've felt appreciated and respected by my professors, inspired by my classmates, and encouraged to develop as a student and a person. As a student employee, I love working on campus, and I'm grateful to work a job that I enjoy. Looking back on the person I was coming into Skidmore, I've grown considerably over the past few years, and I look forward to my continued growth.
Again, no institution is flawless. I think Skidmore has some aspects that it can improve upon. I've had some good experiences with some on-campus resources and some less than stellar. For example, there have been times where Residential Life has really worked well with me and others where I've had to nag them more to resolve a problem. But, for what it's worth, these are problems that all universities have, so I don't mind dealing with them if it means attending Skidmore.

excellent, strong network of alumni. Amazing professors. Careers opportunities too. I would highly recommend anyone interested to take a look. Saratoga is a wonderful place to be and experience four seasons, lots of activities and fun atmosphere

ai_summary: Smith College stands out for its rigorous academics and supportive, dedicated faculty, fostering a strong learning environment. While many students appreciate the welcoming community and distinct house system that enhances social connections, some find the social scene lacking, particularly in terms of traditional college parties. Reviews note that the campus is beautiful and the diverse course offerings , especially with access to the Five College Consortium, enrich the academic experience. However, there are criticisms regarding student services, including mental health support and dining options, with diners expressing concerns about food quality. The atmosphere is notably empowering for many, especially within the LGBTQ+ community, though some students feel that this focus can overshadow other perspectives. Overall, while Smith offers a unique and intellectually stimulating environment, potential students should consider both the academic rigor and social dynamics carefully.

reviews.text: I loved my time at Smith academically, although I was a bit of an outlier as an older Ada Comstock scholar (and as an older Ada Comstock scholar, career counseling at Smith was useless for me since they were not at all tapped in to the needs of women my age). Even so, now that I'm in grad school I can see how well Smith prepared me for it, as I'm leagues ahead of many in my current cohort as far as writing and research skills are concerned. Leverage connections with professors, participate in projects that garner outside recognition, take on a concentration, and make heavy use of the alumni network afterwards.

Pros
- Professors are exceptionally supportive, approachable, and often become strong mentors.
- High degree of freedom and flexibility in choosing majors and exploring interdisciplinary coursework.
- Beautiful campus with well-maintained facilities.
- Accessible pathways into STEM through beginner-friendly introductory courses.
Cons
- Likely out of jobs if you are not a CS major or are not proactive about independently building your resume and seeking opportunities.
- The campus location may feel isolating or claustrophobic, especially for students accustomed to large cities.
- Students with a strong STEM background may need to take many courses at UMass Amherst while still paying private-school tuition.
Overall
An excellent environment for learning and preparation for graduate or professional studies, but not always the strongest place to launch a career immediately after undergrad.

Recently free because it’s a useless degree. And it wasn’t free for me. A few good companies recruit from Smith but if you can’t take advantage of that there’s very little value to this degree. The culture and policies are incredibly rigid. I got great research and internship opportunities while in school due to the college’s reputation but I’ve been unemployed or underemployed since graduating. My degree did not prepare me for any job. I think a lot of people get into good graduate schools after undergrad here but not the place to start a successful career. Going to Smith College made my life a lot harder than it had to be. Not the education for the modern world.
Lack of resources, low teaching quality, I wish I didn’t apply here, Barnard is so much better, I tried to transfer from here, but I couldn't

The people who really enjoy smith are very specific people. It has a different culture compared to most other NE schools. There really isn’t much house community, even though Smith makes a big deal about it. The academics really challenge you to think, but as a stem major, a good portion of my classes were project-based/flipped classroom which wasn’t very helpful for my learning. I feel like smith stem departments sometimes try to make their classrooms so welcoming and no stress that students don’t actually learn anything. This was my experience in intro bio and math classes. Though, the chem department is really strong. Not much happens here after 9pm, and food closes at 7pm, except for the campus cafe. Northampton isn’t a super safe place either, as I’ve had some bad experiences. Also good luck getting a parking spot. It’s easy to get a TA job once you’ve taken the class. Research labs are often competitive to get into. Overall, it’s value doesn’t match the expensive tuition.

At Smith, academic challenges are underpinned with strong support from professors and genuine encouragement from other students. It was everything I needed to thrive. Every class I took helped sharpen my analytical skills because every opinion needed to be supported with evidence. Professors often adopted a "yes, and" attitude so that students were encouraged to contribute in class. Northampton is a fun town with plenty of great dining and entertainment, but the campus abounds with plenty as well. I'd choose the same school all over again if I could. The alum network is also very vibrant and supportive.

I am extremely happy that I applied and was accepted into the 2029 class of Smith College. It is the perfect fit for me. The small class sizes have allowed me to build relationships with all of my professors, and have given me the opportunity to deeply study and investigate subject with experts in the content areas and not teaching assistants. There are so many opportunities and activities for students on campus, and I love that we are matched to live in self-governing houses instead of dorms. The campus is breathtaking! It is a planned botanical garden and it shows. More importantly, everyone on campus is friendly, supportive, and welcoming to all.
I loved my time at Smith. It's a wonderful, cozy community and vibrant academic environment. I would do it all over again if I could!

Smith campus was a gorgeous place to live and a great place to study, with valuable resources and strong community. I enjoyed my classes and wish I'd spent more time in office hours or the career center!

I am enjoying my time at Smith. I appreciate the number of opportunities that have been given to me as someone early in my college education, especially access to research and funding. So far I have really enjoyed all of the professors that I have had, and they have all been very helpful in terms of advise for my education and career.

When I was looking at colleges, I did not think I would end up choosing a historically women's college. But when I first came to tour Smith, I fell in love with the campus. And since I have come to Smith, I have not regretted that decision for a moment. I love my house, I love my classes (as a STEM/bio major), I love my life here. I found my community so quickly, and my professors made me love everything I was learning. Of course, Smith is an acquired taste. Party scene here is pretty much nonexistent. Food gets repetitive. But if Smith works for you, it does in the best ways!

Attending Smith during COVID was challenging, student life never fully bounced back, and I often felt a lack of community and preparation for life after graduation. Still, I found deep friendships and learned to adapt in isolation. Smith’s academic rigor pushed me to think critically and articulate my ideas with confidence. The school’s emphasis on women’s empowerment and student-led learning shaped my voice and perspective. I appreciated the chance to study abroad, which broadened my worldview. While the lack of diversity and limited student activities made engagement difficult, my time at Smith taught me resilience, independence, and how to create meaning even in tough environments.

Pros:
-I've made some truly amazing friends here.
-Professors are quite knowledgeable. Academics are rigorous. Coursework is interesting and engaging.
-The open curriculum allows you to take a variety of classes without having to fulfill distribution requirements.
-Students live in houses rather than dorms, which allows for a nice sense of community.
Cons:
-The library hours and dining hours are limited compared to most colleges.
-I've had multiple experiences where I've been given contradictory information by different staff members, where information on the website wasn't updated, etc., which has created some significant difficulties.
-There are a lot of wealthy students here, which can create an environment I sometimes find frustrating as a middle class student.
-Between work-study, coursework, and a few minor extracurriculars, I'm SO busy all the time.
-Since Smith is a liberal arts college, classes sometimes feel disconnected from any practical application.

I have had only 2 years at Smith College so far, but they have been very fulfilling. The advisors and mentors (tutors, liberal arts advisor, major and minor advisors, concentration advisor, etc.) are all dedicated to helping their advisees and mentees flourish at their time at Smith. Smith academic life is very fast-paced and rigorous, but the support from all these kinds of people and other Smith programs help students like me also feel that they can succeed even if it seems difficult. For example, I am a First Gen student, so I don't know much about the processes for things such as pursuing research or outside programs like study abroad, but I've gotten a lot of help from various mentors at Smith. For classes themselves, the professors have made themselves available even outside their required office hours in order to help their students succeed. I feel like I am truly being supported and that my professors truly believe in my ability to succeed, even when the classes get very hard.

I loved my experience at Smith College. I felt supported by faculty and staff. I was given attention by staff and faculty when I asked for it. Professors clearly cared about me, my academic goals, and how I was doing as a person. I also had a great experience as a member of the Smith College Rowing Team. My coaches and fellow athletes were very supportive and pushed me to my limits. My main critique of Smith College is institutional; Smith is an elite and somewhat insular institution which could benefit from taking action to promote equity within itself. I respect the changes Smith's current president has made to help further these goals.
After my first year here, this university is very similar to high school (or at least mine). It is a very small school, no social aspect, with most students coming from out of the country.

As a student who graduate prior to the pandemic, I thoroughly enjoyed my four year on campus experience. Off-campus access was somewhat difficult if you do not have a car, however I enjoyed going to the beach often, taking day trips to L.A., and the surrounding cities.

There are a lot of benefits associated with going to Soka. For example, you got to take a very wide array of classes, receive financial aid, interact with a very diverse student body, enjoy the beautiful dorms, and the small student faculty ratio. The food is only good while you're in the first month of fall. After that, it is good once or twice a week. What's a party by the way because we don't really have those here anymore. It is almost a cultural thing. Most people just go to nearby places and colleges to party because there are just way to many restrictions in that regard. The school has great facilities in any way you can think of, so if you can change the somewhat boring student life, it will be perfect.

They pride themselves on their “diversity” but it’s all optical. Zero diversity of thought exists at this college. They are a training ground for leftist activists. For a school that preaches tolerance, they have none.

I have had a really wonderful experience at Soka. I like the small class sizes, the study abroad and language experience, the culturally diverse campus, and so much more. The school seems to really value its students and provide us with great events, lots of support from staff, and just generally sets you up to succeed. My main complaint with the school is the amount of prerequisites that you have to take that seem super redundant. Some of them are cool and push you to think differently, but I think there are too many of them.

I enjoy the small community of students and the faculty to student ratio. The employees are very kind. Professors can be understanding if you meet up with them and tell them if you are struggling. There is a lot of diversity in terms of cultures, and it is pretty easy to make friends your first year since there are always events going on. The clubs are fun, but seems like only a handful of people attend the meetings.

I like the campus and most of the staff, however, as a pre-medicine major, I do not feel prepared for medical school or the MCAT. Soka University of America makes students take many unnecessary courses which leaves no room in course selection for the classes we need for our majors. The financial aid package is great as a first year/incoming freshman, but the amount decreases greatly with each year.

I like their study abroad program in which all students can participate when they
are in third grade.

The school is beautiful and all, but the administration sucks. The students suck. There is no system set up to help international students struggling. Worse, their false statistic of this school being a diverse school is false. It is essentially a Japanese school in the US. US citizens and internationals are not really thought of in the day to day running of this place.

This is an incredible school, dedicated to raising capable contributors to society, not just students who join banking or tech solely to pursue financial prosperity. As more and more colleges and university degrees will become less useful, because skills will not be able to be developed for a new world, Soka will be a pioneer at global dialogue and community building to heal the world!

Terrible social scene where parties are just 5 people in a room with one bottle and dimmed lights while a phone speaker plays music. Academics are fine, but the school and students don’t know a healthy relationship between school and work. If you are BIPOC, don’t come; the school doesn’t listen to them. Also, unless you want to go to grad school, your degree from here is useless.
At SUA, I am inspired to take on many opportunities to challenge myself in being an active member of my campus community.

Soka University of America is a university that celebrates diversity on a daily basis, allowing you to broaden your horizons to different perspectives in order to become a global citizen. This college focuses on cultural representation, giving a very unique and welcoming experience to anyone and everyone who steps on campus. It has allowed me to grow into the person I am giving me endless opportunities to research, learn, and develop as a student. With small class sizes you are able to have an experience that has a lot of one on one attention from professors. Professors listen to your concerns, answer questions and make learning something that is enjoyable. It put pride in the diversity it holds. With concentrations on Life Sciences, Social and Behavioral Sciences, International Studies, Environmental Studies, and Humanities, you are able to take a wide variety of courses to truly know what your passions may be. The university requires you to take courses out of your concentration.
Great, beautiful, and amazing University. Amazing professors. The diverse student body and great learning experience.

Soka University attracts mentally unstable people who are drawn to cult-like atmospheres. If you have any interest in your mental health, avoid this school. Most students here aren’t that interested in an education. You won’t find an employable degree here. There are many required fluff courses for a humanities-based education, which teach you what you would already know if you have a basic interest in reading. They pretend their education is worth the money you spend, but it’s not. the required courses added nothing to my life because the concepts they discussed have already been explored in movies and tv shows must people have seen. For example, they will make you discuss good and evil, but most people already think about this in their daily lives. It’s condescending to have professors require you to discuss simple issues like this. This type of degree is meant for privileged individuals who don’t have to worry about getting a job after college.

ai_summary: Southwestern University offers a unique blend of academic rigor and a tight-knit community atmosphere . Students often highlight the strong relationships they build with professors, who are described as caring, accessible, and dedicated to student success. The liberal arts approach encourages critical thinking and interdisciplinary connections, which many appreciate, although some find the curriculum demanding. The campus itself is noted for its beauty and supportive environment , with the surrounding Georgetown area providing a quaint backdrop. However, students express mixed feelings about social life, citing a lack of diverse activities and a somewhat cliquish nature among peers, making it challenging for some to integrate. While the small class sizes foster engagement, they also limit class availability, requiring prompt registration for popular courses. Food quality and tuition costs are recurring concerns, with some feeling the expenses are not justified by the overall experience. Overall, Southwestern presents a supportive academic environment, but prospective students should consider their social preferences and willingness to adapt to a smaller campus culture.

reviews.text: Loved the community and personal feel. Professors and classes feel closer, like everyone is friends and knows each other well.

Overall I enjoyed the small school, close-knit enviornment and small classes. Professors wanted you to succeed and were happy to help, and it was easy to feel close to fellow students with shared majors and interests. However there were staffing issues while I was on campus, high costs to students in tuition that kept rising, and the small town prevented a lot of local network building.

Overall, my experience at Southwestern University has been a positive one, especially if you prefer a small, academically oriented campus. The best part of SU is the ability to interact with professors. The classes are small, professors know your name quickly, and it's also not difficult to form mentorship relationships through office hours, research, or independent projects. This type of attention can be very confidence boosting in my education.

It’s a very small campus with small class sizes, probably 30 students in my largest class and 15 in my average class. Campus culture is busy, there’s always something going on. I enjoy the professors I’ve had, but I think the price is a little much.
I like the school, I transferred from Texas State and I honestly enjoy it much more. It has a nice small community and is a beautiful campus.

Pros: The campus is beautiful, and Georgetown is a great town and close to Austin.
Cons: The school is overly expensive for the education you get, and they continue to raise tuition. The student population is made up of mostly rich, spoiled kids living on their parents' dime. But if you were an average high school soccer player, just have your parents give a bunch of money to the men's soccer program, and you'll be a starter. Money can't buy you love, but it can buy you playing time at SU. I couldn't take it anymore and transferred.
Great environment, well taken care of, staff is nice, opportunities to express yourself are readily available
I am an incoming freshman to the class of 2029 and so far I have loved all of the experiences I've had at Southwestern. Everything has been so welcoming and supportive!

Southwestern University has small classes, which enables excellent access to professors and additional assistance when needed. The university encourages internships with opportunities accross many different career choices.

I deeply regret attending Southwestern. I stayed because I had full tuition, but I honestly wish I had just paid to go somewhere else. The student body is mostly wealthy, legacy kids who are fake, unrelatable, and stuck up. The school pretends to be diverse by constantly posting the same few Black and Hispanic students, but the reality is far from inclusive. Faculty and student turnover is nonstop. Professors leave every year, and so do tons of students. Advisors are discouraging unless you’re one of their favorites. Research opportunities are limited and feel pointless. The social atmosphere is weird and isolating. People act strangely, and I witnessed staff crossing boundaries with students with no consequences. There’s barely any food on campus, athletics are irrelevant, and the only benefit is being kind of close to Austin. This school felt like a bad social experiment. It took a real toll on my mental health. Do not go here. I wouldn’t wish this place on anyone.

Southwestern provides support for adjusting to college living to make the transition seamless. I will say that there is room for improvement in certain areas such as the repetitive food choices and the maintenance issues.

Southwestern University is an excellent choice for students seeking a close-knit academic environment with strong faculty support and undergraduate research opportunities. While it may not suit those looking for a large university atmosphere, its personalized approach and beautiful setting make it a top-tier liberal arts college in Texas. Highly recommended for students who value mentorship, research, and community!

I love the vibes i get from SU.Not only that but it is also an amazing school to attend. When i went tp tour it they told me that it was the first University ever in Texas and also that it original name of the university before SouthWestern was University of Texas At Austin

My experience with Southwestern has been overall positive, though it has its downs as well. It is a small school and many of the freshmen students will be form small cliques that can sometimes last further. It is hard to make friends if you don't participate in the clubs, which, despite what everyone will tell you, are far and few between, especially if you are not interested in anything to do with outdoors. Plus, there has been a history of nothing being done for incidents on campus.
Student life is lackluster, advertisement for anything other than their career fairs and annual academic and/or work symposiums is almost never there. If party life is wanted, Southwestern is definitely not the place because parties are only on weekends (Friday and Saturday nights) and it is only some weekends. Even then, no one really likes the parties outside of the Kappa Sigma frat's ones.

Southwestern University in Georgetown, Texas, is a fantastic institution known for its strong academics, beautiful campus, and welcoming community. As the oldest university in Texas, it blends rich history with innovative programs, offering students a well-rounded liberal arts education. The small class sizes allow for personalized attention from dedicated professors, and the vibrant campus life provides plenty of opportunities for involvement. Its location in charming Georgetown offers a perfect balance between a small-town feel and proximity to Austin’s dynamic culture. Overall, Southwestern University is an excellent choice for students seeking a high-quality education in a supportive environment.

ai_summary: Spelman College is often described as a unique and empowering environment for Black women, fostering a strong sense of sisterhood and community. Many students appreciate the small class sizes , engaging professors , and the availability of diverse programs that encourage personal and academic growth. The culture at Spelman promotes excellence and social change, making it an appealing choice for those wanting to make a difference. However, there are notable challenges, particularly concerning housing conditions, with reports of inadequate facilities and food options . Additionally, the high tuition costs and administrative inefficiencies are common points of frustration. Overall, while Spelman offers a transformative experience enriched by history and tradition, prospective students should be aware of the serious issues regarding living situations and support services.

reviews.text: Spelman has a community that is very focused on academics, and there are a lot of fun things to do in the community and in the Atlanta University Center, especially in the Spring! My only personal downsides are the housing (low availability, underwhelming amenities in the freshman dorms) and the dining, though both have been and still are improving.

I thoroughly enjoy my experience at Spelman College. I just completed my first semester of my freshman year. This was my first choice of colleges and I applied and was accepted with early decision. I visited Spelman many times and completed a summer program before my Junior Year of high school. This helped me make my decision to attend this college in Atlanta, Georgia. I have some family members who had attended before me and their positive experience intrigued me.

I am a first-year student at Spelman College, and I have truly loved my year so far. For the first time, I have professors who genuinely care about my mental health and well-being. Their support has reignited my desire to learn. What was once a closed mind is now open again.

Spelman College was a dream come true. Coming from a background where I knew nothing of HBCUs and to get accepted fresh out of high school was a huge accomplishment I didn’t realize until my freshman year of college at a different institution. So, I reapplied and was admitted again and I can’t thank Spelman enough. Although there were some barriers of cost upon coming to Spelman due to my Hope scholarship not covering the 80% I initially thought due to it being a private institution; I was able to connect with so many lovely individuals and was able to receive a full ride my last couple of years. I cannot thank Spelman enough for the opportunities and the doors that were open just from being a student there. I didn’t get to indulge much into the campus life due to having to work 2 full time jobs while in school, but the benefits of being surrounded by amazing peers and professors during my time here had affects on my life and career even 5 years post graduating.

I love Spelman. I do not love the financial strain it has placed on my family. I recently lost my maternal grandmother earlier this year. It was an unexpected blow to my family in more ways than one. Due to the unforeseen loss, my parents had to contribute to the funeral costs.
Shortly after laying my grandmother to rest, my father lost his job. These two events affected my families finances tremendously. As a result, I am on a payment plan for my tuition.
This chance would help my family out in ways I cannot fully express. I am thankful for the opportunity. Thank you for your consideration.
I would love to see more scholarship opportunities for women who a have decided to make a choice to change the world! Myself included.

Spelman College is a place where Black women are seen, supported, and encouraged to reach their potential. The strong sense of community, both socially and academically, as well as professionally, stands out. Students are driven and empowered, confidently discussing their goals and embracing leadership, which creates an inspiring environment.
Academically, the challenges are rigorous, but they shape Spelman women into strong scholars and professionals. While there are overwhelming moments, the support systems and sisterhood make up for it. Overall, at Spelman, you gain not only an education but also confidence, community, and a clearer sense of your identity.

I have absolutely adored my experience here at Spelman so far. The community is so rich and being able to bond with my sisters has been so fulfilling. I will say it has its lows such as the older buildings and the difficulties that come with that but thankfully that is in the works. Other than that I am having the best time here and I encourage my future little sisters to apply and hope to see them next fall.

From attending the prestigious college, Spelman so far if has been truly empowering. As a black girl trying to find her own balance it’s nice being surrounded by intelligent, driven Black women that are driven with what they do which constantly inspired me to grow. The most important thing is the foundation of sisterhood and support here . Being at Spelman makes me excited for the upcoming opportunities that are hands on and helped me to step into my power.
I adore Spelman! It is only my first year and I’ve already gained so much knowledge and opportunities that can help me with my career.

The campus is really pretty and has improved a lot aesthetically, based on upperclassmen I’ve spoken to. The cafe food is very unpredictable, taste wise, although you can look up the meals being served each day. There are options for dairy free, gluten free, vegetarian, vegan, etc people. Atlanta is definitely a very “alive” city so there’s no shortage of parties, however sometimes it can be annoying trying to get around the city as a whole. The curriculum is amazing, and the professors I currently have are really good at teaching and fostering a respectful learning environment, except for one of them. There are a lot of amazing people here to get to know :)

Attending Spelman College has been one of the most enriching chapters of my life. From the empowering academic environment to the sisterhood that lifts and inspires, Spelman nurtured not only my intellectual growth but also my personal evolution. The faculty are deeply committed, the campus culture is vibrant, and the legacy of excellence is palpable in every classroom and conversation.
That said, my sophomore year did bring a challenge: a shortage of on-campus housing. While it didn’t overshadow the overall experience, it highlighted an area for improvement. I’d strongly recommend Spelman guarantee housing for freshmen and sophomores to ease transitional stress. If upperclassmen housing exceeds capacity, a smooth, pre-arranged transition into offsite student housing would help maintain the sense of community that makes Spelman so special.

Overall, my Spelman College experience has been a rollercoaster. I have learned valuable lessons, expanded my personal and academic insight, and found community. However, it does not come without struggle. For example, my freshman year, I found it hard engaging with student life leaving me lonely and uncertain. I also wanted to participate in fine arts but I soon realized that there wasn't much support -let alone an emphasis- for the arts. Although I sometimes feel behind on my growth, I'm glad I am continuing my education here. I wouldn't have found my niche interests, fundamental resources, or even made the good friends that I have if i decided to leave. I am hopefull that over the next two years, I can continue to find oppurtinities and make valuable connections.

My college experience has been truly transformative. Attending school in Atlanta, a major hub for culture and entertainment, gave me access to opportunities I could not have found elsewhere. I gained hands-on experience through events, internships, and networking that helped shape both my professional path and personal growth. Atlanta was not just where I studied, it was where I learned to lead, create, and connect.
Most importantly, I found a strong sense of community. Being surrounded by brilliant, supportive, and ambitious Black women built my confidence and pushed me to grow in every area of life. The sisterly spirit on campus made me feel seen, heard, and deeply supported. I am grateful for every moment each challenge, lesson, and memory. This experience prepared me not just for a career but for life. I leave proud of the woman I have become and excited for the journey ahead.

Spelman is a college where you will be surrounded by excellence and thus forced to become the most excellent version of yourself possible. The staff are phenomenal, and it is very apparent that they are dedicated to helping you achieve success.
Spelman College has amazing recognition globally, and attending a competitive HBCU opens an abundance of opportunities for students.

ai_summary: St. John's University - New York receives mixed feedback from its students, particularly regarding its academics, student life, and campus environment. Many reviews highlight the helpfulness and dedication of professors , citing their willingness to support students and provide engaging classes, although experiences can vary significantly by individual professor. The university is praised for its diverse student body and vibrant campus culture , with numerous activities, clubs, and events that cater to various interests. However, some students express concerns about high tuition costs and the adequacy of student services, particularly around financial aid and internship opportunities. The social scene is viewed as limited by some, especially for commuters, and there are calls for improved facilities and resources . Overall, while many appreciate the well-rounded experience and supportive community, others feel there are areas needing improvement, particularly regarding campus engagement and accessibility.

reviews.text: I really enjoy St. John’s nursing program as well as the school itself. Most professors are very helpful, supportive, and caring of not just your grades but yourself as a student. There is always some kind of event going on around campus as well as a ton of clubs for all types of hobbies and interests. The school also has so many resources and people that can help you get jobs, prepare for interviews, and talk with about anything you need or are concerned about.

It's the degree mill of Brooklyn/Queens. Felt very impersonal attending here. They love hiking the tuition, especially during the covid-19 pandemic. Administration could care less about their students. I had to switch guidance counselors since mine kept trying to pressure me to study broad (more money for little old St. John's I imagine). Save your money and go to a city/state school.

My overall experience at St. John’s University has been meaningful and growth-oriented, as it offers a supportive academic environment with professors who are knowledgeable, approachable, and genuinely invested in student success. I value the diverse campus community, which provides opportunities to learn from different cultures and perspectives. The Vincentian values are clearly reflected through the emphasis on service, leadership, and social responsibility, contributing to my academic and personal development.
I also appreciate the academic resources available, including tutoring services, career development support, and student organizations that encourage involvement and leadership. However, I would like to see improvements in financial support, particularly for international and continuing students. Increased transparency, flexibility, and availability of financial aid and scholarships would greatly benefit students who are academically committed but face financial challenges.

ai_summary: St. Lawrence University offers a unique college experience characterized by a tight-knit community and a strong emphasis on academics. Many students appreciate the small class sizes and dedicated professors who genuinely care about their students’ success. However, there are mixed feelings about student life; while some highlight the campus's rich traditions and various extracurricular activities, others express concern over a declining party culture and a lack of diversity in the student body. The remote location contributes to both the school's allure—offering access to outdoor activities—and a sense of isolation, especially during harsh winters. Overall, SLU fosters lasting relationships, both among peers and within its extensive alumni network, though navigating social dynamics can be challenging for some students.

reviews.text: St. Lawrence provides a great experience for those who attend, what you put in is what you get out. For a small liberal arts college the social atmoshphere is great, and the alumni network is as advertised. If you are a student athlete the facilities are some of the best among all d3 schools, and the dining services are above average. I hesitiantly chose here over Hamilton & Colby, and I have not been disapointed in my decision.

I have really enjoyed getting to embrace all of the things I want to do and not having to pick and choose. I can be an athlete, work as an admissions ambassador, work as a guide for the outdoor program, and go abroad all while getting a degree setting me up for work in the medical field after my four years here.

I really like how down to earth everyone is and I love the small size. One of my favorite things is the small class sizes, meaning that we get a more engaging class experience and get to know professors well. I love how accessible the faculty is as a whole. One thing I would like to see some change in is the dining services, specifically the ordering website which is often down. I also think it would be great if the school brought back some more of the old traditions.

I work as an admissions ambassador at SLU not because it pays, but because I genuinely love where I go to school. From the people on campus (including the faculty and the greater Canton community), the academics, and so much more, I love my school.
Absolutely love SLU, I feel that I’ve found my place and my people and am the happiest I’ve ever been:)

I love attending St. Lawrence, and I chose it because of the campus community. Everybody is super nice and I've meet a ton of people who will be life long friends. With that said no university is perfect, some of the housing is considerably worse than others and the dining plans have been rough since they instituted a new ordering system. In the end I'm so glad I chose SLU for undergrad and I'm going to miss tis place so much when I graduate. I've grown so much as a person and the memories I've made will stay close to my heart forever.

St. Lawrence is best described as 'home'. From an amazing team of professors who truly care about their students as individuals, to a group of peers who deeply respect the backgrounds and perspectives of each other - I believe that SLU creates a space that fosters the growth of a globally minded and locally centered community.
Feels like one big community. Very good stem programs. lots of off campus study options and internships. Alumni network is great!

The best part of SLU is the community. Being in Laurentian Singers, SLUFunk, and track & field shaped my time here, and support from teammates and captains made it easy to feel welcome. The music scene is active for a small school, with plenty of chances to perform and get involved.
Academically, classes can be excellent, especially with professors who take time to know you. Small class sizes keep you engaged and make learning feel personal.
The downsides mainly come from the environment. Campus can feel isolated in the long winters, and the location means you have to create your own fun or stay very involved. Balancing academics, athletics, and music gets overwhelming, and sometimes resources don’t match how demanding student life is.
I enjoy attending SLU. The environment is super safe and friendly and the professors are incredible!! The only thing I would change is to have pasta salad occur more often in Dana:)
Great experience overall, small school community really makes everything worth it. Life on campus makes you feel like you are in a completely different world.

It's hard to do poorly when there are as many safety nets as SLU provides. Between the career center, multiple advisors, coaches, mentors, and staff, I am surrounded by people who genuinely care about my well-being. Social life is fabulous here because everyone lives on campus. It's not every Thursday night in life that you can watch live music for free!

There seems to be an institutional focus on attracting new students; unfortunately, this enthusiasm seems to cease entirely after your first semester of freshman year.
Absolutely incredible professors; class offerings can feel limited, but every class is phenomenal-- engaging and informed.

I love being at SLU. I think the community is unmatched and truly all types of people come together here. There is something special about wearing SLU merch around because no matter where in the world you are, you will be recognized. SLU is a family and I wouldn't change my experience one bit.

There is a great close knit community at St. Lawrence! I have made amazing friendships that I will carry on with me past my time at St. Lawrence. I would like to see there be healthier food options incorporated into all the meal plans.

ai_summary: St. Mary's College of Maryland (SMCM) offers a beautiful riverside campus and a close-knit community that fosters personal connections between students and professors. Many reviews highlight the exceptional accessibility of faculty and their commitment to student success, particularly in smaller class settings. However, the college faces challenges such as limited diversity and feelings of isolation due to its rural location, which some students find detracts from social opportunities. While academics are generally viewed positively, with rigorous courses that often lead to strong relationships and networking opportunities, critics point out issues with campus life and student services, particularly for commuters. The athletics scene is modest, and though the campus lifestyle can sometimes revolve around weekend activities, many students enjoy the outdoor opportunities and artistic clubs that SMCM provides, distinguishing it from larger institutions.

reviews.text: Everyone here is beyond weird, and there is absolutely nothing to do ever. Everyone went to high school together, so if you're an out of state student like I am, you will really struggle meeting people. I am transferring to a different school next fall.

St. Mary’s is so fun and a good balance of parties and academics. I was able to make many close friends and campus feels like home. There’s great housing variety on campus and some available off campus as well. Academics are challenging, and living by the water in such a beautiful place has been so much fun.

Very good overall. The fact that it is a small campus and that it is such a tight knit community really is the best part of going to school there. The people really make the place what it is and for me the people on campus, including professors, other students, faculty members really made the place special.

I love the academics and professors, however the school could use a little bit more diversity. For some students, there has been a lack of repercussions for major actions such as several Title IX violations, as well as racism (mostly on the sports teams.) The school is also far from the closest town so in order to go anywhere off campus, a car is required. The parking is relatively cheap for $100 per year. The campus is beautiful and relatively walkable. Overall probably 7/10 due to student life.

For anyone who is interested and or currently majoring in something STEM related, this school is likely going to be a good fit for you (especially for marine science students). At SMCM, a vast majority of the STEM professors here genuinely want to see you succeed and will do whatever it takes to help you get there! There is also a pre-health program in place that is designed to aid pre-med students through the medical school application process. However, if you are looking for an active party scene and a large campus, do not come here. While there are occasional parties on weekends, they are certainly not comparable to schools that have fraternities or sororities. I will also say that there are no “walkable” off-campus shops available to you, as the nearest town is about fifteen minutes away from campus. Yet, there are always on-campus activities going on (especially clubs). All in all, if you are an academic person and a nature lover, you will definitely enjoy your time here!

The professors, part time job opportunities, support and connections are awesome! They really set you up for a job or grad school. There is mold in all the dorms - see if they have done deep cleaning or redone anything - and it is small. You’ll figure out quick if this is the right campus for you. It is gorgeous but it is definitely not gonna be the vibe for everyone!

So far, I have had a lovely time here at St. Mary’s! The academics are plenty difficult and it is really easy to connect with professors. The outdoor scene is amazing with kayaking, paddle boarding, swimming, fishing, and more. The dining hall is the best dining hall food I’ve had and the housing is like most other colleges except that it’s guaranteed for all four years (which I find to be a benefit).

St. Mary's has been a positive academic experience for me due to the smaller class size and therefore ability to connect to others easily, as well as personal connections to professors that make learning easier and more understanding.

St. Mary's College of Maryland was my only choice for school, my ride or die, some would say. I was so excited, but my first semester entirely altered my view of this place and myself. Firstly, my experience with the academics here has been superb. I have close connections with my professors, have been challenged in my classes, and have been given extra research and academic opportunities. The student life side of things is where it all goes downhill for me. The facilities are incredibly outdated, not having been updated in at least 50 years. The dorms made for a disgusting and uncomfortable living situation that took a heavy toll on my mental health. I also had lots of issues with apathetic staff and endless bureaucratic loops. I had to move back home after some scary situations, and no one I dealt with in said bureaucracy cared. Additionally, social dynamics and cliques at this school are strange, and events/clubs are poorly attended and organized, making it hard to meet new people.

Its a great academic experience and housing is guaranteed all four years. Not much around but easy to find fun with your friends. Not a huge party school but always something to do. Sports are pretty accessible and the community is very fun and easy to immerse yourself in. Professors are all great resources and very helpful with all things academics and beyond.

I attended St. Mary's College of Maryland with the intention of majoring in Anthropology, as the school is part of Historic St. Mary's City with active archaeological sites. Everywhere you walk on campus you walk on and past history. All of my professors are so passionate and knowledgeable on the subjects the teach. So much so that their passion inspired a greater sense in me. I am now a History major and Anthropology minor, and I have become passionate about learning again. There are many opportunities for on campus work, and many clubs for all different interests. There are some problems with Wi-Fi and the new portal system as we just made the switch last year, but as the school gets adjusted the problems will sort themselves out. Although the school being secluded is something that some are anxious about, I love being surrounded by nature and having a retreat to focus on my studies and connect to the school and campus culture!
I lile the fact that its by the water. And we have many resources for students. But those resources are mainly helpful for students on campus not commuters.

Pretty typical for a liberal arts college - super small therefore the amenities and features are pretty small scale. The campus food is mediocre and the support staff have gone on strike multiple times because their needs and requests were neglected. The professors tend to be dedicated to their work and high quality, but there are for sure a few odd balls. The title 9 office stays pretty busy for how small it is there.
SMCM is a great option for in state commuting students. It's a public honors liberal arts college with great academic programs, but that's about it.

It is a beautiful Camus and I would recommend this school to others!! But it is not super diverse but that's ok because I can tell the school is working on that. I love how pretty the school is I could talk about the beauty all day!!

ai_summary: St. Norbert College (SNC) provides a strong sense of community and personal connection, with many students noting the welcoming atmosphere and the approachable, dedicated professors who strive to build relationships and support academic success. The small class sizes enhance these interactions, allowing students to feel more engaged and valued. While the campus and its food receive high praise, some reviews express concerns about the lack of diversity and high tuition costs, which can feel burdensome for some. Overall, students appreciate the myriad opportunities for involvement on campus, ranging from academics to sports and social activities, making it a fitting choice for those seeking a close-knit college environment.

reviews.text: St Norbert has been a fantastic experience for me. The Catholic roots of this college is present, having many activities and groups to grow in your faith. The professors here truly care about your success, and help you whenever you need it. All the faculty I’ve come across in general are all kind and caring. This school is worth it.

I enjoy the food, classes, gym, parties, friendly atmosphere, and the welcoming feeling from every group on campus. I greatly enjoy my fraternity and the work I do to support it. I would like to see further participation in club life and to make the involvement fair mandatory.
I feel like I am at home with St. Norbert. Everyone is so kind and welcoming. There are many on-campus activities that can broaden your experience.

As a man rooted in my Catholic faith, studying business, and an avid outdoorsman, I can truly say that St. Norbert College provides immense opportunities to learn and grow spiritually, academically, and mentally and physically pursuing hobbies and interest in the beautiful surrounding area and on campus.

The Campus Safety Department has made significant advancements throughout 2025 and into the beginning of 2026. A number of important safety initiatives have been implemented and completed, including—but not limited to—the addition of water and ice rescue equipment, emergency bleeding control kits, an updated reunification plan, full FEMA Incident Command certification for all officers, and enhanced mental health training. The Campus Safety Department has adopted a community policing philosophy, fostering positive relationships through active engagement on social media and meaningful in-person interactions with students and staff. This approach promotes trust, communication, and a stronger sense of safety across campus.
This college has an amazing staff and all the professors are able to connect with students on a more personal level.
St Norberts was a college I had only dreamed of getting into, and I am so glad I had the chance to. It has allowed me to take control of my future and pursue course I actually enjoy.

My experience at SNC was one of a kind. The community of students, professors, and faculty resemble that of a family oriented space. Being apart of campus culture, was profound.
