The greek life, atheltics, faith, and school spirit was unique to any other campus I had visited. Every student at SNC is truly a member of the community, not a number. The neighborhood is safe and friendly, as well as so supportive of the students and their future endeavors. If I could, I would go back in a heart beat, and make an effort to stay as close as I can to continuing to make a greater impact on future classes to come.

Everyone from the faculty to my classmates are very warm and welcoming. All of my professors not only challenge me to grow, but are always there for help and support if needed. It is evident how much I am cared for at SNC and that's exactly what I was looking for in a college. There are so many great opportunities to get involved and have fun aside from the schoolwork. Overall, I have had such a positive experience here and I wouldn't rather be anywhere else.
I am not religious at all but I still love it here at St. Norbert. It was so easy to make friends and there are so many traditions and clubs to take part in!
An amazing college with a beautiful campus! Highly recommend going here you will find lifelong friends!

Love the small class sizes and being able to talk with professors or work with them one-on-one when needed. I think this has been very beneficial to my education and ability to manage athletics with academics. There are many opportunities to be very involved on campus, which makes campus life fun and provides many valuable experiences for personal and professional growth.

I love the campus and small town surrounding, with having Green Bay so close too. It's also nice having so many athletes on campus and the overall shared culture between everyone of community and faith.
Really great school and safe environment. The town is very beautiful and all of the teachers and people around you care about you.
My overall experience with St. Norbert College so far is going very well! I am enjoying the athletics and academics here and can't wait to see what the coming years bring! :)

ai_summary: St. Olaf College offers a unique campus experience characterized by a close-knit community where both students and professors genuinely care about one another. The academic environment is challenging, with many reviewers praising the attentiveness and support of professors who are accessible and invested in their students' success. Students appreciate the vibrant offerings in music, theater, and study abroad opportunities, while the beauty of the campus and Northfield's charm are frequently mentioned. However, some students feel the social scene is somewhat limited, and there are concerns regarding diversity and the isolation some experience due to the college's rural location. Many highlight the strong support services available, particularly for first-generation and low-income students, while others express frustrations with bureaucratic processes and available recreational activities. Overall, St. Olaf seems particularly well-suited for students seeking an enriching liberal arts education in a supportive environment.

reviews.text:
Pluses -
Student environment - very friendly students.
Buildings - lots of study areas.
Scheduling - never was on a waitlist for a class
Work study - always available on campus
Cost - affordable
Minuses -
Housing - was a challenge because of the matching process
Professors - not consistently high quality across departments
Course requirements - too many required components; not enough depth
Classes offered are not consistently available
Dry campus

I've had a great time, with friendly professors and classmates, informative classes, and good food. The gluten-free options are pretty great most of the time! There's a lot of fun clubs to join (I've been enjoying dance and gaming) and there are lots of fun events hosted by campus organizations.
I had a terrific experience! Excellent facility and facilities with great opportunities to get involved. I also developed lifelong friendships with many students and professors!

I received an amazing college education at St. Olaf. Great faculty, fellow classmates, and facilities. My education continues to serve me exceptionally well in my career and I've loved seeing how the College continues to innovate and evolve. Proud to be an alum!

St. Olaf is truly a wonderful college. The price is admittedly intimidating and overwhelming, but apart from that, the faculty and staff do an incredible job of making your experience worthwhile. There are countless extracurriculars, clubs, and events to join or attend, giving endless opportunities to make connections with other students. The campus is quite small, but the location of the school allows for many fun and new experiences to explore off campus.
St. Olaf College supports and enables U.S. military recruitment by arrogant representatives who seemingly don’t care about the collegiate’s “fit”, but rather their recruitment numbers.

I have absolutely enjoyed my three years thus far at St. Olaf. Perhaps the most significant part of my experience has been being a part of the men's club rugby team. I had never played rugby, but I signed up during the club fair in my first year. Through rugby, I became friends with my best friend and had a great support system and feeling of home.
Academically, St. Olaf has surpassed my expectations. Professors truly care and provide office hours, which lead to strong student-professor relationships. SI sessions with student staff also offer excellent academic assistance.
There are many clubs and opportunities to explore interests. As a kinesiology major, I’ve applied what I’ve learned in hands-on settings using the Qualisys motion capture system, the HUMAC system, the BOD POD, and VO₂ max testing.
One thing I’d like to see change is the housing policy. Requiring most students to live on campus all four years limits flexibility and independence, especially for upperclassmen.

I enjoy St. Olaf College for its resources and support and interest in its students. They truly want the best for their student body. However, it is very hard to make friends on campus. The student life is very cliquey and once people are in a group, they do not venture out of it. I would still recommend going to St. Olaf, especially as an introvert. It provides lots of great opportunities academically and professionally.
Campus food get old quick. Could use more variety. Love the small school size. Small Class size makes it easy to talk to professors

I truly enjoyed my time at St. Olaf, I made many close friends and received mentorship from several professors. In general, it's a close-knit community where students feel supported. However, during my time as a student there, we had a few safety concerns on campus. As St. Olaf is a small liberal-arts college, it doesn't have the sports and party scene of larger universities, but it's a great place where I received a quality education.

If you are a more socially outgoing but still academic/studious person, this is a lovely school. I had a great balance between socializing and making friends through clubs and events, whilst still dedicating time to my studies, where I had professors that I really connected with.
It is a good school. There are lots of events going on campus. One thing I dislike is how hard it is to get off campus.

DONT GO HERE. I am telling you, if you’re between this decision or another school please choose the other school. This college has every student unable to have parking permits, constantly getting taxed, and housing is severely an issue.

My Experience at St. Olaf College
Attending St. Olaf College has been an amazing experience. I’ve made so many great friends, especially through club hockey, and I love the sense of community here. Everyone is welcoming, and it’s easy to connect with people who share my interests.
The professors are incredible—they genuinely care about students’ success and are always willing to help. St. Olaf is all about supporting students in reaching their goals, offering so many resources to guide us academically and professionally.
Beyond academics, the campus is absolutely beautiful. Whether it’s the fall colors, winter snow, or spring flowers, every season makes St. Olaf feel like a postcard. Walking around campus always reminds me how lucky I am to be here.
Overall, I love my time at St. Olaf. The friendships, supportive professors, and stunning campus make it feel like home, and I’m excited for all the experiences still to come!

I loved St. Olaf! As a recent graduate, now that I am finished with my time as an Ole I always look back on it fondly. It is a perfect size - and with Carleton close by, there are more social opportunities than a small school would typically provide. As someone with a Carl as a partner, Olaf was definitely the better experience of the two. I am always surprised how many people around the world know St. Olaf. Olaf is a great choice if you're interested in study abroad, building a strong community and has alums that are passionate about the school. Rural Minnesota can be boring, but this leading to students staying on campus and spending time with one another. It can be difficult to get to the cities. The administration is also very compassionate and really wants to help. I also recommend visiting the many cultural events on campus - I loved all of the student theater events throughout the semester! Olaf is also a great choice for avoiding Greek life.

ai_summary: Stonehill College offers a unique blend of strong academics and a close-knit community , with many reviews highlighting the accessibility and dedication of faculty who are invested in student success. The small class sizes foster personal relationships between professors and students, which is a significant advantage for many. However, the social scene seems to be a common point of concern; students describe a lack of vibrant campus life , with limited party options and a need for more engaging student activities. While the campus itself is praised for its beauty and safety, reviews also note issues with the dining options and the overall campus culture, which some find cliquish and less diverse than expected. Despite these criticisms, many alumni express gratitude for the supportive environment that helped shape their educational and personal growth, emphasizing the school's commitment to service and community engagement.

reviews.text: So far, as a sophomore, I have loved my experience here at Stonehill. It has been everything I could have hoped for coming from a small high school. Even though Stonehill is considered “small,” it is still much bigger than my high school and has given me many of the experiences I felt I missed out on. Overall, it offers what I see as the perfect college experience. I really enjoy the vibes of the campus, the friendships I’ve built, and the overall layout. But if I could suggest a change, it would be to improve some of the housing. While we are getting a brand-new gym to replace one that is already modern, some of the dorm buildings have not been updated since the 1990s and could really use serious renovations .I would also make changes to the social scene. Because it is a smaller campus, there aren’t always events or parties on weekends, which makes it feel like there isn’t much to do at times. But, I truly enjoy being here and am grateful for my experience at Stonehill.
Overall, my experience so far at Stonehill has been pretty mid. I would say it is a great school if you are looking to get a good job in the future and nothing else.

Stonehill is a great College in a beautiful town. The Professors are outstanding.
I would like to see the athletic budget increased.

Stonehill is a strange place. I have come to enjoy it, but I cannot say I recommend it fully if you don't cleanly fit into a popular kid/athlete mold. The students here can be weirdly mean and judgmental, but of course, this is not true of everyone. Some of the clubs can be really cool, and most have really dedicated students running them, so I highly recommend getting involved if you attend here. The campus is lovely and perfect if you're looking for small, liberal arts school with some history vibes.
Overall, the academics are strong, the tuition is insane, and the people aren't always ideal, but it's a solid place.
Stonehill professors were very invested in helping me find my way, and the community and school spirit was so welcoming

I have enjoyed my time here at Stonehill! I was able to find my community and build a lot of meaningful relationships. The faculty and staff are very friendly and always willing to get you the support you need. I have been lucky to be offered a lot of different opportunities in my time here. From doing research, going to conferences, networking, leadership positions, cultural immersion experiences, and so much more- Stonehill has a lot to offer!

Stonehill is such a community based campus and offers a very warm and welcoming feel! I fell in love the minute I toured; this place is my home! There is a guarantee of hands-on experience that offers great post grad resources!

Being a student at Stonehill College means more to me than I'll ever be able to type out in words. Stonehill has a certain way of attracting the most amazing people - and if you get involved in programs/events/groups on campus, you'll quickly be able to see that for yourself. The stunning campus, kind staff, and thoughtful professors are truly something you will not find anywhere else. Student athletes are stepping up to D1 competition, the food is on the ups, and SGA/OSE puts on events that students deserve and should take advantage of. Stonehill will forever be my home, and the place that I will be proud to return to and be an alum of. I would not change my experience here for the world.

Stonehill is the perfect environment to find yourself. I feel as though my time at Stonehill has allowed me to become a more curious, well-rounded, and enhanced version of myself. As a student, you have a plethora of opportunities to get involved in whatever area you see fit. Class sizes are small, so you will likely know most people and engage with your professors on a more personal basis. This is an aspect of Stonehill that I find to be most attractive and beneficial, because the connections I have made are unparalleled. I feel very fortunate to have the opportunity to attend an institution like Stonehill and I know for a fact that I am a better version of myself as a result.
I thoroughly enjoy my time at Stonehill! It truly is my second home. I stay here every weekend, and always have a fun time.
The campus itself is beautiful. I feel as though it is important to get involved here. No matter your walk of life, you can find a place a Stonehill, but getting involved is a vital part!

Stonehill College has been a great place for me both as a student and an athlete. With the smaller close knit community I have been able to make an impact and share my voice amongst others, especially in the athletic community. The beautiful campus is more spread out considering the population but it allows for a bigger campus feel. Professors have students best interest in mind, especially working with athletes around their crazy schedules. Stonehill continues to rise to the division one level, making an impact within competition against other schools and then also in the student-athlete experience. The location could not be better, being around the great suburbs of MA with lots around to do and then being only 30 minutes away from the city.

I am currently a double major junior. I love the academic portion of this school. Female Athletes bullying other students and school turns the other way when it is reported. College has no control over students - drugs, alcohol and many sexual assaults within past few weeks. Very rude upper school staff that are not helpful when you need to report bullying. They do not take Mental Health seriously.

Stonehill College offers so much more than just a strong education—it becomes a second home for its students. Transitioning to college is overwhelming with so many unknowns, but at Stonehill, professors, staff, priests, and upperclassmen go out of their way to ensure that new students feel welcomed and supported from day one.
The small class environment form a tight knit community within the classroom that allows the professors to get to know their students on a better level, allowing them to provide help and support. Everyone at Stonehill is so friendly, making it easy to befriend people in class to work on projects together or even to hang out after class. I have truly found my best friends while at Stonehill.
Stonehill has pushed me to be a better, more confident leader through the various clubs and organizations I have joined. I am forever grateful for the time I have spent here already and cannot wait to see what the future holds for me at Stonehill and beyond!

Stonehill is just a longer word for home. I have loved all of my time here and I am a better person for having enrolled at this school. If you're looking for honest: campus life is what you make of it- if you stay in, it'll be boring, if you go to events, it won't. The party scene is there if you want it and absolutely not if you don't. Food is alright but you learn to love it. Dorms are usual college dorms, but I've liked them! Professors are very good, and they genuinely care about you and make connections w/ you. I owe 90% of my happiness to Campus Ministry, which made me realize what true friends are like. Stonehill may not be the fanciest or most expensive- but it's a beautiful place full of beautiful people that has allowed me to grow, and I wouldn't trade coming here for the world. Is every moment here amazing? No, of course not. But is my life more amazing because I came here? Genuinely 100%. Good luck with your college search, I wish you all the best! :) GoHill!

ai_summary: Susquehanna University offers a tight-knit and vibrant community that many students appreciate for its supportive atmosphere and small class sizes , which facilitate strong connections with professors and peers. Students often commend the faculty for being approachable and dedicated to their success, contributing to a positive academic experience. However, dining hall food is a frequent point of concern, with many reviews highlighting its poor quality and limited variety. While the campus boasts beautiful scenery and numerous clubs and extracurricular activities, some students express frustrations regarding the administration's communication and responsiveness. Overall, Susquehanna is characterized as a friendly place to study, but potential students should be aware of its limitations regarding dining options and some organizational aspects.

reviews.text: I enjoy my experience at Susquehanna, and I like many of the people that go here. It is a very safe environment with a beautiful campus, but unfortunately, they recently changed their meal system and has been really controlling how we use our meal swipes which is a big downside for me. Despite this flaw, I love the professors and I love getting to know them. I enjoy the personal and tight-knit community along with the many study spaces on and off campus.

I honestly loved my time at SU and made a lot of good memories... I was hardly a party animal. You would always find me biking around, keeping my eye on campus making sure everyone was behaving and I was always jamming out to music. I had some awesome professors during my time and my department was fun and the professors were chill.
I would love to see the campus food improve, it was decent at times but was mostly bad.
Susquehanna University is a great school, in a beautiful historical town. The food could be a lot better, it is pretty gross and we have very limited options.
Over all really great place. Academics are really good. The professors care about students only bad things that the food is terrible.
Susquehanna was a great small college. It felt exactly like home away from home. The university always provided resources that I needed and I felt that I left equipped to work in my field.

the campus is SO small. if you don’t immediately fit into a group the first week of school you will talk to nobody. everyone is also very nosy and talks about everyone. you will see the same people everywhere you go no matter where you are. if you don’t have a car your out of luck with leaving the campus area at all. nothing to do outside campus either even if you have a car. closest fast food is wendy’s. profs are nice but some are pretentious. party scene here is next to none. very lame and usually gets shut down. food here sucks the main campus dinning hall is like cafeteria food in jail. the cooks actually make good food when important people are on the campus. The high school is down the road from campus and you will see 16 year olds in the frats. they up charges tuition every year. there redoing the campus dining hall every year as well. very much a suitcase school. a good portion of the student body live 5 mins down the road.

Attending Susquehanna University has been a transformative experience for me. As a first-generation college student, stepping onto campus felt both exciting and intimidating, but I quickly found my place through the support of student organizations and the inclusive community here. I’m double majoring in Criminal Justice and Biochemistry, and my professors have been incredibly supportive, helping me balance academics with my ongoing service in the Army Reserves. The university’s close-knit environment allows me to thrive both inside and outside the classroom. I’ve connected with students from all backgrounds through groups like BSU, ALAS, ISA, and the Volleyball Club. Each organization has helped me grow personally and professionally. Susquehanna has pushed me to become more independent, resilient, and focused on my goals. This campus isn’t just where I study—it’s where I’m learning who I am and who I want to be.

Overall, my experience with Susquehanna University has met the expectations of what I thought "college life" would look like. The town of Selinsgrove is picturesc in the fall. Most people are friendly overall and the professors specialize in what they teach. Only thing that needs improvement is the food;

there's barely anything to do on campus and clubs -- whether it's hobby clubs or related to majors -- aren't the friendliest when it comes to trying to get involved. there are a lot of cliques that are formed which makes it hard to establish good relationships especially if you consider yourself an introvert. you're also completely out of luck if you don't drive or don't have friends that drive, since there is nothing near campus. the closest shopping centre, which is just a target, giant, tj maxx, etc. is about fifteen minutes away. there's barely anything in the town walking distance to the campus, and everything there closes extremely early anyway. i wouldn't suggest going there if you can find some place better. (the creative writing major isn't worth all of that isolation.)
Susquehanna has been a great place to call home away from home. Athletics has been a huge part of my collegiate success and i can’t be more grateful.

I liked how close the community was. Susquehanna did a very a great job making sure everyone on campus felt included and as a family. The only issue was food selections. When you need to eat healthy as a lifestyle it was very hard to keep that going with the options we had on campus. Other than that I have no complaints. I know that as years go on it’ll only get better and better.
overall pretty great! The housing lottery needs to change and the change needs to get a little better
I would like to see better dining, and more flexible hours and healthy options for athletes who practice late or get home from games late.

My experience at Susquehanna University has been great. The small-campus environment allowed for a close-knit community where it was easy to build meaningful relationships with peers and professors. The faculty were genuinely invested in student success, often going out of their way to offer guidance, and support. Campus is always filled with students and many fun opportunities to pursue.
I love susquehanna! I play basketball here and the community is so awesome and welcoming! Teachers are amazing as well as coaches and all other staff!

Safe spaces, fragile, radical liberal brainwashed ideology. There’s no diversity of thought. They claim to promote diversity, but not diversity of opinion. The students are uninformed about real world consequences and are spoon-fed sound bites from social justice warrior professors and social media. The cannot handle being faced with actual facts. The students and faculty cannot defend their positions with facts. They can only defend their positions with feelings and cannot engage in any actual calm discussion with anyone who disagrees with them. Institutions like this are why America is failing. They are so afraid to offend anyone, they’re so focused on being victims, they’re so focused on standing up for everyone and anything except for America and for American citizens. Good luck contributing to society after you graduate with your worthless degree, mountain of debt, while you work at a job you could’ve gotten right out of high school.

The commitment and kindness of the professors are one of the things that I like most about Swat. They are completely there to help you (one of my professors had 8 office hours a week!), are very understanding, and are willing to build meaningful relationships with students outside the classroom (some have for example taken us to museums in New York, and I have gotten coffee with some profs just to catch up). Swat offers a lot of cool opportunities (ex: talks about many subjects, funding for unpaid internships, and travel). It is really nice to be near Philly and Media to go out, try restaurants and museums, etc.

The McCabe Scholars program is on par with most of the Ivy+ schools. This is a heavenly school if you're down-to-earth, genuinely curious, and not performative in terms of prestige/status-seeking. If you want to uplift your social class, probably don't go here---too many lower/low income students. If you want to chill while immersing yourself in the life of the mind while having career opportunities in Philly, then no better school exists.
If you're above-average attractive as a male, you should go here. People genuinely tend to skew quirky and introverted so you will stand out to the mostly-female population.

Swarthmore is my favorite place in the world. I feel well supported by my peers and by the academic faulty. There is a ton of opportunities for customization when it comes to one's own academic experience here. I am pursuing a major + double minor, and I have been able to take a ton of fun courses outside of those departments as well. The extracurricular scene is incredibly involved and welcoming. Additionally, its location is really ideal, as it is super easy to access Philly, Media, and Springfield, and even New York (I am able to make a trip there every semester for incredibly cheap). It is a cash free campus, so you do not have to pay for anything, which is incredible. I have been able to work, travel, and learn in such an incredible place.
The campus is beautiful and features a lot of nature. The town of Swarthmore is home-like and humble.

I’m happy I go to Swarthmore and not any other liberal arts college. No other campus is 20 min away from a big city that has public transportation (train) right in front of campus. Swarthmore’s workload is heavy, but those who apply, get in, and commit value education and learning. We all thrive by working hard while also being supportive of one another. The party scene at Swarthmore is a little horrendous (besides that first party thrown, last year was “Bratmore”). I know people go to UPenn a lot as well as clubs around Philly. If you want to party, you’ll make it work.
Also, Swarthmore tends to be a feeder school for PhDs. Law and medical school acceptance rates are high and our college indirectly sets you up for success by challenging you during your undergraduate years.
Departments vary—social sciences and humanities are really good. Natural sciences like bio/chem are a hit or miss. You just need to put in the work and not expect professors to spoon feed you.
Swarthmore doesn't care about their students. They only care about their liberal professors. They do not prepare you for the real world.

Swarthmore College is too self aware that it is part of several instutionalized social power dynamics- especially since all the students want to dismantle them, and the school prevents them from doing so at any cost.
Swarthmore College offers a secluded village vibe of a college outside of Philadelphia and with its ample endowment, it is able to accommodate a lot of students’ needs

Swarthmore can be perfectly described as "mid." My professors that I built a relationship with rock but campus life sucks and is so limiting. There is no sense of community. I am lucky I got to go abroad and I am lucky to have had the externship/internship experiences I've had but I don't think any of it is unique to Swarthmore.

Absolutely love it here - there's a club/affinity group for everything, although most are small. There are 1-2 parties every Saturday, but whether you go or not doesn't really affect your social life Sunday-Friday. Everyone is doing work most of the time, also plenty of D&D campaigns within friend groups. The Counseling and Psychological Services' (CAPS) team is phenomenal - there's a lot of support here from administration, professors, therapists, peers, etc - you've just got a ask for it.
A great place for intellectual students who care deeply about making the world a better place. Swarthmore made me a more thoughtful person and human being.

I have really enjoyed my time at Swarthmore so far! I'm from Hawaii, so the weather was a little bit of a shock, but the community is like no other. The students are intelligent and driven, the professors really care and remember your name for years to come, and the small-school feel really makes it feel like home. I will say that the campus culture can almost be a little "too academic" from time to time, and that you will find students in the library even on Saturday nights. If you are hard-working, quirky, and a little nerdy, Swarthmore is definitely the place for you!

If you immerse yourself for 4 years among the most talented, brightest students in America, what do you think happens? You become one of them. Swarthmore, the professors, AND MY CLASSMATES, set such an extraordinarily high standard that it stayed with me in life for everything I ever did afterwords. Can't beat that! Most impactful experience of my life and paid off in innumerable ways.

I believe that Swat is one of the best liberal art colleges (alongside pomona) that has access to a major city while also being somewhat exclusive. People here are very academically driven, but also take time in the weekend to relax (which can be in various forms).

My overall experience at Thomas Aquinas College has been amazing. The classes are interested, led by tutors who love, or at the very least appreciate the importance of, the subjects they teach. Classes are discussion based and are mostly student led, with the tutor only intervening if the conversation gets too off topic.

Thomas Aquinas College is the perfect undergraduate institution for those seeking education for the sake of Truth and human flourishing. Students read the Great Books of Western thought and discuss the questions they evoke with thoughtful peers and professors. It should be noted that the curriculum is heavy in mathematics and natural science, so a good proficiency in and enthusiasm for these subjects is important. Attending TAC was one of the most impactful decisions I have made in my life, and the virtues I began to develop there will accompany me for the rest of my life.

After one semester at Thomas Aquinas College (TAC), I am quite amazed at how little I knew about this institution. TAC surpasses my expectations for a small Catholic college in so many ways.
The studies here are rigorous but doable. I am actually excited for my classes here. The curriculum consists of the Great Books and classic literature which established Western civilization. Here you learn why America believes what it does and how we came to embrace such views. Should students struggle in a subject and desire additional assistance, TAC offers a helpful student-tutoring program, library, and computer room while the professors are always happy to lend a hand.
The campus life and culture gave me my biggest surprises: there is truly something for everyone her, from intramurals to dances, folk singing, and concerts and theatre. No matter your personality or interests, the students and staff at TAC welcome you immediately.

So far I have had an excellent experience at Thomas Aquinas College. The school greatly cares for its students by providing an environment which facilitates the academic, spiritual, and social health of the student. The school also provides financial aid opportunities such as work study programs that help students be able to afford their education regardless of their financial status. The academics are rigorous and thought-provoking. There are many resources for one's spiritual well-being such as on campus chaplains, a beautiful chapel, and many masses every day. The program could change by being less concerned with the discussion method. The discussion method innately favors certain personalities more than others, putting certain students, such as myself, at a great disadvantage. The school evidently favors those who are vigorous participants in class and looks down on the more reserved students, who due to their personality, struggle to participate as much as their fellow classmates.

I loved the community between all of the students which is greatly influenced by the fact that everyone lives on campus. I love how the classes are all discussion method and such small groups. The campus and surrounding area are both so incredibly beautiful and all of the students that I have come in contact with have been so friendly, kind, and welcoming. I am going to be a freshman there this upcoming fall and I am so excited to join a college that seems in so many respects so excellent. I was also very favorably impressed with their food. I have heard many derogatory things about college food in general, but theirs was absolutely delicious at every single meal and there were lots of options even for people with very strict allergies. Another thing I loved was the dance which I went to while I was visiting. It was a ton of fun and everyone was so into it and having such a good time. I think that Thomas Aquinas College will be a perfect fit for me!

Overall, my first year experience was amazing. As someone who was raised catholic and felt out of place< Thomas Aquinas College provided me with a community that not only guided me faith wise, but has also began to push me out of ignorance. The Great Book are a perfect example as they in partner with the Socratic method allow the student to express thoughts in such a way that would lead them to THE TRUTH. The academic scene in my opinion was very fruitful and developing. The campus life in small but very welcoming and supportive. The food was good. I personally do not find any changes that need to be made.

Thomas Aquinas College offers an integrated, 4-year, liberal arts curriculum that aims to make students competent in mathematics, natural science, philosophy, and theology. Intense study of Aristotle, in particular, paves the way for a study of Thomas Aquinas’ writings in Junior and Senior year. Small classroom sizes and Socratic discussions nourish the academic life, encouraging cross-discipline discussion, and inculcating a habit of critical thought.
Fantastic school. I am so grateful for my time here. The professors truly care about the students' learning, and the community is incredibly supportive.
A wonderful college where one who seeks the Truth will certainly find it. A place where you get out of your comfort zone and learn to be humble in all aspects of life.

Although this is a Catholic college, the diversity is vast. Students of many different religions join the college every year, and are treated as equals to every one else. I have also seen international students, encompassing every race. If you are looking for a small school, but one with amazing diversity and a wonderful community, TAC may be the place for you.
Very intellectually oriented with a very welcoming community. If you are Catholic, and want to learn, visit. It is an abnormal school, but a good one.

Excellent liberal arts education. 10/10 would recommend.
Every class is an in-depth and thought provoking discussion about our reading. I found myself captivated by questions and ideas I had never before considered. Each part of the curriculum is heavily integrated into all the other parts, and every day you get to see more of the big picture.
In my freshman year I made several really good friendships that will last a lifetime, and got to know everyone to some extent. Everyone is really nice, outgoing and fun.
Two campuses are available: one in Santa Paula, CA and another (newer) one in Northfield, MA. I haven't been to CA but the one in MA is extremely beautiful. It's right next to the Connecticut River, and there's a delicious Northfield Creamie down the road, with award winning homemade ice cream.

Thomas Aquinas College is a college that is dedicated to teaching its students to seek what is true, beautiful, and good. It uses the original sources instead of textbooks and the discussions are student-led. The tutors are genuinely supportive and help guide the students to discover truth and the student community is excellent. The students are really supportive and are willing to discuss philosophy and theology.

Admittedly, when the college transitioned to online learning, I was surprised that they were able to pull this off. The students managed to transition to online learning well! However, the discussions do not feel the same. The classes are discussion-based, which is at is best when the discussions are in person.

Thomas Aquinas College does everything it can to equip its students with the skills to dialogue with great works and each other. Rather than turn to the writings of those who may not stand the tests of time, we read the writings of the great minds who are known for being part of the foundation of western society. The aim of the professors is not to teach us what to think, but to lead us into better understanding of the great books so that we can think for ourselves and come to our own opinions through careful thought and discussion. Many of my classes ended in animated disagreement, but no ideas were dismissed if a good argument could be made in its defense. TAC is known for its strict on-campus rules, but I believe them to serve the purpose of the school, to limit distractions and ensure a healthy lifestyle.

I fell in love with the small beautiful campus. Overall the campus was one of the prettiest campuses I had visited. Transy’s campus is easily accessible with everything within a short 6-8 minute walk. The dorms have been upgraded and are super nice and spacious. The dorms are very secure so I never have to worry about someone being in there that shouldn’t be.
My professors are all so helpful. They make everyone feel welcome and important and are always willing to help you succeed in any way they can. The classrooms are very nice and the class size is small which makes it a great learning environment. My advisors are extremely helpful with keeping you on track with your program and talking with you about graduate schools for your future.
I currently play softball for Transy and that is one of the main reasons I chose Transy along with its rich tradition as an upper lever education institution. All of Transy’s sports teams are super competitive and give back to the community.

Transylvania University is a very small campus. Class sizes range from about 18-30 people so you are actually able to engage with your peers and professors. If I could change one thing it would be the dining hall food selection, although it is not awful it definitely could be better.

My first year at Transylvania University was an amazing experience. It was so much fun being able to hang out with a diverse group of people from different backgrounds. I felt I had grown as a person in the beginning; it was rough trying to get into a college routine, but with the help from the school and my friends, I felt I was able to get over that problem and do better in the next semesters. The class was good teachers were understanding, and I felt I learned from them. During my time, I joined a sorority because I enjoyed what they did and wanted to be more involved in campus life. The only downside was my dorm; it wasn't bad, but I dealt with bugs and mold in my bathroom. The washer and dryers kept breaking down, so it was a bit rough. The overall experience was good.

My overall experience at Transylvania University has shaped me into who I am today. Transylvania equipped me with the skills needed to pursue a Doctorate degree, which I will be starting in a few short months. The rigorous coursework at this institution require hard work and dedication, but the faculty and staff are always there to support students with anything they need. The housing options at this university are newly renovated for first and second year students, however, there are two dorms for upper class students that are not ideal to live in. There are many extracurricular activities to get engaged with on campus. Whether a student would like to be involved in greek life, honor societies, or student-involved organizations, there are many ways to gain community and networking opportunities. Transylvania University is a distinguished institution that prepares its students for a life of curiosity and determination.

Transylvania University has been an incredible experience for me as an international student. I was amazed by the beautiful, welcoming campus and the strong sense of community. Small class sizes allow for meaningful interaction with professors, and office hours are always helpful. The support from school counselors makes navigating college life easier. The library is well-stocked with study materials, and diversity is genuinely celebrated on campus. With numerous student clubs, there’s always a place to belong. Transylvania University is more than just a school, it’s a supportive environment where students grow academically, socially, and personally. I truly feel at home here.

It was the most amazing 4 years of my life. My education at Transylvania has more than prepared me for life outside of college. I learned how to be very detail oriented and how to back up my claims. My friends from college are my friends for life.

Transylvania University is a beautiful campus with very welcoming students and staff/Professors, there is help in every field, tutoring even last minute on the spot tutoring. The sororities and frats are very active and take part in community service and activities they hold for others to join on campus. There is a club for everything you could even imagine!
Transy is a small school which is great if you want an academic focused experience. The community is great and you'll make friends quickly.

I would love the professors to actually care about there students well being outside of class. Some of the professors are very inclusive and kind, but there is a slim picking of them that dont care about you outside of the classroom, and no matter how hard you're trying they are not going to help if you don't understand it.

Overall, I’d say Transylvania is a great school! While the academics are very difficult, there are many resources to help students succeed (tutoring, embedded learning forums, office hours, etc.). Professors are very accommodating for the most part, but some are very strict with their attendance and grading. The values-based Greek life makes sororities very diverse and accepting- it’s a community where people help each other in every aspect. Aside from Greek life, there’s various student organizations that one could join. Students can even start their own clubs and design their own majors! Campus life in general is very lively, with traditions like Pumpkin Mania, where students carve their own Jack-O’-Lanterns to be displayed on around campus.

Do not go here if you are POC, diversity is lacking very much and you can tell. Also campus is constantly dead and the parties are so lame. Overall people here are very cliquey once they meet their people so don't expect to make any new friends as the year continues. ALSO food is very bad most of the time , housing is bad especially poole. Not worth you're money, become a commuter asap if you plan on staying.

Do not go here unless you’re 100% committed to greek life. The fraternities and sororities absolutely run the campus, and there is essentially no social life if you aren’t apart of one. Don’t expect to make friends or get involved unless you’re in a frat/sorority. Non-greek students are very ostracized— which is unfortunate, considering there are only 4 options for sororities/fraternities. There is also lots of social drama due to this, and Transy as a whole is very prone to cliques. Being such a small campus, gossip travels fast so don’t expect to have much privacy either. HOWEVER, for the good stuff— the academics are undoubtedly top notch. We have incredible professors who care about their students immensely (not just as pupils, but as people). There are many opportunities to get ahead as well, especially when it comes to making connections and forming mentorships. If you’re a serious student, Transy might be a good fit. Just do yourself a favor and join Greek life.

The college is in a nice location that makes it easy to have a good amount of time to spend between classes and doing things outside the school. The professors are wonderful people, and the other students I've met have all been very kind.

Transylvania University allowed me to be all the things that I wanted to be--I was able to cheer and be involved in social activities, student government, campus employment, student ambassador program, while still providing a rigorous yet varied curriculum. I even had the opportunity to study abroad, which eventually led me to use my Spanish and Anthropology degree to choose a career as a Spanish teacher. I am thankful for the opportunities that I had at Transylvania University and all the memories that I was able to make, as TU provided me with the full college experience.
Good financial aid, good staff. Great academic program, Liberal Arts University. Enjoyed my experience so far.

ai_summary: At Trinity College - Connecticut, the academic experience generally stands out due to small class sizes and engaged professors , which fosters a nurturing environment for student learning. Many students appreciate the personal relationships they can build with faculty, although opinions vary; some express frustration about the quality and commitment of certain professors. The social scene is heavily influenced by Greek life and can feel cliquey, particularly for those from middle or lower socioeconomic backgrounds. This exclusivity can lead to a sense of division among students. While the campus is described as beautiful and situated in a city with internship opportunities, some students find the surrounding area sketchy and lacking in safety, which detracts from the overall experience. The dining options receive mixed reviews, often criticized for quality. Students cite a vibrant party culture , but also mention it can overshadow academic pursuits. Overall, experiences at Trinity can be quite polarizing, with some finding it a perfect fit and others feeling disconnected from the available social dynamics.

reviews.text: I wake up regretting attending Trinity everyday. I poured my heart and soul into this place and left with no meaningful friends and an extremely unfulfilling college experience. The student body is extremely toxic, immature, and cliquey. The social and dating scene here is exclusive and discriminatory. It is very hard to make friends here. The dining food is awful and so are the dorms. The academics are easy here but professors give special treatment to the lazy rich kids here. My lazy classmates that didn’t do the readings, skipped class, and where in Greek life got jobs at fancy investment companies. Massive corruption and fraud here by professors when grading students. If you pay full tuition, are part of KKG, Ivy Society, Stella Society, Psi U, Alpha Delta, or are a varsity athlete you will get special treatment by instructors and admin.

While I had great experiences with my professors, I didn't always feel supported as an athlete. The athletic facilities were very mediocre, and as a high-level athlete, I often felt like my safety was compromised or that my performance was being compromised to keep myself safe on faulty equipment. Much of the athletic staff prioritized male-dominated sports like football and seemed to disregard complaints from other athletes based on a "we don't have enough money to change that" excuse despite funding football expenses.

Worst decision I ever made was coming to Trinity College.
I wake up with regret every day.
Genuinely awful student body. HORRIBLE Deans. So disorganized. So unprofessional. Never know what’s going on yet try to push the narrative they’re “elite.” It’s a bunch of people who care more about feeling powerful and respected than doing good work.
Students are so arrogant and elitist here despite being, often times, not very smart. I entered this college a very good, respectable person. I was given huge scholarship here. I was so proud of the person I was.
Putting myself in the environment of Trinity College destroyed me. The college stripped me of all sense of self. You’re not accepted at this school if you’re not perpetuating a “cool kid” character. Like high school. You can’t leave the campus unless you want to fear for your life. You adapt. By turning into someone you're not. Please don't make the mistake of coming here. Don’t send a loved one here. I implore you, with a full heart.

ai_summary: Trinity University offers a unique blend of rigorous academics and a close-knit community , thanks to its small class sizes and accessible faculty. Many students value the personalized attention they receive from professors, who are described as caring and invested in student success. The picturesque campus located near downtown San Antonio adds to the appeal, providing students with easy access to city life. However, some reviews highlight challenges such as a cliquish atmosphere among the student body, concerns about campus food quality, and a sense of entitlement among peers. While there are plenty of opportunities for involvement in research and extracurricular activities, opinions differ on the overall social scene and school spirit, with some noting that athletics and Greek life dominate campus life. Overall, students appreciate the educational value and support services, but recommendations often come with caveats regarding the environment and living conditions.

reviews.text: Trinity has given me an overall wonderful college experience! The education and faculty are great, and you can tell they really want to see you succeed. There are so many resources in place to help you post graduation, which I think is super important. The campus is beautiful and very walkable, and the buildings are very well-kept and maintained. However, the two things I would like to see improvement on are parking and dining. As a predominantly residential campus, more parking should be available. Along with this, for how expensive the meal plans are, the food should be consistent and good. Besides these two things, I have loved everything else about my time at Trinity.

Trinity is a great pre-professional school! It definitely is not a party school, but most students have the "work hard, play hard" mindset, so there are parties and social things to do. Greek Life is great, as it is a lot more casual since there aren't any national orgs. As for academics, it's pretty hard, but there are many resources to help support you. The STEM classes are difficult, but it is worth it if you are looking to do grad school.

I'm actually so glad that I chose to come to this school. I believe that this school truly helps you find your path in life, no matter where you are on your college journey. The professors are all so interesting, and the labs that I've taken so far have all been amazing. Also, the campus is so pretty! We're constantly surrounded by nature, despite being in San Antonio. There are so many peaceful study/relaxation spots, beautiful plants, and there's even a lot of animals that live on campus despite being so close to the heart of San Antonio! (Look up the trinicats if you haven't already!) I've been so supported by this school, and they always do their best to promote inclusivity as well. Even if the academics are sometimes extremely challenging, (Which makes sense, its a selective school. It's a good thing to be challenged in your academics.) I still am very glad I decided to go here!

I am currently a freshman here at TU. While the academics are good (yet rigorous), DO NOT COME HERE IF YOU WANT A SOCIAL LIFE. I am a very social person and enjoy going out somewhat often (1-2 times a week). It is the most boring place ever and that is a widely agreed upon thing among the student body. Also, I am from out of state and most people here are from in state. I would say that 65% of people go home (or visit their friends at A&M or UTAustin) on the weekends, leaving virtually nothing to do here. Also, San Antonio is a total dump.

Trinity is a school of ambitious students who are involved and well rounded. I think Trinity is in the sweet spot where it is a prestigious school but does not feel cutthroat- everyone wants to see their peers succeed.
I like the community here and how close-knit it is. I think the residential life department can improve by listening to students more.

Overall experience is great! There’s amazing opportunities offered to its students. Only flaw is that Trinity preemptively changed office names given political climate. However when it comes to academics is designed to challenge its students to ensure they are fully prepared for the world
the only thing about the university that people might complain fairly about is that the large dining hall can fall in quality as the semester goes on.
Great school! Perfect if someone is looking to challenge themselves academically, participate in athletics, or looking for a small community.

Trinity has been an amazing experience so far. It has provided me with multiple opportunities to meet other people, engage with academic activities and have fun while also growing. People are very kind and authentic and it has allowed me to find what I like.
Great school that is really built on community. Everything here on campus is very open and so are all the students and faculty.

I am a current student, and I love it! Trinity is such an incredible place, the students are passionate, kind, inclusive, and smart. The professors, for the most part, want to help the students learn, in particular the Environmental Studies department and Music department. The housing is great, and the three year on campus requirement is great for building community. The food is not super great, but it is certainly not a terrible factor. Overall, I love Trinity and highly recommend!

Trinity community is the best! Most of the professors are awesome and it is a great place to study. The classes tend more personalized and not as big as other schools. I would say tho the food can improve!
Unlike any other place I could explain made me feel at home especially when I got involved on campus.
I love their entire academic program as a liberal arts university. Everyone here makes me want to be successful, and the university provides me every opportunity to be that

ai_summary: Union College - New York offers a strong academic environment characterized by passionate professors and small class sizes that facilitate personal connections and engagement. Many students appreciate the opportunities for research and the strong alumni network, which can be beneficial for job placement after graduation. However, the social scene is heavily dominated by Greek life, with some reviews expressing that it limits options for students looking for diverse social activities . The campus is often described as welcoming, although there are concerns about a lack of racial and cultural diversity. Dining options and housing conditions seem to be recurring issues, with food quality receiving significant criticism and housing shortages leading to frustrations among students. On the whole, Union provides a unique balance of rigorous academics and community involvement while promoting a largely homogenous social culture.

reviews.text: Very fond reviews from a closely related alumni. Although Schenectady may not be the nicest area, Union's campus is very intimate and academically encouraging.

I'm almost done with my second term at Union, and I'm loving it! There's a TON of programming early on, so that + going to frats means that I was easily able to meet people and make some great friends. I'm queer, and haven't run into any issues on campus due to that, including at the frats (which was a big concern of mine.) The academics are amazing, and the professors are probably the best part of Union. All of the one's I've had so far care so deeply about their classes, and the smaller size means that you're easily able to make connections with them. I love my dorm too (shoutout fox!!), and I think the only two things I don't love are the surrounding area and some of the food. I highly recommend it!

I’m a freshman, and honestly, do not come here. The food is awful (raw chicken, greasy, receptive) and they can’t even offer chicken tenders daily. All of the staff look exhausted and miserable, which makes the experience worse. Dorms are VERY small—basically a closet with a common room. If you’re not in a corner room, you’re screwed. Everything feels run down. I asked for a shower curtain replacement because we were given HALF of one, and it was never fixed.
There is nothing to do on campus. Most events overlap with common hour. Sports and Greek life dominate everything, yet people don’t even show up to games. There’s no money put into student life, it’s way too expensive, and the location is bad. People stick to their teams and drama is constant. Lots of drugs. Trimesters move insanely fast and the long breaks leave you with weeks of nothing.
professors are good (and underpaid), small classes are great, and some people are chill.
Just consider everything carefully.

Lots of driven people. Many might be considered too driven. A lot of the people at the United States Air Force Academy feel selfish or as if they're only in it for themselves and do not care about the success of others.

My overall experience with the United States Air Force Academy has been both challenging and rewarding. The environment pushes you to grow in every way—academically, physically, and mentally. The discipline and structure teach you time management, teamwork, and leadership under pressure. The sense of camaraderie among cadets is strong, and everyone is focused on a common goal of service and excellence. While the schedule is demanding and expectations are high, the opportunities to learn, fly, and develop as a leader make it an experience like no other. It’s tough, but incredibly fulfilling.
The United States Air Force Academy is a senior military academy that prepares officer candidates for evntually commissioning as an officer into the armed forces.

Great academics with some passionate professors. The food is not spectacular but the surrounding area is much more built up and would be fun for students/emerging young adults. Balancing being an excellent cadet and having a personal life can be tough.

Very cliqey I was always called out and made fun of. Every cadet in my squadron dated each other which is a big no no. Had to basically have sex to have a good report card. Never made friends and never had a boyfriend. Was always getting sexually abused by my classmates as they would go off with other cadets and leave me stranded was the only cadet from a small town. Came from a poor family.
The Air Force Academy is a wonderful school that allows students to be good scholars and good soldiers that will serve their country at the same time. A wonderful experience!

Recently completing my freshmen year at the United States Air Force Academy, I feel that this institution has given me some amazing opportunities while pursuing a Bachelor of Science degree of my choice. Room and board, campus dining, and other perks are all covered by this institution. Some of the experiences I have had include the opportunity to fly solo in a glider, fire assault rifles, go through basic cadet training, and make lifetime bonds with people I love. I have experienced the military lifestyle and training environment. I personally love this institution and how it has set me up for success in the future. I would recommend the Academy to anyone who is willing to challenge themselves and take their studies to the next level. You are expected to maintain high standards and customs, maintain good grades, stay physically fit, and maintain high military performance. Overall, I love the diversity, climate, and culture at the Academy and how I have challenged myself my first year.

As freshman, it is known that military training will be difficult. During our culminating event toward the end of the year, Covid hit and we all got sent home. When we returned, we were isolated and we all had mental health issues. From then on, I don’t feel like anyone really recovered. In addition, having commanders come into living quarters was extremely awkward considering they got to go home at the end of every day. However, with all of the toxic traits of the school, the people that went through it with you made it worth it. Everyone, for the most part, had each others back and we will forever hold those memories.
Rigorous, challenging, and transformative. The United States Air Force Academy fosters leadership, discipline, and academic excellence while instilling a strong sense of service and duty.

This school is hard. A lot of students can get cynical about how much we are asked to do as cadets, balancing school, athletics, military, and personal life. But you get paid every month to be a student so if you can graduate you can be set for life. If you find good friends and get the right support from either friends leadership or teachers you can get through hard points at you time here. As long as you keep pushing you get to be one lucky undergraduate in America with absolutely no student debt a for sure job and 60 days paid leave after graduation day! Oh also skydiving over the Rocky Mountains is a once in a lifetime experience that practically everyone can do here so that’s awesome!

If you have ever seen "Groundhog Day" it is exactly like that here. Every day is the same so that being said, the days go by slow, and the weeks go by fast. A lot of people will say how awful this place is, however being paid to go to school and having a job right out of here, I would say, is worth it. It is a great place to be from, not a super amazing place to be at.

USAFA is a very trying school that does everything possible to bring out the best in each of its students. It's not easy, but the school does a great job in growing each student each and every day. While it is very difficult, especially freshman year, I would recommend that any person who wants to be the best version of themselves to go here.
Loved it. Lots of little things to complain about, but it changed my life for the better and gave me access to opportunities I could never have imagined.

USAFA is a very challenging school with great academics, rigorous military training, and a diverse system of clubs, teams, and athletic opportunities. While military life and academics can make life suck sometimes. The opportunities provided outweigh the negatives.
The academy is challenging but worth it. It will force you to change the way you think and function, for the better.

ai_summary: The United States Military Academy at West Point is described as a unique institution that prioritizes leadership development and character-building over typical college experiences. Academically, it offers rigorous coursework with highly accessible professors, but students face a demanding schedule filled with military duties and strict regulations. Cadets appreciate the strong sense of community and the bonds formed through shared challenges, though many express that the environment can be overwhelming and isolating at times. While the campus provides excellent opportunities for leadership training , athletics, and research, the absence of traditional college social life is noted, making it less appealing to those seeking a typical college experience. The financial aspect is a highlight, with free tuition and guaranteed employment as a second lieutenant upon graduation. However, the academy's adherence to tradition and at times lack of focus on cadet well-being are points of contention. Overall, West Point is seen as a suitable choice for those committed to a military career who are prepared for a challenging but potentially rewarding journey.

reviews.text: The United States Military Academy is unlike any college in the United States. It is a military focused 47 month experience with the most competent and capable instructors and officers committed to see your professional and personal development. You will leave with an Ivy League level education, lifelong friends forged through a shared difficult experience, and a leadership development experience that compares to no where else.

A challenging program that sets you up for success with 100% job placement. This Program is not for the feint of heart but the comradery along the way is what makes its graduates so strong when they leave.

West Point was one of the most challenging and rewarding experiences of my life. It offers top-tier leadership training, a rigorous academic environment, and a strong sense of camaraderie. The values of Duty, Honor, Country are deeply ingrained in everything you do. I gained lifelong friendships and left feeling prepared to lead in high-pressure environments. The physical and mental demands are intense, and while the structure builds resilience, I believe more emphasis on mental health and balance would benefit cadets. Overall, West Point developed my character, leadership, and discipline in ways I’ll carry with me for life.

Attending this college was one of the best decisions I’ve ever made. The academic system was rigorous yet supportive, with passionate professors and resources that truly helped me grow. Beyond the classroom, it was a place where I formed deep, lifelong friendships that made every moment memorable. I’m incredibly grateful for the experience and opportunities it provided.

No party scene here, but a great way to get a good education and be qualified for a future career. Be ready to work and to be a part of something bigger than yourself. This is the best experience for a young college student to become a leader of character after four grueling years. Not for the faint of heart.

They try to put so many things into the schedule a semester is more an exercise in time management than academic excellence. You are forced to take violent sports that often result in broken bones, torn ligaments and concussions. The leadership cares more about themselves and “the Academy” than the cadets. There is an extraordinary amount of time wasting activities that distract from academics. Academics are led by officers who avoided the real Army and deployments for a cush life where they can lord over their fiefdoms. There are destructive “traditions” that target anyone that is not a model cadet, with the unwritten standard being arbitrarily established by bullies with God complexes. It is a horrible experience for independent thinkers or anyone who is not a physical stud. Arrogance is rewarded creating a hostile environment causing nightmares and PTSD in hundred of graduates and ex-cadets every year. Do not send your sons and daughters here.

West Point is a leadership school. I’ve learned countless valuable skills that I will take with me when I transition into the civilian sphere, but first I have the unique opportunity to serve my country as a 2nd Lieutenant in the United States Army. I like how I’m constantly pushed physically, academically, and militarily. I’ve seen myself grow as a person and a leader of character. Countless CEOs, presidents, and other leaders have been apart of West Points “long grey line” and I’m honored to be apart of it. I don’t see any changes to the current situation of the school.

You will meet billionaires, the elite children of politicians, and you will get to know future generals. And you may also discover that if you are not in this category, then no matter how hard you work, no matter how much effort you give, no matter how much you bleed, no matter how many concussions you get from mandatory sports and training, you may find yourself ostracized for life from people who are convinced they are better than you. My wonderful spouse keeps me going. Otherwise this institution would have destroyed me. According to many (myself included) the stress of going to USMA brings nightmares forever.
Challenging but rewarding! Definitely recommend visiting first before you make your decision. Wouldn’t change anything
It is AMAZING! Perfectly prepared students for the rigors of military life and installs lots of discipline
The academy is unlike any civilian college but will teach you the characteristics to be the best version of yourself everyday.
I loved my experience at West Point so far. The workload is heavy and sacrifices from daily life have to be made for success but I feel like I have become a better person for it.

Much more than just a college. This institution takes in kids and graduates competent leaders. It's not for everyone, but the academics are top tier, with access to well funded research, semesters abroad, and military training and education. USMA maintains a professional standard not found at other schools like the US Naval Academy or Air Force Academy.

Be prepared to work, in all aspects, physically, mentally, and academically. Your experience here will be hard and frustrating but you will be better for it, in all ways. You will be challenged everyday here and that should hopefully prepare you for the job you will be required to do once you graduate.
This academy will develop all of its students to become some of the most effective and influential leaders of the 21st century.

ai_summary: The United States Naval Academy offers a unique and demanding experience , ideal for students aiming for a military career. Academics are rigorous and focus on leadership development, often compared to Ivy League standards, while class sizes are manageable, enabling personalized interaction with professors. Some reviews highlight the strong camaraderie among midshipmen, describing friendships as lifelong bonds forged through shared challenges. However, the social atmosphere is noted as different, lacking some traditional college experiences, which can lead to mixed feelings about the balance of work and enjoyment. The campus in Annapolis is praised for its beauty and location, providing access to both city life and military training opportunities. While many appreciate the guaranteed job placement post-graduation and the minimal financial cost, some criticisms are directed at the quality of facilities and the pressures of the lifestyle that can lead to a challenging environment. Overall, those who thrive at the Academy tend to be motivated individuals committed to personal growth and serving their country.

reviews.text: I loved my first, year. It is tough, but very rewarding, good for people who want to join the armed forces.

The Naval Academy is not for everyone, but it can be a life changing experience for a young person. It is the best possible choice for students considering a career in the military. The excellent academic opportunities there are complemented well by the school’s location in downtown Annapolis.

It's worse than I thought. People here are quite stuck up and many of them are "bots". A lot of upper-class have been brainwashed to the academy life that they act like this is only thing in their life and make Naval Academy their personality.

It is a great community with people who push each other to better every day through a competitive nature to defend the freedoms and liberties of the United States while instilling the values of leadership and ethics into every midshipman.

The Academy's structured environment and rigorous training prepare midshipmen for leadership roles in the Navy and Marine Corps. From study abroad programs to summer training cruises, the Academy provides unique experiences that most colleges don’t offer.
Plebe year sucks, but it will get better after that. Embrace the suck and grit yourself through it. Find your people and everything will be alright.

USNA provides a ton of training and leadership experience to its Midshipmen. Despite how busy every day at the academy is, I feel very accomplished at my growth and development not only as a Midshipman but also as a young adult. There are so many events and services on the Yard, and if I need help with academics or advice, there is somewhere to ask for it. Although my sleep has definitely fallen during my time here, I understand that it is because the American public is counting on the academy to train future officers to protect American safety and defend the American people in just 4 years. However, there are still many fun events on the Yard to help you relax, such as the fantastic Halloween concert and the miscellaneous squad outings, etc. Something that could use some change would be the aptitude system at the academy. Although it isn’t worth too much as a part of one’s QPR, it is still frustrating to see your ranking be affected by the subjective reviews of upperclassmen.
It's a military academy. It's not like normal college. Their primary purpose is to develop you morally mentally and physically to commission you as an officer in the Navy or Marine Corps.

The Naval Academy is an incredibly rigorous college. Midshipmen develop physically, academically, morally, and mentally. The professors are second-to-none and the athletic opportunities are impressive. Each midshipmen is set up with several mentors to help with their development. The comradery is one of the most special aspects. The academy fosters tight-knit relationships between each midshipmen.
USNA is a great school for those who want a free education and an opportunity to become a United States Navy or Marine Corps officer.
The teachers and faculty treat you terribly, the students are rude, and your personal life is taken from you. If you can get past that, then you’ll love it here.

My experience at the Naval Academy has been very unique and memorable one. This school is founded upon the creation of military leaders, academic scholars, and future leaders of America. I love the mission of the Academy and the extremely demanding daily grind that we must persist through. However, I feel that the only change I would like to see is the culture of the student body which can only be changed by the leadership. We are told everyday to love and respect each other but it seems like those words only go in one ear and out the other the second we are left alone. Only company leadership can restructure some of the toxic culture that can build up. Leave no one left behind and helping out one another is the main issue.

Best school ever, not only do you meet people and become friends with those that you would never have thought of interacting with, but you also learn how to lead. Despite all the mandatory events and physical evolutions, the school is a fantastic place to adapt and overcome that hardships to strive to our best abilities.

It is a great place, but only because this is really where I want to be. If I had been pressured into attending the Naval Academy my experience here would be much less enjoyable. Even though there are not so fun times where I wish had attended a more traditional school, I am happy with my decision because I know that my career will have a purpose. I will be serving in the Navy or the Marine Corps for the United States and its people. I understand that my time here is not supposed to be fun all the time, but that is part of the process of developing myself into a leader.

This institution provides its students with a number of opportunities to practice leadership and also participate in activities not provided by any other institution. There are plenty of resources that the students are able to use to better academic success, broaden their interactions in terms of learning about the world around us, and support systems that help to deal with the daily stress of balancing the academic rigor with the military lifestyle. The Academy takes feedback from its students very seriously and continues to develop programs and revise policy in order to continue to graduate leaders.

ai_summary: At the University of Minnesota Morris, many students appreciate the close-knit community fostered by its small campus and class sizes, allowing for personalized interactions with professors. The academic programs are generally viewed positively, especially for those pursuing fields like pre-med, with an emphasis on experiential learning . Student life is enriched by a variety of clubs and activities, although some find the social scene limited due to the town's size, which often requires students to create their own entertainment. While the general atmosphere is welcoming and inclusive , some students have raised concerns about dorm conditions and dining options, advocating for improvements in these areas. Overall, UMM is seen as a strong choice for those seeking an intimate college experience with supportive faculty, though students should be prepared for a quieter social environment .

reviews.text: If you are looking for a school that is safe, academically focused, and overall just amazing, I highly recommend UMN Morris!
Everything is awesome! The people are amazing and nice. I made a large group of friends right off the bat.
Everyone has been so incredibly kind and welcoming, it feels like a dream. I almost can't believe this is the real world, but everwhere I look here I am. I'm so grateful to be here.
Coming from a small town and being away from home, the University of Minnesota Morris is small, a has a hometown and family feeling to it. I adjusted well.
