I enjoyed the small classes, the housing and food services, the extracurriculars and student organizations, and the personal interaction and guidance with advisors. The only thing I would like to see changed is a small increase in the variety of classes offered.

My overall experience at University of Minnesota Morris these past few months have been full of highs and lows. While here at the University of Minnesota Morris I have found and surrounded myself with God loving Christians who pursue God as well as support me and push me in faith through the on campus Christian club Intervarsity. I have also made good relationships with my professors and many of them have been extremely helpful when it comes to needing extra help understanding concepts. I am also a part of the softball team, which has also surrounded me with supportive teammates and coaches. However, the dining hall is no the best. I have found mold on several things before several times. They also have scarce protein options at times, and being an athlete and not having adequate protein and food to fuel my body the right way is not good. Lastly, they are not open until 10 am on the weekends, which means that I have to buy extra food on top of my meal plan if I want to eat.

This college is awesome! it is small the professors care about each student the campus runs the town! Plenty of outdoor life such as Fishing and Hunting. Cougar sports is incredible the whole town and each staff member care more about cougar sports than themselves. Nobody hates each other everyone is friends with everyone! very diverse campus friendly to non-binaries as well as any cultural traits a person wants to keep. I love my M-I-N-N-E-S-O-T-A Morris!

My freshman year at the University of Minnesota Morris has been an exhilarating blend of challenges, growth, and discovery.
Academically, the University of Minnesota Morris has exceeded my expectations. The smaller class sizes have allowed for more personalized interactions with professors, fostering a supportive and engaging learning environment. The liberal arts curriculum has encouraged me to explore a wide range of disciplines, helping me discover new interests and develop a well-rounded perspective.
Joining the Pre-Health Club has been particularly rewarding, as it aligns with my aspiration to pursue a career in gynecology and obstetrics. Living on campus has also been a significant part of my experience. The residence halls at Morris foster a strong sense of community, making it easy to form lasting friendships.
In conclusion, As I continue my college journey, I am grateful for the opportunities and experiences that Morris has provided.

It's a very quiet place to be. Going from Anoka Highschool to University of Minnesota Morris was quite the easy shift. They have a similar population size which is really nice. You get to know the professors well, and also your fellow students! I don't think I could have managed in a big college! I may be an introvert, but I need to get to know the people around me too! I also need a way to easily find friends. The clubs were a really good way to do that.

Overall, looking back on my first year it has been a good year of learning at Morris. I have met many new friends I came in nervous about meeting new people and making new friends but it is not hard at Morris. I have enjoyed getting to learn more and will be happy next year when I get into more of my classes for my major.
I love the college community! Everyone is very welcoming and the atmosphere is always fun! There are a lot of events that happen on campus which makes meeting new people super easy.

The professors I have had so far have been good! They have been compassionate, caring, understanding, and engaging. I have not had a good experience living on campus though. I broke my foot last year and was moved three times to different dorms. Some of them had mold and they were all very hot. I always felt kinda dizzy in the different dorms. I ended up moving home after the semester was done. Some of the staff on campus are very nice, but others are rude and seem to not care. It would be nice to have more student activities as the campus is usually pretty dead and there is not much to do for entertainment. The dining hall staff are nice, but the food is not very good. It also does not stay open past 7, which could make food unavailable for some students.

Morris was a very good school. I was able to get the major I wanted while also participating in sports. Professors were welcoming and willing to help you. Coaches were able to show you your potential.
I really enjoy the staff on campus they are super friendly and easy to work with. Morris strives to help students succeed.

What I enjoy about being a student at University of Minnesota Morris is the ethnic diversity. Additionally, UMM worked out for me financially. The cost of attendance is very affordable especially for Native American students as they offer a Native American Tuition Waiver. With the help of scholarships and grants I am able to not worry much about financial needs. More so, UMM offers a unique program where one can create their own major based on their interests and abilities. Although, some change I would like to see is more food options, as some may not be able to afford a food plan due to personal responsibilities such as car and or phone bills. Perhaps a more advertised food pantry, or themed potluck events.

ai_summary: At the University of Puget Sound, students appreciate the intimate, small class sizes that foster supportive relationships with professors, many of whom are noted for their dedication and engagement in students' academic journeys. The campus itself receives high marks for its beauty and welcoming atmosphere , with many students feeling a strong sense of community . However, some reviews highlight a lack of diversity in the student body, which largely consists of upper-middle-class white students, and point out issues related to food options, campus resources, and a social scene that can feel cliquey. Academically, while professors and educational opportunities are often praised, some students express frustration with the administration regarding course availability and support for issues like mental health. Overall, UPS offers a tight-knit educational environment with both strengths and areas for improvement.

reviews.text: I love the people at this school. Everyone is so cheerful and kind, and because of the size of the school you'll always see people when you're walking around on campus. It's also one of the most beautiful campuses ever. The professors and classes I've taken so far are great.

I was very nervous to be away from home, in another country, not knowing know what to expect from a college student life and education. The University of Puget Sound did a wonderful job in recognizing that that was the reality of many, if not all, students who were headed in this path and created a unique experience were students could feel welcomed into the university. But the support didn't stop there. Throughout my freshman year of high school I felt supported by faculty and staff with anything that came up. They even contacted me before I arrived to get to know me and tell me what to except of this new journey on campus. The University of Puget Sound supported me physically with great housing and food, emotionally with programs designed to expand my friend group and contact among my peers, as well as financially with great financial aid that allowed me to attended college with a full tuition scholarship. I'm lucky to receive my degree from the University of Puget Sound.

My college experience at the University of Puget Sound honestly exceeded my expectations of all that I hoped to get out of these 4 years. The students here are very involved and passionate. There is such an enthusiasm for learning which is inspiring. The environment is not competitive and instead is uplifting and encouraging. Students and faculty here know your name and you are seen. I hope that the university continues to think about ways to cultivate student input in decision making and the future of the university’s endeavors.

I absolutely love University of Puget Sound. I wanted a college that would genuinely care about me and my passions and UPS has that. I love all of the support that is offered on campus and how they make sure no one falls through the cracks. I feel very loved and cared for by the students, staff, and faculty. The classes and Faculty here are amazing! I am a STEM major and for one of my introductory chemistry classes when there where more students who wanted to take the class than actual seats available, my professor decided to simply just teach another class and lab. The school genuinely wants each and every student can have the best experience possible.
Puget Sound changed my life! The relationships that I built and experiences I had inside and outside of the classroom have been so pivotal to my success.
I had an abundance of opportunities at UPS to explore what I was interested in, and be a part of many different groups on campus. It was easy to find help when needed

My past three years at the University of Puget Sound have been unforgettable. When I came here as a freshman, I was terrified I wouldn't make any friends. The day after move-in, we met our orientation groups, and I've never felt alone since. This campus has given me the gift of so many precious friendships, from my best friends who are my rocks throughout the highs and lows of college, to my friends within my major who help push me to be the best student I can be, to my co-workers who love to make new friends--- even when we have nothing in common. I feel so incredibly lucky that I get to live on this beautiful campus in our amazing city. Tacoma is walkable and has so much fun things to do, on and off of campus. Even after I graduate, I know that my forever happy place will be Todd Field when it's sunny and my friends drag a picnic blanket out so we can play cards, or early morning bagel runs to Oppenhimer café. I am so grateful for how UPS has changed my life. I am forever changed!
Very good school academically and the vibes are amazing! the food is ok and i would prefer some variety for people with dietary restrictions.

My experience at Puget Sound was nothing short of amazing! I am a recent graduate from Tacoma, WA. I choose to stay close to home for college, but Puget Sound helped me go far and accomplish my goals. I gained way more than just a college degree at Puget Sound; I learned how to be a critical thinker and life-long learner. I made meaningful connections both inside and outside the classroom that allowed me to feel supported and seen on campus. I left feeling confident abut navigating the real-world. I am forever grateful for the education I received at Puget Sound!

I’ve had a great experience with Puget Sound. What really stands out about it is the close relationships with professors, the experiential learning opportunities I’ve gotten to have (studying abroad, doing summer research), and the beautiful campus! There is also always something to do—between clubs, musical performances, sports, classes, study sessions, you stay super busy as a student here.

Community is such a big thing at Puget Sound, and paves the way for meaningful connections that lead to opportunities. My experience as a student allowed me to grow beyond just academically. Thanks to the liberal arts and my time at Puget Sound I grew as a critical thinker, writer, and communicator. As a recent alum, I felt prepared and supported by the years I spent in the Puget Sound classroom and community.
The biology department is super great with a lot of awesome professors willing to support you. They are extremely easy to talk to and are doing a lot of interesting and cool research.

I had a great time at the university of puget sound. The biggest difference between UPS and other small schools is the effort our campus puts into “experiential learning” with the commitment to do an internship, study abroad, research, community engagement or class experience before graduation. I’ve had the pleasure of doing many of these experiences, all of which have contributed to me getting a job right after graduation in a really rough job market. I love the small pond big fish part of UPS where i’ve gotten tons of leadership experience and know all my classmates really well but that’s a niche experience to look for or avoid depending on what you want. I’d also say the diversity is lacking as a predominantly white institution. One other thing is there’s a lot more women than men I’ve found that are engaged in campus life which is just interesting is all.

I’ve had a great time at UPS thus far. It’s a pretty small campus which allows you to make great connections with your professors and friends. Because of this I’ve also been given tons of leadership opportunities and hands on learning outside of the classroom. I think UPS does a great job of setting students up for success in the future. One thing that they are working on and needs change is the diversity on campus as it’s a predominant white institution with only small pockets of diversity.

The school site is easy to navigate, and the school does a great job of supporting its new students as they come in, whether they are transfers or freshmen. I feel safe on campus and the food is better than expected. Housing varies wildly, and I was lucky to get a good roommate and class schedule.

ai_summary: The University of Richmond receives mixed reviews , particularly focusing on academics, student life, and campus culture. Many students praise the academic experience, highlighting dedicated professors , small class sizes, and a supportive environment that fosters critical thinking and personal growth. The alumni network and career services are often noted as strong assets, providing excellent opportunities for internships and employment post-graduation. However, the social scene has been described as overly centered around Greek life, which can create a cliquish atmosphere that may leave some students feeling isolated, particularly those from diverse backgrounds. While the campus is beautiful and offers plenty of activities, there are concerns about inclusivity and safety, with reports of incidents regarding racism and sexual assault. For some, the excitement of a preppy, active social life coexists with a sense of disconnect, especially regarding class dynamics and financial aid issues.

reviews.text: I am the parent of a recent graduate and a current freshman. We couldn't be happier with UR. Both of our children applied Early Decision and were so excited to be admitted, and both immediately felt at home there, in and outside of the classrooms. We've visited many colleges over the years and UR is absolutely one of the most beautiful. Both kids have been/are academically challenged, but also involved in a variety of extras on campus including club sports, fraternity/sorority life, and a campus job. We had some challenging experiences - including a sick child who needed to come home for a week - and the deans, professors and office personnel were incredibly supportive and responsive to our kids, while appropriately helping us support our kids without getting in the way. After that sick child experience, I wrote a note to President Hallock to thank them for the support -never expecting to hear back- and he replied within hours! They really care about their students. Go Spiders!
The school is great, but the students are an oddball collection (and not in the fun way). Maybe it’s because I’m from the West Coast, but I find it hard to really click with these people.
Fantastic classes. Supportive deans. Not my kind of students but still a great school! I really love the department I’m in (Gottwald Supremacy!!!)

I am a first semester freshman from a large private high school. Professors care and teach the students first and the subject second. Students here are genuine and kind with rarely any snobbiness. Night life consists of bars and frat events first semester which is open to all freshmen. Frats are very welcoming and nice which is a good change from the typical stereotypes. It is said that frats become more exclusive second semester and that you need to be in frat to go to frats. But there are a lot of alternatives outside of greek life. Food is incredible. Dorms are nice and well maintained. Students are from all over the country and world with not too many pre-made cliques formed.

I love Richmond, beautiful campus, awesome food, great people. It's a small campus, so it can be hard to find your group at first, but if you look hard enough, they are definitely there. It's easy to make friends because everybody is a friend of a friend. There is a lot of money here, and the career resources are absolutely amazing. Going abroad is pretty much expected here, and the university basically holds your hand through a lot of the process. The resources are amazing, you just have to know how to take advantage of them.

In terms of academics and overall schooling, U of R is pretty much perfect. Faculty and professors go above and beyond to help you and truly care about the standard of education. Where this school fails is its students. If you went to public school, do not come here. If you do not want to join Greek life, do not come here. If you want to join active clubs, do not come here. This school is super cliquey and almost immediately separates upon orientation and even beforehand. Essentially, this school is phenomenal if your goal is to only get a degree and job after. But if you want a great college experience, you’re taking a gamble coming here.

The professors really care about the students. The campus is by far the most beautiful campus I visited. A lot to do on campus but at the same time, just 10 min from downtown Richmond!! Love the food and the gym!!! Just amazing! So happy here!
Great academics and financial aid available. Lots of resources and great classes to take. Amazing professors and campus is beautiful.

The academic environment at the University of Richmond is rigorous but incredibly supportive. The small class sizes make a huge difference—professors know your name, and it's easy to ask questions and participate in discussions. There's a strong emphasis on critical thinking, writing, and interdisciplinary learning. The resources, like the writing center and faculty office hours, make it clear that student success is a priority. It can be challenging, but in a way that pushes you to grow.The social scene is vibrant but not overwhelming. Because it's a smaller campus, it’s easy to get to know people across different years and majors. There’s a nice mix of social events—some hosted by the university, others by student groups or Greek life. Whether you're into late-night events, relaxed hangouts, or outdoor activities, there's usually something going on. It’s a tight-knit community, and people are generally friendly and welcoming.
The best part about UR is how much the professors care about their student and are willing to help them succeed both academically and in their future careers.

I absolutely love it here. Coming to UR was one of my best decisions ever. I have found my best friends, all of the professors truly care about me, and the city of Richmond is awesome. I could not give the school higher praise.
University of Richmond is a wonderful program. The area in Richmond is beautiful and the campus is beautiful as well. The staff are very professional and helpful.

As a first year student at the University of Richmond, there is plenty I have yet to experience but a lot that I already have experienced. I would say that the hardest part of this school is the social life. As a freshman, groups get cliquey very quickly which can be difficult for many. However there are plenty of way to branch out, join clubs, play sports, etc. that definitely help with the transition process. Although it is not a majority of the school that is associated with Greek Life it often feels like a large part of the campus life and therefore interested students should be aware of that.

University of Richmond is a very small collegiate campus, which is a dream for many. It has a lively party scene but the size can be seen as a deterrent to some people who are expecting parties that match that of an SEC school. The academics are great, and professors are all passionate but very few stand out, in my opinion. Overall fun school but your experience entirely depends on how you get connected and who you meet.
I have had a postive academic and social expericne. The professors the quality of teaching are excellent.

ai_summary: Ursinus College offers a unique and intimate academic environment characterized by small class sizes and a supportive faculty committed to student success. Many reviews highlight the close-knit community feel , where students quickly form friendships and develop personal relationships with professors. However, some students express concerns about the limited social scene and activities outside of academics, noting that the surrounding area can feel isolating, especially for those who are not involved in sports or Greek life. While the campus is often described as beautiful, there are complaints regarding outdated facilities , housing issues, and limited food options . Overall, Ursinus seems to be a good fit for students who appreciate a tight community and rigorous academic challenges but may not suit those seeking a vibrant social scene or extensive extracurricular options.

reviews.text: I am a transfer student and I realized quickly after transfering to ursinus that I made the right choice. Ursinus is filled with so many people, who like me, strive for excellence. Everyone here studies hard and is so welcoming. I love the academics here as well. You can easily tell how passionate these professors are about their job and what they are teaching. Even the dining works and staff are always upbeat and love chatting with students to provide the most excellent service. The campus is beautiful and there are always people outside, running, walking, walking their pets.
The experience was pretty good. I was a student athlete but if I were to redo my 4 years it would not be there.

Ursinus has a nice administrative staff and the education is good but the school environment is not good. Students are toxic and mean-spirited. People aren’t friendly and there is nothing to do. It basically feels like high school, part two. Had a really hard time meeting nice people. The team I was on was incredibly toxic, so I quit the team and the school. Best decision ever. This place was terrible for my mental health.

As an alumnus of Ursinus College, I’m grateful for the education and community it provided. Small classes and dedicated professors pushed me to think critically and gave me opportunities for research and mentorship that shaped my career. The Common Intellectual Experience and strong sense of community made Ursinus unique, fostering curiosity and lifelong friendships. Beyond academics, campus life offered countless ways to get involved, from athletics to service. Most importantly, Ursinus prepared me for life after graduation with adaptable skills and a supportive alumni network. Choosing Ursinus was one of the best decisions I ever made.

In my now fourth year being at Ursinus, and I am going to be honest, never in my life have I dealt with more and more crap from a school. The food accessibility on campus all year round is severely limited, and the student life and engagement on campus is horrendous, and not to mention the athletics in terms of inclusion and also accepting people who are different and there being tolerance for putting others down. Then on top of that I have had to deal with the power going out on campus (lost $74 worth of groceries the same day), facilities completely turning off wifi for hours on end which is vital for students to do work on campus during the school year, and over the summer. They get paid so much by the government and also by their attending students, and they still can't utilize their funds properly to pay professors while probably for the most part paying general faculty more than their salary, and students having to pay more every year. Overall, my time at Ursinus has been horrid.
I love it. I feel prepared to continue my career in Engineering through the Ursinus Pre-Engineering program!
I really love the community! The professors, students and staff are all extremely nice and welcoming! It is also very easy to make friends!

I am really enjoying my time at Ursinus. There are so many amazing opportunities offered by campus organizations and professors. I have been able to complete undergraduate research, have two on campus jobs, and present at an academic conference all by the end of my sophmore year. Ursinus is a great place to branch out and grow as a student and a person.

I would not choose any other school. Being at Ursinus is like a second home to me. I have been able to make so many friends and enjoy all my weekends. I’d rather be at Ursinus than at home some times!

The community here at Ursinus is unmatched! Everyone on campus is kind and welcoming, and helps all students feel at home. Our athletics department has a fabulous reputation of uplifting students, as athletes, and overall as people. The opportunities across the many areas of interest are open for all students even during freshmen year. I feel significantly more prepared for my career after attending Ursinus.

Ursinus allowed me to discover myself and my full potential through close knit connections with professors and classmates. I was able to be included, personified, and understood on a personal level through all of my classes. I was not just a number, but a name. And I was able to do so safely as I lived on campus and explored my social life.

The professors at Ursinus are really incredible. Every academic field has expert faculty that truly care about their students and want them to succeed. You’ll get hands-on experience no matter what your discipline is. I feel like I learn so much from them, even in the classes I took outside of my majors. The professors are the reason that you’re going to get a well-rounded education if you come to Ursinus.
I love Ursinus. Yes there are things not everyone is going to like, but most are super friendly! I was able to find friends the minute I arrived on campus and have never felt alone.

I currently attend Ursinus and love it! I transferred from another institution and all of my credits transferred easily. I grew up in the area and never realized how much Ursinus had to offer until I visited. The campus is beautiful. Even though it’s in the suburbs, there’s plenty to do and it’s not too far from Philly! They are very generous with scholarships and aid; don’t let the tuition cost scare you! I highly recommend Ursinus and hope to see you here!

Professors are extremely nice! They are always there to help you if you have questions. I’m on a first name basis with a lot of the professors I’ve had so far. They really do want to get to know you as a person. The class sizes also help make those connections with professors.

ai_summary: Vassar College offers a distinctive liberal arts experience characterized by a vibrant and inclusive community , featuring a diverse student body that is generally supportive and intellectually curious. Academically, students appreciate the small class sizes and approachable professors who are dedicated to helping them succeed, with many noting that the rigorous coursework is paired with plenty of resources and opportunities for mentorship, such as in-person office hours and research collaborations. The campus itself is praised for its beauty and character, although some reviews highlight that the social scene can feel cliquey and not particularly vibrant, with a perception of limited campus activities , particularly on weekends. While the dining options have seen improvements, and financial aid services are recognized as generous, there are concerns about the administration's responsiveness and the difficulties some students face, particularly regarding social integration and finding their niche in a sometimes insular environment. Overall, Vassar is seen as a place that fosters deep learning and strong connections, though students with different backgrounds may find varying experiences.

reviews.text: Vassar is a place that encourages learning and experience. I learned a lot there. I also got to express myself, which is what I needed when I was eighteen.

Vassar has provided me with an exceptional network of academic giants in their field, perhaps the best professor-student relationships I could ask for. The student body is just small enough so that it doesn't feel insular, and the general social dynamic is that the school itself is clichey, which is actually a benefit as a first-year. Generally, Vassar is known as one of the most leftist colleges in the nation, and that lack of political diversity can be a fault at times. The political scene is certainly a bubble, and even if you agree with the student body's politics, escaping that bubble can be refreshing at times. That said, this does not outweigh the general strength of the institution and the immeasurable intellectual network it has provided me.

Vassar is an extremely inclusive and diverse liberal arts college. Through my short time here I have already made multiple deep connections to others around me, and everyone overall is always in a positive mood which spreads to the surrounding student population.

More understanding assignment turn-in lateness policies (especially on grounds of expressive perfectionism as opposed to performative negligence, perhaps a negotiated arrangement to attend meetings with professors explicating progress and roadblocks i.e. inconsequential academic support)

Vassar is a good college for a very niche type of student. It won't fit for everybody, but if you enjoy geeking out about your interests and things a little left of center, this is the college for you.

Everyone is super passionate and hard working. By far the ideal environment to grow as a learner and an individual. While party scene doesnt compare to a big school, very much so work hard play hard and we have a lot of fun

I enjoyed the liberal arts education I received at Vassar College. I was able to take a variety of courses regarding gender, economics, history, and religion alongside my STEM education. The opportunity to have an open curriculum allowed me to explore all avenues of life. I also enjoyed the various opportunities to engage in my community via the Office of Community Engagement Learning, including volunteering at the local hospital and elderly care center. I am glad Vassar was able to provide so many fully funded opportunities to me--a woman of color from a low income, disadvantaged background. However, I would love to see an increase in diversity in the school and more events to increase school spirit.

I'm an international student from the Dominican Republic, and I personally love Vassar. They are very welcoming and inclusive of people from all kinds of backgrounds, and there are student organizations for almost anything you can think of. The campus is beautiful, the food is decent, the people are pretty nice, and I love my professors. However, I will say that the parties are not all that. If you really care about having crazy campus parties, a bigger school might be what you're looking for. Other than that though, it's a great school.

I enjoyed my time at Vassar. I felt that the academics were an especially strong part of my experience. Class sizes were small enough to feel personal, and professors were often willing to go above and beyond to support students. Socially, it can be difficult to find your niche in the beginning, but if you are patient and persistent you can find your people.
Vassar is a great school, there are so many interesting and cool people on this campus. The campus itself is beautiful and feels very safe.

As a small liberal arts school, the classes at Vassar were always small and the professors were easy to get to know if you made the effort. The workload was manageable, and there were many interesting courses that were offered. Dining options and the dormitories were decent, but the overall campus is incredibly beautiful in all seasons. The "Vassar Bubble" is very much a real thing however, and there is a significant amount of separation between the students on campus and the Poughkeepsie community. It can also be difficult to find things to do outside of the campus if you don't have a car, however the Metro North to New York City is nearby and is only about a two hour ride. The student population is diverse, and students care greatly about issues around them and making the world a better place. The classroom environment is not competitive between students, and the workload is difficult but manageable.

Vassar changed my life. I learned, for the first time, how to think critically, and how to write with clarity and meaning. I learned how to listen, because so many of my professors were brilliant, and took an interest in helping me to develop MY ideas, via writing, speaking, and taking chances outside the campus. More generally, the Students with whom I was friendly, and/or were fellow English majors, were eloquent, critical thinkers, who also had a sense of social responsibility, and spoke truth to power when needed. This is NOT a party school, although I remember some wild, fun and creative parties! It is most of all about relationships and relatedness. I am 64(!) and my 2 best friends in the Universe are my classmates from Vassar. One went on to become an academic at Berkeley, another a Ph.D. psychoanalyst.
I love the community and access to fun activities on and around the campus. I would definitely change the approach to equity.

I would absolutely not recommend going here! For reference, I majored in Biochemistry and am graduating this spring. Here are a few reasons:
- The research environment is toxic. Many of the professors demand a lot out of you, but are unwilling to act as a mentor. With the exception of one professor, I did not receive any support while applying to graduate school. I had to seek outside mentorship opportunities and had a much better experience doing research elsewhere (UCLA, Weill Cornell). There is no accountability for professors that act inappropriately.
- The social scene here is awful. Vassar is incredibly cliquey and it was difficult to make friends, as people mainly stuck to the friends they made freshman year. Many people here are very self-centered, immature, entitled, and judgmental. There is virtually nothing to do on campus most weekends. A lot of people pretend to be happy here, but many friend groups are toxic/many people are pretty depressed.
Bottom line: don't go here!

One thing that attracts people to Vassar is the campus. The campus is really beautiful. Most of the buildings are built in an old fashioned way and there is a variety of trees planted around. From what others told me, the community isn’t the best but in my personal experience, I met a lot of wonderful people by studying at Vassar.

If you are looking for a military school that emphasizes standards, discipline, integrity, etc. This is the school for you; I will say that VMI is definitely an acquired taste, so if you don't like what you're doing, you will be miserable all four years.

Overall, my experience at Virginia Military Institute has been both challenging and rewarding. VMI pushes you to grow not just academically, but also in terms of leadership, discipline, and resilience. The military structure teaches you time management, responsibility, and how to work as part of a team, which I see as valuable skills for my future.
What I like most about VMI is the sense of brotherhood and community. Everyone goes through the same demanding process, and that creates strong bonds and lasting friendships. I also appreciate the academic rigor and the opportunities to push myself physically, mentally, and personally.
If I could suggest a change, it would be to expand more student support resources, whether academic tutoring, mental health services, or career counseling. The challenges are worthwhile, but more support would help cadets balance the pressure and succeed in every area.
I did not appreciate the benefits until after graduation. Great school to be from; very challenging school to graduate from.

We used to say that VMI is a place you don’t want to be at, but you want to be from. To say that VMI is challenging academically, physically, mentally or otherwise is an understatement. However, the lessons that you take into your personal and professional life upon graduation are invaluable. Discipline, grit and a realization that you can always dig deeper and excel augment and amplify the top-notch education that you have received. Whether entering the military or civilian world, you will find that the Alumni Network is so deep and so strong, you have tens of thousands of men to lean on for personal and professional support-and they always answer when you reach out. They don’t need to know you. If they hear you are from VMI, they take your call and help you.
Amazing place to be. If you're looking for something hard then this is the perfect place for you. "Dont do ordinary"

Well this place is like no other college in the nation. We spend six months getting screamed at everyday, coupled with grueling physical exercises, along with extreme sleep deprivation. Words can't even sum up my experiences here. We have marched 20-mile ruck marches, scaled mountains and cliffs, and low-crawled through mud while they fire guns over or heads. Every day I wake up on my "rack" (thats what we call our beds, they are essentially slabs of hardwood) I wonder what the hell Im doing here. Every time I have guard duty, (walking in circles for hours on end, or waking up at 1-3 AM) or have to stand at attention in the subzero weather for formations, I wonder if it's all worth it. That being said despite all the bad I won't change a thing. The bonds I have formed here with my fellow-classmates will stand the test of time. The sense of pride and accomplishment I get just by going here is unmatched. Definitely a life changing experience for the better.

Attending VMI is one of the best decisions I have ever made. This strict Military atmosphere is perfect for someone who needs extra support to stay on top of their responsibilities. It is also the perfect place for highly motivated leaders to thrive.

Virginia Military Institute (VMI) excels in fostering discipline, leadership, and academic excellence. Cadets benefit from rigorous military training, a strong alumni network, and a sense of camaraderie. The structure and discipline help develop time management skills, resilience, and a strong work ethic. The institute’s academic programs are robust, with small class sizes allowing for personalized attention, creating a well-rounded educational experience. The supportive alumni network provides valuable career opportunities and mentorship. Areas for improvement include modernizing facilities, balancing academics and training, and enhancing diversity and inclusion efforts. Overall, VMI offers a unique and valuable educational experience, preparing cadets to excel as capable and confident leaders in both military and civilian fields.

Virginia Military Institute is a well-rounded military school. The cadet life is full of hardships, but the cadet bond allows your time during your cadetships to go by efficiently. The professors are often times more than willing to give additional aid to support you throughout their classes.

VMI is a very welcoming place. In my first year, it was hard with the miliary training, but no one there wants to see you fail. No matter how much you get yelled at, they really do care about your success. Everyone does their best to see you succeed. The food is good and the workers are friendly. While you are there, you learn to embrace the hardships, because with them come lifelong friends who will always be by your side. By the end of your first year, you'll be in the best shape of your life. There are, however, some things that I believe should change. First and foremost, the NCAA athletes get out of most ratline activities and end up growing apart from their brother rats. I feel they need to fix this, as I understand that they could get hurt, but its unfair to the other Cadets who don't do sports.

I am actually transferring from the Virginia Military Institute to the University of Virginia. Next year will be my first year at UVA and I am very excited because I hear UVA has amazing academics. My academic experience at VMI was challenging. If you haven't heard of VMI, it is a military college located in Lexington, Virginia. The first seven months at VMI, you go through something called the "rat line". During this seven-month period, you are pushed to the limits both mentally and physically. Personally, the real challenge wasn't the exhausting workouts or being yelled at for hours on end. It was actually balancing my academics that really challenged me. For example, I remember having to be up every morning between 5:00am-6:00am for mandatory workouts or formations and then having to take a huge test or go to a full day of classes right after. It was really hard to balance academics with being in the constant state of the "rat line", but I finished the semester with a 3.59 GPA!
Pretty good so far, just putting up with a lot of random bs sometimes. You make good relationships with the people around you and as long as you put effort in you will succeed.

It is what you make it. If you get involved it will be an awesome place to be for four years, but if you don’t it will be miserable. Find the right people and it’s a great school. Academics are amazing but professors vary from major to major.

Virginia Military Institute has served me more than I ever imagines it would. When I first found out I was going to attend a military college I bawled my eyes out so hard I could barely breathe. I was terrified of a culture that was so foreign to me. I showed up as a blank slate and decided I was going to trust the process. In the two years I have attended this college I have been painted with new motivation and drive that I had never truly tapped into. The idea of trying something new and terrifying does not paralyze me anymore, it entices me. I wish to not just expand my capabilities, but to expand the capabilities of everyone beside me as well. I feel as though I have been given the tools to bring up myself and the people who are willing to trust me as well.

I don’t know if I’d like to see anything change. VMI is what you make of it, and no matter which way I spin it - was a valuable experience. I mean, I didn’t go there to have fun and be comfortable in the first place lol. I wanted a challenge and to make close friendships.

ai_summary: Virginia Wesleyan University offers a unique experience characterized by its small class sizes and close-knit community, allowing for personal connections between students and professors. Many reviews highlight strong student-faculty relationships , supportive teaching staff, and a variety of clubs and activities to engage with. However, there are repeated concerns about the dormitory conditions, with many students noting issues like outdated facilities , cleanliness problems, and inadequate maintenance. The campus is described as beautiful and conveniently located near Virginia Beach, providing opportunities for nightlife and outdoor activities. While the academics receive praise, some students feel the dining options and campus life could be improved. Overall, the college's emphasis on community and individualized attention stands out, though the higher tuition costs and living conditions remain points of contention.

reviews.text: I enjoyed my time at Wesleyan. The student body is often an after thought of the administration there but the community, minus the administration, is great.

My overall experience at Virginia Wesleyen University has been okay. It could definately be improved on. I would like to see more inclusion in advisors with their students and checking upon on them to ensure they are getting the right classes and help to pass/enroll in their programs.

Most of the Professors don't teach what the course is, the dorms are disgusting, the food is below average, and the financial aid/ finance office will not do their job unless you bother them. If you do online, they will charge you a technology fee, you still have to opt out of their health insurance plan of over 3k, and they don't offer any useful courses such as science and math.

Easy to make lifetime friends there thanks to a small campus. Professors are awesome and care. Some were clearly only there to get paid. Every single dorm I went in had mold and ruined personal items. All but two types of buildings run on a two pipe system. Changing of seasons was horrible since they wait to change to AC/heat. They wait until roommate conflicts get bad before any interference. Unfortunately security of the campus is now questionable since the museum is being build. Dining hall is okay. The school administration really cares about image. Campus is welcoming and beautiful but dont show important problems. Once they get you enrolled, they dont bother with concerns after. Going to VWU is not something I recommend to anyone in the future. I do not believe it was worth the money to go there with all the problems I faced. They prioritize their image to donors and future students instead of the current students. Please go have a true college experience elsewhere

My experience at Virginia Wesleyan University has been a little bit challenging to say the least; My freshman year was a not a good year for me to say the least. They don't care about your mental health until you're on the verge of not wanting to be around anymore. I had to take a month off of school causing me to find behind in all of my classes causing my first semester to look really bad despite me putting in the effort for me to do well. I am now a sophomore at Virginia Wesleyan and it's gotten better at least to be more mental health surrounded. The best thing they could've possible have done was to allow emotional support animals on campus.
Apparently, from the photo above, the Honors College is located in an underground sea cave. How cool is that?
I am very excited to attend Virginia Wesleyan University this fall. Just talking to my enrollment counselor I feel very welcomed to the school.
The instructors are understanding and helpful. The school made sure I paid as little as possible. The opportunities are great

For a private institution, the dorms and overall living spaces are not as up to date as they should be. The food is average and the equipment is average. For the money you pay, the school is not worth it. Unless you are very interested in Arts, this school is not for you.

The environment is pretty welcoming. Everyone here is really nice but also down to earth and real- not putting on a face. I applied to and visited a lot of colleges, and this one turned out to be the best for me. It's a small school but that doesn't take away from the experience.

Virginia Wesleyan is a small school. The class sizes are small which I like. The classes have a lot of discussions and are easy to learn. The classes are easy to find when you get the hang of things. Freshman and Sophomores have to take a January Term which isn't my favorite. It is at least a 2 hour class everyday for 3 weeks. The social aspect is okay, again its a small school. Most people know each other.

As a student athlete I love the school experience. Meal, practice, work and class schedules clashes a lot but if your are able figure out how to manage them it’s an amazing experience. I am able to hang out with my teammates pretty often and join school activities and events. There are also many student lead clubs and events that truly gives the students power over what they value.

Very unprofessional place only been there 6 months and I’m over it students and staff are rude it’s definitely not exactly clean and sanitary mold infested roach infested and the audacity of the energy and attitudes of the people there when it’s clearly not even a clean school try hiring a company to get rid of the mold and roaches before you look down on people

Ya'll don't waste your time or money here. I've only been here a semester and i'm thinking about leaving. The dorms and bathrooms are riddled with bugs and mold and the food from the dining hall is not good, i've found hair and dead bugs in my food multiple times. Almost every professor ive come into contact with has some kind of superiority complex, some not even allowing laptops in class or complaining about having to help students. Like what??
My biggest grievance is about the dorms though, the sinks dont work well and neither do the showers and its all filled with mold. The halls smell from the washers that produce a smell of rotting eggs and it seeps into your clothes. I dont think the custodians have ever cleaned the showers and they only clean the bathrooms like once a week (which if you think living with over 20 girls on one floor is bad, trust me its worse).
Overall, this place lowkey sucks and I want to leave already..

I am from a diverse county coming from Prince Georges County, Maryland and coming here down to the Virginia Beach area has been an experience. The schools aura and vibe may seen a little down and boring but never gloomy, whether it's walking around the campus or taking the 30 minute walk towards the outlet. You will always have something to do. The batten student learning center is a great place to chill, hangout and make new friends. From the grill to the cafe several selections of eats. You'll always keep company and stay active.

It was a roller coaster as the balance and expectations from peers on campus, being highly academically achieving and heavily involved on campus, created a lot of stress on me and led me to burn out as a freshman and underachieve and get bad grades while maintaining a happy appearance. However, giving myself space from my friend group and quitting my collegiate sport allowed me to come back with new priorities; however fell into different problems as the food quality became worse and the facilities worsened with malfunctioning water temperatures.

Wabash College offers a very specific type of college experience. Crawfordsville's lack of restaurants, activities, and cultural options quickly becomes apparent—I often feel stranded by what’s available off campus. The campus itself is extremely small and insular, with 900 students, so it can feel isolating if you’re looking for variety or a wider social circle.
Being an all-male school, the environment has a reputation for feeling like a tightly controlled fraternity or even a military camp at times. The close-knit nature can intensify social pressures, and it can be hard to find your place if you don’t fit the dominant campus culture. For those who want options and a more balanced, inclusive college experience, Wabash’s environment and limited surroundings is challenging.

I have only been at Wabash for the month of July for my summer class. This class is called Wabash Liberal Arts Immersion Program (WLAIP) and my experience with being on campus was amazing. I got to experience what class will be like and how I need to manage my time. We were given a total of three essays with three hours of scheduled library time to work on them each week. On weekends, we planned many activities with the group to go to Indianapolis to have fun and gain closer connections. Overall, I enjoyed my experience while on campus and I cannot wait until I go back to Wabash in the fall.
I’m staring first year in wabash, I have being part of 2 summer programs and I’m loving it !! I looking forward to play football, I played 4 years in high school and we wing the state championship

Wabash College has provided me with many diverse opportunities that will allow me to succeed. These opportunities will also allow me to build the strongest application for graduate school in my pursuit of becoming a veterinarian. I joined Tau Kappa Epsilon my freshman year which provided me a close network of brothers that helped me to succeed on campus by requiring us to join one club on campus. Next semester, I will be tasked with leading the Wabash Riley Dance Marathon where we will fundraise and hold philanthropy events for Riley Children's hospital. This will require me to both recruit new members, and plan these events to ensure the success of the club. I am also dedicated to continuing DEI at Wabash as the DEI chair in our fraternity. Despite this, there has been a lack of support on campus for DEI as shown by the various LGBTQ hate crimes we have seen on campus. The lack of a true response by the administration is what needs to change at our college if DEI is going to thrive.

I love Wabash College. This has been one of the best decisions of my life coming here. The small knit community aspect and individual connections you meet are great. The Wabash Mafia / Alumni network is REAL. I have meet friends that I will call friends for the rest of my life as well. And the community around Wabash loves us, and we directly support them back, and that is another reason why I will always see Crawfordsville as my home away from home.

In my time at Wabash College, I have truly embraced the atmosphere of the college. This place makes you into an outspoken leader and friend because of the curriculum designed to better suit how men learn. Here at Wabash being involved with highly competitive athletics shapes and molds you into the man you will become for the rest of your life. With the professors being so accessible to all students this also allows for more learning outside the classroom. But again, what makes Wabash special is the people. I have met my best friends who will eventually be at my wedding. The people you meet here stick with you like glue through thick and thin. Which in a man's life, is needed to become the best man he can. And at larger schools it is difficult to socialize with multiple people because of the sheer number of students at the institution. I am forever grateful for the opportunity to study and become a man at Wabash College. Thank you, Wabash! #WAF
One of the first things you notice when coming to the Wabash campus is the sense of community. A very unique school with excellent professors and academics.

Wabash has been life changing. The school and professors are amazing, providing challenging yet intriguing coursework. Bu most importantly, Wabash will change your life. The people you meet, the relationships you build here will last a lifetime. When selecting colleges, I didn't experience anything like Wabash. If you give this place a chance, that chance will prove worthwhile.
Wabash College challenges the students and promotes critical thinking along with excellent oral and written communication skills. It is a small, intimate campus with an incredible faculty.

Wabash College excells across the board. The primary way it achieves this excellence is through fostering strong community bonds among all who are part of the Wabash Family. Students, professors, staff, alumni, and anyone who is related to those people all work hard to support one another, before and after graduation. The academics are rigorous and manageable at the same time because the faculty is so devoted to ensuring the highest level of instruction and out-of-class support. The award-winning student newspaper, well-rounded student athletes, and many other clubs provide spaces for students to grow beyond the classroom by facing the challenges of non-academic self-deceloped group projects and events that prepare these men for life ahead in ways a class cannot. But watch out! The school is small enough that there is always a chance you may be late to class because you got caught talking to a professor or another student on the way because at Wabash there is danger of real, deep talk.

Good school for academics. Sad that when you earn an academic scholarship from a donor they deduct that exact amount from your Wabash presidential scholarship. That just punishes the students striving and excelling. It isn’t fair! You are not motivating your high achievers when you take from them just to redistribute the money. Do your donor’s know this is happening?

Wabash College is an incredible institution. They have rigorous academics, successful athletics, and a fantastic student body. They produce quality young men who become very successful. In my opinion, Wabash is the best college in the world.

I liked the adaptability from not only the students but the teachers of this college in how they are able to teach and allow us to learn so many different things depending on certain people’s abilities.

Wabash College was a wonderful place for an undergraduate education due to its small class sizes, extensive alumni connections, and incredible professor accessibility. I enjoyed Wabash since every student can participate in class, extracurricular clubs and organizations, and academic presentations or events of interest. I enjoyed Wabash due to my ability to take control of my education and reach out for help when needed. I would like to see more specific internship opportunities for individuals looking to pursue various healthcare-related fields, but also humanities-related careers.

ai_summary: Washington College offers a close-knit community experience, characterized by its small class sizes that foster strong relationships between students and professors. Many reviews highlight the dedicated faculty who are invested in student success, providing personalized attention and various opportunities, including internships and experiential learning. While the campus and surrounding town are described as charming and supportive , some reviews indicate concerns about limited social life , potential cliquishness, and a lack of diversity. Student life is more vibrant for those involved in athletics or extracurricular activities, but others may find it less engaging. Additionally, while academics are generally praised, reviews note the tuition can be steep for what is provided. Overall, Washington College is seen as a place for students seeking a tight community, engaging academics, and meaningful connections, though potential applicants should consider the social environment and location.

reviews.text: I’ve really enjoyed attending WAC! I feel like I’ve been given an opportunity to grow in my future and build new self confidence in what my future holds. The staff and most professors have been supportive and helpful in my time at the college. I have the best teammates on our Trap and Skeet team as well as phenomenal coaches.

At Washington College, one of my favorite aspects of it is the overall environment. The campus is so beautiful during all seasons, and you can always walk down to the water or around town during the spring or fall. The local community is so inviting of students and offer discounts which is super cool! The professors here are all super communicative and welcoming. But, my favorite part is the Greek organizations on campus, me being in one of them, it really gave me a family and is so different than other schools because of how small of a college we are.

Great academics and opportunities at the price of having nothing to do on the weekends. Easy to do networking and find intern/externships, and great alumni contacts. They need to improve student activities/events, though.
My experience has been wonderful. The community you build at WAC is so welcoming and friendly and they offer so many great opportunities.

My overall experience with Washington College is going well; I like how everyone knows each other and how the professor tries to get to know you and help you to be better and get you what you need to grow in everything.
I have loved my 4 years here and am graduating soon, but it has been life-changing! I have built close connections with peers, professors, and other staff who all set me up for success in the future.

I love Washington College. The school is a place like no other, and being a student-athlete has made the experience here all the more rewarding. I love the location and the connection to the Chestertown community. Everyone here really has each other’s backs from faculty to peers you’ve never even met before!

I love WACs campus environment, and since it is a smaller school, I see familiar faces everywhere, professors included! A variety of unique classes are offered to cater towards many different interests. Staff is so friendly and accommodating, it’s so nice to know that they have your best interest in mind. When I have scheduling conflicts or concerns, professors/advisors are quick to answer and fix any questions. They truly get to know you and what works best for you. Beyond the campus school, the surrounding area of Chestertown is charming and great for stress-free weekend activities. Although it can be a bit quiet on campus sometimes, it’s what some people prefer.

I loved my experience at Washington College, and can truly not say enough good things about the institution. Washington College is small, but incredibly unique. With small resources, the college provides an abundance of "large school" opportunities. For example, I am a part of the American Marketing Association on campus, which sends our crew to a National Conference in New Orleans every year free of cost to the members through the generous donations of alumni! We have a Brown Advisory Board, countless research opportunities, organizations that directly fund student research, and a wide variety of clubs to choose from. Alongside the opportunities available to students, the community both at WAC and in the surrounding area is tight-knit, caring, and supportive. There can always be improvements to housing and dining accommodations, but I believe that the college is taking steps in the right direction to ensure comfortability for its students.

I love Washington College. It instantly felt like home when I first stepped on the campus. Everyone is so nice and respectful. The dorm are great and the professors take pride in connecting with the students to make sure that everyone understands what the material is that they are learning. I fully recommend Washington College to anyone that comes to tour here. It’s the best decision I’ve ever made.

Washington College was an unreal experience. The college, coupled with Chestertown, and the surrounding Eastern Shore area was a picturesque place to attend college. The school is small, which makes for a much more tight knit, family atmosphere. Everyone supports each other. Professors were sharp, and insanely helpful. The dining hall might be the best restaurant I have ever been too. Not kidding. All you can eat and so many different options. The waterfront campus on the Chester River is also something that is very unique to WC.

I loved the small town, the community was so tight knit and was really good for making connection sin the profession world. Athletics is also very important, over 50% of student body is an athlete, but the arts are also popular, with theatre and dance. Loved being able to have so many friends involved in so many different things!

Washington College was a wonderfully intimate community, allowing direct and personal relationships to be built between students and professors. During my time, COVID shut the school down, but the professors did an amazing job at keeping classes as close to normal as possible and providing resources to help students succeed during an unknown time.

I thought I would really enjoy this school but boy was I wrong. I have never hated a place more. There is nothing to do here for fun the most fun thing here is shoremal and birthday ball but its basically hoco and prom. Everyone here is nice it's just super boring and there is nothing to do on the weekends.
Washington College has been a great fit for me because I like the quietness of the town. I’ve enjoyed most of my classes so far and the college has many clubs and activities during the entire year.

ai_summary: Washington & Jefferson College (W&J) stands out for its close-knit community and supportive academic environment . Students appreciate the small class sizes and the personal attention from professors, who are generally described as caring and invested in student success, fostering a transformative education experience . Campus life is lively, with a variety of extracurricular activities and social opportunities, although some students feel the options could be more diverse and robust. While the campus is beautiful and well-maintained, there's a mix of opinions regarding the quality of housing and dining services, with calls for improvements in these areas. The college also offers strong networking opportunities , particularly through its alumni connections. Overall, W&J appeals to those seeking a more personalized college experience, though students note challenges with campus life dynamics and living conditions.

reviews.text: It's a nice small campus. Close to the town, with some decent places. Heavily athletic oriented but also very academically too.

Ah, Washington & Jefferson College (W&J) was definitely an experience I won’t forget. The campus itself is beautiful, with those classic red brick buildings and green spaces that just made everything feel cozy and like you were part of a community. The professors there are a big highlight—they're not just about lectures, but genuinely care about your growth, both academically and personally. It was easy to build connections, and they were always available for a chat or guidance.
I also loved how involved the school is in pushing students to get out into the world. There’s a solid focus on leadership and service. Whether it was through internships, study abroad, or community service projects, you could tell they were all about making sure you left not only with a degree but with real-world experience. Plus, the small class sizes meant you weren’t just a number—you could really engage in discussions and dive deep into topics.

After completing my first year of college, I have realized that it is more than just about striving to receive your undergraduate/bachelor's degree. Through W&J I have learned an assortment of skills, in and out of the classroom, and I am proud of myself for taking the next step in furthering my education. The professors challenge you in the classroom, but the biggest advantaged is that class sizes are small; so, you are seen as more than just a number, you are heard, and they take great care in making sure their students are successful. The student body overall is tight knit, so even if you do not find your clique, there is no discrimination on what type of person you are, so it is easy to fit in, and feel safe to be yourself. The housing is great, depending on whether you are in a renovated building or one of the older dorms. Either way, the RA's and faculty make sure their residents are comfortable, and adjust well to college life, which is extremely helpful if you live far away.

I have had an overall enjoyable experience at W&J. I have met a lot of great people and made many memorable memories. I really enjoy the academic aspect of the school, most of the professors I have had were great and it was clear they truly cared about their students. I also enjoy the fact that since it is such a small school it is really easy to connect with the professors and to get help. Also, since the school is small it is a lot easier to make friends than a bigger school would be, and most people are very nice and welcoming. However, I would like to see changes in the food; it is not that good. The diversity also lacks a lot, and as a person of color, that can be difficult sometimes, because there are not many people I feel that I can relate with. I also wish there were more activities and events because the campus is so small there is not much to do a lot of time. The dorms are also not the best, they are not terrible, but they are very small.

In my first two semesters here at W&J, I've grown academically more than anything. I've learned the best way for me to study and how to understand the content. With the exception of a few professors, I've felt like I know where I can go to get help if I need it.

My experience on this campus has been great, I have made fantastic connections with both students and professors alike. The atmosphere of learning and engagement has allowed me to grow both intellectually and socially. I have never been to a better place than W&J.

I’m still fairly new to the school, so I don’t really know many people. I’m shy so it’s harder for me to make friends, but everyone on campus is very nice. Every interaction that I have had was enjoyable to be in, you get greeted with a smile and a kind hearted voice. Drama and conflict does happen to go on in the dormitory between floors/ rooms and I do believe there’s different approaches that the school could take instead of just kicking people out of dorms and moving them. Other than a few inconveniences, W&J is a great place to be. I have came across so many major life changing opportunities and have learned so much in a short amount of time. The professors are very willing to be a helping hand or mentor, and so far I have been treated with nothing but kindness every time I reach out to them.
The one thing I love most about being a student here at W&J is the welcoming community. From the moment I stepped foot on campus, it just felt like home.

I can confidently say that my time there was transformative. W&J is a place where academics and community come together to provide an exceptional experience. The professors are outstanding. You can’t hide in the classroom—they take the time to know you, challenge you, and invest in you. They truly care about their students, not just as learners but as individuals. Campus life is vibrant—there’s something for everyone. It’s what you make of it, and I made the most of it. From joining a fraternity to serving in student government and contributing to The Red & Black newspaper, I was involved across campus. Those experiences taught me leadership, collaboration, and how to find my voice. What really sets W&J apart is the strength of its alumni network. The opportunities I’ve had and the connections I’ve made are testament to the fact that W&J alumni truly punch above their weight class. I wouldn’t be where I am today without the education, experiences, and network I gained at W&J.

W&J was a great small school that a student can get a diverse education. I appreciated my professors and the way they helped me define my career path. Dr. Bennett was an amazing advisor. I would recommend that a student that want a great education, close experience, and good food. Come to W&J!

So far I have enjoyed my college experience here at W&J. I wanted a smaller college so that was one of the main factors that led to be here. The professors are good overall and the staff are all great. The dorms could be a little better but they're not that bad. Campus life is pretty good especially considering that W&J is in a smaller town.

My experiences of more than fifty years ago are not relevant. The academics were outstanding as were the qualifications of the students in the late sixties. However, based on my personal experience as a former adjunt and former member of the AEC, the current faculty are outstanding. Highly motivated students can realize a top tier education at W&J in a small nurturing environment.

I like the close-knit community of faculty and students here on campus. Everyone is so welcoming and always willing to lend a hand. The professors have their students' best interests in mind and help us all to achieve our academic and career goals. The campus is lively on weekdays and there are always events on or around campus to attend.

My overall experience at college has been great! I have made some very good friends and have had well connections with most of my professors. I enjoy the atmosphere and community of the school the most.

Washington and Jefferson College is a very nice school in terms of education, and academics. The lectures that take place only have around 15 to a maximum of 50 students in them, so it allows for a personal connection with the Professors. The only thing that is troublesome might I say is the environment surrounding the college. At night it can be very noisy with all the traffic and cars with loud exhaust. Also with the living situation, it isn't to awful, however it definitely could be better.

ai_summary: At Washington and Lee University, many reviews highlight the strong academic programs and the close relationships students develop with their professors, thanks to small class sizes that foster engagement and support. The campus life is described as vibrant, heavily influenced by Greek life, which is both a draw and a point of contention, particularly for those seeking more inclusive social options. While students generally appreciate the resources available, including generous financial aid and diverse extracurricular opportunities, some note a lack of diversity in the student body and a prevalent culture that can feel exclusive, particularly for those from non-traditional backgrounds. Furthermore, the picturesque Lexington setting adds to the appeal of the university, though some express concerns about limited activities outside of campus life. Overall, W&L is characterized as a place where strong academic achievement intertwines with a lively social scene—but one that may not fully embrace every student's experience.

reviews.text: I am an alum of the university as well as parent of a graduate, current student, and incoming freshman. My kids chose it themselves and applied early, granted they were likely influenced by my pride in the institution, volunteer work I’ve done since graduation, as well as my network of friends, social and professional. It is a true liberal arts college and one of the top ones in the country. I’m 100% fine making the investment to their tuition as I’ve already seen the benefit with my graduate getting the job she wanted before graduation as well as already excelling early in her career. I gave the school high marks as part of this review and, in full disclosure, only three stars for “Diversity,” but W&L has taken great strides in the 30 years since my being there in increasing a more diverse student body (given its size and rural setting) as well as the applicants I interview for admission continue to be more diverse.

As a student at Washington and Lee University, I’ve really appreciated the close-knit community and the strong relationships I’ve been able to build with professors. The Honor System creates a level of trust and respect that’s rare, and it honestly makes the campus feel safe and mature. I love the blend of challenging academics and vibrant traditions—things like the Speaking Tradition make even small moments on campus feel special. The combination of business and liberal arts has been perfect for someone like me with diverse interests. That said, I’d love to see more diversity and continued efforts to expand perspectives socially and culturally. More support for students who don’t participate in Greek life would also make the social scene more inclusive.

I can say without a doubt that attending W&L enabled me to be a well-rounded academic and researcher. The liberal arts education empowered me to engage in conversations all over campus and apply my knowledge to any discussion. Something I’ve found to be very powerful about W&L students compared to those I’ve met at other universities is their ability to view the world through an interdisciplinary lens and think about topics carefully and critically. W&L prepares students to be active participants in the world around them and engage compassionately with others. Although W&L isn’t considered diverse, it is important to recognize the efforts to not only recruit diverse students but retain diverse populations by fostering a welcoming community with a plethora of resources (that continues to grow) for everyone. I say that as a person of color. No place is perfect, but I felt that W&L was pretty close to that over my past 4 years. I’m excited to be an active member of the alumni community!
This is the best decision I have ever made. Going in, I played a sport, but many people don't and I have still found that there is a vibrant campus life.
the food here is the best i have ever had. i really enjoy the options and the variety. we have an all you can eat place that is amazing and for a quick bite, I can go to our late night venue.

As an alum, I am so grateful for my W&L experience. Was it challenging as a first gen, Latino student from a public school? Hell, yes! Tough academics. Tough to adjustment to a different social environment. But unlike some of the people who gave low scores, I actually did my research and knew exactly was I was getting into when I applied. (Don’t believe the non-sense about the Civil War being relitigated on campus - it’s nonsense. Any discussions about Robert E Lee were in the context of his Presidency of the school and the honor code. I NEVER had a conversation where the Battle of Chancellorsville came up. The only conflicts that were discussed were around which frat to go to first for a party.) It’s a highly competitive (but not cut-throat) culture. I received a great education and built life long friendships. As an alum, I have never had a job interview where the interviewer didn’t say “Oh, W&L. Great school.” I credit my professional success to my W&L experience.
There is an incredible amount of funding for every program, and the departments are very generous when handing out grants for student experiences!
For people looking for the ideal small college experience, Washington and Lee offers the best of it with strong academics, resources, and student culture.
Academics, athletics, campus life, off-campus environment, safety, quality of teaching, quality of extracurricular involvement, depth and breadth of student opportunities, job preparation

I loved my time at W&L. That school helped me grow into the person I am today. The small classes allowed me to have one-on-one time with my professors, many of whom I still speak with to this day. My friends and classmates continue to support me post grad, and the alumni network has been very accessible as I have applied to graduate programs. The school was very safe -- the only time we had a public safety announcement was when there was a bear on the outskirts of campus (living in the mountains has its perks and drawbacks). W&L is in a very very small town -- which I loved -- but may not be for everyone. I felt the academics were challenging but not impossible, and professors were accessible when I had questions. The school is not as diverse as other schools, and is more diverse than it gets credit for. There is a lot of geographic diversity -- very few students are from Virginia -- as well as wealth diversity. Most students are on some scholarship. I'd highly recommend W&L.
