I went to W&L because of the small class sizes, academic rigor, and relationships with professors. Overall I had a great time.

Washington and Lee is a University with excellent financial aid and amazing career prospects, even for low-income students and minorities. My experience here has been incredible in all aspects: academics, social life, etc.

Overall, Washington and lee has been a good experience. My time here has had its ups and downs, but I truly feel like it can/ is an amazing place for a lot of students. I believe that there needs to be more transparency when it comes to the social life here which is not for everyone. (Greek -life is very big ) Academics here are amazing and the professors truly care so much about their students.

I'm a third year and my amazing has been amazing so far. The people are so open and welcoming. Classes are pretty difficult but doable if you do the work. The work-life balance is definitely great since most people have a work hard play hard attitude

I could not imagine going to any other school besides W&L. The majority of the professors are amazing and care so deeply about their students, and the small class sizes allow you foster a relationship with your professors. The community is one factor that is truly unmatched here in Lexington. When I was touring other schools, I felt a disconnect on campus. When I was at W&L, everyone I interacted with seemed to care so much about me as an individual and went above and beyond to answer any questions I had and to help me in any way. This sentiment continued for me as a student. The community will bend over backwards for others, and that it seen very distinctly within the alumni network. The alumni are always looking out for students; they will always want to speak with you if you reach out and try and find any opportunities that they can for you.

ai_summary: Wellesley College offers a beautiful , serene campus that is often described as a “wonderland,” and many students appreciate the small class sizes and the supportive , engaging faculty who are approachable and commit to their students' success. The academic rigor is frequently highlighted, with a significant focus on independent work, though some students express that introspective learning can feel isolating at times, particularly in collaboration with peers who are competitive. The college is regarded as lacking in social life, with few party options on campus, leading many students to venture to nearby Boston for activities. Diversity is a mixed bag; while there are celebrations of various identities, some students wish for more inclusivity and connection, particularly for marginalized groups. Overall, Wellesley is celebrated for its strong alum network , emphasis on empowering women, and opportunities for academic growth, though some administrative and dining services face criticism.

reviews.text: It's nice to be away from the city sometimes (not too busy or loud) and the scenery is great. However, it is a little inconvenient because there aren't many things to do near campus.

Wellesley is a women's college, so it provides an extremely unique, enriching experience for people who identify as women from all walks of life. It's secluded, very much the east coast liberal arts experience. Not a place you want to go if you're interested in partying, but it has an amazing, inclusive campus culture.

I was never planning on attending, but after visiting and having the chance to get a feel for the community here, I knew it was where I wanted to be. I absolutely love the Wellesley community and have never regretted my choice to attend!

Wellesley is a great school academically (very challenging) but if you’re looking for a robust social scene or an engaging area go somewhere else. The town of Wellesley is small and wealthy and Boston isn’t as close as admissions would like you to think.
Great school academically, but main complaint (from daughter attending) is the food. Hours are limited and the variety and quality is seriously lacking. Many students end up ordering via DoorDash.
This is my first semester at Wellesley College, and I have had an incredible experience already. The environment is healthy to express oneself socially and academically.

The academics at Wellesley college are immensely enjoyable. The professors are extremely motivated to teach and highly knowledgable about their respective topics. The dining situation is not as good as one would hope with the food is often being bland and repetitive. But the school has 4 different dining halls to choose from so there are always ways around the boredom. The social scene on campus is semi-dead. There are still parties and organized events but, because of school mandated policies, any event over 50 people is really hard to organize. That being said, there are so many opportunities and ways to get into Boston and party or go have a nice dinner that is doesn't really matter what happens on campus. The one real bummer is the food and shops in Wellesley proper are geared towards a non-college demographic. Most of the restaurants are higher-end and the stores are pricier boutiques so its hard to go into town and actually do something. I would love to see more cafes as well.
Wellesley is beautiful and a fantastic place to go to school! The school truly believes in the alum connection while prioritizing students' aspirations in reaching their career aspirations.
I like beautiful campus, strong academic atmosphere and dedicated professors and staff. Housing and food are not appealing.
I loved being a student at Wellesley College, and I would make the decision to attend all over again. I found it to be an incredibly empowering space to develop most fully into myself.

Wellesley College is full of amazing professors who really want you to grow. Even for intro-level classes, it is extremely easy to create connections with any faculty. Every office hour felt like a one-to-one tutoring session with professors. Sometimes, my OH turns into a tea time to get to know each other in-depth. Unlike its peer colleges, student organizations/clubs don't have a "cutthroat" environment.
Although admins seem to hinder its social life, I like how I can have multiple options for "fun" to balance the student life and academics through the public transportation and hourly shuttle to Boston.
Lastly, Wellesley networking is real. There are lots of social and email chain groups that connect current students and alums. Those active alums actually care about the current students. It truly defines Wellesley as "Wellesley," and it makes such a huge difference to get direct advice from these women role models who are on the career path of your interests.

Wellesley College has excellent professors and courses. Classes are a combination of lecture and discussion, allowing students to learn multiple perspectives. The campus is quiet, but the free bus to Boston allows easy access to a big city, without having to live in one. If I had to do my college experience again, I would go to Wellesley again!

Wellesley is 100% what one would think of when you think about a liberal arts college, but it has absolutely none of the flexibility you would expect. The school is NOT close to Boston as advertised by many (its at least a 1.5 hour round trip on the bus). The environment here is stifling, over half of the student population goes abroad for a semester or longer truly because they need a break from the close minded and small campus. Professors are the best thing about this school depending on what department you are in. They always want to help you and there are opportunities for research and internships with Wellesley, MIT and surrounding colleges however if you do not know what you want to do in life Wellesley is not the place to figure it out. These opportunities are very limited. To get the best out of the resources here you need a clear goal and need to start early making connections and getting off campus.

Wellesley is a strange and sometimes wonderful place marred by an administration bent on limiting free speech and expression. Currently, the non-tenure track faculty are on strike for better compensation and benefits, as the institution comparatively underpays the workforce that teaches 40% of classes. The administration converted all striking classes to 0.5 credits, leaving seniors, those on financial aid, and international students floundering. This type of selfish and short-sighted thinking is exemplary of many of the administrative troubles Wellesley suffers.
Wellesley nominally celebrates its protest history and its place as a progressive institution while limiting free speech through regressive “protest policies” that determine where, how, when, if students may protest.
The academics are fantastic and broad. The campus is gorgeous and the student body is creative, motivated and thoughtful. The food is often good and the community is there if you just dare to leave your dorm.

I really enjoyed my time at Wellesley. The academics are rigorous, but the small campus allows you to have close connections with professors, which was incredibly meaningful to me. I met amazing professors and classmates, and I believe the small population played a big role in that. While it has its pros and cons, I personally benefited a lot from the close community. If you prefer a larger school experience, Wellesley might not be the best fit, but for those who value strong relationships and an intimate learning environment, it’s a great choice.

ai_summary: Wesleyan University is often praised for its vibrant and diverse community , where students feel welcomed and encouraged to explore a range of interests through a unique open curriculum . Many reviews highlight the accessibility and quality of the professors, alongside the camaraderie among students , particularly in the context of extracurricular activities and athletics. However, some students express concerns about wealth disparities within the campus culture and issues with the administration regarding student support services, particularly mental health resources. Academically, while students appreciate the challenging coursework , they report difficulty in class registration and a lack of support from career services. The social scene is described as both lively and cliquish, with a mix of parties and quieter social activities, though some feel that the campus can feel isolated due to its location. Overall, Wesleyan is seen as an intellectually stimulating environment , but potential students are advised to consider whether its unique atmosphere aligns with their needs and preferences.

reviews.text: Good experience overall. Would would a great school for all kinds of creative students (arts, film, theater etc
Great community, great education. Not a party school if that is what you're looking for. Also, no job guarantee win this economy. You still have to work for it

Nice liberal arts school. Good class sizes and professor engagement. Wide range of courses and options for students. Small town adjacent with some decent restaurants and a theater. Overall good experience.

I enjoy Wesleyan overall. I am a student-athlete and I find that I am able to manage that with my courses and a part-time job. I have great friends on campus and there are many opportunities to meet people. I wish our party scene was a little better but overall I don't have many complaints.

Wesleyan University is an incredible institution for students who want to pursue a well-rounded education in the humanity, arts, and sciences, in a rigorous scholarly environment with an open and welcoming community. No matter your interests, students are encouraged to learn and grow beyond their comfort zones, and are offered the opportunity to fully immerse and challenge themselves in a supportive community, with inspiring faculty and career advisors who will support you well beyond graduation. You will meet future world leaders, scholar-athletes, and brilliant creative artists and everyone in between. The common element among all students is curiosity and a genuine desire to learn, and you will find your peers have a common desire to use their knowledge to make the world a better place. Wesleyan University students are driven, ambitious critical thinkers, and a Wesleyan education prepares you well for responding to real-world challenges once you graduate.

From my limited experience so far (freshman), Wes' academics are top notch and the professors as a whole are incredibly knowledgable leaders in their respective fields, and have surprised me with how much they care about the students. The open curriculum is so underrated, so make sure to make use of it! My biggest complaint is the people: the 'just outside of [insert major city]' groups stick together as they've known each other for a long time already and are very exclusive about it. As someone coming here knowing no one, finding a good group of people is unnecessarily difficult. The classic 'Wes vibe' is alive and well, but not as large as I thought it'd be. The party scene is better than expected, but the best options are organizing your own hangouts. Wes also does a great job of making sure we're safe. 90% of dorms are fantastic. Food is even better (make sure to try all dining options available). Athletics are fun to watch and managing the student-athlete life is relatively easy.
Wesleyan was a wonderful experience. I graduated with an honors from the College of Social Studies, which combined Economics, Government, History, and Political and Social theory.

I'm a freshman, and so far Wesleyan has been a great experience, but came with some bumps (as any college freshman experiences). The people are smart, kind, and welcoming, but, as someone from a small town who came in not knowing anyone, the first few weeks can be rough, as the overwhelmingly large amount of NYC, LA, and Boston kids tend to form large groups almost immediately because they all already knew each other. Additionally, Wesleyan is not as true to its artsy, stoner reputation as I expected (and wanted). Many students are pretty preppy or just plain nerds, but the cooler crowds do exist, just at a smaller quantity; this seems to be a trend that is growing every year. I think the administration is trying to lean away from Wesleyan's cultural roots, to more of a corporate university vibe, comparable to larger schools, like Tufts or Georgetown. But, I hope this is not true. Also, as someone who went out a ton in HS, the party scene is pretty good and better than I expected.

I transferred to Wesleyan university in 2024 as a junior transfer student. Overall I feel Wesleyan is an accepting place with rigorous academics prosperity and achieving alumnus. Students here are friendly, but I haven’t been really involved with the community because of my own mental health situation. However Wesleyan is still a very ideal community for me!

It's a great school with excellent academics/classes, and superb professors. Rigorous, but worth it. Food is good, no complaints about that. Fair little town with hidden gems. Campus isn't the bestt, but she's cute. On-campus housing is good as well. The biggest thing I would say is that it's lacking social structure. As a person of color from a big city, I and other POC found that parties were rarely fun, always ended super early, campus groups were clique-y, and the overall campus community/unity/school spirit was lacking. Definitely has a good, unique student body, and I met some great people, but it was a constant culture shock, and I always had a wayy better social experience visiting other schools around the Northeast. I entered in 2020 and graduated in 2024, which definitely affected my experience. So I would say really think about what's important to you before selecting Wesleyan.

My experience at Wesleyan University has been really rewarding overall. The community is open-minded, creative, and full of people who care deeply about what they’re studying and the world around them. Professors are passionate and approachable, and the small class sizes make it easy to really engage and have meaningful discussions. There’s also a great sense of independence here — you’re encouraged to explore your interests and shape your own academic path, which makes learning feel exciting and personal.
On the flip side, the freedom can sometimes feel overwhelming, especially when you’re trying to figure out your direction or balance everything. It can also get pretty intense during midterms and finals. I think the school could do a bit more to help students manage stress and find balance.

What drew me in at Wesleyan—and what kept me excited—is that the college encourages you to explore your passions, not just follow a set path.
Wesleyan opens doors—whether it’s research, internships, or networking—opportunities to grow both academically and professionally.
You're never just a number here. What I love most about Wesleyan is the strong sense of community. Professors and students genuinely want to see you succeed.
Wesleyan provided the best balance of academics, social life, and personal growth—it’s a place where you can discover yourself while preparing for your future.

Wesleyan is a great place where I have met amazing, thoughtful and intelligent people. As well as gained a completely new perspectives from professors and classes taken. Professors really have a genuine care for what they teach and take articulating that quite seriously.
Science research is pretty easy to get into as a freshman and grad school placement is excellent. Professors are top notch.
The financial aid here is AMAZING, I'm paying 1/5 of what the UC's wanted (with in-state aid) and 1/3 of what similar liberal arts schools wanted. Couldn't be happier and food is great.

ai_summary: Westminster College in Pennsylvania is characterized by a strong sense of community and personalized academic attention , with many reviews highlighting the close relationships between students and professors due to small class sizes. The charming town of New Wilmington contributes to a relaxed atmosphere , though some students mention limited recreational options outside campus. Academically, students feel well-prepared for their future careers , with many praising the quality of programs , particularly in sciences, and the dedication of faculty. However, concerns about campus dining options and the high cost of tuition are recurring themes, as are mixed feelings about administrative responsiveness to student needs. Overall, Westminster is noted for its supportive environment , making it a worthwhile choice for students seeking a small college experience with a focus on education and community.

reviews.text:
I really enjoy Westminster College as a whole, but its issues are pretty apparent now that I've been a student here for 6+ months now.
For starters, the food options are mediocre at best, which feels a little insulting for how much I have to pay for the meal plan itself. Secondly, the biggest one of them all, is that the school is obsessed with sports. There's nothing wrong with providing that as enrichment after all, but every other day the stadium where sports players go to practice is so loud you can hear the players' cheers and music echo from hundreds of yards away.
The campus is literally spread across hundreds of yards and a whole valley, and you can hear their screams and shouts from all the way at the top; it's ridiculous. The amount of noise they make is incredibly frustrating and distracting.
Besides its issues, though, everyone is quite kind on campus. The staff are kind, and so are the students.

During my time as a freshman at Westminster College, I have felt nothing but welcomed and supported by not only the professors and staff but all of the students attending the college. The academics at Westminster prepare you for your future careers and prepare you to attend grad school. The professors are so kind and compassionate towards every student, they are always willing to lend a helping hand no matter what. The athletics at Westminster are also nothing short of amazing. No matter what sport it is, the support is unmatched and everyone is so motivating towards each other. Being at Westminster this first year has showed me what a great choice I made when picking a college. Westminster is truly my home away from home and has given me such an incredible experience. I love Westminster and I am so grateful for each and every opportunity this college has given me to be the best version of myself!
Ive been able to really be myself here, along with the fact that some of my professors are exceeding my expectations when helping me. I have been keeping up with classes just fine due to that fact

I really enjoyed my time at Westminster College majoring in accounting. The small class sizes allowed the professors to mentor me in one-on-one settings. The school has twice the national average of students accepted into medical and law schools showing the quality of the education. I felt so strongly about my experience there that I encouraged my son to also attend. He leveraged his Westminster undergraduate degree into an academic scholarship at Georgetown University's master's program and a career in private equity. Westminster provided extremely strong value for the quality of education provided to both of us.

I fell in love with Westminster the first time I set foot on campus. The professors are so accessible - they truly want every student to succeed. Their STEM programs are top notch - especially for women! I made life long connections and had such a well rounded education that allowed me to thrive and grow throughout my career.

Westminster College, with nearly 175 years dedicated to Liberal Arts education, profoundly shapes students’ personal and professional growth. Its small student-to-faculty ratio ensures a supportive, relationship-focused environment. Much like my own undergraduate experience, Westminster excels at fostering intellectual, social, spiritual, and civic development through academic exploration and strong connections.

Westminster College provided an amazing academic experience; its small college environment allowed for access to faculty during their free time for both mentorship and interpersonal /career guidance. The flexibility of the curriculum granted me great exposure to a wide variety of disciplines related to my major, and also allowed me to explore other areas for my own learning and pleasure. The campus live was vibrant with a lively Greek life component; the emphasis on sports and school spirit fostered an amazing sense of community. I still keep in touch with alumni and faculty, after graduation. It really was a pivotal platform in developing critical thinking skills and an integrated knowledge base that, in turn, propelled my career, and solidified adult values that I have applied throughout my life.

Overall, Westminster has made my life great but lacking and healthy and diverse food choices really made it hard with my dietary restrictions. Also being a student color it was a huge adjustment into a PWI as well as Greek life.

I like the people here for the most part, but professors can be really narrow-minded. We also lack diversity and our food quality is incredibly poor. We pay a lot of money and it feels like it goes nowhere.

Professors are very nice and understanding. Classes are small. Food is definitely a low point. Students have expressed concerns about the dropping food quality for years. Administration seems to have very little interest in improving quality.

From the moment I began working at Westminster, it was very evident how much the faculty, administration and support staff are student focused. They want to support the students in any way they can and see them succeed. The entire campus is gorgeous, a lot of the buildings have been updated, there is so much for the students to participate in and I appreciate that the student body is becoming so much more diverse. I am so happy to be working here with such wonderful people.

Westminster College provided an opportunity for me to get away from the city to a place where I could focus on academics. Social life at the time centered around the Greek system, which also provided opportunities to develop leadership skills and volunteer/service. I developed lifetime relationships with friends and married my college sweetheart. Academically, we had a 4-1-4 calendar and I learned to use mornings and afternoons to study, to free up a few evenings each week to attend athletic or social events. WC provides many opportunities, it is up to you as an individual to figure out what you want to do and take advantage of what WC provides. I have no regrets attending WC and look back on my days as a college student with fond memories.
If there was something I would change about my college experience, it would be to have had personal computers (it was the 80's). We had to share the dozen or so computers in the computer lab with the rest of the campus.
I love the school. I wish I lived closer so I can visit the campus more often. The small town of New Wilmington was an awesome backstop to a wonderful school.

The Westminster experience was amazing. For a small school I got to experience many things I would probably never have done at a major university. I spent a semester in Europe, I met with the senior executives of 10 top Pittsburgh area based companies to discuss their business at that time, and I had 4 internships with firms all over Pittsburgh. All of this while I actually knew personally every one of the professors in my major and actually hung out with a few of them outside of class. That small school attention and more one on one teaching was a great experience and helped me to learn. I was in a fraternity at that time (1980’s) and it provided a great social life and created life long friendships. Finally my Westminster education enabled me to get into a top 10 MBA program at Duke University.

Westminster was a great college for both my undergraduate and graduate work. I am glad there were fraternities and sororities around because there would not have been much to do if not for them. The intramural athletics were fun too.

ai_summary: Westmont College offers a unique college experience marked by a strong sense of community and dedicated professors who genuinely care about their students' academic and personal growth. Many reviews highlight the close-knit atmosphere , with small class sizes fostering meaningful relationships and engagement between students and faculty. The beautiful Santa Barbara location enhances the campus experience, providing opportunities for outdoor activities. However, some reviewers express concerns over diversity, inclusivity, and the college's high cost, despite available scholarships. The social scene at Westmont is often described as centered around faith-based activities rather than traditional partying, which may appeal to some students while leaving others feeling isolated. Overall, Westmont stands out for its committed faculty and supportive community but may not be the best fit for those seeking a more diverse or typical college experience.

reviews.text: Westmont has a bit of a "church camp" reputation, and while I think that's true to some extent, if you're like me and never thrived in that environment--don't worry! I absolutely found my people, and I found a lot of them. If you're looking for a school where you feel seen by your professors and peers, this is a good place for you! Professor--student connections are easy to make and incredibly beneficial (especially if you're trying to get a research position or a glowing letter of recommendation). People really love Westmont, and if you're willing to look a little silly and love it too, it'll be a great time.

I like my time at Westmont, but I feel like my counselor doesn’t help me schedule my classes. I feel like it’s not fair that I need to take an internship class to be able to intern at a church and get paid.

Westmont is a truly great school. The academics are intellectually stimulating and challenging, and the campus is beautiful. Though it make take a while to settle in, there are many genuine students who care for you and will want to make freshmen feel welcomed. Food is also very good at best and mediocre at worst so far.

I haven't been at this school longer than a month and the fire alarm has gone off at least ten times throughout the whole campus. Some people here are impossible to get along with, but the teachers are pretty good at their jobs.
Absolutely incredible 4 years here. If I could live at Westmont forever I would. Community is engaging and real.

Westmont is a good school academically. the professors always go out of their way to make sure the students are succeeding and even help outside of their office hour times. this school is perfect for people focusing on their education and don't care so much about partying, etc...
Amazing school! Lots to do, great community. Very safe. I really enjoy the whole atmosphere, the students are great.

I love how welcoming Westmont is with all of their students coming from different places just like me. Not only that their campus is very beautiful and taken care of. Everyone there is very nice and will help you if you need help with anything!

Looking back as a Westmont alum, what really stands out are the people and the everyday moments. I made some of my best friends there—hanging out in the dorms, grabbing coffee after class, or just walking around campus. Being on the tennis team was a blast. We trained hard, but we also had a lot of fun and built a real bond. Playing in the chapel band was one of my favorite parts of college. It was cool to lead worship with friends and be part of something meaningful. Westmont felt like home, and I still miss it sometimes.
Great opportunity I am blessed to go there just too expensive I would like for there to be more options to help you pay for tuition it’s expensive

Westmont provides a valuable liberal arts education with small classroom size geared for productive in-class discussion and stronger professor-student relationships. I enjoy campus life with a club for every occasion. Although the student population is not remarkably diverse, I enjoy connecting with fellow people of color through our Intercultural Programs.

At Westmont College, the people are very nice and it is a very great community. The classes aren't large so the professors are able to get to know each of their students and are able to help them out in any way possible. They are always trying to help us achieve our goals.

was part of an athletic team for four years. great coaches and teammates, all professors were very accommodating for athletes. the santa barbara area is the most beautiful place i have ever lived. I would love to go back, very loving and caring community.

I transferred out of Westmont after one semester, but I really wanted to like it there. Like others have said, if you don't fit the stereotype of people there, you will struggle to make lifelong friends. Sure, everyone there is really nice, but that's about it. If you are an adventurous, spontaneous person who is afraid of being average (not in the academic sense, rather in your approach to life) you will have a hard time fitting in at Westmont. People there are very similar to each other, it's hard to describe the exact personality but everyone is very sheltered/homeschooled type kids. For me it was an extremely boring and mundane experience where every day felt the same. 90% of campus events are some new kind of bible study thing (seriously).
Academics wise, it's okay... If you are a STEM major, DO NOT GO TO WESTMONT. Especially engineering, the program is a joke compared to other colleges. My high school had far better engineering. Don't make the same mistake I did and waste $20k.

Though many classes are intellectually stimulating, if you're looking to develop professionally, this probably is not the place for you. Outside a small community, there is a lack of respect in an era of prestige. Not many will get the top consulting/finance/software jobs or companies without an alternative connection. But Westmont is a small Christian liberal arts school, so I can’t complain. Socially, there's definitely a type. As stated by others, it is Bible camp lovers. If this is you, this is your place, go! Definitely a lot less going on than your "normal" university, any struggle to find a clique after the first semester. The blissful, serene campus silence can morph into personal madness (kidding). Food mid, dorms mid, and no cars in early college can feel like a prison sentence. Ok, I am being a little too harsh. I don’t share the criticisms about diversity or the administration. They actively push for diversity and work to balance complex issues while listening to students.

ai_summary: At Wheaton College in Massachusetts, students often highlight the close-knit community and the supportive faculty as standout features. The small class sizes foster strong relationships between students and professors, enabling personalized attention and mentorship. However, the college has faced challenges like limited course offerings due to faculty layoffs, which can hinder academic progress in certain majors. Student life can feel cliquey, with a lack of diverse social activities, and many express disappointment with the dining options and campus facilities, including older dorms. While the college is situated near Boston and Providence for off-campus activities, the immediate area lacks vibrancy. Tuition is considered high, and some reviews indicate that financial aid services could improve, particularly for low-income students. Overall, Wheaton is seen as a good choice for those seeking a small liberal arts college atmosphere, provided they are aware of its limitations.

reviews.text: Wheaton is honestly a pretty good school. What they lack in resources and academics, they make up for with really good partnerships and study away programs. Professors are really helpful and will want to build a genuine relationship with you if you try. Class size is small and participation is heavily encouraged. There are campus events almost every week which makes up for how boring the local area is. Weekends can be boring if you don’t have a car or a friend with a car because there is truly nothing to do in Norton and there are no events either. Parties are what you would expect at a small liberal arts school. They’re fun if you’re with a good group of friends and don’t mind seeing the same people every weekend. Usually pretty tame and lowkey. Wheaton is a predominantly white school but what they lack in student body diversity, they make up for with regular intercultural events and big cultural club awareness on campus. The food leaves much to be desired but is usually alright.

I think the dining situation needs to be fixed. A big draw to Wheaton was all the different options. As someone with a strict dietary restriction, I was excited by how Wheaton had organized dining. Now, I've gotten food I cannot eat multiple times, which costs real money. I think the professors are wonderful and do a great job connecting to the students.

Wheaton College has a flexible curriculum and amazing, dedicated professors. I love getting to easily double major and still have time to take random classes like photography, acting, or sociology just because they interest me, since I don't have gen ed requirements. I appreciate Wheaton's dedication to providing students with generous merit scholarships. There are so many clubs and organizations, so lots of little communities to find your place in. I feel like I'm always at an event making a craft or getting free food or watching a performance. Norton itself doesn't have the most to do, but we have the woods and I still go to Boston or Providence sometimes, and I'm glad to have access to nature and to city.

I have had a wonderful time living and studying at Wheaton College (MA). As a senior, my last four years have been full of great friends, loud laughs, fun assignments, and much more. I know so many people across campus who collaborate to create a pleasant campus environment. I particularly love my professors in the History department, who support and push me not only through my college career but also into what comes after. I am forever grateful to the Wheaton community for making this place my home.

I have really enjoyed my experience at Wheaton College so far. I believe that the open curriculum really let's you explore different departments, and enrich your experience. I love working on one of our literary magazines, and as intern in the marketing department.

As an international student, i love the small community we have here. everyone is so nice and supportive. the professors are so nice too! you get to go to so many events on campus and you can find your own people. the location is a bit off since it’s in norton and doesn’t have much things to do but if you have a car you can go to nearby places! and the train station is near by too

Wheaton is truly what you make of it! I have so many amazing friends that I have met by being involved in different clubs and attending events. The compass curriculum is really nice, I am en education major and I don’t have to take courses that are not related to my major. The food is not the best but many people cook with their friends, everyone has access to a kitchen in their theme house or dorm. The big downside is that there is nothing you can really walk to in Norton. You need friends with cars or you will be bored.
Great experience. Exactly what you make your experience is what it will be, make an effort and connect with student groups and you’ll be busy and fulfilled!

Small liberal art school with very open communities that fall on a liberal standpoint if you’re pursuing humanities, strong for biology/chemistry majors and nursing + pre-med track is a common destination. Professors are dedicated but choose them well to not lose time and expect to be competitive aside from campus life connecting with other seniors/already graduates class and build long term connections with key profiles.

Wheaton College allowed for me to explore and expand upon my passions beyond just what I was studying. Thanks to the diverse student body and hands-on campus life offices, I was able to join many different clubs and live in one of our residential Theme Houses, which helped me truly find my community within this school. As far as academics, thanks to the helpful and attentive professors, I was able to find my major path and explore a field of study that was incredibly meaningful to me. On top of this, Wheaton staff assisted me in securing many volunteer and internship positions that support my entrance into a career field that aligned with my studies. Now, as a senior, Wheaton has given me the resources and support to apply and get accepted into graduate school - therefore helping me successfully secure my future.

My overall experience at Wheaton has been amazing. Before coming to Wheaton, I was nervous about fitting in. However, once I arrived on campus, I quickly noticed how friendly and welcoming everyone was. I found a very supportive group of friends who I now call my home away from home. Wheaton has helped me step out of my comfort zone, meet new people, and explore different activities. Because of this, I even discovered how fun rugby is. Academically, the environment is very flexible, and the professors are supportive and understanding. Wheaton has helped me grow and realize how important social connections and networking are.
I really enjoy and the small community we have . And also the support system is really and professors support is great .

Overall, people like to complain about everything, but deep down I actually cannot imagine going anywhere else. I love the friends I’ve made, most of my classes have been really good, and most of the professors I’ve had have seemed to really enjoy what they do.

Wheaton offers great scholarships and that’s why most people end up coming here. It is a very progressive school and because of its size you can really get to know your professors. However, it is a bit in the middle of nowhere. There are many ways to be involved and be a student leader.

I really loved Wheaton college, it is very community oriented. People are all so friendly and welcoming, and the teachers to student ratio is super helpful for you to get to know your professors in a deeper level.

ai_summary: Whitman College is characterized by its tight-knit community and beautiful campus in the charming town of Walla Walla, which many students find appealing. Academically, it's recognized for its engaged professors and rigorous classes, but experiences can vary widely among them. Many reviews highlight the accessibility and support of faculty, with a strong emphasis on small class sizes that foster interaction. Socially, while some students find it easy to make connections, others feel a lack of diversity and inclusivity, particularly for marginalized groups, leading to feelings of isolation for some individuals. Reviews also indicate that the college has good dining options and an active outdoor program , yet concerns about tuition and the administration's commitment to student welfare, particularly regarding issues of diversity and safety, persist. Overall, Whitman is seen as a solid liberal arts institution, but it is important for prospective students to consider the campus culture and overall environment when making their decision.

reviews.text:
Whitman is an excellent college both academically and in terms of community. I’m nearing the end of my freshman year and have absolutely loved my experience. The school offers strong support systems, especially through its Community Engagement and Career Counseling resources. With their help refining my resume and job search skills, I secured three jobs during my first semester.
Academically, my experience has been just as rewarding. Although Whitman has limited filmmaking courses, its film studies classes are outstanding—I’ve already taken three and recently declared a major in Film and Media Studies. Professors are approachable, supportive, and genuinely invested in students’ growth.
Whitman also creates meaningful opportunities, such as fully student-led theater productions where students write, direct, and perform their own work, fostering creativity and leadership.

I really enjoyed my time at Whitman. There are many options of ways to get involved in campus life, and I was engaged in multiple of those. I felt welcomed by the community, cared for by the professors, and very supported by the career coaches. I might like to see Whitman College provide more opportunities for students to have a job or internship that counts toward academic credit in a certain relevant subject area. I know of one course that allows for that (environmental internship), but it would be good to provide more of those types of classes in other subject areas as well.
This college is honestly a great experience and very accommodating to other stuff going on and if you need more of finances.

Whitman is a great college for a well-rounded education and quality of life. The small town atmosphere lends well to cultivating meaningful relationships with professors, students, and staff that last well beyond college. These one-on-one interactions help students really discover who they are and can serve as excellent preparation for graduate school. I've even met some underclassmen doing graduate-level research with their professors (thanks to them being able to explore their passions and learn of openings in their preferred fields of study). They're innovating the curriculum to enable students to combine disparate interests into cohesive passions, so there's something for everyone. A great place to spend your formative years as an academic and as a person.

Whitman is a great school academically. Every professor I have interacted with has been super encouraging and committed to my success. The campus is clean, safe, and very pretty all year round. Every student looks forward to the spring when the ducklings are roaming around. Whitman has a lot of great opportunities attached to it in name alone and a great alumni network that is super important for post college life.

The opportunities offered, especially in STEM, are incredible. Professors are likely the best in the country, students are some of the most frustrating. Maybe a majority, but more likely an extraordinarily loud minority were rich kids from Seattle/Portland/Bay/SoCal, or kids from UWC (United World Colleges). This group is scared of the surrounding town in the people in it, referring to every local as a "townie," and dismissing them as dirty and/or not worth their time. Working class students are quietly ostracized, as well as students without previous "worldly" experience. Extremism is rampant, with a Portland-level of ideology forcing and identity politics. All drugs are normalized, weed and drinking is common, and the party scene is small but well-established. Unfortunately this school is filled with people who don't care enough about their lives to be productive and respectful while they're here, or people who do care but are too scared to establish themselves as an individual.

I had a pretty good experience at Whitman college and it is a memorable and strong community. however I have trouble recommending Whitman college to any incoming students as they have slowly become slumlords, focusing on charging students rent instead of providing them quality education. They will do everything they can to squeeze every dollar of the room and board out of you and they have recently brought up a huge amount of the off campus housing in a bid to force students to stick to their more expensive rent and meal plan. They also might throw you in a dorm like North hall, which is an old barely renovated hospital, and charge you the same amount as everyone else. Don’t go here unless you want to deal with the slumlords in the housing department

As a transfer student, Whitman was my second chance to get that real college experience. What originally brought me to Whitman was the friendly, chill atmosphere combined with the academically rigorous courses. Whitman has also received a massive boost in funding recently thanks to the Upward Together campaign and some other donor events. As a first-generation, low-income student, Whitman was originally daunting since I was coming from a multi-billion dollar endowment prestigious research university and I thought I'd get less financial aid due to their lower endowment. Luckily, and to my surprise, the recent donations have greatly increased their ability to give aid (now meeting 100% of demonstrated need), landing me a full ride alongside a $30k merit scholarship and a fly-in.
I believe more colleges should follow their example of making school affordable and accessible, as it results in an alumni network who love their school and want to give back. Whitman is on the rise, IMO.

Serious academics. Beautiful campus. Lovely downtown and countryside.
Whitman is a strong liberal arts school in a beautiful setting. Student are curious and hard working. It's a pretty wealthy student body which can be engaging/helpful but can spill into priveledge/ arrogance. Low but rising level of minorities. Amazing outdoor opportunities.
One annoying challenge the availability of courses. Admin just can't seem to get it right. Prof's go on leave with no replacements. Popular courses don't get added sessions. Not a fatal flaw, but pretty annoying.

Whitman College has a beautiful campus, fun clubs, and engaging professors. They provide ample academic support and opportunities. That being said, it is very expensive and I've personally found that the opportunities to engage in my area of study are lacking.
Ever since coming here I have loved it. The sports culture here is welcoming. The food is delicious and has a wide variety of choices which is nice.

I’m the parent of a second-year Whitman student who has been thriving and is truly satisfied with their experience so far. As a parent, it’s natural to hope your child finds a place where they are challenged to grow, lead, and discover more about themselves. I’m pleased to say Whitman has been a wonderful fit for our student. They’ve shared especially high praise for the professors, the academic rigor, and the engaging class environment.

Whitman is a fundamentally unsafe place for marginalized and disabled students. The school will interfere with your healthcare, will not lift a finger to keep you safe, and will tell you to drop out if they see you as a liability (after putting you on the admissions pamphlet of course). Because the school is so isolated, you will find yourself in a health desert with few local resources. The only reason I stayed all four years was that I had amazing professors who took the time to help me advocate for myself. The current admin is incompetent, corrupt, and routinely covers up sexual assault to save the school's reputation. Unless you straight, white, rich, able bodied, and mentally well, the school and environment is hostile and hateful. Go somewhere that values you as a person and has local resources and healthcare. Whitman doesn't even provide health insurance to out of state students.
Whitman College has a great Outdoors Program for all students. The academics are rigorous. Though, It's a small school, the community here is great. It is easier to make close friends.
Like all colleges, Whitman College has their pros and cons. Most professors are amazing! The food is good for college cafeteria food.

ai_summary: Whittier College is characterized by its small class sizes and a close-knit community , which allows students to build meaningful relationships with professors and peers. Many reviews highlight the accessibility and willingness of faculty to provide individualized support , contributing to a positive academic experience. However, the college faces criticism regarding its student life, with some noting a lack of activities and a subdued social scene , particularly in comparison to larger institutions. The campus itself is described as beautiful and welcoming , though there are concerns about the quality of dorms and the cost of tuition. Additionally, while students appreciate the diversity on campus, some expressed frustrations with the administration and the availability of resources. Overall, Whittier offers a supportive educational environment, but prospective students should weigh the potential drawbacks in terms of campus life and cost.

reviews.text:
My overall experience at Whittier College has been positive and growth-oriented. I appreciate the small class sizes because they allow for meaningful discussions and strong connections with professors. The faculty are approachable and genuinely care about student success, which creates a supportive academic environment. I also value the close-knit campus community. It’s easy to get involved in clubs, athletics, and leadership opportunities, and students are encouraged to take initiative.
One change I would like to see is stronger career development support, including more internship connections and alumni networking opportunities. Expanding campus events and increasing school spirit would also enhance student life. Overall, Whittier offers a supportive and engaging college experience.

I went on a football visit to Whitter College, which involved experiencing every single aspect of the college life. The tour went over the academics, athletics, student life, and overall college lifestyle at Whitter College. It went over each subject very well and covered every single aspect in great detail. I have no doubt the life at Whitter College is something unimaginable. Just from a 3 hour tour, I was able to experience and witness the many different things Whitter has to offer, from the uncountable opportunities to the incredible person it helps you turn into!

I love Whittier much more than I initially thought I would when I first applied. Campus life, professors, and the student-to-professor ratio make me feel at home here, in an environment that caters to how I learn best. Everyone on campus is incredibly kind and accommodating to me, as well as other students, when I need it. Whenever someone asks me how I like it here, I say I love it, and they should apply too.
Whittier College is a good school. It's small, so if you don't want a big school, this is the place for you!

The food and the dorms are both pretty good and having the campus be small is advantageous when you don’t want to be late to classes. But if your looking for a vibrant party scene it is not my first choice e
I like how the community is at Whittier and how there are many things to do on campus. I do wish activities were a bit timed better for commuters.

Whittier College is a great place for community, and community gets you EVERYWHERE. The professors are super passionate and hands-on, so it’s very easy to develop relationships with them and go beyond a ‘usual’ academic experience. The campus always has something going on, from daily events to large-scale events that let you sleep over at a ski lodge for a day for less than $50, meals included. The experience is really unique, and you build friendships and relationships that are really meaningful and last. Whittier College is so fun, and really gives you the opportunity to grow as a person, but do unique things. There’s a lot of potential, and it’s very easy to get involved and get worthwhile experiences.
It is a very small school but gives such a strong family feeling. The class sizes are small, so it gives more 1-on-1 learning.

Whittier College is quite small, the campus is made up predominately of athletes. Kinesiology is the most popular major, most of my teammates were studying it. The campus is beautiful as is the rest of Whittier. The city is full of restaurants and small businesses to explore.
It's been amazing! With the amount of students growing every year, campus life is thriving! I've met many amazing people, and feel so lucky to call Whittier College home.

I love the small community here at Whittier College. I see so many familiar faces everyday as I walk to classes and other buildings, making it easier to form friendships and connections. The small classes sizes also greatly help, as it is easier to get to know the professor and get extra help outside of class. The athletic teams on campus are very active and the amount of support that large home games get is heartwarming.

I have loved my experience at Whittier College. I have made so many friends and connections. My favorite part about Whittier is how familiar you get with faculty, even if you only took one class from them!

Whittier College is a very communal school. You get the opportunity to build relationships with professors and staff, allowing for more stuff within the community. It is a small school, so everyone is a mutual friend. It has a wonderful community, and really gives small town vibes.

Whittier is a great college if you want to get involved and learn how to do things hands-on. Since it is such a small school, I have gotten experiences to do things such as research in biology. I would not have gotten these opportunities at other school for nearly as easy as I do here. I am also able to balance a major, minor, athletics, and a job all at the same time here while also being able to explore Los Angeles.

My overall experience here at Whittier College, was very much of growing as an individual. I was able to meet multiple people from different parts of the world, and also get to know myself as a student leader here on campus. I was able to be part of so many communities here on campus and my relationship with professors is something, I was not expecting. My professors and I here at Whittier College have a very close connections and I feel very heard, seen, and felt very welcomed.

ai_summary: At Willamette University, students frequently highlight the close-knit community and strong relationships formed with professors due to small class sizes. Many reviews emphasize the supportive atmosphere , with faculty genuinely invested in student success and personal growth. Academically, the courses are praised for their rigor, allowing for enriching discussions and unique learning experiences, although some students express dissatisfaction with the limited diversity and dining options. Student life is described as less focused on partying or sports, favoring a more academic-centered culture, which may appeal to those looking for a quieter college experience . While the beauty of the campus and its location in downtown Salem receive positive remarks, there are concerns about the university’s cost and resources, particularly in terms of facilities and administration responsiveness to student needs. Overall, students appreciate the opportunities for personal development and the unique collegiate environment , despite some drawbacks.

reviews.text: Willamette is a very open and inclusive space and is also very central. Being downtown Salem means you are close to stores, parks, the Willamette River, and there's plenty to do. Willamette regularly has shuttles to take students to do activities further away as well, some of my friends are skiers/snowboarders and Willamette occasionally has shuttles that take them to Mt. Hood. The people here are very open to people of all backgrounds and are very dedicated to caring for the environment and each other. Being downtown does come with being close to protests that occasionally turn violent, but anybody participating is not allowed on campus for student and faculty safety.

Willamette is honestly a great school, they are just bad with communication--especially about money. There is great opportunity for social life, but I will say that if you don't make friends right in the beginning it can get increasingly harder to make them as people settle in to their groups. Joining clubs is a great way to try and remedy this--I personally joined Dance Co. and AXO! Most professors are great, but of course there's a few that you hear about being extra tough or kooky. I will say eating at Goudy is not pleasant, and considering the cost of the meal plan, they REALLY need to step it up. The party scene is also lacking, but there definitely is one! Most parties are thrown at the same house by the same people, but there's also the occasional house show with one of the school bands. Honestly, of you find the right people to go with, any party is fun (but definitely small). Housing the first year is rough but after that it can be nice depending on which dorm you get!
Willamette is a very nice school with a lot of potential! Has history and bring good things from that!

I am so grateful I attended Willamette! While there were things that I struggled with or disliked about WU while I attended, after 2+ years post-grad I am very happy with the education I received.
The professors are amazing and I enjoyed the vast majority of my classes. At least in my program, the professors were extremely knowledgeable while also dedicated to teaching and mentoring their students. I still keep in contact with several of my professors to this day.
The various clubs, sports, fraternities, and sororities make it easy to find people you have common interests with and can help create lasting friendships. Plus the small campus makes it easy to run into people you know, or to walk anywhere to meet.
The food at Goudy left a lot to be desired, especially if you have any dietary restrictions. The dorms were fine when I attended, though I believe they are becoming more crowded now. Several boilers for dorms were down during various periods, but I never was affected.

I personally believe that Willamette University is an excellent institution. I appreciate the opportunity to have close connections with my professors. Many different types of clubs can suit many people's tastes. Everyone is also very welcoming to new students. I also believe that because we are such a small campus, there are many different opportunities for students, whether it's leadership roles, jobs, or internships.

The campus was beautiful and the people were amazing - students, faculty, and staff. I appreciated the locally sourced food at Goudy, as well as variety. There were so many fun sporting events. Willamette is small but mighty - and I hope more students find it and have the amazing experience I did.
It's a great university. I really like the outdoor activities, the ability to do research and participate in the hearths.

Willamette University stands out as an excellent choice thanks to its strong liberal arts foundation, intimate learning environment, and unparalleled proximity to the Oregon State Capitol. The university boasts small class sizes, and abundant opportunities for direct engagement with professors—leading to high scores for student–faculty interaction. Its location directly across from the state capitol opens doors to political internships and civic involvement, uniquely integrating academic learning with real‑world experience . These strengths—rigorous academics, engaged faculty, hands‑on opportunities, and civic connectivity—make Willamette a compelling option for students seeking both personal growth and professional launch.

I valued the connections I made with my professors. The school prioritizes relationships with professors by maintaining small classroom sizes, which helps students receive the necessary resources for their learning and education.

The university is quite small located in the heart of Salem right across from the capitol building. The academics are relatively difficult but the teachers seem to truly care about the students. However, some teachers feel like they’re only there to have their own personal research developed. There are no summer classes but instead opportunities to work with the teachers on their research projects. Its not easy to find community here when transferring, but if you start as a freshman here, you’re entered into a colloquium which bonds you to your peers.

Overall I enjoyed going to Willamette. The small campus made it easy to get to know people and I really liked having smaller, discussion based classes instead of large lectures. I loved that it was easy to find research opportunities as a undergraduate, as I've heard that can be difficult at other universities. The diversity of students could be better, and tuition is comparable to similar schools but still very expensive.

From the perspective of someone whose first language is not English, Willamette provides a really good education. The teaching style is easy to follow and suitable for non-native speakers, as well as freshmen just starting out. What I like the most is the variety in the arts department. You can have a dream and realize it at Willamette because they consider every major to be equally important, and you have a voice no matter what major you choose.
Another thing that stands out is Bearcat Day and the Opening Day programs. These events make you feel connected to the people there, even if you're still deciding on your choice. They give you a 24-hour vibe of what it’s like to be a Bearcat.
There is always something to do: conferences, activities, club meetings, guest speakers. You can't get bored living on campus because every day offers a new opportunity to learn from these events.
I’d like to see more diversity in instructors and more food options for students without meal plans.

My overall experience with willamentte has been the best experience of my life , iv never had so much fun in my life. I have had so much fun being able to learn new things i never thought that id be doing. its been so helpfull and confidence boosting

Aside from a few standout professors, this school has no redeeming qualities. Adjunct professors teach poorly and many departments are filled with them. The lack of student life is astounding. Nothing to do in the surrounding area campus consists of a tiny insular community that gets stale fast. Wouldn't wish it on my worst enemy.

Pros: awesome classes and professors. Professors are understanding of your needs and classes are very engaging. The administration is generally very friendly and no matter who you ask, your question always gets answered or forwarded to someone who can answer it for you.
Cons: Small school means a good amount of gossip and the like. The food is uninspiring at best.

My time at Williams College was severely impacted by the Sars-Cov-2 pandemic, but I ultimately received a really excellent education from some amazing professors who I knew really cared about me and my learning. Williamstown is a beautiful location, but student life leaves some to be desired.

At Williams I have enjoyed the amount of opportunities available in research despite not being a Research 1 university. I have also enjoyed living in a rural environment because I did not grow up in one and do not see myself living in one in the future.

I’ve had an excellent experience at Williams College so far. The student life is vibrant and diverse, and the academic environment is strong, with engaging courses taught by highly accomplished professors. Campus life is always active, from fairs and conferences to off-campus opportunities like skiing at Jiminy Peak, and the sports culture adds even more energy to the community.

Academics and professors are top-tier. The only downside is the location. You don't notice it much because there's so much going on on campus. You'll only notice it if you need to leave for a specific reason.

The career resources and opportunities provided are easy to access as the alumni base is also extremely helpful. Residential living in regards to dorms is amazing, most students at Williams College have singles (no roommate), and usually only have a roommate one out of the four years they are here. The biggest downfall of this school is the dining hall food and the lack of activity in the local area.
My experience at Williams College was great. super easy to get to know professors and even get research with them. they truly want to see you succeed. students are very friendly as well.
I really liked the support from the psychology department that I received. They were so many opportunities for research and to grow and be advising by wonderful professors and other faculty members.

The academics at this institution are unmatched, you are being educated by world-class professors, who care deeply about your learning. The career resources and opportunities provided are easy to access as the alumni base is also extremely helpful. Residential living in regards to dorms is amazing, most students at Williams College have singles (no roommate), and usually only have a roommate one out of the four years they are here. The biggest downfall of this school is the dining hall food and the lack of activity in the local area.

Williams College is often considered one of the best schools because of its incredible sense of community. The people here truly make the place special, fostering an environment where everyone encourages each other to excel. Whether it’s through late-night study sessions, cheering each other on at athletic events, or simply offering a kind word when someone is feeling overwhelmed, the support among students is unmatched. This culture of encouragement not only drives personal success but also creates lasting bonds that define the Williams experience. It’s this collective spirit that sets Williams apart, making it more than just a school—it’s a home.

My son is a sophomore. He cannot be happier in his college choice. He has a group of close friends, with a looser group of lots of others. Williams is an academic rigorous school; yet provides a supportive environment. Living in a beautiful rural area, students can really focus on their learning and growth without a lot of distraction, such as bars, clubs, etc. A lot of students do a study abroad in their junior year to balance their learning at a small, rural LAC environment with a more cosmopolitan one. Also, the school's "Winter Study" program is outstanding providing many experiential learnings, everything from doing internships in Boston to visiting companies in NYC. Five stars!
The school is quite special. There's a sort of ineffable community and love for place that you can't find elsewhere.

I really enjoyed my time at Williams College! It is what you make of it - if you reach out and get involved in things you are 100% going to meet really cool, passionate, and extremely smart people that drive you to be better yourself. If you don't- your experience definitely won't be as good. Most professors are excellent and are focused on helping undergraduate students thrive through research and learning.
I was part of an athletics team and it really made my time enjoyable there. It was tough to balance everything sometimes but it prepared me for after college life really well. I will say that the food as an athlete was sometimes tough to find enough protein options. Also, the party scene is there but isn't anything crazy.

I would like to see more diversity. The campus community is amazing, it is very easy to find people to connect with. The professors are 10/10. Since the school is small, the professors are always there for the students. They always have office hours and there are other types of support available.
Williams was cool. I enjoyed my time there being a student-athlete and a musician. It’s the people you meet along the way that make the place so special.

This is a charming, safe space to get away from everything. There is nature everywhere you look, and there are SO MANY resources for when you're struggling. Because it's so small, it's an extremely safe school, and you don't have to worry about your possessions disappearing if you leave them somewhere. However, the size also means that there aren't a lot of crazy events with LOTS of people (this can be comforting but sometimes isolating). Most people have cars, though, so even if you don't have one, some of your friends are bound to, and going on trips over the weekend is a common pastime and super fun. Returning to your safe little home after is so lovely, and you can get the best of both worlds. Winter is tough because of the cold and seasonal depression but that happens everywhere in New England. If you can't afford to go somewhere warm over breaks, Williams gives students who receive financial aid like 900 dollars a semester (not necessarily enough for all needs, but it's sweet).
Wofford and its professors help any student reach their full potential. All the professors care immensely about their students.

Wofford is a college centered around community; it is a school that truly feels like one big family. Students live on campus for all four years: freshmen in traditional dorms, sophomores and juniors in suite-style housing, and seniors in the senior village with Charleston-style homes. Living on campus contributes to our sense of community and provides a fun and safe party scene. Interim is the best thing ever and allows you to take a class on campus, travel, or get experience in research or work.
Wofford College is a great school for anyone looking to find their second home. The staff and students are as welcoming as they are kind and their really is a place for everyone.

I felt at home at Wofford since I stepped on campus. There is something for everyone at Wofford, and it didn't take long for me to find clubs, organizations, and activities that appealed to me and helped me connect with some of my best friends. I would consider Wofford to be very aware of ways they can help their students get involved, and are constantly hosting events, speakers, interest fairs, etc. Admittedly, the parking situation isn't perfect, and is my only complaint when it comes to on-campus living (the food is great as far as college cafeterias go, and the dorms are well maintained and conveniently located to make walking to class super easy). Although I've never been forced to park off campus, just not in the lot I would prefer. All of the professors I have had have been wonderful, and truly care about the students and their success inside and outside of the classroom. Not only do they teach the class content, but many teach life and graduate school skills.

Wofford has by far been one of the best decisions I have ever made. Although I was hesitant about college in the beginning, the sense of community at this school created a comfortable atmosphere to be myself and make friends. Everyone at the school truly cares about education, and the professors are very invested in their classes, as well as making personal connections with each student. With a rigorous course load, there are still many opportunities to relax on campus and connect with others through a variety of different organizations. The campus is always in pristine condition, and all the facilities are well cared for. Everyone at Wofford wants what is best for you and makes sure to emphasize the importance of connection. At Wofford, you will have a variety of opportunities for networking, internships, abroad programs, undergraduate research, and educational achievements.
Horrible college. numerous attempts too persuade students out of perfect academic standing. Unrecognizable to many of the worlds prestigious institutions.

I love Wofford! I feel like I've improved so much on all levels over the past 2 years, and I can't wait to come back in the fall! I've seriously made some of my greatest connections at Wofford, and made amazing friends. Everyone here is so nice, and really driven academically. It really pushes you to take on more/go out of your comfort zone, and I really feel like it's taught me so much about my own career priorities and time management skills. Interim is an incredibly major aspect of Wofford- I'm able to have access to so many fun things during that time that I wouldn't have had otherwise. I really appreciate Wofford's overall care for their students, it's clear in everything that they do that they strive for improvement and excellence in all areas.

Reflecting back on my first-year experience at Wofford, it has overall been a wonderful experience. In terms of academics, Wofford caps classes at 25 students with a few override exceptions. Therefore, you are able to get to know your professors very well. I also like the small classes because it allows me to stay focused better compared to a 100+ person lecture hall. Secondly, your classes will always be taught by a professor with the highest level degree they can earn in their field because Wofford is a college, not a university, meaning you will never have a class that is taught by a graduate student or a TA. Lastly in terms of academics, if you are struggling in a particular class, the school offers countless resources to help bring you up to speed. Meeting with professors during office hours is very easy because of Wofford's small student population, and you also have the option to meet with a TA or peer tutor for the class you are struggling in.

I love Wofford so far, I love how the party scene isn't too crazy to where you are distracted from school but it is a good balance to where you can have fun without sacrificing your grades. I love the small community and knowing someone everywhere I go. The only complaints I have are with parking and housing.

So far, I've had an amazing experience at Wofford. The campus is beautiful and professors are great for the most part. I love the small class sizes because it allows me to form close connections with my professors and peers. Wofford is also the perfect balance between academics and socializing. Go Terriers!!

I have had a great experience at Wofford in every capacity. There are many clubs, activities, and other involvement opportunities. I am in Greek life and have found an amazing group of women and friends within and outside of Greek life. I have also found an on campus student job and there are many options for students to work. The classes and professors are amazing and I have had no problem with being advised or finding professors to go to about questions. There is such a great community at Wofford, the majority of students live on campus all 4 years and it is so fun and convenient to live with all your friends and 5 minutes away from your school buildings. There is not enough good things I could say about this school and the friends I have made here at this school.

Choosing to attend Wofford College is one of the greatest decisions I ever made. It has everything I was looking for in a collegiate education including a small student-faculty ratio, a lively campus, academic rigor, and on-campus housing all four years of college. I have grown exponentially as a student by taking a variety of liberal arts classes and building on my academic strengths. In addition to this, I have also grown as a person. My social skills have developed beyond what I thought was ever possible, and I have had the opportunity to study abroad and experience life beyond Wofford's borders, which is highly encouraged by this college. Wofford's community may be small compared to other colleges, but its impact is deeply meaningful.

I didn't love Wofford, but if you fit in with the traditional southern crowd you’ll likely be fine. You can find all sorts of people, but the status quo can be pretty suffocating. There are plenty of social opportunities on campus. Professors are passionate about their classes and accessible. The administration cares much more about appearing safe and diverse than being it. Don’t expect support from the administration for sexual assault cases.
It is a great place to be for those who appreciate it. Small, tight knit community that gives what you want, if that’s what you want. Good academics, sports, people.

Wofford is great if you already have friends who go there or know other people going there in your grade. As an outsider, it is extremely hard to fit in. The extremely conservative majority of voices on campus can be isolating and many went to high schools that failed to teach them proper sex ed and aren’t very accepting of people who aren’t straight and white. This school is great for building connections and career opportunities. But at what cost? Sometimes it can get so exhausting being surrounded by a student body that doesn’t even care about your own rights. The dining hall food is awful, it’s made me throw up on multiple occasions and this campus is in no way a safe space. The scholarship and study abroad opportunities are magnificent and there are small groups on campus that are accepting and make campus better. I just think it’s important to note the struggles as well. Wofford at times can feel very cliquish and hard to meet people
